title: PLDR-LLMs Reason At Self-Organized Criticality
summary: We show that PLDR-LLMs pretrained at self-organized criticality exhibit reasoning at inference time. The characteristics of PLDR-LLM deductive outputs at criticality is similar to second-order phase transitions. At criticality, the correlation length diverges, and the deductive outputs attain a metastable steady state. The steady state behaviour suggests that deductive outputs learn representations equivalent to scaling functions, universality classes and renormalization groups from the training dataset, leading to generalization and reasoning capabilities in the process. We can then define an order parameter from the global statistics of the model's deductive output parameters at inference. The reasoning capabilities of a PLDR-LLM is better when its order parameter is close to zero at criticality. This observation is supported by the benchmark scores of the models trained at near-criticality and sub-criticality. Our results provide a self-contained explanation on how reasoning manifests in large language models, and the ability to reason can be quantified solely from global model parameter values of the deductive outputs at steady state, without any need for evaluation of curated benchmark datasets through inductive output for reasoning and comprehension.

AI.tldr: PLDR-LLMs pretrained at self-organized criticality exhibit inference-time reasoning, with deductive outputs at criticality behaving like second-order phase transitions. An order parameter derived from global inference-time statistics near zero correlates with stronger reasoning; reasoning can be quantified from steady-state model parameters without relying on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Provide a physics-inspired explanation for emergent reasoning in large language models and establish a global, dataset-agnostic metric for reasoning capability.
AI.method: Frame PLDR-LLMs within self-organized criticality and phase-transition theory. Define an order parameter from global statistics of deductive outputs at inference and analyze correlation length, metastable steady states, and scaling-function-like representations. Compare near-critical and sub-critical models using benchmark performance to assess reasoning.
AI.result: At criticality, correlation length diverges and deductive outputs reach a metastable steady state. Outputs appear to learn representations akin to scaling functions, universality classes, and renormalization group structures from the training data, fostering generalization and reasoning. The proposed order parameter, computed from global output statistics, is optimally near zero at criticality for reasoning performance. Near-critical models outperform sub-critical ones on benchmarks, supporting a self-contained explanation of emergent reasoning in LLMs that can be quantified from steady-state global parameters without curated benchmark evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a physics-grounded account of reasoning in LLMs and proposes a global-parameter-based metric to quantify reasoning, eliminating the need for evaluation on curated datasets.

title: Environment Maps: Structured Environmental Representations for Long-Horizon Agents
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures, accepted to ICLR 2026 the 2nd Workshop on World Models
summary: Although large language models (LLMs) have advanced rapidly, robust automation of complex software workflows remains an open problem. In long-horizon settings, agents frequently suffer from cascading errors and environmental stochasticity; a single misstep in a dynamic interface can lead to task failure, resulting in hallucinations or trial-and-error. This paper introduces $\textit{Environment Maps}$: a persistent, agent-agnostic representation that mitigates these failures by consolidating heterogeneous evidence, such as screen recordings and execution traces, into a structured graph. The representation consists of four core components: (1) Contexts (abstracted locations), (2) Actions (parameterized affordances), (3) Workflows (observed trajectories), and (4) Tacit Knowledge (domain definitions and reusable procedures). We evaluate this framework on the WebArena benchmark across five domains. Agents equipped with environment maps achieve a 28.2% success rate, nearly doubling the performance of baselines limited to session-bound context (14.2%) and outperforming agents that have access to the raw trajectory data used to generate the environment maps (23.3%). By providing a structured interface between the model and the environment, Environment Maps establish a persistent foundation for long-horizon planning that is human-interpretable, editable, and incrementally refinable.

AI.tldr: Environment Maps provide a persistent, agent-agnostic graph-based representation to mitigate long-horizon planning failures in automated tasks by consolidating heterogeneous evidence; it yields substantial performance gains on WebArena.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon LLM-driven automation suffers from cascading errors, environmental stochasticity, hallucinations, and trial-and-error. A persistent, interpretable representation that aggregates diverse evidence can stabilize planning across sessions and interfaces.
AI.method: Propose Environment Maps with four components—Contexts (abstracted locations), Actions (parameterized affordances), Workflows (observed trajectories), and Tacit Knowledge (domain definitions and reusable procedures)—that integrate screen recordings and execution traces into a structured graph. Evaluate on WebArena across five domains, comparing agents with maps, session-bound context baselines, and raw-trajectory baselines.
AI.result: Agents with Environment Maps achieve 28.2% success on WebArena, about double the session-bound baseline (14.2%), and outperform those with access to the raw trajectories used to build the maps (23.3%). The representation is human-interpretable, editable, and incrementally refinable.
AI.conclusion: Environment Maps provide a persistent foundation for long-horizon planning by offering a structured, human-interpretable interface between models and environments, enabling more robust and reusable automation across domains.

title: Evaluating a Multi-Agent Voice-Enabled Smart Speaker for Care Homes: A Safety-Focused Framework
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly being explored in health and social care to reduce administrative workload and allow staff to spend more time on patient care. This paper evaluates a voice-enabled Care Home Smart Speaker designed to support everyday activities in residential care homes, including spoken access to resident records, reminders, and scheduling tasks. A safety-focused evaluation framework is presented that examines the system end-to-end, combining Whisper-based speech recognition with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches (hybrid, sparse, and dense). Using supervised care-home trials and controlled testing, we evaluated 330 spoken transcripts across 11 care categories, including 184 reminder-containing interactions. These evaluations focus on (i) correct identification of residents and care categories, (ii) reminder recognition and extraction, and (iii) end-to-end scheduling correctness under uncertainty (including safe deferral/clarification). Given the safety-critical nature of care homes, particular attention is also paid to reliability in noisy environments and across diverse accents, supported by confidence scoring, clarification prompts, and human-in-the-loop oversight. In the best-performing configuration (GPT-5.2), resident ID and care category matching reached 100% (95% CI: 98.86-100), while reminder recognition reached 89.09\% (95% CI: 83.81-92.80) with zero missed reminders (100% recall) but some false positives. End-to-end scheduling via calendar integration achieved 84.65% exact reminder-count agreement (95% CI: 78.00-89.56), indicating remaining edge cases in converting informal spoken instructions into actionable events. The findings suggest that voice-enabled systems, when carefully evaluated and appropriately safeguarded, can support accurate documentation, effective task management, and trustworthy use of AI in care home settings.

AI.tldr: A safety-focused evaluation of a voice-enabled Care Home Smart Speaker using Whisper ASR and Retrieval-Augmented Generation across 330 transcripts in 11 categories shows high accuracy for resident identity and categories, strong reminder recall with some false positives, and 84.65% exact scheduling agreement, indicating potential for reducing admin load in care homes when guarded by safeguards.
AI.motivation: Address administrative burden and safety concerns in care homes by enabling voice-driven access to resident records, reminders, and scheduling, while ensuring reliability in noisy environments and across accents.
AI.method: End-to-end evaluation of a voice-enabled system (Whisper ASR + RAG: hybrid, sparse, dense) in supervised care-home trials and controlled tests. Analyzed 330 transcripts (11 categories; 184 with reminders). Metrics: resident ID and category accuracy, reminder extraction, end-to-end scheduling; includes confidence scoring, prompts for clarification, and human-in-the-loop. Best configuration GPT-5.2.
AI.result: Resident ID and care-category matching: 100% (CI 98.86–100). Reminder recognition: 89.09% (CI 83.81–92.80) with zero missed reminders but some false positives. End-to-end scheduling: 84.65% exact reminder-count agreement (CI 78.00–89.56). Reliability emphasized in noisy settings and diverse accents.
AI.conclusion: Voice-enabled AI can support accurate documentation, task management, and trustworthy usage in care homes when safety safeguards, evaluation, and human oversight are in place.

title: Can LLM Agents Be CFOs? A Benchmark for Resource Allocation in Dynamic Enterprise Environments
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic systems that can reason, plan, and act across complex tasks, but it remains unclear whether they can allocate resources effectively under uncertainty. Unlike short-horizon reactive decisions, allocation requires committing scarce resources over time while balancing competing objectives and preserving flexibility for future needs. We introduce EnterpriseArena, the first benchmark for evaluating agents on long-horizon enterprise resource allocation. It instantiates CFO-style decision-making in a 132-month enterprise simulator combining firm-level financial data, anonymized business documents, macroeconomic and industry signals, and expert-validated operating rules. The environment is partially observable and reveals the state only through budgeted organizational tools, forcing agents to trade off information acquisition against conserving scarce resources. Experiments on eleven advanced LLMs show that this setting remains highly challenging: only 16% of runs survive the full horizon, and larger models do not reliably outperform smaller ones. These results identify long-horizon resource allocation under uncertainty as a distinct capability gap for current LLM agents.

AI.tldr: EnterpriseArena introduces a long-horizon enterprise resource allocation benchmark; results show LLMs struggle; larger models do not consistently outperform smaller ones; highlights a capability gap.
AI.motivation: Evaluate executive-level CFO-like planning under uncertainty over extended horizons, addressing the lack of benchmarks for long-horizon, resource-constrained decision making.
AI.method: 132-month enterprise simulator integrating firm financial data, anonymized documents, macro/industry signals, and expert operating rules; environment is partially observable via budgeted tools; assess 11 LLMs and measure survival across the horizon.
AI.result: Only 16% of runs survive to the full horizon; larger models do not reliably outperform smaller ones.
AI.conclusion: Long-horizon resource allocation under uncertainty is a distinct capability gap for current LLM agents, signaling the need for methods beyond mere scaling (e.g., explicit planning and information-acquisition strategies).

title: Quadrature Oscillation System for Coordinated Motion in Crawling Origami Robot
summary: Origami-inspired robots offer rapid, accessible design and manufacture with diverse functionalities. In particular, origami robots without conventional electronics have the unique advantage of functioning in extreme environments such as ones with high radiation or large magnetic fields. However, the absence of sophisticated control systems limits these robots to simple autonomous behaviors. In our previous studies, we developed a printable, electronics-free, and self-sustained oscillator that generates simple complementary square-wave signals. Our study presents a quadrature oscillation system capable of generating four square-wave signals a quarter-cycle out of phase, enabling four distinct states. Such control signals are important in various engineering and robotics applications, such as orchestrating limb movements in bio-inspired robots. We demonstrate the practicality and value of this oscillation system by designing and constructing an origami crawling robot that utilizes the quadrature oscillator to achieve coordinated locomotion. Together, the oscillator and robot illustrate the potential for more complex control and functions in origami robotics, paving the way for more electronics-free, rapid-design origami robots with advanced autonomous behaviors.

AI.tldr: Quadrature oscillator enables four-phase square-wave signals without electronics for origami robots, enabling coordinated locomotion with a four-state oscillator.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on electronics in origami robotics, enabling rapid design and operation in extreme environments; extend autonomous capabilities beyond simple autonomous behaviors.
AI.method: Extend prior printable, electronics-free oscillator to a quadrature version producing four phase-shifted square waves; design and fabricate an origami crawling robot that uses the oscillator to coordinate limb movements; validate via demonstration.
AI.result: Demonstrated four phase signals (quarter-cycle apart) enabling coordinated locomotion in an origami robot; practical demonstration of enhanced electronics-free control.
AI.conclusion: Electronics-free origami robotics can achieve more complex autonomous behavior with quadrature multi-phase control; this approach broadens applications in extreme environments and rapid design.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Introducing a Symbolic-Mechanistic Approach to Interpretable Evaluation
summary: Accuracy-based evaluation cannot reliably distinguish genuine generalization from shortcuts like memorization, leakage, or brittle heuristics, especially in small-data regimes. In this position paper, we argue for mechanism-aware evaluation that combines task-relevant symbolic rules with mechanistic interpretability, yielding algorithmic pass/fail scores that show exactly where models generalize versus exploit patterns. We demonstrate this on NL-to-SQL by training two identical architectures under different conditions: one without schema information (forcing memorization), one with schema (enabling grounding). Standard evaluation shows the memorization model achieves 94% field-name accuracy on unseen data, falsely suggesting competence. Our symbolic-mechanistic evaluation reveals this model violates core schema generalization rules, a failure invisible to accuracy metrics.

AI.tldr: Accuracy metrics can mask generalization failures; mechanism-aware evaluation combines symbolic rules with mechanistic interpretability to reveal when models generalize vs memorize. Demonstrated on NL-to-SQL with schema grounding vs memorization; accuracy misleads for memorization, while symbolic-mechanistic evaluation shows schema-generalization violations.
AI.motivation: Address the insufficiency of accuracy-based evaluation which conflates genuine generalization with shortcuts like memorization, leakage, or brittle heuristics, especially in small-data regimes.
AI.method: Train two identical NL-to-SQL models under different conditioning: one without schema information (forces memorization) and one with schema (enables grounding). Apply a symbolic-mechanistic evaluation that integrates task-relevant symbolic rules with mechanistic interpretability to produce algorithmic pass/fail scores indicating where generalization holds versus patterns being exploited.
AI.result: Standard accuracy on unseen data shows 94% field-name accuracy for the memorization model, suggesting competence, but the symbolic-mechanistic evaluation reveals violations of core schema-generalization rules—failures invisible to accuracy metrics.
AI.conclusion: Accuracy-based evaluation is insufficient to assess genuine generalization. Mechanism-aware evaluation can identify where models truly generalize, and localize failures, enabling more reliable assessment in data-scarce settings.

title: GTO Wizard Benchmark
summary: We introduce GTO Wizard Benchmark, a public API and standardized evaluation framework for benchmarking algorithms in Heads-Up No-Limit Texas Hold'em (HUNL). The benchmark evaluates agents against GTO Wizard AI, a state-of-the-art superhuman poker agent that approximates Nash Equilibria, and defeated Slumbot, the 2018 Annual Computer Poker Competition champion and previous strongest publicly accessible HUNL benchmark, by $19.4$ $\pm$ $4.1$ bb/100. Variance is a fundamental challenge in poker evaluation; we address this by integrating AIVAT, a provably unbiased variance reduction technique that achieves equivalent statistical significance with ten times fewer hands than naive Monte Carlo evaluation. We conduct a comprehensive benchmarking study of state-of-the-art large language models under zero-shot conditions, including GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok 4, and others. Initial results and analysis reveal dramatic progress in LLM reasoning over recent years, yet all models remain far below the baseline established by our benchmark. Qualitative analysis reveals clear opportunities for improvement, including representation and the ability to reason over hidden states. This benchmark provides researchers with a precise and quantifiable setting to evaluate advances in planning and reasoning in multi-agent systems with partial observability.

AI.tldr: Public HUNL benchmarking framework (GTO Wizard Benchmark) evaluating agents vs GTO Wizard and Slumbot; uses AIVAT variance reduction; tests zero-shot LLMs; finds progress but still below baseline; offers a precise evaluation for planning/reasoning in multi-agent partial observability.
AI.motivation: Need standardized, unbiased, scalable benchmarking in heads-up no-limit hold’em to compare agents and evaluate reasoning/planning under partial observability; variance in poker makes stats noisy; assess modern LLM capabilities in game reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce GTO Wizard Benchmark public API; evaluate against GTO Wizard AI and Slumbot; apply AIVAT variance reduction to reduce sample size; perform zero-shot evaluation of leading LLMs (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok 4, etc.); conduct qualitative analysis on representation and hidden-state reasoning.
AI.result: GTO Wizard defeated Slumbot by 19.4 ± 4.1 bb/100. AIVAT provides unbiased variance reduction, achieving equivalent statistical significance with ~10x fewer hands than naive Monte Carlo. LLMs show substantial progress but remain far below the benchmark's baseline. Qualitative analyses identify representation and hidden-state reasoning as key gaps.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark gives a precise, quantitative setting to evaluate planning and reasoning in multi-agent, partially observable environments; facilitates progress tracking and methodology improvements for AI agents in strategic settings.

title: Bio-Inspired Event-Based Visual Servoing for Ground Robots
summary: Biological sensory systems are inherently adaptive, filtering out constant stimuli and prioritizing relative changes, likely enhancing computational and metabolic efficiency. Inspired by active sensing behaviors across a wide range of animals, this paper presents a novel event-based visual servoing framework for ground robots. Utilizing a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS), we demonstrate that by applying a fixed spatial kernel to the asynchronous event stream generated from structured logarithmic intensity-change patterns, the resulting net event flux analytically isolates specific kinematic states. We establish a generalized theoretical bound for this event rate estimator and show that linear and quadratic spatial profiles isolate the robot's velocity and position-velocity product, respectively. Leveraging these properties, we employ a multi-pattern stimulus to directly synthesize a nonlinear state-feedback term entirely without traditional state estimation. To overcome the inescapable loss of linear observability at equilibrium inherent in event sensing, we propose a bio-inspired active sensing limit-cycle controller. Experimental validation on a 1/10-scale autonomous ground vehicle confirms the efficacy, extreme low-latency, and computational efficiency of the proposed direct-sensing approach.

AI.tldr: An event-based visual servoing approach using a Dynamic Vision Sensor with fixed spatial kernels on asynchronous event streams from structured logarithmic intensity-change patterns; it analytically isolates velocity and position-velocity states, provides a generalized bound for event-rate estimation, and enables direct nonlinear state-feedback via multi-pattern stimuli, complemented by a bio-inspired limit-cycle controller to address observability loss; validated on a 1/10-scale ground robot with ultra-low latency and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Leverage neuromorphic sensing and active sensing principles to achieve fast, low-latency robot control without explicit state estimation, inspired by adaptive biological sensory processing.
AI.method: Process DVS event streams generated by structured logarithmic intensity-change patterns using fixed spatial kernels. The kernels analytically map to kinematic quantities: linear kernels isolate velocity, quadratic kernels isolate position-velocity product. A multi-pattern stimulus is used to synthesize a nonlinear state-feedback term directly from the events. To mitigate loss of linear observability at equilibrium in event sensing, a bio-inspired limit-cycle controller is introduced. Experimental validation is conducted on a 1/10-scale autonomous ground vehicle.
AI.result: The authors establish a generalized bound for the event-rate estimator and demonstrate, analytically, that linear and quadratic spatial profiles isolate velocity and position-velocity respectively. They implement direct-sensing nonlinear feedback from stimulus patterns and validate the approach on a scale robot, achieving extreme low-latency and computational efficiency while maintaining stable closed-loop behavior without traditional state estimation.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the viability of direct-state-inspired control from neuromorphic event streams, enabling ultra-fast, computation-light feedback for ground robots. It highlights a pathway toward fully neuromorphic control architectures, with future work needed to extend to richer state representations, real-world robustness, and scaling.”

title: Implicit Turn-Wise Policy Optimization for Proactive User-LLM Interaction
summary: Multi-turn human-AI collaboration is fundamental to deploying interactive services such as adaptive tutoring, conversational recommendation, and professional consultation. However, optimizing these interactions via reinforcement learning is hindered by the sparsity of verifiable intermediate rewards and the high stochasticity of user responses. To address these challenges, we introduce Implicit Turn-wise Policy Optimization (ITPO). ITPO leverages an implicit process reward model to derive fine-grained, turn-wise process rewards from sparse outcome signals. Unlike volatile token-level rewards, these turn-level signals exhibit superior robustness and may utilize a normalization mechanism to further enhance training stability. We evaluate ITPO across three representative multi-turn collaborative tasks: math tutoring, document writing, and medical recommendation. Empirical results demonstrate that ITPO, when combined with PPO, GRPO, or RLOO, consistently achieves improved convergence than existing baselines. Elaborate trajectory analysis confirms that ITPO infers turn-wise preferences that are semantically aligned with human judgment. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/Graph-COM/ITPO.

AI.tldr: ITPO provides a turn-wise implicit reward model to derive robust, fine-grained process rewards from sparse outcomes for multi-turn collaboration; demonstrates improved convergence with PPO variants across math tutoring, document writing, and medical recommendation; code released.
AI.motivation: Overcome sparse intermediate rewards and high stochasticity in multi-turn RL by extracting turn-level signals that align with human judgments and stabilize training.
AI.method: Introduce Implicit Turn-wise Policy Optimization (ITPO) using an implicit process reward model to generate turn-wise rewards from sparse outcomes, optionally with normalization; evaluate with PPO, GRPO, RLOO across three tasks; analyze trajectories for alignment with human judgments.
AI.result: Consistent convergence improvements over baselines; turn-wise rewards more robust than token-level signals; normalization enhances training stability; trajectory analysis indicates alignment with human preferences; code publicly available.
AI.conclusion: ITPO offers a robust, scalable approach to turn-based reward shaping in multi-turn interaction RL, enabling more stable learning and better alignment with human expectations.

title: Grounding Vision and Language to 3D Masks for Long-Horizon Box Rearrangement
summary: We study long-horizon planning in 3D environments from under-specified natural-language goals using only visual observations, focusing on multi-step 3D box rearrangement tasks. Existing approaches typically rely on symbolic planners with brittle relational grounding of states and goals, or on direct action-sequence generation from 2D vision-language models (VLMs). Both approaches struggle with reasoning over many objects, rich 3D geometry, and implicit semantic constraints. Recent advances in 3D VLMs demonstrate strong grounding of natural-language referents to 3D segmentation masks, suggesting the potential for more general planning capabilities. We extend existing 3D grounding models and propose Reactive Action Mask Planner (RAMP-3D), which formulates long-horizon planning as sequential reactive prediction of paired 3D masks: a "which-object" mask indicating what to pick and a "which-target-region" mask specifying where to place it. The resulting system processes RGB-D observations and natural-language task specifications to reactively generate multi-step pick-and-place actions for 3D box rearrangement. We conduct experiments across 11 task variants in warehouse-style environments with 1-30 boxes and diverse natural-language constraints. RAMP-3D achieves 79.5% success rate on long-horizon rearrangement tasks and significantly outperforms 2D VLM-based baselines, establishing mask-based reactive policies as a promising alternative to symbolic pipelines for long-horizon planning.

AI.tldr: 3D mask-based reactive planning for long-horizon 3D box rearrangement via RAMP-3D; predicts paired 3D masks (which-object, which-target-region) from RGB-D and natural language to generate multi-step pick-and-place actions; outperforms 2D VLM baselines with 79.5% success on 11 task variants.
AI.motivation: Current approaches either rely on brittle symbolic planners with fragile grounding or on 2D vision-language models for action sequences, which struggle with many objects, rich 3D geometry, and implicit semantic constraints. Advances in 3D VLM grounding of referents to 3D segmentation masks suggest the potential to support more general planning in 3D.
AI.method: Extend existing 3D grounding models to produce sequential reactive predictions of paired 3D masks: a which-object mask and a which-target-region mask. The system consumes RGB-D observations and natural-language task specifications to produce multi-step pick-and-place actions for 3D box rearrangement. Evaluation across 11 task variants in warehouse-style environments with 1–30 boxes.
AI.result: RAMP-3D achieves 79.5% success on long-horizon rearrangement tasks and significantly outperforms 2D VLM-based baselines, demonstrating that mask-based reactive policies can be a robust alternative to symbolic pipelines for long-horizon planning.
AI.conclusion: Mask-based reactive policies grounded in 3D segmentation masks offer a scalable and effective approach for long-horizon planning in 3D environments, addressing limitations of brittle symbolic grounding and 2D VLM-based planning.

title: Learning What Can Be Picked: Active Reachability Estimation for Efficient Robotic Fruit Harvesting
summary: Agriculture remains a cornerstone of global health and economic sustainability, yet labor-intensive tasks such as harvesting high-value crops continue to face growing workforce shortages. Robotic harvesting systems offer a promising solution; however, their deployment in unstructured orchard environments is constrained by inefficient perception-to-action pipelines. In particular, existing approaches often rely on exhaustive inverse kinematics or motion planning to determine whether a target fruit is reachable, leading to unnecessary computation and delayed decision-making. Our approach combines RGB-D perception with active learning to directly learn reachability as a binary decision problem. We then leverage active learning to selectively query the most informative samples for reachability labeling, significantly reducing annotation effort while maintaining high predictive accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves accurate reachability prediction with substantially fewer labeled samples, yielding approximately 6--8% higher accuracy than random sampling and enabling label-efficient adaptation to new orchard configurations. Among the evaluated strategies, entropy- and margin-based sampling outperform Query-by-Committee and standard uncertainty sampling in low-label regimes, while all strategies converge to comparable performance as the labeled set grows. These results highlight the effectiveness of active learning for task-level perception in agricultural robotics and position our approach as a scalable alternative to computation-heavy kinematic reachability analysis. Our code is available through https://github.com/wsu-cyber-security-lab-ai/active-learning.

AI.tldr: Active-learning-based reachability for robotic fruit harvesting using RGB-D perception to bypass heavy kinematic reachability analysis; achieves label-efficient, high-accuracy reachability prediction.
AI.motivation: Reduce computation and annotation effort in reachability checks for robotic harvesting in unstructured orchards, enabling faster and more scalable task-level perception.
AI.method: Binary reachability classifier trained on RGB-D data; uses active learning to selectively label the most informative samples. Evaluates entropy- and margin-based sampling, Query-by-Committee, and standard uncertainty sampling. Demonstrates label-efficient adaptation to new orchard configurations.
AI.result: Achieves ~6–8% higher accuracy than random sampling with substantially fewer labeled samples; entropy- and margin-based sampling outperform QBC and uncertainty sampling in low-label regimes; all strategies converge when labels are abundant; code released.
AI.conclusion: Active learning provides a scalable, compute-efficient alternative to exhaustive kinematic reachability analysis for agricultural robotics, enabling efficient task-level perception and adaptable deployment in varied orchard setups.

title: Upper Entropy for 2-Monotone Lower Probabilities
summary: Uncertainty quantification is a key aspect in many tasks such as model selection/regularization, or quantifying prediction uncertainties to perform active learning or OOD detection. Within credal approaches that consider modeling uncertainty as probability sets, upper entropy plays a central role as an uncertainty measure. This paper is devoted to the computational aspect of upper entropies, providing an exhaustive algorithmic and complexity analysis of the problem. In particular, we show that the problem has a strongly polynomial solution, and propose many significant improvements over past algorithms proposed for 2-monotone lower probabilities and their specific cases.

AI.tldr: The abstract develops an algorithmic and complexity study of upper entropy within credal uncertainty quantification, presenting a strongly polynomial-time solution and substantial improvements for 2-monotone lower probabilities.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification is crucial for tasks like model selection, active learning, and OOD detection. Upper entropy in probability-sets (credal) frameworks provides a central uncertainty measure, necessitating efficient computation.
AI.method: Comprehensive algorithmic and complexity analysis of upper entropy; proposes improvements over existing algorithms for 2-monotone lower probabilities and their special cases.
AI.result: Proves a strongly polynomial-time solution for computing upper entropy; delivers significant algorithmic improvements over prior methods for 2-monotone lower probabilities.
AI.conclusion: Upper entropy within credal frameworks can be computed efficiently (strongly polynomial time), enabling practical uncertainty quantification in applications like active learning and OOD detection.

title: LLMs Do Not Grade Essays Like Humans
summary: Large language models have recently been proposed as tools for automated essay scoring, but their agreement with human grading remains unclear. In this work, we evaluate how LLM-generated scores compare with human grades and analyze the grading behavior of several models from the GPT and Llama families in an out-of-the-box setting, without task-specific training. Our results show that agreement between LLM and human scores remains relatively weak and varies with essay characteristics. In particular, compared to human raters, LLMs tend to assign higher scores to short or underdeveloped essays, while assigning lower scores to longer essays that contain minor grammatical or spelling errors. We also find that the scores generated by LLMs are generally consistent with the feedback they generate: essays receiving more praise tend to receive higher scores, while essays receiving more criticism tend to receive lower scores. These results suggest that LLM-generated scores and feedback follow coherent patterns but rely on signals that differ from those used by human raters, resulting in limited alignment with human grading practices. Nevertheless, our work shows that LLMs produce feedback that is consistent with their grading and that they can be reliably used in supporting essay scoring.

AI.tldr: LLMs show weak alignment with human grading for essay scoring in an out-of-the-box setting, with biases: over-scoring short/underdeveloped essays and under-scoring longer essays with minor errors; scores align with the models' own feedback; can support scoring but not replace human raters.
AI.motivation: Assess whether zero-shot/generative models (GPT/Llama) can be used for automated essay scoring without task-specific training and understand how their scores align with human grading and relate to the feedback they generate.
AI.method: Evaluate several GPT- and Llama-family models in an out-of-the-box, zero-shot setting on essays; compare LLM-generated scores to human scores; analyze scoring biases relative to essay length and error presence; examine correlation between scores and critique/praise in feedback.
AI.result: Agreement between LLM scores and human scores is relatively weak and varies with essay characteristics. LLMs tend to rate short/underdeveloped essays higher and longer essays with minor grammar errors lower. Scores tend to be coherent with the feedback: essays receiving more praise receive higher scores, and those with more criticism receive lower scores. Signals used by LLMs differ from human grading, leading to limited alignment, but feedback from LLMs is consistent with their scoring and can be used to support automated scoring.
AI.conclusion: LLMs produce coherent, model-consistent feedback and scoring signals that differ from human grading, resulting in limited alignment with human practices. Nonetheless, they can be reliably used to support essay scoring under zero-shot conditions, serving as a complementary tool rather than a replacement for human raters.

title: ROSCell: A ROS2-Based Framework for Automated Formation and Orchestration of Multi-Robot Systems
summary: Modern manufacturing under High-Mix-Low-Volume requirements increasingly relies on flexible and adaptive matrix production systems, which depend on interconnected heterogeneous devices and rapid task reconfiguration. To address these needs, we present ROSCell, a ROS2-based framework that enables the flexible formation and management of a computing continuum across various devices. ROSCell allows users to package existing robotic software as deployable skills and, with simple requests, assemble isolated cells, automatically deploy skill instances, and coordinate their communication to meet task objectives. It provides a scalable and low-overhead foundation for adaptive multi-robot computing in dynamic production environments. Experimental results show that, in the idle state, ROSCell substantially reduces CPU, memory, and network overhead compared to K3s-based solutions on edge devices, highlighting its energy efficiency and cost-effectiveness for large-scale deployment in production settings. The source code, examples, and documentation will be provided on Github.

AI.tldr: ROSCell is a ROS2-based framework that forms and manages a computing continuum across heterogeneous devices by packaging robotic software as deployable skills, enabling automated cell assembly, skill deployment, and coordinated communication for adaptive multi-robot tasks; it claims lower idle overhead than K3s on edge devices and energy efficiency for large-scale production deployment.
AI.motivation: High-Mix-Low-Volume (HMLV) manufacturing demands flexible, rapidly reconfigurable, and scalable multi-robot computing environments. Existing solutions may incur high overhead on edge devices; there is a need for a lightweight, decentralized framework to form and manage computing continua and robot skills.
AI.method: Introduce ROSCell, a ROS2-based framework that lets users package software as deployable skills, form isolated cells, automatically deploy skill instances, and coordinate inter-skill communication to meet task objectives. The framework emphasizes low overhead and edge-device efficiency, with experimental comparison against K3s-based solutions, and will release source, examples, and docs on GitHub.
AI.result: In idle state, ROSCell substantially reduces CPU, memory, and network overhead compared to K3s-based edge deployments, demonstrating energy efficiency and cost-effectiveness for large-scale adoption in production environments.
AI.conclusion: ROSCell provides a scalable, low-overhead foundation for adaptive multi-robot computing in dynamic production settings, enabling flexible assembly and management of robot computing cells; the project will be released as open-source to support broad adoption.

title: Efficient Benchmarking of AI Agents
summary: Evaluating AI agents on comprehensive benchmarks is expensive because each evaluation requires interactive rollouts with tool use and multi-step reasoning. We study whether small task subsets can preserve agent rankings at substantially lower cost. Unlike static language model benchmarks, agent evaluation is subject to scaffold-driven distribution shift, since performance depends on the framework wrapping the underlying model. Across eight benchmarks, 33 agent scaffolds, and 70+ model configurations, we find that absolute score prediction degrades under this shift, while rank-order prediction remains stable. Exploiting this asymmetry, we propose a simple optimization-free protocol: evaluate new agents only on tasks with intermediate historical pass rates (30-70%). This mid-range difficulty filter, motivated by Item Response Theory, reduces the number of evaluation tasks by 44-70% while maintaining high rank fidelity under scaffold and temporal shifts. It provides more reliable rankings than random sampling, which exhibits high variance across seeds, and outperforms greedy task selection under distribution shift. These results suggest that reliable leaderboard ranking does not require full-benchmark evaluation.

AI.tldr: Mid-range task subset preserves agent rankings while reducing evaluation cost; absolute scores degrade under scaffold shift, but ranks stay stable; propose a 30-70% historical pass-rate filter to cut tasks by 44-70% and maintain rank fidelity; outperforms random and greedy task selection; full benchmarks unnecessary for ranking.
AI.motivation: Aim to reduce benchmarking costs for AI agents by exploiting stable rank-order despite distribution shifts introduced by scaffolds; leverage task subset selection.
AI.method: Across eight benchmarks, 33 scaffolds, 70+ model configurations, assess score prediction vs rank prediction under scaffolding and temporal shifts; define a mid-range difficulty threshold (30-70% pass rate) to select tasks; compare to random and greedy selections; assess rank fidelity.
AI.result: Absolute scores degrade under scaffold/temporal shift, but rank-orders remain robust; the mid-range filter reduces task count by 44-70% while preserving high rank fidelity; ranking more reliable than random seeds and better than greedy task selection under shift.
AI.conclusion: Efficient leaderboard ranking can be achieved with partial benchmarks; task subset selection guided by historical pass rates yields cost-effective but reliable rankings; aligns with Item Response Theory insights.

title: Form-Fitting, Large-Area Sensor Mounting for Obstacle Detection
comment: Accepted at 2025 Humanoids Workshop on Advances in Contact-Rich Robotics: Rich Tactile-Based Physical Interaction [ConRich]
summary: We introduce a low-cost method for mounting sensors onto robot links for large-area sensing coverage that does not require the sensor's positions or orientations to be calibrated before use. Using computer aided design (CAD), a robot skin covering, or skin unit, can be procedurally generated to fit around a nondevelopable surface, a 3D surface that cannot be flattened into a 2D plane without distortion, of a robot. The skin unit embeds mounts for printed circuit boards of any size to keep sensors in fixed and known locations. We demonstrate our method by constructing point cloud images of obstacles within the proximity of a Franka Research 3 robot's operational environment using an array of time of flight (ToF) imagers mounted on a printed skin unit and attached to the robot arm.

AI.tldr: CAD-generated robot skin units enable calibration-free, large-area sensing by mounting ToF imagers on nondevelopable robot surfaces; demonstrated on a Franka arm to produce obstacle point clouds.
AI.motivation: Reduce calibration overhead and cost for sensor networks on curved robot surfaces; achieve fixed, known sensor locations and orientations without pre-use calibration to enable scalable sensing coverage.
AI.method: Procedurally generate skin units via CAD to wrap around nondevelopable surfaces; embed mounts for PCBs to fix sensors in known positions; 3D-print and attach a skin unit with an array of ToF imagers on a Franka Research 3 robot.
AI.result: Demonstration of obstacle sensing through point cloud images obtained from the ToF array mounted on the skin unit, validating large-area sensing coverage without pre-calibration.
AI.conclusion: CAD-driven skin-unit integration enables low-cost, calibration-free large-area sensing on complex robot geometries, enabling scalable perception with embedded sensor networks.

title: Learning-guided Prioritized Planning for Lifelong Multi-Agent Path Finding in Warehouse Automation
summary: Lifelong Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) is critical for modern warehouse automation, which requires multiple robots to continuously navigate conflict-free paths to optimize the overall system throughput. However, the complexity of warehouse environments and the long-term dynamics of lifelong MAPF often demand costly adaptations to classical search-based solvers. While machine learning methods have been explored, their superiority over search-based methods remains inconclusive. In this paper, we introduce Reinforcement Learning (RL) guided Rolling Horizon Prioritized Planning (RL-RH-PP), the first framework integrating RL with search-based planning for lifelong MAPF. Specifically, we leverage classical Prioritized Planning (PP) as a backbone for its simplicity and flexibility in integrating with a learning-based priority assignment policy. By formulating dynamic priority assignment as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP), RL-RH-PP exploits the sequential decision-making nature of lifelong planning while delegating complex spatial-temporal interactions among agents to reinforcement learning. An attention-based neural network autoregressively decodes priority orders on-the-fly, enabling efficient sequential single-agent planning by the PP planner. Evaluations in realistic warehouse simulations show that RL-RH-PP achieves the highest total throughput among baselines and generalizes effectively across agent densities, planning horizons, and warehouse layouts. Our interpretive analyses reveal that RL-RH-PP proactively prioritizes congested agents and strategically redirects agents from congestion, easing traffic flow and boosting throughput. These findings highlight the potential of learning-guided approaches to augment traditional heuristics in modern warehouse automation.

AI.tldr: RL-guided rolling horizon prioritized planning (RL-RH-PP) integrates reinforcement learning with a Prioritized Planning backbone to tackle lifelong MAPF in warehouses, achieving superior throughput and generalization over baselines.
AI.motivation: Classical search-based planners struggle with long-term dynamics and scalability in lifelong MAPF, and existing ML approaches have not decisively outperformed them. A framework that combines learning with a simple, flexible planner could better handle dynamic, congested warehouse environments.
AI.method: Formulate dynamic priority assignment as a POMDP. Use rolling-horizon prioritized planning with an attention-based neural network that autoregressively decodes priority orders on-the-fly. RL training optimizes throughput, delegating spatial-temporal interactions to the PP planner for sequential single-agent planning.
AI.result: In realistic warehouse simulations, RL-RH-PP achieves the highest total throughput among baselines and generalizes across agent densities, planning horizons, and layouts. Interpretability analyses indicate it prioritizes congested agents and redirects them to ease traffic, boosting throughput.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the viability of learning-guided augmentation of classical heuristics for lifelong MAPF, presenting an actionable framework (RL-RH-PP) with a POMDP formulation and an attention-based decoder, supported by empirical validation.

title: Task-Space Singularity Avoidance for Control Affine Systems Using Control Barrier Functions
summary: Singularities in robotic and dynamical systems arise when the mapping from control inputs to task-space motion loses rank, leading to an inability to determine inputs. This limits the system's ability to generate forces and torques in desired directions and prevents accurate trajectory tracking. This paper presents a control barrier function (CBF) framework for avoiding such singularities in control-affine systems. Singular configurations are identified through the eigenvalues of a state-dependent input-output mapping matrix, and barrier functions are constructed to maintain a safety margin from rank-deficient regions. Conditions for theoretical guarantees on safety are provided as a function of actuator dynamics. Simulations on a planar 2-link manipulator and a magnetically actuated needle demonstrate smooth trajectory tracking while avoiding singular configurations and reducing control input spikes by up to 100x compared to the nominal controller.

AI.tldr: A CBF-based strategy to avoid rank-deficient singularities in control-affine robotic systems, ensuring safe operation and improved tracking while reducing control spikes.
AI.motivation: Singular configurations limit controllability by causing the input–output map to lose rank, hindering force/torque generation and accurate tracking. A safety mechanism is needed to maintain operability near nominal tasks.
AI.method: Identify singularities via eigenvalues of a state-dependent input–output mapping matrix. Construct control barrier functions that enforce a safe margin away from rank-deficient regions. Provide conditions for safety guarantees considering actuator dynamics. Validate with simulations on a 2‑link planar manipulator and a magnetically actuated needle.
AI.result: The approach achieves smooth trajectory tracking while avoiding singularities and significantly reduces control input spikes (up to 100x vs. nominal controller).
AI.conclusion: A CBF-based framework can guarantee safety relative to singular configurations in control-affine systems under realistic actuator dynamics, enabling robust and efficient trajectory execution without encountering rank deficiency.

title: AscendOptimizer: Episodic Agent for Ascend NPU Operator Optimization
summary: AscendC (Ascend C) operator optimization on Huawei Ascend neural processing units (NPUs) faces a two-fold knowledge bottleneck: unlike the CUDA ecosystem, there are few public reference implementations to learn from, and performance hinges on a coupled two-part artifact - a host-side tiling program that orchestrates data movement and a kernel program that schedules and pipelines instructions. We present AscendOptimizer, an episodic agent that bootstraps this missing expertise by turning execution into experience. On the host side, AscendOptimizer performs profiling-in-the-loop evolutionary search to discover valid and high-performing tiling and data-movement configurations directly from hardware feedback. On the kernel side, it mines transferable optimization motifs by rewinding optimized kernels - systematically de-optimizing them to synthesize instructive "bad-to-good" trajectories - and distills these motifs into a retrievable experience bank for guided rewriting. By alternating host tuning and kernel rewriting in a closed loop, AscendOptimizer steadily expands feasibility and pushes latency down. On a benchmark of 127 real AscendC operators, AscendOptimizer achieves a 1.19x geometric-mean speedup over the open-source baseline, with 49.61% of operators outperforming their references, outperforming strong agent and search baselines.

AI.tldr: An episodic-agent framework, AscendOptimizer, learns host tiling and kernel optimizations for Huawei Ascend NPUs via profiling-in-the-loop search and
AI.motivation: There are few public reference implementations for Ascend C optimization, and performance depends on coupled host tiling and kernel scheduling; automated learning is needed to bootstrap expertise from hardware feedback.
AI.method: A two-branch, closed-loop approach: (1) host-side profiling-in-the-loop evolutionary search to discover high-performing tiling/data-movement configurations; (2) kernel-side 'bad-to-good' motif learning by systematically de-optimizing optimized kernels to distill transferable optimization patterns into an experience bank; alternating host tuning and kernel rewriting.
AI.result: On 127 real AscendC operators, AscendOptimizer achieves a 1.19x geometric-mean speedup over the open-source baseline; 49.61% of operators outperform their references; outperforms strong agent and search baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that coupling episodic learning with automated kernel rewrites can expand feasible optimizations and materially reduce latency for Ascend C workloads, suggesting a practical pathway to learn transferable optimization motifs for NPUs.

title: VehicleMemBench: An Executable Benchmark for Multi-User Long-Term Memory in In-Vehicle Agents
summary: With the growing demand for intelligent in-vehicle experiences, vehicle-based agents are evolving from simple assistants to long-term companions. This evolution requires agents to continuously model multi-user preferences and make reliable decisions in the face of inter-user preference conflicts and changing habits over time. However, existing benchmarks are largely limited to single-user, static question-answer settings, failing to capture the temporal evolution of preferences and the multi-user, tool-interactive nature of real vehicle environments. To address this gap, we introduce VehicleMemBench, a multi-user long-context memory benchmark built on an executable in-vehicle simulation environment. The benchmark evaluates tool use and memory by comparing the post-action environment state with a predefined target state, enabling objective and reproducible evaluation without LLM-based or human scoring. VehicleMemBench includes 23 tool modules, and each sample contains over 80 historical memory events. Experiments show that powerful models perform well on direct instruction tasks but struggle in scenarios involving memory evolution, particularly when user preferences change dynamically. Even advanced memory systems struggle to handle domain-specific memory requirements in this environment. These findings highlight the need for more robust and specialized memory management mechanisms to support long-term adaptive decision-making in real-world in-vehicle systems. To facilitate future research, we release the data and code.

AI.tldr: VehicleMemBench introduces a multi-user, long-context memory benchmark for in-vehicle agents, evaluated in an executable simulation with 23 tools and 80+ historical memory events per sample. It reveals that current models excel at direct instructions but struggle with memory evolution and dynamic user preferences, indicating a need for robust domain-specific memory management; data and code are released for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks largely isolate single-user, static Q&A tasks and fail to capture temporal evolution of preferences and inter-user conflicts in real-world vehicle environments. There is a need to evaluate memory, tool use, and long-term decision-making in multi-user, tool-interactive settings.
AI.method: An executable in-vehicle simulation environment is used to build VehicleMemBench. Each sample includes 80+ historical memory events and 23 tool modules. Performance is measured by comparing the post-action environment state to a predefined target state, enabling objective, reproducible scoring without LLM-based or human evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments show strong models perform well on direct instruction tasks but struggle with memory-evolution scenarios and dynamic user preferences. Even advanced memory systems have difficulty with domain-specific memory requirements in this environment.
AI.conclusion: Highlights the need for more robust and specialized memory management mechanisms to support long-term adaptive decision-making in real-world in-vehicle systems. The authors release data and code to facilitate future research.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Pareto Optimization: Trade-off Characterization for Assist-as-Needed Training and Performance Evaluation
comment: Under review for publication in IEEE Transactions on Haptics
summary: During human motor skill training and physical rehabilitation, there is an inherent trade-off between task difficulty and user performance. Characterizing this trade-off is crucial for evaluating user performance, designing assist-as-needed (AAN) protocols, and assessing the efficacy of training protocols. In this study, we propose a novel human-in-the-loop (HiL) Pareto optimization approach to characterize the trade-off between task performance and the perceived challenge level of motor learning or rehabilitation tasks. We adapt Bayesian multi-criteria optimization to systematically and efficiently perform HiL Pareto characterizations. Our HiL optimization employs a hybrid model that measures performance with a quantitative metric, while the perceived challenge level is captured with a qualitative metric. We demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed HiL Pareto characterization through a user study. Furthermore, we present the utility of the framework through three use cases in the context of a manual skill training task with haptic feedback. First, we demonstrate how the characterized trade-off can be used to design a sample AAN training protocol for a motor learning task and to evaluate the group-level efficacy of the proposed AAN protocol relative to a baseline adaptive assistance protocol. Second, we demonstrate that individual-level comparisons of the trade-offs characterized before and after the training session enable fair evaluation of training progress under different assistance levels. This evaluation method is more general than standard performance evaluations, as it can provide insights even when users cannot perform the task without assistance. Third, we show that the characterized trade-offs also enable fair performance comparisons among different users, as they capture the best possible performance of each user under all feasible assistance levels.

AI.tldr: HiL Pareto optimization for motor learning/rehab trade-off between task performance and perceived challenge, using Bayesian multi-criteria optimization with a hybrid performance/qualitative model; demonstrated feasibility and applied to AAN protocol design, progress assessment, and cross-user fairness in a haptic/manual skill task.
AI.motivation: To quantify and exploit the trade-off between performance and perceived difficulty in motor skill training and rehabilitation, enabling evaluation, AAN design, and efficacy assessment.
AI.method: Bayesian multi-criteria HiL Pareto characterization with a hybrid model combining a quantitative performance metric and a qualitative challenge metric; user study validation; three use cases: designing AAN protocol, group-level efficacy comparison, and individual-level progress/fairness analyses across users.
AI.result: Feasibility demonstrated via a user study; the framework supports design/evaluation of AAN protocols, enables fair progress assessment under varying assistance, and allows cross-user performance comparisons by the best achievable performance under feasible assistance.
AI.conclusion: A systematic HiL Pareto optimization framework for motor learning/rehab that jointly models performance and perceived challenge, enabling efficient protocol design and fair, task-adaptive evaluation across users.

title: Causal Reconstruction of Sentiment Signals from Sparse News Data
summary: Sentiment signals derived from sparse news are commonly used in financial analysis and technology monitoring, yet transforming raw article-level observations into reliable temporal series remains a largely unsolved engineering problem. Rather than treating this as a classification challenge, we propose to frame it as a causal signal reconstruction problem: given probabilistic sentiment outputs from a fixed classifier, recover a stable latent sentiment series that is robust to the structural pathologies of news data such as sparsity, redundancy, and classifier uncertainty. We present a modular three-stage pipeline that (i) aggregates article-level scores onto a regular temporal grid with uncertainty-aware and redundancy-aware weights, (ii) fills coverage gaps through strictly causal projection rules, and (iii) applies causal smoothing to reduce residual noise. Because ground-truth longitudinal sentiment labels are typically unavailable, we introduce a label-free evaluation framework based on signal stability diagnostics, information preservation lag proxies, and counterfactual tests for causality compliance and redundancy robustness. As a secondary external check, we evaluate the consistency of reconstructed signals against stock-price data for a multi-firm dataset of AI-related news titles (November 2024 to February 2026). The key empirical finding is a three-week lead lag pattern between reconstructed sentiment and price that persists across all tested pipeline configurations and aggregation regimes, a structural regularity more informative than any single correlation coefficient. Overall, the results support the view that stable, deployable sentiment indicators require careful reconstruction, not only better classifiers.

AI.tldr: A causal-signal reconstruction pipeline converts sparse article sentiment into a stable latent series; shows a ~3-week lead in sentiment over stock prices and provides label-free evaluation; emphasizes reconstruction over classifier quality.
AI.motivation: Sparse news data, redundancy, and classifier uncertainty hinder reliable sentiment time series; propose reconstruction to obtain deployable indicators.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (i) aggregate article-level scores to a temporal grid with uncertainty-aware and redundancy-aware weights; (ii) fill gaps via strictly causal projection; (iii) apply causal smoothing; plus label-free evaluation (stability diagnostics, lag proxies, counterfactual causality tests) and external price consistency check.
AI.result: Consistent three-week lead-lag between reconstructed sentiment and AI-related stock prices across pipeline configurations; this pattern is more informative than single correlation. Reconstructed signals deemed more robust.
AI.conclusion: Stable sentiment indicators require careful reconstruction to mitigate sparsity/redundancy and uncertainty; this approach improves deployable signal quality beyond better classifiers.

title: SCoOP: Semantic Consistent Opinion Pooling for Uncertainty Quantification in Multiple Vision-Language Model Systems
comment: Accepted to ICLR 2024 Workshop on Agentic AI in the Wild: From Hallucinations to Reliable Autonomy
summary: Combining multiple Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can enhance multimodal reasoning and robustness, but aggregating heterogeneous models' outputs amplifies uncertainty and increases the risk of hallucinations. We propose SCoOP (Semantic-Consistent Opinion Pooling), a training-free uncertainty quantification (UQ) framework multi-VLM systems through uncertainty-weighted linear opinion pooling. Unlike prior UQ methods designed for single models, SCoOP explicitly measures collective, system-level uncertainty across multiple VLMs, enabling effective hallucination detection and abstention for highly uncertain samples. On ScienceQA, SCoOP achieves an AUROC of 0.866 for hallucination detection, outperforming baselines (0.732-0.757) by approximately 10-13%. For abstention, it attains an AURAC of 0.907, exceeding baselines (0.818-0.840) by 7-9%. Despite these gains, SCoOP introduces only microsecond-level aggregation overhead relative to the baselines, which is trivial compared to typical VLM inference time (on the order of seconds). These results demonstrate that SCoOP provides an efficient and principled mechanism for uncertainty-aware aggregation, advancing the reliability of multimodal AI systems.

AI.tldr: SCoOP is a training-free uncertainty quantification method that adaptively weights outputs from multiple Vision-Language Models to detect hallucinations and enable abstention, with negligible aggregation overhead.
AI.motivation: Aggregating heterogeneous VLMs improves multimodal reasoning but increases collective uncertainty and the risk of hallucinations; robust system-level uncertainty quantification is needed for reliable ensemble deployments.
AI.method: Uncertainty-weighted linear opinion pooling that measures and combines per-model uncertainties to produce a system-level uncertainty; training-free; applied to hallucination detection and abstention in multimodal tasks.
AI.result: On ScienceQA, AUROC for hallucination detection reaches 0.866 (baselines 0.732-0.757, ~10-13% gain); abstention AURAC reaches 0.907 (baselines 0.818-0.840, ~7-9% gain); aggregation incurs microsecond-level overhead, negligible vs seconds-scale VLM inference.
AI.conclusion: SCoOP provides an efficient, principled framework for uncertainty-aware aggregation across multiple VLMs, improving reliability and robustness of multimodal AI systems with minimal runtime cost.

title: Object Search in Partially-Known Environments via LLM-informed Model-based Planning and Prompt Selection
summary: We present a novel LLM-informed model-based planning framework, and a novel prompt selection method, for object search in partially-known environments. Our approach uses an LLM to estimate statistics about the likelihood of finding the target object when searching various locations throughout the scene that, combined with travel costs extracted from the environment map, are used to instantiate a model, thus using the LLM to inform planning and achieve effective search performance. Moreover, the abstraction upon which our approach relies is amenable to deployment-time model selection via the recent offline replay approach, an insight we leverage to enable fast prompt and LLM selection during deployment. Simulation experiments demonstrate that our LLM-informed model-based planning approach outperforms the baseline planning strategy that fully relies on LLM and optimistic strategy with as much as 11.8% and 39.2% improvements respectively, and our bandit-like selection approach enables quick selection of best prompts and LLMs resulting in 6.5% lower average cost and 33.8% lower average cumulative regret over baseline UCB bandit selection. Real-robot experiments in an apartment demonstrate similar improvements and so further validate our approach.

AI.tldr: A novel LLM-informed, model-based planning framework for object search in partially-known environments, using LLMs to estimate location likelihoods, travel costs from maps, and a bandit-like prompt/LLM selection method. Deployment-time offline replay enables fast model selection. Demonstrates improvements over LLM-only or optimistic baselines in simulation and real-robot experiments.
AI.motivation: To improve object search efficiency in partially-known environments by leveraging LLMs to inform planning with probabilistic location priors and to reduce search costs through fast, deployment-time prompt/model selection.
AI.method: Integrates an LLM to estimate target-object likelihoods across locations, combines with travel costs from the environment map to instantiate a model-based planner. Introduces a prompt selection method and an offline replay deployment approach to enable rapid prompt/LLM selection. Evaluates via simulation comparing to LLM-only and optimistic strategies, and through real-robot experiments in an apartment.
AI.result: The proposed LLM-informed planning framework achieves up to 11.8% improvement over a baseline planning strategy that relies entirely on LLMs and 39.2% over an optimistic strategy. The bandit-like prompt/LLM selection reduces average cost by 6.5% and average cumulative regret by 33.8% relative to a baseline UCB bandit. Real-robot experiments show similar gains, validating practicality and transferability.
AI.conclusion: LLM-informed model-based planning with deployment-time prompt/LLM selection yields tangible performance gains in object search under partial knowledge, with practical benefits demonstrated in simulation and real-world robotics.

title: StateLinFormer: Stateful Training Enhancing Long-term Memory in Navigation
summary: Effective navigation intelligence relies on long-term memory to support both immediate generalization and sustained adaptation. However, existing approaches face a dilemma: modular systems rely on explicit mapping but lack flexibility, while Transformer-based end-to-end models are constrained by fixed context windows, limiting persistent memory across extended interactions. We introduce StateLinFormer, a linear-attention navigation model trained with a stateful memory mechanism that preserves recurrent memory states across consecutive training segments instead of reinitializing them at each batch boundary. This training paradigm effectively approximates learning on infinitely long sequences, enabling the model to achieve long-horizon memory retention. Experiments across both MAZE and ProcTHOR environments demonstrate that StateLinFormer significantly outperforms its stateless linear-attention counterpart and standard Transformer baselines with fixed context windows. Notably, as interaction length increases, persistent stateful training substantially improves context-dependent adaptation, suggesting an enhancement in the model's In-Context Learning (ICL) capabilities for navigation tasks.

AI.tldr: StateLinFormer uses linear attention with a stateful memory to retain recurrent memory across training segments, approximating infinite-long-sequence learning. It outperforms stateless baselines and fixed-context Transformers in long-horizon navigation tasks and shows improved in-context learning as interaction length grows.
AI.motivation: To overcome memory and flexibility limitations in navigation models: modular systems rely on explicit mappings (limited flexibility), while Transformer-based models are restricted by fixed context windows, hindering long-term memory and adaptation.
AI.method: Introduce StateLinFormer, a linear-attention navigation model trained with a stateful memory mechanism that preserves recurrent memory states across consecutive training segments, effectively simulating training on infinitely long sequences.
AI.result: StateLinFormer significantly outperforms stateless linear-attention baselines and standard Transformers with fixed context windows in MAZE and ProcTHOR. Persistent stateful training yields better context-dependent adaptation as interaction length increases, indicating enhanced in-context learning.
AI.conclusion: Stateful memory-based, long-horizon training improves long-term memory retention and adaptation in navigation tasks, boosting ICL capabilities for extended interactions.

title: When AI output tips to bad but nobody notices: Legal implications of AI's mistakes
summary: The adoption of generative AI across commercial and legal professions offers dramatic efficiency gains -- yet for law in particular, it introduces a perilous failure mode in which the AI fabricates fictitious case law, statutes, and judicial holdings that appear entirely authentic. Attorneys who unknowingly file such fabrications face professional sanctions, malpractice exposure, and reputational harm, while courts confront a novel threat to the integrity of the adversarial process. This failure mode is commonly dismissed as random `hallucination', but recent physics-based analysis of the Transformer's core mechanism reveals a deterministic component: the AI's internal state can cross a calculable threshold, causing its output to flip from reliable legal reasoning to authoritative-sounding fabrication. Here we present this science in a legal-industry setting, walking through a simulated brief-drafting scenario. Our analysis suggests that fabrication risk is not an anomalous glitch but a foreseeable consequence of the technology's design, with direct implications for the evolving duty of technological competence. We propose that legal professionals, courts, and regulators replace the outdated `black box' mental model with verification protocols based on how these systems actually fail.

AI.tldr: The abstract argues that AI-generated legal fabrications are not purely random hallucinations but result from a deterministic threshold in the Transformer’s internal state, with practical implications for verification, regulation, and professional competence in law.
AI.motivation: Address the high-stakes risk of fabricated legal authority produced by generative AI in professional legal work, threatening sanctions, malpractice exposure, and the integrity of adversarial judicial processes; push for a verifiable, competence-based framework rather than a black-box view.
AI.method: Present a physics-based analysis of Transformer core mechanisms in a legal setting, supplemented by a simulated brief-drafting scenario to identify conditions under which output shifts from reliable reasoning to fabrication; argue for a threshold-based, deterministic failure mode.
AI.result: Fabrication risk is a foreseeable, design-driven phenomenon rather than a random glitch; a calculable threshold in the AI’s internal state can trigger fabrication, prompting the need for verification protocols.
AI.conclusion: Urges replacing the 'black box' mental model with verifiable failure-mode protocols and integrating this understanding into the evolving duty of technological competence for legal professionals, courts, and regulators.

title: AgentChemist: A Multi-Agent Experimental Robotic Platform Integrating Chemical Perception and Precise Control
summary: Chemical laboratory automation has long been constrained by rigid workflows and poor adaptability to the long-tail distribution of experimental tasks. While most automated platforms perform well on a narrow set of standardized procedures, real laboratories involve diverse, infrequent, and evolving operations that fall outside predefined protocols. This mismatch prevents existing systems from generalizing to novel reaction conditions, uncommon instrument configurations, and unexpected procedural variations. We present a multi-agent robotic platform designed to address this long-tail challenge through collaborative task decomposition, dynamic scheduling, and adaptive control. The system integrates chemical perception for real-time reaction monitoring with feedback-driven execution, enabling it to adjust actions based on evolving experimental states rather than fixed scripts. Validation via acid-base titration demonstrates autonomous progress tracking, adaptive dispensing control, and reliable end-to-end experiment execution. By improving generalization across diverse laboratory scenarios, this platform provides a practical pathway toward intelligent, flexible, and scalable laboratory automation.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent robotic platform for flexible chemical automation that tackles long-tail tasks via collaborative task decomposition, dynamic scheduling, and adaptive control, with real-time chemical perception and feedback-driven execution, validated on acid-base titration.
AI.motivation: Current chemical laboratory automation is rigid, excelling only on standardized procedures and poorly generalizing to diverse, infrequent, and evolving tasks; there is a need for generalizable, adaptable automation that can handle novel reaction conditions and configurations.
AI.method: A multi-agent architecture enabling collaborative task decomposition, dynamic scheduling, and adaptive control; integration of real-time chemical perception for monitoring and feedback-driven execution; end-to-end autonomous experiment management; validation through acid-base titration.
AI.result: Autonomous progress tracking, adaptive dispensing control, and reliable end-to-end execution demonstrated; improved generalization across diverse laboratory scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The platform offers a practical path toward intelligent, flexible, and scalable laboratory automation capable of handling long-tail laboratory tasks.

title: Dual-Criterion Curriculum Learning: Application to Temporal Data
summary: Curriculum Learning (CL) is a meta-learning paradigm that trains a model by feeding the data instances incrementally according to a schedule, which is based on difficulty progression. Defining meaningful difficulty assessment measures is crucial and most usually the main bottleneck for effective learning, while also in many cases the employed heuristics are only application-specific. In this work, we propose the Dual-Criterion Curriculum Learning (DCCL) framework that combines two views of assessing instance-wise difficulty: a loss-based criterion is complemented by a density-based criterion learned in the data representation space. Essentially, DCCL calibrates training-based evidence (loss) under the consideration that data sparseness amplifies the learning difficulty. As a testbed, we choose the time-series forecasting task. We evaluate our framework on multivariate time-series benchmarks under standard One-Pass and Baby-Steps training schedules. Empirical results show the interest of density-based and hybrid dual-criterion curricula over loss-only baselines and standard non-CL training in this setting.

AI.tldr: DCCL couples a loss-based difficulty signal with a density-based signal learned in feature space to guide curriculum scheduling, improving time-series forecasting under standard CL regimes.
AI.motivation: Effective curriculum learning hinges on meaningful instance difficulty measures; conventional heuristics are often ad hoc and dataset-specific, neglecting data sparsity.
AI.method: Introduce Dual-Criterion Curriculum Learning (DCCL) that jointly uses a loss-based criterion and a density-based criterion in representation space to modulate the training order, accounting for data sparsity as a factor in difficulty. Evaluate on multivariate time-series forecasting using One-Pass and Baby-Steps schedules.
AI.result: Density-based and hybrid (loss + density) curricula outperform loss-only curricula and standard non-CL training on time-series benchmarks under the considered schedules.
AI.conclusion: A dual-criterion approach to curriculum design can enhance CL effectiveness for time-series forecasting and potentially other domains by integrating both training signal and data distribution information.

title: The DeepXube Software Package for Solving Pathfinding Problems with Learned Heuristic Functions and Search
summary: DeepXube is a free and open-source Python package and command-line tool that seeks to automate the solution of pathfinding problems by using machine learning to learn heuristic functions that guide heuristic search algorithms tailored to deep neural networks (DNNs). DeepXube is comprised of the latest advances in deep reinforcement learning, heuristic search, and formal logic for solving pathfinding problems. This includes limited-horizon Bellman-based learning, hindsight experience replay, batched heuristic search, and specifying goals with answer-set programming. A robust multiple-inheritance structure simplifies the definition of pathfinding domains and the generation of training data. Training heuristic functions is made efficient through the automatic parallelization of the generation of training data across central processing units (CPUs) and reinforcement learning updates across graphics processing units (GPUs).
