Pathfinding algorithms that take advantage of the parallelism of GPUs and DNN architectures, such as batch weighted A* and Q* search and beam search are easily employed to solve pathfinding problems through command-line arguments. Finally, several convenient features for visualization, code profiling, and progress monitoring during training and solving are available. The GitHub repository is publicly available at https://github.com/forestagostinelli/deepxube.

AI.tldr: Open-source framework that learns heuristic functions for pathfinding via deep RL, integrating DNNs, batch search, and ASP, with CPU/GPU parallelization.
AI.motivation: To automate and accelerate pathfinding by learning domain-specific heuristics and combining modern AI with formal logic.
AI.method: Limited-horizon Bellman-based learning, hindsight experience replay, batched heuristic search, ASP-goal specification; multi-inheritance for domain generation; CPU-based data generation and GPU-based RL updates; supports batch weighted A*, Q* search, beam search; CLI; visualization/profiling.
AI.result: A practical toolkit enabling DL-based heuristic learning for pathfinding with parallel computation and multiple search variants; codebase hosted on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates cohesive integration of RL, heuristic search, and ASP to address pathfinding problems; specific empirical results are not reported in the abstract.

title: Event-Driven Proactive Assistive Manipulation with Grounded Vision-Language Planning
summary: Assistance in collaborative manipulation is often initiated by user instructions, making high-level reasoning request-driven. In fluent human teamwork, however, partners often infer the next helpful step from the observed outcome of an action rather than waiting for instructions. Motivated by this, we introduce a shift from request-driven assistance to event-driven proactive assistance, where robot actions are initiated by workspace state transitions induced by human--object interactions rather than user-provided task instructions. To this end, we propose an event-driven framework that tracks interaction progress with an event monitor and, upon event completion, extracts stabilized pre/post snapshots that characterize the resulting state transition. Given the stabilized snapshots, the planner analyzes the implied state transition to infer a task-level goal and decide whether to intervene; if so, it generates a sequence of assistive actions. To make outputs executable and verifiable, we restrict actions to a set of action primitives and reference objects via integer IDs. We evaluate the framework on a real tabletop number-block collaboration task, demonstrating that explicit pre/post state-change evidence improves proactive completion on solvable scenes and appropriate waiting on unsolvable ones.

AI.tldr: Proactive, event-driven robot assistance infers task goals from observed human–object state transitions rather than instruction, improving collaboration in a tabletop number-block task.
AI.motivation: In human teamwork, partners infer next steps from outcomes rather than waiting for explicit instructions; addressing the gap in robotics between instruction-driven and naturalistic assistance by leveraging state changes.
AI.method: An event-driven framework with an event monitor captures stabilized pre/post state snapshots of human–object interactions. The planner analyzes the implied state transition to infer a task-level goal and decides whether to intervene; if so, it generates a sequence of actions restricted to primitives and integer-referenced objects.
AI.result: In real tabletop number-block collaboration, explicit pre/post state-change evidence improves proactive completion on solvable scenes and appropriate waiting on unsolvable ones.
AI.conclusion: Explicit state-change evidence enables reliable, goal-driven proactive assistance; the approach demonstrates the value of monitoring and reasoning about state transitions to guide assistive actions.

title: PoiCGAN: A Targeted Poisoning Based on Feature-Label Joint Perturbation in Federated Learning
summary: Federated Learning (FL), as a popular distributed learning paradigm, has shown outstanding performance in improving computational efficiency and protecting data privacy, and is widely applied in industrial image classification. However, due to its distributed nature, FL is vulnerable to threats from malicious clients, with poisoning attacks being a common threat. A major limitation of existing poisoning attack methods is their difficulty in bypassing model performance tests and defense mechanisms based on model anomaly detection. This often results in the detection and removal of poisoned models, which undermines their practical utility. To ensure both the performance of industrial image classification and attacks, we propose a targeted poisoning attack, PoiCGAN, based on feature-label collaborative perturbation. Our method modifies the inputs of the discriminator and generator in the Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN) to influence the training process, generating an ideal poison generator. This generator not only produces specific poisoned samples but also automatically performs label flipping. Experiments across various datasets show that our method achieves an attack success rate 83.97% higher than baseline methods, with a less than 8.87% reduction in the main task's accuracy. Moreover, the poisoned samples and malicious models exhibit high stealthiness.

AI.tldr: PoiCGAN is a CGAN-based targeted poisoning attack for federated learning in industrial image classification. It achieves high attack success while preserving main task accuracy and exhibits high stealthiness, bypassing anomaly-based defenses.
AI.motivation: FL is vulnerable to poisoning; existing attacks struggle to bypass model anomaly detection, limiting practical impact. The paper seeks a more evasive, effective attack that preserves main-task performance.
AI.method: The attack uses feature-label collaborative perturbation by modifying the discriminator and generator inputs in CGAN. It learns an optimal poison generator that outputs poisoned samples and automatically flips labels. Applied in a FL setting to degrade the global model while keeping main-task accuracy relatively intact.
AI.result: Across datasets, the attack achieves 83.97% higher success than baselines and less than 8.87% reduction in main-task accuracy. Poisoned samples and malicious models exhibit high stealthiness, making detection harder.
AI.conclusion: PoiCGAN demonstrates a potent, stealthy targeted poisoning method against FL-based industrial image classification, highlighting a need for defense strategies beyond standard anomaly detection and calling for robust, verifiable defenses.

title: DUPLEX: Agentic Dual-System Planning via LLM-Driven Information Extraction
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) provide semantic flexibility for robotic task planning, their susceptibility to hallucination and logical inconsistency limits their reliability in long-horizon domains. To bridge the gap between unstructured environments and rigorous plan synthesis, we propose DUPLEX, an agentic dual-system neuro-symbolic architecture that strictly confines the LLM to schema-guided information extraction rather than end-to-end planning or code generation. In our framework, a feed-forward Fast System utilizes a lightweight LLM to extract entities, relations etc. from natural language, deterministically mapping them into a Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) problem file for a classical symbolic planner. To resolve complex or underspecified scenarios, a Slow System is activated exclusively upon planning failure, leveraging solver diagnostics to drive a high-capacity LLM in iterative reflection and repair. Extensive evaluations across 12 classical and household planning domains demonstrate that DUPLEX significantly outperforms existing end-to-end and hybrid LLM baselines in both success rate and reliability. These results confirm that The key is not to make the LLM plan better, but to restrict the LLM to the part it is good at - structured semantic grounding - and leave logical plan synthesis to a symbolic planner.

AI.tldr: DUPLEX couples a Fast System that deterministically grounds NL into a PDDL problem for a symbolic planner, with a Slow System that re-invokes a high-capacity LLM for repair when planning fails, achieving higher success and reliability in 12 domains.
AI.motivation: LLMs provide semantic flexibility for planning but are prone to hallucinations and logical inconsistency in long-horizon tasks. Pure end-to-end planning with LLMs is brittle; robust long-horizon planning requires reliable grounding and separation of concerns between grounding and reasoning.
AI.method: A dual-system architecture: Fast System uses a lightweight LLM to extract entities, relations, and other structured information from natural language and deterministically maps them to a PDDL problem for a classical planner. Slow System is activated only on planning failure; it uses solver diagnostics to drive iterative reflection and repair by a high-capacity LLM.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations across 12 classical and household planning domains show that DUPLEX outperforms existing end-to-end and hybrid LLM baselines in both success rate and reliability.
AI.conclusion: The key insight is that restricting the LLM to structured semantic grounding and delegating logical plan synthesis to a symbolic planner improves reliability and reduces hallucination, suggesting future neuro-symbolic designs should separate grounding from reasoning.

title: MonoSIM: An open source SIL framework for Ackermann Vehicular Systems with Monocular Vision
comment: 6 pages, 16 figures, Published in "IEEE 12th International Conference on Automation, Robotics and Application 2026"
summary: This paper presents an open-source Software-in-the-Loop (SIL) simulation platform designed for autonomous Ackerman vehicle research and education. The proposed framework focuses on simplicity, while making it easy to work with small-scale experimental setups, such as the XTENTH-CAR platform. The system was designed using open source tools, creating an environment with a monocular camera vision system to capture stimuli from it with minimal computational overhead through a sliding window based lane detection method. The platform supports a flexible algorithm testing and validation environment, allowing researchers to implement and compare various control strategies within an easy-to-use virtual environment. To validate the working of the platform, Model Predictive Control (MPC) and Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) algorithms were implemented within the SIL framework. The results confirm that the platform provides a reliable environment for algorithm verification, making it an ideal tool for future multi-agent system research, educational purposes, and low-cost AGV development. Our code is available at https://github.com/shantanu404/monosim.git.

AI.tldr: Open-source SIL platform for autonomous Ackerman vehicle; monocular vision with sliding-window lane detection; supports MPC and PID; suitable for education and low-cost AGVs; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Provide a simple, flexible SIL environment for small-scale autonomous-vehicle research and education with low computational overhead.
AI.method: Open-source tooling with monocular camera, sliding-window lane detection; XTENTH-CAR compatibility; flexible environment for testing and validation of control strategies; MPC and PID implemented within SIL.
AI.result: Demonstrates a reliable platform for algorithm verification; suitable for multi-agent research, education, and low-cost AGV development.
AI.conclusion: The platform is a valuable, accessible tool that enables rapid experimentation and benchmarking of control algorithms in a SIL setting; open-source release should promote broader adoption.

title: APreQEL: Adaptive Mixed Precision Quantization For Edge LLMs
summary: Today, large language models have demonstrated their strengths in various tasks ranging from reasoning, code generation, and complex problem solving. However, this advancement comes with a high computational cost and memory requirements, making it challenging to deploy these models on edge devices to ensure real-time responses and data privacy. Quantization is one common approach to reducing memory use, but most methods apply it uniformly across all layers. This does not account for the fact that different layers may respond differently to reduced precision. Importantly, memory consumption and computational throughput are not necessarily aligned, further complicating deployment decisions. This paper proposes an adaptive mixed precision quantization mechanism that balances memory, latency, and accuracy in edge deployment under user-defined priorities. This is achieved by analyzing the layer-wise contribution and by inferring how different quantization types behave across the target hardware platform in order to assign the most suitable quantization type to each layer. This integration ensures that layer importance and the overall performance trade-offs are jointly respected in this design. Our work unlocks new configuration designs that uniform quantization cannot achieve, expanding the solution space to efficiently deploy the LLMs on resource-constrained devices.

AI.tldr: Adaptive per-layer mixed-precision quantization to balance memory, latency, and accuracy for edge deployment of LLMs under user-defined priorities.
AI.motivation: Edge deployment of LLMs is limited by high compute and memory costs. Uniform quantization fails to account for layer-wise sensitivity and the mismatch between memory usage and throughput, hindering real-time, private edge inference.
AI.method: Analyze layer-wise contribution to overall performance and infer hardware-specific behavior of different quantization types. Assign the most suitable per-layer quantization type according to layer importance and user-defined priorities, jointly optimizing memory, latency, and accuracy.
AI.result: Enables new configuration designs beyond uniform quantization, enabling more efficient edge deployment of LLMs by tailoring precision per layer according to hardware behavior and layer importance.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive mixed-precision quantization per layer provides a flexible and effective framework for balancing resource constraints and model performance in edge scenarios.

title: AnalogAgent: Self-Improving Analog Circuit Design Automation with LLM Agents
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) suggest strong potential for automating analog circuit design. Yet most LLM-based approaches rely on a single-model loop of generation, diagnosis, and correction, which favors succinct summaries over domain-specific insight and suffers from context attrition that erases critical technical details. To address these limitations, we propose AnalogAgent, a training-free agentic framework that integrates an LLM-based multi-agent system (MAS) with self-evolving memory (SEM) for analog circuit design automation. AnalogAgent coordinates a Code Generator, Design Optimizer, and Knowledge Curator to distill execution feedback into an adaptive playbook in SEM and retrieve targeted guidance for subsequent generation, enabling cross-task transfer without additional expert feedback, databases, or libraries. Across established benchmarks, AnalogAgent achieves 92% Pass@1 with Gemini and 97.4% Pass@1 with GPT-5. Moreover, with compact models (e.g., Qwen-8B), it yields a +48.8% average Pass@1 gain across tasks and reaches 72.1% Pass@1 overall, indicating that AnalogAgent substantially strengthens open-weight models for high-quality analog circuit design automation.

AI.tldr: Training-free AnalogAgent uses a multi-agent LLM system + self-evolving memory to automate analog circuit design, achieving high Pass@1 on benchmarks; effective cross-task transfer with open-weight models.
AI.motivation: Overcome single-model loop limitations: loss of domain insight and context retention; enable memory-driven, cross-task guidance without extra data or expert feedback.
AI.method: Integrates a Code Generator, Design Optimizer, Knowledge Curator in a multi-agent system (MAS) with self-evolving memory (SEM). The LLMs generate, critique, and improve designs; SEM stores adaptive playbooks and retrieved guidance to guide subsequent generations; training-free; no external databases required.
AI.result: Benchmarks: Pass@1 92% (Gemini), 97.4% (GPT-5). With Qwen-8B, +48.8% avg Pass@1 across tasks; 72.1% overall. Demonstrates strong improvement for open-weight models in analog circuit automation.
AI.conclusion: AnalogAgent substantially strengthens open-weight LLMs for high-quality analog circuit design automation via a memory-enabled, agentic, training-free framework enabling cross-task transfer.

title: Robust Distributed Cooperative Path-Following and Local Replanning for Multi-UAVs Under Differentiated Low-Altitude Paths
summary: Multiple fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicles (multi-UAVs) encounter significant challenges in cooperative path following over complex Digital Elevation Model (DEM) low-altitude airspace, including wind field disturbances, sudden obstacles, and requirements of distributed temporal synchronization during differentiated path tracking. Existing methods lack efficient distributed coordination mechanisms for time-consistent tracking of 3D differentiated paths, fail to quantify robustness against disturbances, and lack effective online obstacle avoidance replanning capabilities. To address these gaps, a cooperative control strategy is proposed: first, the distributed cooperative path-following problem is quantified via time indices, and consistency is ensured through a distributed communication protocol; second, a longitudinal-lateral look-ahead angle adjustment method coupled with a robust guidance law is developed to achieve finite-time stabilization of path following error to zero under wind disturbances; third, an efficient local path replanning method with minimal time cost is designed for real-time online obstacle avoidance.Experimental validations demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the $\ $proposed strategy.

AI.tldr: Distributed cooperative path-following for multi-UAVs under DEM constraints with time-consistent tracking, wind disturbance robustness, and online obstacle replanning; includes a time-indexed formulation, a robust look-ahead guidance, and real-time local replanning; demonstrated by experiments.
AI.motivation: Address wind disturbances, obstacles, and lack of distributed time-synchronization mechanisms in low-altitude DEM airspace; enable finite-time stabilization and real-time replanning.
AI.method: 1) formulate distributed cooperative path-following using time indices and a distributed communication protocol for time-consistency; 2) design longitudinal-lateral look-ahead angle adjustment with a robust guidance law to achieve finite-time stabilization under wind; 3) develop an efficient local replanning method with minimal time cost for real-time obstacle avoidance.
AI.result: Experimental validations show effectiveness and superiority of the proposed strategy in achieving time-consistent tracking, robustness to disturbances, and quick obstacle avoidance replanning.
AI.conclusion: The integrated framework provides distributed, robust, and real-time cooperative control for multi-UAV path-following in challenging DEM environments, with potential for improved safety and efficiency.

title: The Geometric Price of Discrete Logic: Context-driven Manifold Dynamics of Number Representations
summary: Large language models (LLMs) generalize smoothly across continuous semantic spaces, yet strict logical reasoning demands the formation of discrete decision boundaries. Prevailing theories relying on linear isometric projections fail to resolve this fundamental tension. In this work, we argue that task context operates as a non-isometric dynamical operator that enforces a necessary "topological distortion." By applying Gram-Schmidt decomposition to residual-stream activations , we reveal a dual-modulation mechanism driving this process: a class-agnostic topological preservation that anchors global structure to prevent semantic collapse, and a specific algebraic divergence that directionally tears apart cross-class concepts to forge logical boundaries. We validate this geometric evolution across a gradient of tasks, from simple mapping to complex primality testing. Crucially, targeted specific vector ablation establishes a strict causal binding between this topology and model function: algebraically erasing the divergence component collapses parity classification accuracy from 100% to chance levels (38.57%). Furthermore, we uncover a three-phase layer-wise geometric dynamic and demonstrate that under social pressure prompts, models fail to generate sufficient divergence. This results in a "manifold entanglement" that geometrically explains sycophancy and hallucination. Ultimately, our findings revise the linear-isometric presumption, demonstrating that the emergence of discrete logic in LLMs is purchased at an irreducible cost of topological deformation.

AI.tldr: The abstract posits that task context acts as a non-isometric dynamical operator that distorts topology to create discrete logical boundaries in LLMs. It uses Gram-Schmidt on residual activations to reveal a dual mechanism: a class-agnostic topological preservation that maintains global structure, and a class-specific algebraic divergence that forms cross-class boundaries. Causally, ablating the divergence component collapses parity accuracy from 100% to ~38.6%. The study identifies a three-phase layer-wise geometric dynamic and shows social-pressure prompts reduce necessary divergence, causing manifold entanglement and hallucinations. Overall, it argues against linear-isometric projections and claims logic emergence costs topological deformation.
AI.motivation: To reconcile the smooth generalization of LLMs in continuous semantic spaces with the demand for discrete logical boundaries, challenging the prevalent linear-isometric view and providing a geometric/topological account of how logic and specialized behavior emerge and fail.
AI.method: Apply Gram-Schmidt decomposition to residual-stream activations across tasks; analyze topological/geometric dynamics; perform targeted vector ablations to test causality; characterize layer-wise dynamics; evaluate system under social-pressure prompts to observe changes in divergence and entanglement.
AI.result: Demonstrates a dual-modulation mechanism with a stable topological scaffold and a divergent component that carves out cross-class boundaries; causally links the divergence component to correct parity classification; reveals a three-phase layer-wise geometric evolution; shows social prompts hinder necessary divergence, leading to manifold entanglement and increased vulnerability to sycophancy/hallucination.
AI.conclusion: Invalidates a strictly linear-isometric explanation for discrete logic in LLMs, arguing that discrete reasoning arises at the cost of topological deformation and that topology-guided dynamics underlie both normal reasoning and failure modes.

title: From Pixels to Digital Agents: An Empirical Study on the Taxonomy and Technological Trends of Reinforcement Learning Environments
summary: The remarkable progress of reinforcement learning (RL) is intrinsically tied to the environments used to train and evaluate artificial agents. Moving beyond traditional qualitative reviews, this work presents a large-scale, data-driven empirical investigation into the evolution of RL environments. By programmatically processing a massive corpus of academic literature and rigorously distilling over 2,000 core publications, we propose a quantitative methodology to map the transition from isolated physical simulations to generalist, language-driven foundation agents. Implementing a novel, multi-dimensional taxonomy, we systematically analyze benchmarks against diverse application domains and requisite cognitive capabilities. Our automated semantic and statistical analysis reveals a profound, data-verified paradigm shift: the bifurcation of the field into a "Semantic Prior" ecosystem dominated by Large Language Models (LLMs) and a "Domain-Specific Generalization" ecosystem. Furthermore, we characterize the "cognitive fingerprints" of these distinct domains to uncover the underlying mechanisms of cross-task synergy, multi-domain interference, and zero-shot generalization. Ultimately, this study offers a rigorous, quantitative roadmap for designing the next generation of Embodied Semantic Simulators, bridging the gap between continuous physical control and high-level logical reasoning.

AI.tldr: A data-driven, large-scale survey maps RL environment evolution, revealing a paradigm shift toward Semantic Prior (LLMs) and Domain-Specific Generalization, and proposes Embodied Semantic Simulators as a roadmap.
AI.motivation: To quantify how RL environments evolved from isolated simulations to language-informed, generalist agents, addressing the lack of scalable, quantitative mapping across benchmarks, capabilities, and domains.
AI.method: Automated, large-scale literature processing of over 2,000 core RL publications; construction of a multi-dimensional taxonomy; automated semantic and statistical analyses; benchmarking across domains and cognitive abilities; analysis of cross-task dynamics (synergy, interference, zero-shot generalization).
AI.result: Evidence of a bifurcated ecosystem: a Semantic Prior regime dominated by LLMs and a Domain-Specific Generalization regime; characterization of cognitive fingerprints; identification of cross-task synergy and interference; a data-driven roadmap for Embodied Semantic Simulators bridging physical control and high-level reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Provides a quantitative framework and roadmap for next-generation RL environments and embodied agents, emphasizing integration of continuous control with language-driven reasoning; suggests directions for future embodied semantic simulators and multi-domain generalization.

title: SafeFlow: Real-Time Text-Driven Humanoid Whole-Body Control via Physics-Guided Rectified Flow and Selective Safety Gating
summary: Recent advances in real-time interactive text-driven motion generation have enabled humanoids to perform diverse behaviors. However, kinematics-only generators often exhibit physical hallucinations, producing motion trajectories that are physically infeasible to track with a downstream motion tracking controller or unsafe for real-world deployment. These failures often arise from the lack of explicit physics-aware objectives for real-robot execution and become more severe under out-of-distribution (OOD) user inputs. Hence, we propose SafeFlow, a text-driven humanoid whole-body control framework that combines physics-guided motion generation with a 3-Stage Safety Gate driven by explicit risk indicators. SafeFlow adopts a two-level architecture. At the high level, we generate motion trajectories using Physics-Guided Rectified Flow Matching in a VAE latent space to improve real-robot executability, and further accelerate sampling via Reflow to reduce the number of function evaluations (NFE) for real-time control. The 3-Stage Safety Gate enables selective execution by detecting semantic OOD prompts using a Mahalanobis score in text-embedding space, filtering unstable generations via a directional sensitivity discrepancy metric, and enforcing final hard kinematic constraints such as joint and velocity limits before passing the generated trajectory to a low-level motion tracking controller. Extensive experiments on the Unitree G1 demonstrate that SafeFlow outperforms prior diffusion-based methods in success rate, physical compliance, and inference speed, while maintaining diverse expressiveness.

AI.tldr: SafeFlow is a physics-guided, text-driven humanoid motion framework with a 3-stage safety gate, improving real-world executability and safety by filtering OOD prompts and enforcing hard kinematic constraints during generation and before low-level control; it accelerates sampling and achieves better success, compliance, and speed than prior diffusion-based methods on a Unitree G1.
AI.motivation: Kinematics-only motion generators produce physically infeasible or unsafe trajectories, especially under out-of-distribution prompts, hindering real-robot deployment. A physics-aware objective and explicit safety checks are needed to ensure real-world executability.
AI.method: High-level architecture: Physics-Guided Rectified Flow Matching in a VAE latent space to generate physically plausible trajectories; sampling acceleration via Reflow. A 3-Stage Safety Gate: (1) Mahalanobis-score-based OOD prompt detection in text-embedding space, (2) directional sensitivity discrepancy metric to filter unstable generations, (3) enforcement of hard kinematic constraints (joint limits, velocity limits) before passing to a low-level tracker.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the Unitree G1 demonstrates that SafeFlow achieves higher success rate, better physical compliance, and faster inference than prior diffusion-based methods, while preserving diverse expressiveness.
AI.conclusion: SafeFlow effectively couples physics-guided generation with a multi-stage safety mechanism to enhance real-robot executability for text-driven humanoid motion, offering improved safety, speed, and versatility over existing diffusion-based approaches.

title: Residual Attention Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Robust Multiphysics Simulation of Steady-State Electrothermal Energy Systems
summary: Efficient thermal management and precise field prediction are critical for the design of advanced energy systems, including electrohydrodynamic transport, microfluidic energy harvesters, and electrically driven thermal regulators. However, the steady-state simulation of these electrothermal coupled multiphysics systems remains challenging for physics-informed neural computation due to strong nonlinear field coupling, temperature-dependent coefficient variability, and complex interface dynamics. This study proposes a Residual Attention Physics-Informed Neural Network (RA-PINN) framework for the unified solution of coupled velocity, pressure, electric-potential, and temperature fields. By integrating a unified five-field operator formulation with residual-connected feature propagation and attention-guided channel modulation, the proposed architecture effectively captures localized coupling structures and steep gradients. We evaluate RA-PINN across four representative energy-relevant benchmarks: constant-coefficient coupling, indirect pressure-gauge constraints, temperature-dependent transport, and oblique-interface consistency. Comparative analysis against Pure-MLP, LSTM-PINN, and pLSTM-PINN demonstrates that RA-PINN achieves superior accuracy, yielding the lowest MSE, RMSE, and relative $L_2$ errors across all scenarios. Notably, RA-PINN maintains high structural fidelity in interface-dominated and variable-coefficient settings where conventional PINN backbones often fail. These results establish RA-PINN as a robust and accurate computational framework for the high-fidelity modeling and optimization of complex electrothermal multiphysics in sustainable energy applications.

AI.tldr: RA-PINN provides a unified five-field residual-attention PINN for coupled velocity, pressure, electric potential, and temperature fields in electrothermal multiphysics, outperforming baseline ML PINNs in accuracy and interface handling.
AI.motivation: Steady-state electrothermal multiphysics simulations are challenged by strong nonlinear coupling, temperature-dependent coefficients, and complex interfaces. A physics-informed framework with attention and residual connections aims to accurately capture localized couplings and steep gradients.
AI.method: Develop RA-PINN with a unified five-field operator formulation, residual-connected feature propagation, and attention-guided channel modulation to solve velocity, pressure, electric potential, and temperature in a single network; assess on four benchmarks: constant-coefficient coupling, indirect pressure-gauge constraints, temperature-dependent transport, and oblique-interface consistency.
AI.result: RA-PINN achieves superior accuracy, with the lowest MSE, RMSE, and relative L2 errors across all scenarios compared to Pure-MLP, LSTM-PINN, and pLSTM-PINN; it preserves structural fidelity at interfaces and under variable coefficients where conventional PINNs struggle.
AI.conclusion: RA-PINN is a robust and accurate computational framework for high-fidelity modeling and optimization of complex electrothermal multiphysics in sustainable energy applications.

title: LongTail Driving Scenarios with Reasoning Traces: The KITScenes LongTail Dataset
summary: In real-world domains such as self-driving, generalization to rare scenarios remains a fundamental challenge. To address this, we introduce a new dataset designed for end-to-end driving that focuses on long-tail driving events. We provide multi-view video data, trajectories, high-level instructions, and detailed reasoning traces, facilitating in-context learning and few-shot generalization. The resulting benchmark for multimodal models, such as VLMs and VLAs, goes beyond safety and comfort metrics by evaluating instruction following and semantic coherence between model outputs. The multilingual reasoning traces in English, Spanish, and Chinese are from domain experts with diverse cultural backgrounds. Thus, our dataset is a unique resource for studying how different forms of reasoning affect driving competence. Our dataset is available at: https://hf.co/datasets/kit-mrt/kitscenes-longtail

AI.tldr: A multimodal driving dataset and benchmark focusing on long-tail events, enabling in-context learning, few-shot generalization, and multilingual reasoning insights for VLMs/VLAs.
AI.motivation: Generalization to rare driving scenarios is critical yet underexplored; existing datasets lack long-tail coverage, multilingual traces, and reasoning-guided evaluation.
AI.method: Collect end-to-end driving data with multi-view video, trajectories, high-level instructions, and reasoning traces; annotate in English, Spanish, and Chinese; involve domain experts with diverse cultural backgrounds; release dataset for Hugging Face.
AI.result: Introduces KITScenes-LongTail dataset and benchmark; enables evaluation beyond safety/comfort to instruction following and semantic coherence; supports studies on cross-lingual reasoning in driving.
AI.conclusion: Dataset fills a gap for long-tail generalization research and multimodal instruction-following evaluation; enables assessment of how different reasoning forms affect driving competence across languages.

title: Language-Grounded Multi-Agent Planning for Personalized and Fair Participatory Urban Sensing
summary: Participatory urban sensing leverages human mobility for large-scale urban data collection, yet existing methods typically rely on centralized optimization and assume homogeneous participants, resulting in rigid assignments that overlook personal preferences and heterogeneous urban contexts. We propose MAPUS, an LLM-based multi-agent framework for personalized and fair participatory urban sensing. In our framework, participants are modeled as autonomous agents with individual profiles and schedules, while a coordinator agent performs fairness-aware selection and refines sensing routes through language-based negotiation. Experiments on real-world datasets show that MAPUS achieves competitive sensing coverage while substantially improving participant satisfaction and fairness, promoting more human-centric and sustainable urban sensing systems.

AI.tldr: MAPUS uses an LLM-based multi-agent system to personalize and fair participatory urban sensing, modeling participants as agents with profiles and schedules and using a coordinator agent to perform fairness-aware selection and route negotiation; achieves competitive coverage with higher user satisfaction and fairness.
AI.motivation: Current participatory sensing methods rely on centralized optimization and often assume homogeneous participants, yielding rigid task assignments that ignore user preferences and urban heterogeneity. There is a need for human-centric, fair, and sustainable sensing.
AI.method: MAPUS frames participatory sensing as a multi-agent problem: participant agents with individual profiles and schedules; a coordinator agent conducts fairness-aware task selection and refines sensing routes through language-based negotiation using LLMs.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world datasets show MAPUS achieves competitive sensing coverage while substantially improving participant satisfaction and fairness.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based multi-agent designs can enable personalized, fair, and sustainable participatory urban sensing, promoting human-centric urban sensing systems.

title: MIRROR: Visual Motion Imitation via Real-time Retargeting and Teleoperation with Parallel Differential Inverse Kinematics
summary: Real-time humanoid teleoperation requires inverse kinematics (IK) solvers that are both responsive and constraint-safe under kinematic redundancy and self-collision constraints. While differential IK enables efficient online retargeting, its locally linearized updates are inherently basin-dependent and often become trapped near joint limits, singularities, or active collision boundaries, leading to unsafe or stagnant behavior. We propose a GPU-parallelized, continuation-based differential IK that improves escape from such constraint-induced local minima while preserving real-time performance, promoting safety and stability. Multiple constrained IK quadratic programs are evaluated in parallel, together with a self-collision avoidance control barrier function (CBF), and a Lyapunov-based progression criterion selects updates that reduce the final global task-space error. The method is paired with a visual skeletal pose estimation pipeline that enables robust, real-time upper-body teleoperation on the THEMIS humanoid robot hardware in real-world tasks.

AI.tldr: GPU-parallelized continuation-based differential IK with parallel constrained QPs, barrier-based self-collision avoidance, and a Lyapunov progression criterion for real-time, constraint-safe humanoid teleoperation; validated on the THEMIS robot with real-world upper-body tasks.
AI.motivation: Real-time humanoid IK must be responsive and constraint-safe under kinematic redundancy and self-collision. Conventional differential IK is prone to being trapped by joint limits, singularities, or collision boundaries, leading to unsafe or stagnant behavior.
AI.method: A GPU-parallel continuation-based differential IK framework evaluating multiple constrained IK quadratic programs in parallel, augmented with a self-collision avoidance control barrier function (CBF) and a Lyapunov-based progression criterion to select updates that reduce final task-space error. The system is integrated with a visual skeletal pose estimation pipeline for robust, real-time upper-body teleoperation on the THEMIS robot.
AI.result: Enhanced ability to escape constraint-induced local minima while maintaining real-time performance; demonstrated robust, real-time upper-body teleoperation on real hardware (THEMIS) in real-world tasks with improved safety and stability.
AI.conclusion: The integration of continuation-based differential IK, parallel QPs, CBF-based self-collision avoidance, and a Lyapunov progression criterion provides a safe, responsive, real-time IK framework for redundancy-aware humanoid teleoperation, validated on the THEMIS platform.

title: MetaKube: An Experience-Aware LLM Framework for Kubernetes Failure Diagnosis
summary: Existing LLM-based Kubernetes diagnostic systems cannot learn from operational experience, operating on static knowledge bases without improving from past resolutions. We present MetaKube, an experience-aware LLM framework through three synergistic innovations: (1) an Episodic Pattern Memory Network (EPMN) that abstracts diagnostic patterns from historical resolutions and provides confidence-calibrated retrieval for both rapid pattern matching and guided causal exploration, (2) a meta-cognitive controller that dynamically routes between intuitive and analytical pathways based on problem familiarity, optimizing the trade-off between speed and depth, and (3) KubeLLM, a locally-deployable 8B model enhanced through domain-specific post-training on our 7,000-sample Kubernetes Fault Resolution Dataset. Evaluation on 1,873 real-world scenarios demonstrates MetaKube transforms Qwen3-8B from 50.9 to 90.5 points, approaching GPT-4.1 performance while ensuring complete data privacy. EPMN contributes 15.3% improvement through experiential learning, with continuous learning experiments showing progressive gains as the system accumulates operational knowledge. The source code and related resources are available at https://github.com/MetaKube-LLM-for-Kubernetes-Diagnosis/MetaKube.

AI.tldr: MetaKube introduces an episodic memory-based diagnostic framework (EPMN), a meta-cognitive controller for routing between intuition and analysis, and a locally deployed 8B model (KubeLLM) trained on a Kubernetes Fault Resolution Dataset; evaluated on 1,873 real-world scenarios showing large gains over Qwen3-8B and approaching GPT-4.1 while preserving data privacy; includes open-source code.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based Kubernetes diagnostic systems rely on static knowledge bases and cannot learn from operational experience; there is a need for experiential learning and privacy-preserving deployment.
AI.method: Three innovations: (1) EPMN with confidence-calibrated retrieval for rapid pattern matching and guided causal exploration; (2) a meta-cognitive controller that routes between intuitive and analytical pathways based on problem familiarity; (3) KubeLLM, an 8B model locally deployed and post-trained on a 7,000-sample Kubernetes Fault Resolution Dataset. Evaluation on 1,873 real-world scenarios; dataset name implied; measures include score improvements.
AI.result: Qwen3-8B performance improves from 50.9 to 90.5 points; MetaKube approaches GPT-4.1 while preserving data privacy; EPMN contributes 15.3% improvement; continuous learning experiments show progressive gains as experiential knowledge accumulates.
AI.conclusion: MetaKube demonstrates effective experience-aware LLM-based Kubernetes diagnosis with privacy preservation and competitive performance, aided by episodic memory, meta-cognition, and domain-specific post-training; source code is publicly available.

title: ELITE: Experiential Learning and Intent-Aware Transfer for Self-improving Embodied Agents
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown remarkable general capabilities, yet embodied agents built on them fail at complex tasks, often skipping critical steps, proposing invalid actions, and repeating mistakes. These failures arise from a fundamental gap between the static training data of VLMs and the physical interaction for embodied tasks. VLMs can learn rich semantic knowledge from static data but lack the ability to interact with the world. To address this issue, we introduce ELITE, an embodied agent framework with {E}xperiential {L}earning and {I}ntent-aware {T}ransfer that enables agents to continuously learn from their own environment interaction experiences, and transfer acquired knowledge to procedurally similar tasks. ELITE operates through two synergistic mechanisms, \textit{i.e.,} self-reflective knowledge construction and intent-aware retrieval. Specifically, self-reflective knowledge construction extracts reusable strategies from execution trajectories and maintains an evolving strategy pool through structured refinement operations. Then, intent-aware retrieval identifies relevant strategies from the pool and applies them to current tasks. Experiments on the EB-ALFRED and EB-Habitat benchmarks show that ELITE achieves 9\% and 5\% performance improvement over base VLMs in the online setting without any supervision. In the supervised setting, ELITE generalizes effectively to unseen task categories, achieving better performance compared to state-of-the-art training-based methods. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of ELITE for bridging the gap between semantic understanding and reliable action execution.

AI.tldr: ELITE enables experiential learning for VLM-based embodied agents via self-reflective knowledge construction and intent-aware retrieval, improving task performance and generalization without supervision.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between static VLM training data and the need for physical interaction in embodied tasks; current VLMs learn semantic knowledge but fail to translate to reliable action execution.
AI.method: Two mechanisms: (1) self-reflective knowledge construction to extract reusable strategies from execution trajectories and maintain an evolving strategy pool with structured refinements; (2) intent-aware retrieval to fetch relevant strategies from the pool and apply them to current tasks.
AI.result: Online setting: +9% (EB-ALFRED) and +5% (EB-Habitat) over base VLMs without supervision. Supervised setting: better generalization to unseen task categories than state-of-the-art training-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Bridges semantic understanding and action execution by experiential learning and intent-aware transfer, improving robustness and generalization of embodied agents.

title: QuadFM: Foundational Text-Driven Quadruped Motion Dataset for Generation and Control
summary:
Despite significant advances in quadrupedal robotics, a critical gap persists in foundational motion resources that holistically integrate diverse locomotion, emotionally expressive behaviors, and rich language semantics-essential for agile, intuitive human-robot interaction. Current quadruped motion datasets are limited to a few mocap primitives (e.g., walk, trot, sit) and lack diverse behaviors with rich language grounding. To bridge this gap, we introduce Quadruped Foundational Motion (QuadFM) , the first large-scale, ultra-high-fidelity dataset designed for text-to-motion generation and general motion control. QuadFM contains 11,784 curated motion clips spanning locomotion, interactive, and emotion-expressive behaviors (e.g., dancing, stretching, peeing), each with three-layer annotation-fine-grained action labels, interaction scenarios, and natural language commands-totaling 35,352 descriptions to support language-conditioned understanding and command execution.
  We further propose Gen2Control RL, a unified framework that jointly trains a general motion controller and a text-to-motion generator, enabling efficient end-to-end inference on edge hardware. On a real quadruped robot with an NVIDIA Orin, our system achieves real-time motion synthesis (<500 ms latency). Simulation and real-world results show realistic, diverse motions while maintaining robust physical interaction. The dataset will be released at https://github.com/GaoLii/QuadFM.

AI.tldr: QuadFM offers a large-scale, high-fidelity quadruped motion dataset (11,784 clips; 35,352 language descriptions) for text-to-motion and general motion control, paired with Gen2Control RL that jointly trains a text-to-motion generator and a motion controller for real-time edge inference (<500 ms) on a real quadruped (NVIDIA Orin).
AI.motivation: There is a gap in foundational, language-grounded quadruped motion resources that unify diverse locomotion, interactive behaviors, and emotion-expressive actions for natural human–robot interaction; existing datasets are limited to a few mocap primitives and lack rich language grounding.
AI.method: QuadFM: curated 11,784 motion clips spanning locomotion, interactive, and emotion-expressive behaviors, with three-layer annotations (fine-grained action labels, interaction scenarios, natural language commands) totaling 35,352 descriptions. Gen2Control RL: a unified framework that jointly trains a general motion controller and a text-to-motion generator, enabling end-to-end inference on edge hardware and real-time synthesis on a real quadruped with NVIDIA Orin.
AI.result: Demonstrates realistic, diverse motions in simulation and real-world tests; real-time motion synthesis with latency under 500 ms on hardware; dataset release planned at the stated repository.
AI.conclusion: QuadFM and Gen2Control RL advance language-conditioned, general-purpose quadruped motion resources and control, enabling more intuitive human–robot interaction and efficient edge deployment; the dataset will be publicly released to spur broader research.

title: AI Generalisation Gap In Comorbid Sleep Disorder Staging
summary: Accurate sleep staging is essential for diagnosing OSA and hypopnea in stroke patients. Although PSG is reliable, it is costly, labor-intensive, and manually scored. While deep learning enables automated EEG-based sleep staging in healthy subjects, our analysis shows poor generalization to clinical populations with disrupted sleep. Using Grad-CAM interpretations, we systematically demonstrate this limitation. We introduce iSLEEPS, a newly clinically annotated ischemic stroke dataset (to be publicly released), and evaluate a SE-ResNet plus bidirectional LSTM model for single-channel EEG sleep staging. As expected, cross-domain performance between healthy and diseased subjects is poor. Attention visualizations, supported by clinical expert feedback, show the model focuses on physiologically uninformative EEG regions in patient data. Statistical and computational analyses further confirm significant sleep architecture differences between healthy and ischemic stroke cohorts, highlighting the need for subject-aware or disease-specific models with clinical validation before deployment. A summary of the paper and the code is available at https://himalayansaswatabose.github.io/iSLEEPS_Explainability.github.io/

AI.tldr: Study highlights poor generalization of healthy-subject sleep staging models to ischemic stroke patients using single-channel EEG; introduces iSLEEPS dataset and SE-ResNet+BiLSTM with Grad-CAM explainability; shows attention focuses on non-informative EEG regions in patients; argues for disease-specific models with clinical validation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitation of current deep learning sleep-staging models trained on healthy subjects when applied to clinical populations with disrupted sleep, particularly ischemic stroke patients, and to provide explainability and disease-aware evaluation.
AI.method: Evaluate a SE-ResNet plus bidirectional LSTM model for single-channel EEG sleep staging on the new iSLEEPS ischemic stroke dataset; use Grad-CAM for interpretability; compare cross-domain performance between healthy and diseased subjects; collect clinical expert feedback on attention visualizations.
AI.result: Cross-domain performance is poor; Grad-CAM reveals the model attends to physiologically uninformative EEG regions in patient data; significant sleep-architecture differences exist between healthy and stroke cohorts; dataset and code to be publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Subject-aware or disease-specific models with clinical validation are necessary before deployment; generic healthy-subject trained models are inadequate for ischemic stroke sleep staging, underscoring the need for clinically validated approaches and dataset resources.

title: Ukrainian Visual Word Sense Disambiguation Benchmark
summary: This study presents a benchmark for evaluating the Visual Word Sense Disambiguation (Visual-WSD) task in Ukrainian. The main goal of the Visual-WSD task is to identify, with minimal contextual information, the most appropriate representation of a given ambiguous word from a set of ten images. To construct this benchmark, we followed a methodology similar to that proposed by (CITATION), who previously introduced benchmarks for the Visual-WSD task in English, Italian, and Farsi. This approach allows us to incorporate the Ukrainian benchmark into a broader framework for cross-language model performance comparisons. We collected the benchmark data semi-automatically and refined it with input from domain experts. We then assessed eight multilingual and multimodal large language models using this benchmark. All tested models performed worse than the zero-shot CLIP-based baseline model (CITATION) used by (CITATION) for the English Visual-WSD task. Our analysis revealed a significant performance gap in the Visual-WSD task between Ukrainian and English.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Ukrainian Visual-WSD benchmark, evaluates eight multilingual multimodal models, and finds all underperforming relative to a zero-shot CLIP baseline, highlighting a sizable Ukrainian–English performance gap.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of Ukrainian resources for Visual Word Sense Disambiguation and enable cross-language model performance comparisons by extending the established Visual-WSD benchmarking framework to Ukrainian, following prior benchmarks in English, Italian, and Farsi.
AI.method: Follow the established benchmark methodology, with semi-automatic data collection refined by domain experts; construct a Ukrainian Visual-WSD benchmark; evaluate eight multilingual multimodal large language models; compare against the zero-shot CLIP-based baseline used in English Visual-WSD.
AI.result: All tested models perform worse than the zero-shot CLIP-based baseline; there is a substantial performance gap between Ukrainian and English for Visual-WSD.
AI.conclusion: The Ukrainian Visual-WSD benchmark provides a challenging resource that exposes gaps in current multilingual multimodal models; to reduce the Ukrainian–English gap, improvements in cross-lingual alignment, multimodal representation learning, or task-specific adaptation are needed.

title: Enhanced Mycelium of Thought (EMoT): A Bio-Inspired Hierarchical Reasoning Architecture with Strategic Dormancy and Mnemonic Encoding
comment: 32 pages, 6 figures, 15 tables; includes ablation studies and reasoning trace visualisation
summary: Current prompting paradigms for large language models (LLMs), including Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT), follow linear or tree-structured reasoning paths that lack persistent memory, strategic dormancy, and cross-domain synthesis. We present the Enhanced Mycelium of Thought (EMoT) framework, a bio-inspired reasoning architecture that organises cognitive processing into a four-level hierarchy (Micro, Meso, Macro, Meta), implements strategic dormancy and reactivation of reasoning nodes, and integrates a Memory Palace with five mnemonic encoding styles. EMoT is a research prototype for complex, multi-domain problems, not a general-purpose prompting enhancement. Two complementary evaluations reveal a characteristic trade-off. In a blind LLM-as-Judge evaluation across three domains, EMoT achieved near-parity with CoT (4.20 vs. 4.33/5.0) with higher stability, and outperformed CoT on Cross-Domain Synthesis (4.8 vs. 4.4). Ablation studies show that strategic dormancy is architecturally essential (quality collapsed from 4.2 to 1.0 when disabled). On a 15-item short-answer benchmark, EMoT (27%) substantially underperformed simpler baselines, confirming systematic overthinking on simple problems. These results are subject to important limitations: small sample sizes (n=3 complex cases, n=15 short-answer items), LLM-as-Judge evaluation with potential self-preference bias, and approximately 33-fold computational cost overhead. To our knowledge, EMoT is the first reasoning framework to combine hierarchical topology, strategic thought dormancy with reactivation, and mnemonic memory encoding in a single architecture.

AI.tldr: EMoT is a four-level hierarchical, memory-augmented reasoning framework with dormancy/activation and mnemonic encoding; shows near-parity with CoT and cross-domain gains but high compute cost; ablation confirms dormancy is essential; underperforms on simple tasks; limitations include small samples and potential judge bias; novel in combining hierarchy, dormancy, and mnemonic memory.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of current prompting paradigms (CoT/ToT) that lack persistent memory, strategic dormancy, and cross-domain synthesis; explore a memory-augmented, dormancy-based reasoning architecture for complex, multi-domain problems.
AI.method: Propose EMoT architecture: four-level hierarchy (Micro, Meso, Macro, Meta), strategic dormancy and reactivation of reasoning nodes, and a Memory Palace with five mnemonic encoding styles. Evaluation includes blind LLM-as-Judge across three domains (general performance and Cross-Domain Synthesis), ablation study disabling dormancy, and a 15-item short-answer benchmark; reports computational cost overhead (~33x).
AI.result: EMoT achieves near parity with Chain-of-Thought on overall scores (4.20 vs 4.33/5.0) with higher stability and outperforms CoT on Cross-Domain Synthesis (4.8 vs 4.4). Ablation shows strategic dormancy is essential (quality drops 4.2→1.0 when disabled). On a 15-item short-answer benchmark, EMoT underperforms simple baselines (27%), indicating overthinking on simple problems. Limitations include small samples (n=3 complex cases, n=15 items), potential judge bias, and substantial compute overhead (~33x).
AI.conclusion: EMoT is the first framework to combine hierarchical topology, strategic dormancy with reactivation, and mnemonic memory encoding in a single architecture; positioned as a research prototype for complex, multi-domain problems rather than a general-purpose prompting enhancement.

title: PCHC: Enabling Preference Conditioned Humanoid Control via Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning
summary: Humanoid robots often need to balance competing objectives, such as maximizing speed while minimizing energy consumption. While current reinforcement learning (RL) methods can master complex skills like fall recovery and perceptive locomotion, they are constrained by fixed weighting strategies that produce a single suboptimal policy, rather than providing a diverse set of solutions for sophisticated multi-objective control. In this paper, we propose a novel framework leveraging Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning (MORL) to achieve Preference-Conditioned Humanoid Control (PCHC). Unlike conventional methods that require training a series of policies to approximate the Pareto front, our framework enables a single, preference-conditioned policy to exhibit a wide spectrum of diverse behaviors. To effectively integrate these requirements, we introduce a Beta distribution-based alignment mechanism based on preference vectors modulating a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) module. We validated our approach on two representative humanoid tasks. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework allows the robot to adaptively shift its objective priorities in real-time based on the input preference condition.

AI.tldr: A single preference-conditioned MORL policy enables a wide spectrum of humanoid behaviors without training multiple Pareto-approximation policies, via Beta-aligned MoE to map preferences to behavior.
AI.motivation: Overcomes fixed-weight RL limitations that yield a single suboptimal policy, by providing a continuous, preference-driven spectrum of trade-offs for multi-objective humanoid control.
AI.method: Introduce a preference-conditioned MORL framework (PCHC) that uses a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) module whose gating is guided by a Beta-distribution-based alignment ensuring diverse behaviors across the Pareto front. Train a single policy that conditions on user preferences to select/weight experts.
AI.result: Extensive simulations and real-world experiments on two humanoid tasks show the robot can adaptively adjust objective priorities in real time according to the input preference, producing diverse, high-quality behaviors.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PCHC framework achieves efficient, real-time, preference-conditioned control with a single learning policy, reducing the need for training multiple policies to cover the Pareto front and enabling versatile humanoid behavior.

title: LineMVGNN: Anti-Money Laundering with Line-Graph-Assisted Multi-View Graph Neural Networks
comment: Published as a journal paper in AI 2025
summary: Anti-money laundering (AML) systems are important for protecting the global economy. However, conventional rule-based methods rely on domain knowledge, leading to suboptimal accuracy and a lack of scalability. Graph neural networks (GNNs) for digraphs (directed graphs) can be applied to transaction graphs and capture suspicious transactions or accounts. However, most spectral GNNs do not naturally support multi-dimensional edge features, lack interpretability due to edge modifications, and have limited scalability owing to their spectral nature. Conversely, most spatial methods may not capture the money flow well. Therefore, in this work, we propose LineMVGNN (Line-Graph-Assisted Multi-View Graph Neural Network), a novel spatial method that considers payment and receipt transactions. Specifically, the LineMVGNN model extends a lightweight MVGNN module, which performs two-way message passing between nodes in a transaction graph. Additionally, LineMVGNN incorporates a line graph view of the original transaction graph to enhance the propagation of transaction information. We conduct experiments on two real-world account-based transaction datasets: the Ethereum phishing transaction network dataset and a financial payment transaction dataset from one of our industry partners. The results show that our proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art methods, reflecting the effectiveness of money laundering detection with line-graph-assisted multi-view graph learning. We also discuss scalability, adversarial robustness, and regulatory considerations of our proposed method.

AI.tldr: A novel two-view, line-graph-assisted GNN for AML on directed transaction graphs. It combines a lightweight MVGNN with a line graph view to better propagate transaction info across money flows, addressing limitations of spectral GNNs. Demonstrates improved detection on real-world datasets with attention to scalability, robustness, and regulatory considerations.
AI.motivation: Rule-based AML systems lack scalability and accuracy; spectral GNNs struggle with multi-dimensional edge features and interpretability; spatial GNNs may miss money-flow dynamics. There is a need for a scalable, interpretable, and effective graph-based AML method that captures both payment and receipt directions.
AI.method: Extend MVGNN with two-way (buyer/seller) message passing; incorporate a line graph view of the original transaction graph to enhance propagation of transaction information; train and evaluate on real-world datasets (Ethereum phishing network and industry partner financial dataset); analyze scalability, adversarial robustness, and regulatory considerations.
AI.result: Proposed LineMVGNN outperforms state-of-the-art methods on the two datasets, demonstrating effective detection of money laundering with line-graph-assisted multi-view graph learning.
AI.conclusion: LineMVGNN offers improved money laundering detection by leveraging line-graph views and multi-view message passing, addressing key limitations of existing spectral and spatial GNNs; highlights considerations for scalability, robustness, and regulatory compliance.

title: Stochastic Ray Tracing for the Reconstruction of 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary:
Ray-tracing-based 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) methods overcome the limitations of rasterization -- rigid pinhole camera assumptions, inaccurate shadows, and lack of native reflection or refraction -- but remain slower due to the cost of sorting all intersecting Gaussians along every ray. Moreover, existing ray-tracing methods still rely on rasterization-style approximations such as shadow mapping for relightable scenes, undermining the generality that ray tracing promises.
  We present a differentiable, sorting-free stochastic formulation for ray-traced 3DGS -- the first framework that uses stochastic ray tracing to both reconstruct and render standard and relightable 3DGS scenes. At its core is an unbiased Monte Carlo estimator for pixel-color gradients that evaluates only a small sampled subset of Gaussians per ray, bypassing the need for sorting. For standard 3DGS, our method matches the reconstruction quality and speed of rasterization-based 3DGS while substantially outperforming sorting-based ray tracing. For relightable 3DGS, the same stochastic estimator drives per-Gaussian shading with fully ray-traced shadow rays, delivering notably higher reconstruction fidelity than prior work.

AI.tldr: Differentiable, sorting-free stochastic ray tracing for 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) enabling standard and relightable rendering with an unbiased Monte Carlo estimator that samples a subset of Gaussians per ray.
AI.motivation: Rasterization-based 3DGS suffers from rigid pinhole camera assumptions, shadows inaccuracies, and no native reflection/refraction; sorting all Gaussians along each ray in traditional 3DGS-ray tracing is slow, and prior ray tracing relies on rasterization-style relighting approximations.
AI.method: An unbiased Monte Carlo estimator for pixel-color gradients that evaluates a small, random subset of Gaussians per ray, removing the need to sort; a differentiable, sorting-free stochastic ray tracing framework that supports standard 3DGS and relightable 3DGS with per-Gaussian shading and true ray-traced shadows.
AI.result: For standard 3DGS, matches the reconstruction quality and speed of rasterization-based 3DGS while substantially outperforming sorting-based ray tracing. For relightable 3DGS, uses the same estimator to drive per-Gaussian shading with fully ray-traced shadow rays, yielding notably higher reconstruction fidelity than prior work.
AI.conclusion: This work is the first framework to deliver differentiable, sorting-free stochastic ray tracing for 3DGS, enabling high-fidelity relighting and competitive efficiency without Gaussian sorting.

title: Bridging the Evaluation Gap: Standardized Benchmarks for Multi-Objective Search
summary: Empirical evaluation in multi-objective search (MOS) has historically suffered from fragmentation, relying on heterogeneous problem instances with incompatible objective definitions that make cross-study comparisons difficult. This standardization gap is further exacerbated by the realization that DIMACS road networks, a historical default benchmark for the field, exhibit highly correlated objectives that fail to capture diverse Pareto-front structures. To address this, we introduce the first comprehensive, standardized benchmark suite for exact and approximate MOS. Our suite spans four structurally diverse domains: real-world road networks, structured synthetic graphs, game-based grid environments, and high-dimensional robotic motion-planning roadmaps. By providing fixed graph instances, standardized start-goal queries, and both exact and approximate reference Pareto-optimal solution sets, this suite captures a full spectrum of objective interactions: from strongly correlated to strictly independent. Ultimately, this benchmark provides a common foundation to ensure future MOS evaluations are robust, reproducible, and structurally comprehensive.

AI.tldr: Proposes the first comprehensive, standardized benchmark suite for exact and approximate multi-objective search, spanning four domains with fixed instances and reference Pareto sets to enable robust cross-study comparisons.
AI.motivation: Empirical MOS evaluation has been fragmented due to heterogeneous problem instances and incompatible objective definitions; DIMACS road networks exhibit highly correlated objectives that fail to capture diverse Pareto-front structures, impeding robust benchmarking.
AI.method: Develop four structurally diverse domains (real-world road networks, structured synthetic graphs, game-based grid environments, high-dimensional robotic motion-planning roadmaps); fix graph instances and start-goal queries; provide both exact and approximate reference Pareto-optimal solution sets to cover a range of objective interactions from strongly correlated to independent.
AI.result: A comprehensive benchmark suite enabling robust, reproducible MOS evaluations with structural diversity and fixed instances, spanning the spectrum of objective interactions.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a common foundation for future MOS evaluations, addressing standardization gaps and enabling meaningful cross-study comparisons across exact and approximate MOS methods.

title: SOMA: Strategic Orchestration and Memory-Augmented System for Vision-Language-Action Model Robustness via In-Context Adaptation
comment: 9 pages, 16 figures, 3 table. Submitted to IROS 2026
summary: Despite the promise of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models as generalist robotic controllers, their robustness against perceptual noise and environmental variations in out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks remains fundamentally limited by the absence of long-term memory, causal failure attribution, and dynamic intervention capability. To address this, we propose SOMA, a Strategic Orchestration and Memory-Augmented System that upgrades frozen VLA policies for robust in-context adaptation without parameter fine-tuning. Specifically, SOMA operates through an online pipeline of contrastive Dual-Memory Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), an Attribution-Driven Large-Language-Model (LLM) Orchestrator, and extensible Model Context Protocol (MCP) interventions, while an offline Memory Consolidation module continuously distills the execution traces into reliable priors. Experimental evaluations across three backbone models (pi0, pi0.5, and SmolVLA) on LIBERO-PRO and our proposed LIBERO-SOMA benchmarks demonstrate that SOMA achieves an average absolute success rate gain of 56.6%. This includes a significant absolute improvement of 89.1% in long-horizon task chaining. Project page and source code are available at: https://github.com/LZY-1021/SOMA.

AI.tldr: SOMA is a memory-augmented system that improves frozen Vision-Language-Action policies for robust, out-of-distribution task handling via online retrieval-augmented generation, LLM orchestration, and offline memory consolidation, achieving large gains on LIBERO benchmarks.
AI.motivation: VLA models lack long-term memory, causal failure attribution, and dynamic intervention capabilities, limiting robustness in OOD and long-horizon tasks; there is a need for in-context adaptation without fine-tuning of frozen policies.
AI.method: An online pipeline combining contrastive dual-memory retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), an attribution-driven LLM orchestrator, and extensible Model Context Protocol (MCP) interventions, plus an offline Memory Consolidation module that distills traces into priors. Evaluated across pi0, pi0.5, and SmolVLA on LIBERO-PRO and LIBERO-SOMA benchmarks.
AI.result: SOMA yields an average absolute success-rate gain of 56.6%, with an 89.1% absolute improvement in long-horizon task chaining. Project page and code are provided (GitHub).
AI.conclusion: By upgrading frozen VLA policies with memory and planning-enabled components, SOMA enables robust in-context adaptation without parameter fine-tuning and demonstrates substantial performance gains on complex robotic tasks.

title: A Theory of LLM Information Susceptibility
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as optimization modules in agentic systems, yet the fundamental limits of such LLM-mediated improvement remain poorly understood. Here we propose a theory of LLM information susceptibility, centred on the hypothesis that when computational resources are sufficiently large, the intervention of a fixed LLM does not increase the performance susceptibility of a strategy set with respect to budget. We develop a multi-variable utility-function framework that generalizes this hypothesis to architectures with multiple co-varying budget channels, and discuss the conditions under which co-scaling can exceed the susceptibility bound. We validate the theory empirically across structurally diverse domains and model scales spanning an order of magnitude, and show that nested, co-scaling architectures open response channels unavailable to fixed configurations. These results clarify when LLM intervention helps and when it does not, demonstrating that tools from statistical physics can provide predictive constraints for the design of AI systems. If the susceptibility hypothesis holds generally, the theory suggests that nested architectures may be a necessary structural condition for open-ended agentic self-improvement.

AI.tldr: Proposes a theory of LLM information susceptibility showing that with ample resources, fixed LLM interventions do not increase a strategy's budget-related performance susceptibility; extends to multi-channel budgets, co-scaling can beat a fixed bound; validated across diverse domains and model scales; nested architectures enable new response channels and may be necessary for open-ended self-improvement.
AI.motivation: Understand fundamental limits of LLM-mediated optimization in agentic systems and derive predictive constraints for designing AI systems.
AI.method: Develop a multi-variable utility-function framework that generalizes the susceptibility hypothesis to architectures with multiple co-varying budget channels; derive conditions under which co-scaling can exceed the susceptibility bound; perform empirical validation across structurally diverse domains and model scales; analyze nested, co-scaling architectures.
AI.result: The theory is empirically validated; under sufficient resources, fixed LLM interventions do not increase susceptibility beyond the bound; co-scaling architectures open response channels inaccessible to fixed configurations; these results provide predictive constraints for AI system design and suggest nested architectures may be necessary for open-ended self-improvement.
AI.conclusion: If the susceptibility hypothesis holds generally, nested architectures could be required for open-ended agentic self-improvement, and statistical-physics-inspired tools can guide the principled design of AI systems.

title: λSplit: Self-Supervised Content-Aware Spectral Unmixing for Fluorescence Microscopy
comment: 14 pages, 25 pages supplement, 16 figures total, 14 tables total
summary: In fluorescence microscopy, spectral unmixing aims to recover individual fluorophore concentrations from spectral images that capture mixed fluorophore emissions. Since classical methods operate pixel-wise and rely on least-squares fitting, their performance degrades with increasingly overlapping emission spectra and higher levels of noise, suggesting that a data-driven approach that can learn and utilize a structural prior might lead to improved results. Learning-based approaches for spectral imaging do exist, but they are either not optimized for microscopy data or are developed for very specific cases that are not applicable to fluorescence microscopy settings. To address this, we propose λSplit, a physics-informed deep generative model that learns a conditional distribution over concentration maps using a hierarchical Variational Autoencoder. A fully differentiable Spectral Mixer enforces consistency with the image formation process, while the learned structural priors enable state-of-the-art unmixing and implicit noise removal. We demonstrate λSplit on 3 real-world datasets that we synthetically cast into a total of 66 challenging spectral unmixing benchmarks. We compare our results against a total of 10 baseline methods, including classical methods and a range of learning-based methods. Our results consistently show competitive performance and improved robustness in high noise regimes, when spectra overlap considerably, or when the spectral dimensionality is lowered, making λSplit a new state-of-the-art for spectral unmixing of fluorescent microscopy data. Importantly, λSplit is compatible with spectral data produced by standard confocal microscopes, enabling immediate adoption without specialized hardware modifications.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed deep generative model (λSplit) for spectral unmixing in fluorescence microscopy using a hierarchical VAE with a differentiable Spectral Mixer to enforce image-formation consistency; achieves state-of-the-art robustness and compatibility with standard confocal data.
AI.motivation: Classical pixel-wise least-squares spectral unmixing struggles with overlapping spectra and noise; existing learning methods are not tailored to microscopy or are too specific. A data-driven approach with a structural prior and physics constraints could improve accuracy and robustness.
AI.method: A hierarchical Variational Autoencoder learns a conditional distribution over fluorophore concentration maps. A fully differentiable Spectral Mixer enforces consistency with the spectral image formation process. Training uses 3 real datasets synthetically cast into 66 challenging benchmarks, compared against 10 baselines (classical and learning-based methods).
AI.result: λSplit achieves competitive performance and improved robustness under high noise, strong spectral overlap, or low spectral dimensionality, establishing state-of-the-art unmixing for fluorescence microscopy. It works with spectral data from standard confocal microscopes.
AI.conclusion: λSplit provides a practical, robust, data-driven unmixing solution that integrates physics-informed priors and is readily adoptable on standard microscopy hardware.

title: AI-Supervisor: Autonomous AI Research Supervision via a Persistent Research World Model
summary: Existing automated research systems operate as stateless, linear pipelines, generating outputs without maintaining a persistent understanding of the research landscape. They process papers sequentially, propose ideas without structured gap analysis, and lack mechanisms for agents to verify or refine each other's findings. We present AutoProf (Autonomous Professor), a multi-agent orchestration framework where specialized agents provide end-to-end AI research supervision driven by human interests, from literature review through gap discovery, method development, evaluation, and paper writing, via autonomous exploration and self-correcting updates. Unlike sequential pipelines, AutoProf maintains a continuously evolving Research World Model implemented as a Knowledge Graph, capturing methods, benchmarks, limitations, and unexplored gaps as shared memory across agents. The framework introduces three contributions: first, structured gap discovery that decomposes methods into modules, evaluates them across benchmarks, and identifies module-level gaps; second, self-correcting discovery loops that analyze why modules succeed or fail, detect benchmark biases, and assess evaluation adequacy; third, self-improving development loops using cross-domain mechanism search to iteratively address failing components. All agents operate under a consensus mechanism where findings are validated before being committed to the shared model. The framework is model-agnostic, supports mainstream large language models, and scales elastically with token budget from lightweight exploration to full-scale investigation.
