AI.tldr: AutoProf is a multi-agent autonomous research framework that maintains a shared Knowledge Graph (Research World Model) to enable structured gap discovery, self-correcting loops, and self-improving development, with consensus-based validation and model-agnostic scalability.
AI.motivation: Existing automated research systems are stateless, linear pipelines delivering outputs without persistent understanding of the research landscape; this hampers discovery, verification, and refinement of research results.
AI.method: A multi-agent orchestration framework where specialized agents perform literature review, gap discovery, method development, evaluation, and paper writing under human interests; maintains a continuously evolving Research World Model as a Knowledge Graph; introduces structured gap discovery (modular decomposition and benchmark-aware evaluation), self-correcting discovery loops (analyze success/failure, detect biases, assess evaluation), and self-improving development loops (cross-domain mechanism search); all agents operate under a consensus mechanism ensuring validation before updating the shared model; model-agnostic and scalable with token budget.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with three contributions; a persistent, evolving knowledge-graph-based approach enabling end-to-end AI research supervision across literature review, gap discovery, development, evaluation, and writing; supports mainstream LLMs and elastic scaling.
AI.conclusion: AutoProf offers an integrated system with a continuously updated Research World Model, enabling end-to-end autonomous AI research supervision and self-improvement through structured gap discovery, consensus-based validation, and multi-agent collaboration.

title: Steering Code LLMs with Activation Directions for Language and Library Control
summary: Code LLMs often default to particular programming languages and libraries under neutral prompts. We investigate whether these preferences are encoded as approximately linear directions in activation space that can be manipulated at inference time. Using a difference-in-means method, we estimate layer-wise steering vectors for five language/library pairs and add them to model hidden states during generation. Across three open-weight code LLMs, these interventions substantially increase generation toward the target ecosystem under neutral prompts and often remain effective even when prompts explicitly request the opposite choice. Steering strength varies by model and target, with common ecosystems easier to induce than rarer alternatives, and overly strong interventions can reduce output quality. Overall, our results suggest that code-style preferences in LLMs are partly represented by compact, steerable structure in activation space.

AI.tldr: Code LLMs encode steerable, approximately linear activation-space directions that bias code generation toward specific languages/libraries; inference-time manipulation can steer outputs, but excessive steering harms quality.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether model-coded biases toward programming ecosystems are stored as compact, manipulable directions in hidden representations, enabling runtime control.
AI.method: Estimate layer-wise steering vectors for five language/library pairs via a difference-in-means approach; add these vectors to hidden states during generation; test across three open-weight code LLMs under neutral prompts and prompts requesting opposite choices.
AI.result: Steering increases generation toward the target ecosystem under neutral prompts and often remains effective when prompts oppose the choice; effectiveness and strength vary by model and target; common ecosystems are easier to induce; overly strong steering degrades output quality.
AI.conclusion: Code-style preferences in LLMs are partly captured by compact, steerable structure in activation space, enabling manipulation at inference time with trade-offs in quality.

title: Foundation Model Embeddings Meet Blended Emotions: A Multimodal Fusion Approach for the BLEMORE Challenge
summary: We present our system for the BLEMORE Challenge at FG 2026 on blended emotion recognition with relative salience prediction. Our approach combines six encoder families through late probability fusion: an S4D-ViTMoE face encoder adapted with soft-label KL training, frozen layer-selective Wav2Vec2 audio features, finetuned body-language encoders (TimeSformer, VideoMAE), and -- for the first time in emotion recognition -- Gemini Embedding 2.0, a large multimodal model whose video embeddings produce competitive presence accuracy (ACCP = 0.320) from only 2 seconds of input. Three key findings emerge from our experiments: selecting prosody-encoding layers (6--12) from frozen Wav2Vec2 outperforms end-to-end finetuning (Score 0.207 vs. 0.161), as the non-verbal nature of BLEMORE audio makes phonetic layers irrelevant; the post-processing salience threshold $β$ varies from 0.05 to 0.43 across folds, revealing that personalized expression styles are the primary bottleneck; and task-adapted encoders collectively receive 62\% of ensemble weight over general-purpose baselines. Our 12-encoder system achieves Score = 0.279 (ACCP = 0.391, ACCS = 0.168) on the test set, placing 6th.

AI.tldr: A 12-encoder late-fusion system for blended emotion recognition in BLEMORE, achieving ACCP 0.391 and Score 0.279, with insights on prosody layer selection, salience threshold, and encoder weight distribution; sixth place.
AI.motivation: Address blended emotion recognition with relative salience prediction by leveraging diverse encoders and a large multimodal model to capture multimodal cues and personalization bottlenecks.
AI.method: Late probability fusion of six encoders: S4D-ViTMoE face encoder with soft-label KL training; frozen layer-selective Wav2Vec2 audio features (prosody layers 6–12); finetuned body-language encoders (TimeSformer, VideoMAE); Gemini Embedding 2.0 video embeddings; salience post-processing with threshold β; evaluation on BLEMORE data with cross-validation folds.
AI.result: Individual components show prosody layers outperform end-to-end finetuning (0.207 vs 0.161); Gemini ACCP 0.320 with 2-second input; ensemble achieves 0.279 Score (ACCP 0.391, ACCS 0.168) and 6th place on test.
AI.conclusion: Task-adapted encoders collectively receive 62% of ensemble weight; personalized expression styles are primary bottleneck; non-verbal BLEMORE audio renders phonetic layers irrelevant; demonstrates effectiveness of gated late fusion with diverse encoders and multimodal embeddings.

title: Equivariant Filter Transformations for Consistent and Efficient Visual--Inertial Navigation
summary: This paper presents an equivariant filter (EqF) transformation approach for visual--inertial navigation. By establishing analytical links between EqFs with different symmetries, the proposed approach enables systematic consistency design and efficient implementation. First, we formalize the mapping from the global system state to the local error-state and prove that it induces a nonsingular linear transformation between the error-states of any two EqFs. Second, we derive transformation laws for the associated linearized error-state systems and unobservable subspaces. These results yield a general consistency design principle: for any unobservable system, a consistent EqF with a state-independent unobservable subspace can be synthesized by transforming the local coordinate chart, thereby avoiding ad hoc symmetry analysis. Third, to mitigate the computational burden arising from the non-block-diagonal Jacobians required for consistency, we propose two efficient implementation strategies. These strategies exploit the Jacobians of a simpler EqF with block-diagonal structure to accelerate covariance operations while preserving consistency. Extensive Monte Carlo simulations and real-world experiments validate the proposed approach in terms of both accuracy and runtime.

AI.tldr: Equivariant filter transformation (EqF) enables systematic consistency design for visual–inertial navigation across different EqFs, by mapping global to local error-states, deriving transformation laws for linearized error systems and unobservable subspaces, and proposing two efficient Jacobian-based acceleration strategies; validated by simulations and real-world experiments.
AI.motivation: To address consistency and computational efficiency in visual–inertial navigation by leveraging equivariant filtering, enabling state mappings between EqFs and avoiding ad hoc symmetry analyses.
AI.method: 1) Formalize the mapping from the global state to the local error-state and prove it induces a nonsingular linear transformation between error-states of any two EqFs. 2) Derive transformation laws for the linearized error-state systems and their unobservable subspaces. 3) Propose two efficient implementation strategies that exploit Jacobians of a simpler, block-diagonal EqF to accelerate covariance operations while preserving consistency.
AI.result: Establish a general consistency design principle: for any unobservable system, a consistent EqF with a state-independent unobservable subspace can be synthesized by transforming the local coordinate chart. Propose acceleration strategies to reduce computational burden without sacrificing consistency. Demonstrate through extensive Monte Carlo simulations and real-world experiments that the approach improves accuracy and runtime.
AI.conclusion: The proposed EqF transformation framework enables systematic consistency design across EqFs, provides a practical method to handle unobservable subspaces via coordinate-chart transformation, and offers computationally efficient strategies validated experimentally.

title: Boost Like a (Var)Pro: Trust-Region Gradient Boosting via Variable Projection
summary: Gradient boosting, a method of building additive ensembles from weak learners, has established itself as a practical and theoretically-motivated approach to approximate functions, especially using decision tree weak learners. Comparable methods for smooth parametric learners, such as neural networks, remain less developed in both training methodology and theory. To this end, we introduce \texttt{VPBoost} ({\bf V}ariable {\bf P}rojection {\bf Boost}ing), a gradient boosting algorithm for separable smooth approximators, i.e., models with a smooth nonlinear featurizer followed by a final linear mapping. \texttt{VPBoost} fuses variable projection, a training paradigm for separable models that enforces optimality of the linear weights, with a second-order weak learning strategy. The combination of second-order boosting, separable models, and variable projection give rise to a closed-form solution for the optimal linear weights and a natural interpretation of \VPBoost as a functional trust-region method. We thereby leverage trust-region theory to prove \VPBoost converges to a stationary point under mild geometric conditions and, under stronger assumptions, achieves a superlinear convergence rate. Comprehensive numerical experiments on synthetic data, image recognition, and scientific machine learning benchmarks demonstrate that \VPBoost learns an ensemble with improved evaluation metrics in comparison to gradient-descent-based boosting and attains competitive performance relative to an industry-standard decision tree boosting algorithm.

AI.tldr: VPBoost is a gradient boosting method designed for separable smooth approximators (smooth feature map followed by a linear head). It combines variable projection with second-order boosting to yield a closed-form optimal linear weight, and interprets the method as a functional trust-region procedure, with convergence to a stationary point (and potential superlinear rate) under mild-to-strong conditions. Empirically, it achieves improved metrics over gradient-descent boosting and is competitive with tree-based boosting on various benchmarks.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in both training methodology and theory for boosting with smooth parametric learners (e.g., neural networks). The goal is to extend boosting to separable nonlinear features while obtaining principled optimization for the final linear layer and theoretical convergence guarantees.
AI.method: The authors propose VPBoost, which uses variable projection for separable models (a smooth feature map followed by a linear layer) and a second-order weak learning scheme. This yields a closed-form solution for the optimal linear weights and frames the algorithm as a functional trust-region method. Theoretical results establish convergence to a stationary point under mild geometric conditions and, under stronger assumptions, superlinear convergence.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic data, image recognition, and scientific ML benchmarks show that VPBoost learns ensembles with improved evaluation metrics compared with gradient-descent-based boosting and achieves competitive performance relative to a standard industry-grade decision-tree boosting algorithm.
AI.conclusion: VPBoost provides a principled, theoretically grounded boosting framework for separable smooth approximators, delivering convergence guarantees and competitive empirical performance, thereby bridging gradient boosting with smooth parametric learners.

title: Estimating Individual Tree Height and Species from UAV Imagery
summary: Accurate estimation of forest biomass, a major carbon sink, relies heavily on tree-level traits such as height and species. Unoccupied Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) capturing high-resolution imagery from a single RGB camera offer a cost-effective and scalable approach for mapping and measuring individual trees. We introduce BIRCH-Trees, the first benchmark for individual tree height and species estimation from tree-centered UAV images, spanning three datasets: temperate forests, tropical forests, and boreal plantations. We also present DINOvTree, a unified approach using a Vision Foundation Model (VFM) backbone with task-specific heads for simultaneous height and species prediction. Through extensive evaluations on BIRCH-Trees, we compare DINOvTree against commonly used vision methods, including VFMs, as well as biological allometric equations. We find that DINOvTree achieves top overall results with accurate height predictions and competitive classification accuracy while using only 54% to 58% of the parameters of the second-best approach.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark for unit-tree height and species estimation from tree-centered UAV RGB imagery (BIRCH-Trees) and a unified VFM-based model (DINOvTree) that jointly predicts height and species, achieving top results with 54-58% fewer parameters on temperate, tropical, and boreal datasets.
AI.motivation: Accurate forest biomass estimation hinges on tree-level traits (height, species). UAV-based RGB imagery offers a scalable, cost-effective data source, but there is a lack of standardized benchmarks and joint height–species models.
AI.method: Introduce BIRCH-Trees benchmark across three forest types. Propose DINOvTree, a Vision Foundation Model backbone with task-specific heads for height regression and species classification. Conduct extensive evaluations against standard vision methods (including VFMs) and allometric equations.
AI.result: DINOvTree achieves top overall performance with high height accuracy and competitive species classification, using only 54–58% of the parameters of the second-best approach.
AI.conclusion: A VFM-based approach can effectively jointly estimate tree height and species from UAV RGB imagery; BIRCH-Trees provides a valuable multi-ecosystem benchmark for future methods.

title: From Liar Paradox to Incongruent Sets: A Normal Form for Self-Reference
summary: We introduce incongruent normal form (INF), a structural representation for self-referential semantic sentences. An INF replaces a self-referential sentence with a finite family of non-self-referential sentences that are individually satisfiable but not jointly satisfiable. This transformation isolates the semantic obstruction created by self-reference while preserving classical semantics locally and is accompanied by correctness theorems characterizing when global inconsistency arises from locally compatible commitments. We then study the role of incongruence as a structural source of semantic informativeness. Using a minimal model-theoretic notion of informativeness-understood as the ability of sentences to distinguish among admissible models-we show that semantic completeness precludes informativeness, while incongruence preserves it. Moreover, incongruence is not confined to paradoxical constructions: any consistent incomplete first-order theory admits finite incongruent families arising from incompatible complete extensions. In this sense, incompleteness manifests structurally as locally realizable but globally incompatible semantic commitments, providing a minimal formal basis for semantic knowledge. Finally, we introduce a quantitative semantic framework. In a canonical finite semantic-state setting, we model semantic commitments as Boolean functions and define a Fourier-analytic notion of semantic energy based on total influence. We derive uncertainty-style bounds relating semantic determinacy, informativeness, and spectral simplicity, and establish a matrix inequality bounding aggregate semantic variance by total semantic energy. These results show quantitatively that semantic informativeness cannot collapse into a single determinate state without unbounded energy cost, identifying incongruence as a fundamental structural and quantitative feature of semantic representation.

AI.tldr: Introduces incongruent normal form (INF) to isolate self-reference via a finite family of non-self-referential sentences that are individually satisfiable but not jointly satisfiable; argues that incongruence is a structural source of semantic informativeness; provides a quantitative framework using Boolean functions and Fourier analysis to relate determinacy, informativeness, and energy.
AI.motivation: Address the semantic obstruction caused by self-reference and self-referential sentences. Provide a structural, local-to-global analysis of semantic commitments and a quantitative notion of informativeness to ground semantic knowledge.
AI.method: Define INF as a finite incongruent decomposition of self-referential sentences; prove correctness theorems linking local satisfiability to global inconsistency; analyze incongruence as a source of informativeness via a minimal model-theoretic notion; show that every consistent incomplete first-order theory yields finite incongruent families; introduce a finite semantic-state setting where commitments are Boolean functions; apply Fourier-analysis to define semantic energy (total influence) and derive bounds on determinacy, informativeness, and spectral simplicity; establish a matrix inequality bounding semantic variance by total energy.
AI.result: INF provides a local representation that isolates self-reference; establishes theoretical connection between local compatibility and global inconsistency; demonstrates that incongruence enhances semantic informativeness; proves an existence result for finite incongruent families in incomplete theories; introduces a quantitative semantic energy framework with uncertainty-type bounds and matrix inequality.
AI.conclusion: Incongruence is a fundamental structural and quantitative feature of semantic representation: semantic informativeness cannot be achieved in a single determinate state without incurred energy cost; the INF framework and the Fourier-analytic energy notion offer a minimal, robust basis for semantic knowledge.

title: Accelerated Spline-Based Time-Optimal Motion Planning with Continuous Safety Guarantees for Non-Differentially Flat Systems
comment: Submitted to the 2026 10th IEEE Conference on Control Technology and Applications (CCTA)
summary: Generating time-optimal, collision-free trajectories for autonomous mobile robots involves a fundamental trade-off between guaranteeing safety and managing computational complexity. State-of-the-art approaches formulate spline-based motion planning as a single Optimal Control Problem (OCP) but often suffer from high computational cost because they include separating hyperplane parameters as decision variables to enforce continuous collision avoidance. This paper presents a novel method that alleviates this bottleneck by decoupling the determination of separating hyperplanes from the OCP. By treating the separation theorem as an independent classification problem solvable via a linear system or quadratic program, the proposed method eliminates hyperplane parameters from the optimisation variables, effectively transforming non-convex constraints into linear ones. Experimental validation demonstrates that this decoupled approach reduces trajectory computation times up to almost 60% compared to fully coupled methods in obstacle-rich environments, while maintaining rigorous continuous safety guarantees.

AI.tldr: Decouples separating hyperplanes from the OCP in spline-based motion planning, turning collision-avoidance constraints into a separate classification problem, yielding up to ~60% faster trajectory computation while preserving continuous safety.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational burden of time-optimal, collision-free trajectory planning by removing hyperplane parameter optimization from the main OCP, addressing non-convex collision-avoidance constraints.
AI.method: Treat separation theorem as independent classifier; compute separating hyperplanes via linear system or QP rather than optimizing them within the OCP; thus non-convex constraints become linear and the OCP has fewer decision variables.
AI.result: Empirical validation shows computing times reduced by up to almost 60% in obstacle-rich environments; maintains continuous safety guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling hyperplane determination from the OCP is an effective way to speed up spline-based trajectory planning without sacrificing safety, enabling faster real-time planning in cluttered spaces.

title: CDMT-EHR: A Continuous-Time Diffusion Framework for Generating Mixed-Type Time-Series Electronic Health Records
summary: Electronic health records (EHRs) are invaluable for clinical research, yet privacy concerns severely restrict data sharing. Synthetic data generation offers a promising solution, but EHRs present unique challenges: they contain both numerical and categorical features that evolve over time. While diffusion models have demonstrated strong performance in EHR synthesis, existing approaches predominantly rely on discrete-time formulations, which suffer from finite-step approximation errors and coupled training-sampling step counts. We propose a continuous-time diffusion framework for generating mixed-type time-series EHRs with three contributions: (1) continuous-time diffusion with a bidirectional gated recurrent unit backbone for capturing temporal dependencies, (2) unified Gaussian diffusion via learnable continuous embeddings for categorical variables, enabling joint cross-feature modeling, and (3) a factorized learnable noise schedule that adapts to per-feature-per-timestep learning difficulties. Experiments on two large-scale intensive care unit datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches in downstream task performance, distribution fidelity, and discriminability, while requiring only 50 sampling steps compared to 1,000 for baseline methods. Classifier-free guidance further enables effective conditional generation for class-imbalanced clinical scenarios.

AI.tldr: A continuous-time diffusion model for mixed-type time-series EHR data using a GRU backbone, unified Gaussian diffusion for categoricals, and a per-feature noise schedule, achieving competitive performance with significantly fewer sampling steps and enabling conditional generation.
AI.motivation: Generate privacy-preserving synthetic EHR data that preserves temporal and cross-feature dependencies across numerical and categorical features, addressing limitations of discrete-time diffusion and data-sharing restrictions.
AI.method: A continuous-time diffusion framework with a bidirectional GRU backbone to capture temporal dynamics; a unified Gaussian diffusion process with learnable continuous embeddings for categorical variables enabling joint cross-feature modeling; a factorized, learnable noise schedule that adapts per feature and per timestep; classifier-free guidance for conditional generation in imbalanced clinical settings.
AI.result: Outperforms existing diffusion-based EHR synthesis methods on two large ICU datasets in downstream task performance, distribution fidelity, and discriminability, while requiring only 50 sampling steps (vs. 1,000 for baselines).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that continuous-time diffusion with mixed-type handling and adaptive noise scheduling can efficiently generate high-quality synthetic EHR data and support conditional generation for clinically relevant scenarios.

title: Prototype Fusion: A Training-Free Multi-Layer Approach to OOD Detection
summary: Deep learning models are increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications, where reliable out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is essential to ensure robustness. Existing methods predominantly rely on the penultimate-layer activations of neural networks, assuming they encapsulate the most informative in-distribution (ID) representations. In this work, we revisit this assumption to show that intermediate layers encode equally rich and discriminative information for OOD detection. Based on this observation, we propose a simple yet effective model-agnostic approach that leverages internal representations across multiple layers. Our scheme aggregates features from successive convolutional blocks, computes class-wise mean embeddings, and applies L_2 normalization to form compact ID prototypes capturing class semantics. During inference, cosine similarity between test features and these prototypes serves as an OOD score--ID samples exhibit strong affinity to at least one prototype, whereas OOD samples remain uniformly distant. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art OOD benchmarks across diverse architectures demonstrate that our approach delivers robust, architecture-agnostic performance and strong generalization for image classification. Notably, it improves AUROC by up to 4.41% and reduces FPR by 13.58%, highlighting multi-layer feature aggregation as a powerful yet underexplored signal for OOD detection, challenging the dominance of penultimate-layer-based methods. Our code is available at: https://github.com/sgchr273/cosine-layers.git.

AI.tldr: A simple, architecture-agnostic OOD detector that aggregates multi-layer features into class prototypes and uses cosine similarity for decision, improving AUROC and FPR across models.
AI.motivation: Challenging the reliance on penultimate-layer representations for OOD detection by showing intermediate layers hold equally rich, discriminative information; seek a simple, model-agnostic approach leveraging multiple layers.
AI.method: Collect and aggregate features from successive convolutional blocks across multiple layers; form class-wise mean embeddings (prototypes) with L2 normalization; at test time, compute cosine similarity between test features and prototypes as the OOD score, assigning ID if similar to any prototype and OOD otherwise.
AI.result: Empirical results on standard OOD benchmarks across diverse architectures show robust, architecture-agnostic performance, with AUROC improvements up to 4.41% and FPR reductions up to 13.58%; code available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: Multi-layer feature aggregation provides a strong, underexplored signal for OOD detection, challenging the dominance of penultimate-layer methods and enabling robust, architecture-agnostic performance.

title: Completeness of Unbounded Best-First Minimax and Descent Minimax
summary:
In this article, we focus on search algorithms for two-player perfect information games, whose objective is to determine the best possible strategy, and ideally a winning strategy.
  Unfortunately, some search algorithms for games in the literature are not able to always determine a winning strategy, even with an infinite search time. This is the case, for example, of the following algorithms: Unbounded Best-First Minimax and Descent Minimax, which are core algorithms in state-of-the-art knowledge-free reinforcement learning.
  They were then improved with the so-called completion technique. However, whether this technique sufficiently improves these algorithms to allow them to always determine a winning strategy remained an open question until now.
  To answer this question, we generalize the two algorithms (their versions using the completion technique), and we show that any algorithm of this class of algorithms computes the best strategy.
  Finally, we experimentally show that the completion technique improves winning performance.

AI.tldr: Generalizes Unbounded Best-First Minimax and Descent Minimax with the completion technique, proving that any algorithm in this class computes the optimal (best) strategy; empirically, completion improves winning performance.
AI.motivation: Address whether the completion technique suffices to guarantee finding a winning strategy in two-player perfect-information games under infinite search, filling a theoretical gap.
AI.method: Extend the two baseline algorithms with the completion technique; prove a universal optimality result for this class of algorithms; conduct experiments to assess practical impact on winning performance.
AI.result: Theoretical result: any algorithm in the considered class computes the best strategy. Empirical result: applying the completion technique improves winning performance.
AI.conclusion: The completion technique is sufficient within the studied algorithm class to guarantee optimal strategy and enhances practical performance; motivates broader empirical validation and potential adoption in knowledge-free reinforcement learning for perfect-information games.

title: Goal-Oriented Reactive Simulation for Closed-Loop Trajectory Prediction
summary: Current trajectory prediction models are primarily trained in an open-loop manner, which often leads to covariate shift and compounding errors when deployed in real-world, closed-loop settings. Furthermore, relying on static datasets or non-reactive log-replay simulators severs the interactive loop, preventing the ego agent from learning to actively negotiate surrounding traffic. In this work, we propose an on-policy closed-loop training paradigm optimized for high-frequency, receding horizon ego prediction. To ground the ego prediction in a realistic representation of traffic interactions and to achieve reactive consistency, we introduce a goal-oriented, transformer-based scene decoder, resulting in an inherently reactive training simulation. By exposing the ego agent to a mixture of open-loop data and simulated, self-induced states, the model learns recovery behaviors to correct its own execution errors. Extensive evaluation demonstrates that closed-loop training significantly enhances collision avoidance capabilities at high replanning frequencies, yielding relative collision rate reductions of up to 27.0% on nuScenes and 79.5% in dense DeepScenario intersections compared to open-loop baselines. Additionally, we show that a hybrid simulation combining reactive with non-reactive surrounding agents achieves optimal balance between immediate interactivity and long-term behavioral stability.

AI.tldr: On-policy, closed-loop training for ego trajectory prediction using a goal-oriented transformer scene decoder; leverages mixed open-loop data and self-induced states to learn recovery; achieves substantial collision-rate reductions at high replanning frequencies; proposes a hybrid reactive/non-reactive simulation for stability.
AI.motivation: Open-loop training causes covariate shift and compounding errors in real-world closed-loop deployment; static datasets and non-reactive simulators break the interactive loop; need reactive, high-frequency ego prediction with recovery capabilities.
AI.method: Propose an on-policy closed-loop training paradigm for high-frequency, receding-horizon ego prediction; introduce a goal-oriented transformer-based scene decoder for realistic traffic interactions and reactive consistency; expose ego to mixture of open-loop data and self-induced states; evaluate against baselines; propose a hybrid simulation combining reactive and non-reactive agents.
AI.result: Collision avoidance improves; relative collision rate reductions up to 27.0% on nuScenes and 79.5% in dense DeepScenario intersections vs open-loop baselines; shows reactive training enhances performance; hybrid simulation balances interactivity and stability.
AI.conclusion: Closed-loop, on-policy training with a reactive transformer scene decoder yields better collision avoidance at high replanning frequencies; a hybrid reactive/non-reactive simulation yields optimal balance between immediate interactivity and long-term stability.

title: BXRL: Behavior-Explainable Reinforcement Learning
summary:
A major challenge of Reinforcement Learning is that agents often learn undesired behaviors that seem to defy the reward structure they were given. Explainable Reinforcement Learning (XRL) methods can answer queries such as "explain this specific action", "explain this specific trajectory", and "explain the entire policy". However, XRL lacks a formal definition for behavior as a pattern of actions across many episodes. We provide such a definition, and use it to enable a new query: "Explain this behavior".
  We present Behavior-Explainable Reinforcement Learning (BXRL), a new problem formulation that treats behaviors as first-class objects. BXRL defines a behavior measure as any function $m : Π\to \mathbb{R}$, allowing users to precisely express the pattern of actions that they find interesting and measure how strongly the policy exhibits it. We define contrastive behaviors that reduce the question "why does the agent prefer $a$ to $a'$?" to "why is $m(π)$ high?" which can be explored with differentiation. We do not implement an explainability method; we instead analyze three existing methods and propose how they could be adapted to explain behavior. We present a port of the HighwayEnv driving environment to JAX, which provides an interface for defining, measuring, and differentiating behaviors with respect to the model parameters.

AI.tldr: BXRL defines behavior as a first-class object and introduces a formal behavior measure m: Π -> R so users can specify patterns of actions across episodes and quantify their presence in a policy; it introduces contrastive behaviors to compare actions, analyzes existing XRL methods for adaptation, and ports HighwayEnv to JAX to support differentiable behavior measurement.
AI.motivation: XRL currently lacks a formal, general notion of behavior as a pattern across episodes, hindering queries like 'Explain this behavior'. By treating behaviors as first-class objects and defining a differentiable behavior measure, BXRL enables precise, contrastive explanations of policy behavior and facilitates differentiation with respect to model parameters.
AI.method: Define a general behavior measure m: Π -> R to quantify patterns of actions across trajectories. Introduce contrastive behaviors to transform 'why preferred a to a'' into 'why is m(π) high?'. Provide a framework for adapting existing XRL methods to explain behavior. Port HighwayEnv to JAX to offer an interface for defining, measuring, and differentiating behaviors with respect to model parameters.
AI.result: Proposes a new problem formulation (BXRL) and a concrete framework for representing and measuring behaviors. Demonstrates the approach by porting a driving environment (HighwayEnv) to JAX, enabling defining, measuring, and differentiating behaviors with respect to policy parameters. No fully implemented explainability method is provided within this work; it focuses on conceptual formulation and a practical platform.
AI.conclusion: BXRL provides a principled way to treat behavior as a first-class object, enabling 'Explain this behavior' queries via a formal behavior measure and contrastive analysis. It outlines how existing XRL methods could be adapted and sets up a practical, differentiable platform (via the HighwayEnv port) to study and evaluate behavior explanations.

title: MoCHA: Denoising Caption Supervision for Motion-Text Retrieval
summary: Text-motion retrieval systems learn shared embedding spaces from motion-caption pairs via contrastive objectives. However, each caption is not a deterministic label but a sample from a distribution of valid descriptions: different annotators produce different text for the same motion, mixing motion-recoverable semantics (action type, body parts, directionality) with annotator-specific style and inferred context that cannot be determined from 3D joint coordinates alone. Standard contrastive training treats each caption as the single positive target, overlooking this distributional structure and inducing within-motion embedding variance that weakens alignment. We propose MoCHA, a text canonicalization framework that reduces this variance by projecting each caption onto its motion-recoverable content prior to encoding, producing tighter positive clusters and better-separated embeddings. Canonicalization is a general principle: even deterministic rule-based methods improve cross-dataset transfer, though learned canonicalizers provide substantially larger gains. We present two learned variants: an LLM-based approach (GPT-5.2) and a distilled FlanT5 model requiring no LLM at inference time. MoCHA operates as a preprocessing step compatible with any retrieval architecture. Applied to MoPa (MotionPatches), MoCHA sets a new state of the art on both HumanML3D (H) and KIT-ML (K): the LLM variant achieves 13.9% T2M R@1 on H (+3.1pp) and 24.3% on K (+10.3pp), while the LLM-free T5 variant achieves gains of +2.5pp and +8.1pp. Canonicalization reduces within-motion text-embedding variance by 11-19% and improves cross-dataset transfer substantially, with H to K improving by 94% and K to H by 52%, demonstrating that standardizing the language space yields more transferable motion-language representations.

AI.tldr: MoCHA: a text canonicalization preprocessing framework for text-motion retrieval that reduces caption-induced variance by projecting captions to motion-recoverable content, improving cross-dataset transfer and achieving state-of-the-art results on MoPa-based benchmarks; available in two variants: a large LLM (GPT-5.2) and a distilled FlanT5 model.
AI.motivation: Caption variance arising from annotator style and inferred context biases contrastive learning in text-motion retrieval by treating multiple captions for the same motion as independent positives; need to isolate motion-recoverable semantics.
AI.method: MoCHA canonicalizes captions by projecting them onto motion-recoverable content before encoding. Implementations include a large LLM-based approach (GPT-5.2) and a distilled FlanT5 model; can be applied as a preprocessing step with any retrieval architecture.
AI.result: On MoPa (MotionPatches), MoCHA achieves SOTA on HumanML3D and KIT-ML: GPT-5.2 variant yields T2M R@1 of 13.9% on HumanML3D (+3.1pp) and 24.3% on KIT-ML (+10.3pp); FlanT5 variant yields gains of +2.5pp (H) and +8.1pp (K). Canonicalization reduces within-motion text-embedding variance by 11-19% and substantially improves cross-dataset transfer (H→K +94%, K→H +52%).
AI.conclusion: Canonicalization improves alignment and transfer by standardizing language space; deterministic canonicalizers help, but learned canonicalizers provide larger gains. MoCHA is a general preprocessing step compatible with any retrieval model.

title: The Stochastic Gap: A Markovian Framework for Pre-Deployment Reliability and Oversight-Cost Auditing in Agentic Artificial Intelligence
comment: 22 pages, 5 figures, submitted to Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence
summary:
Agentic artificial intelligence (AI) in organizations is a sequential decision problem constrained by reliability and oversight cost. When deterministic workflows are replaced by stochastic policies over actions and tool calls, the key question is not whether a next step appears plausible, but whether the resulting trajectory remains statistically supported, locally unambiguous, and economically governable. We develop a measure-theoretic Markov framework for this setting. The core quantities are state blind-spot mass B_n(tau), state-action blind mass B^SA_{pi,n}(tau), an entropy-based human-in-the-loop escalation gate, and an expected oversight-cost identity over the workflow visitation measure.
  We instantiate the framework on the Business Process Intelligence Challenge 2019 purchase-to-pay log (251,734 cases, 1,595,923 events, 42 distinct workflow actions) and construct a log-driven simulated agent from a chronological 80/20 split of the same process. The main empirical finding is that a large workflow can appear well supported at the state level while retaining substantial blind mass over next-step decisions: refining the operational state to include case context, economic magnitude, and actor class expands the state space from 42 to 668 and raises state-action blind mass from 0.0165 at tau=50 to 0.1253 at tau=1000. On the held-out split, m(s) = max_a pi-hat(a|s) tracks realized autonomous step accuracy within 3.4 percentage points on average.
  The same quantities that delimit statistically credible autonomy also determine expected oversight burden. The framework is demonstrated on a large-scale enterprise procurement workflow and is designed for direct application to engineering processes for which operational event logs are available.

AI.tldr: Proposes a measure-theoretic Markov framework to analyze agentic AI in organizational workflows, introducing state blind-spot mass and state-action blind mass, plus an entropy-based escalation gate and an oversight-cost identity. Demonstrates on a large purchase-to-pay log, showing that enriching state context increases blind mass and that held-out autonomy remains accurate, with manageable oversight burden.
AI.motivation: Address reliability and oversight costs in stochastic, agentic AI workflows; quantify when autonomous trajectories are statistically credible, locally unambiguous, and economically governable using a log-driven, measure-theoretic approach.
AI.method: Develop a measure-theoretic Markov framework with core quantities: state blind-spot mass B_n(tau), state-action blind mass B^SA_{pi,n}(tau), an entropy-based human-in-the-loop escalation gate, and an expected oversight-cost identity over the workflow visitation measure. Instantiate on the BPM Challenge 2019 purchase-to-pay log (251,734 cases, 1,595,923 events, 42 actions). Build a log-driven simulated agent from an 80/20 chronological split. Evaluate state-level vs richer state representations and held-out performance.
AI.result: Finds that large workflows can appear well supported at the state level while retaining substantial blind mass for next-step decisions. Expanding the operational state from 42 to 668 actions/contexts increases state-action blind mass from 0.0165 (tau=50) to 0.1253 (tau=1000). On held-out data, m(s) = max_a π_hat(a|s) tracks autonomous step accuracy within 3.4 percentage points. The same quantities governing autonomy also determine expected oversight burden. Demonstrated on a large-scale procurement workflow; framework ready for engineering processes with event logs.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a practical, log-driven approach to quantify when autonomous workflow decisions are statistically credible and economically governable, balancing state-space granularity, uncertainty (blind mass), and oversight costs; applicable to enterprise engineering processes and potentially generalizable to similar sequential decision settings.

title: Environment-Grounded Multi-Agent Workflow for Autonomous Penetration Testing
summary: The increasing complexity and interconnectivity of digital infrastructures make scalable and reliable security assessment methods essential. Robotic systems represent a particularly important class of operational technology, as modern robots are highly networked cyber-physical systems deployed in domains such as industrial automation, logistics, and autonomous services. This paper explores the use of large language models for automated penetration testing in robotic environments. We propose an environment-grounded multi-agent architecture tailored to Robotics-based systems. The approach dynamically constructs a shared graph-based memory during execution that captures the observable system state, including network topology, communication channels, vulnerabilities, and attempted exploits. This enables structured automation while maintaining traceability and effective context management throughout the testing process. Evaluated across multiple iterations within a specialized robotics Capture-the-Flag scenario (ROS/ROS2), the system demonstrated high reliability, successfully completing the challenge in 100\% of test runs (n=5). This performance significantly exceeds literature benchmarks while maintaining the traceability and human oversight required by frameworks like the EU AI Act.

AI.tldr: Environment-grounded, multi-agent LLM system for automated pentesting in robotic OT; uses a dynamic graph memory to track state; achieves 100% success in ROS/ROS2 CTF (n=5), with traceability and human oversight.
AI.motivation: As digital infrastructures grow in complexity and interconnectivity, scalable, reliable security assessment becomes essential. Robotic systems are critical, networked cyber-physical systems requiring automated penetration testing with traceability and regulatory alignment.
AI.method: An environment-grounded multi-agent architecture using large language models to conduct automated penetration testing in robotics. The system dynamically constructs a shared graph-based memory during execution that records observable state (network topology, channels, vulnerabilities, exploits) to enable structured automation, traceability, and effective context management. Evaluation performed in a ROS/ROS2-based Capture-the-Flag scenario.
AI.result: The approach achieved 100% success across 5 test runs (n=5), significantly surpassing literature benchmarks while preserving traceability and human oversight mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable, reliable automated pentesting framework for robotics that maintains traceability and aligns with regulatory frameworks (e.g., EU AI Act).

title: Kronecker-Structured Nonparametric Spatiotemporal Point Processes
summary: Events in spatiotemporal domains arise in numerous real-world applications, where uncovering event relationships and enabling accurate prediction are central challenges. Classical Poisson and Hawkes processes rely on restrictive parametric assumptions that limit their ability to capture complex interaction patterns, while recent neural point process models increase representational capacity but integrate event information in a black-box manner, hindering interpretable relationship discovery. To address these limitations, we propose a Kronecker-Structured Nonparametric Spatiotemporal Point Process (KSTPP) that enables transparent event-wise relationship discovery while retaining high modeling flexibility. We model the background intensity with a spatial Gaussian process (GP) and the influence kernel as a spatiotemporal GP, allowing rich interaction patterns including excitation, inhibition, neutrality, and time-varying effects. To enable scalable training and prediction, we adopt separable product kernels and represent the GPs on structured grids, inducing Kronecker-structured covariance matrices. Exploiting Kronecker algebra substantially reduces computational cost and allows the model to scale to large event collections. In addition, we develop a tensor-product Gauss-Legendre quadrature scheme to efficiently evaluate intractable likelihood integrals. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework.

AI.tldr: A scalable, transparent spatiotemporal point process using Kronecker-structured nonparametric GP priors for background intensity and interaction kernel, with separable kernels and grid-based Kronecker covariances; uses tensor-product Gauss–Legendre quadrature for tractable likelihoods; demonstrates strong empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Limitations of classical Poisson/Hawkes processes (restrictive parametrics) and current neural point processes (black-box, poor interpretability) motivate a model that preserves flexibility while enabling interpretable, event-wise relationship discovery.
AI.method: Model background intensity with a spatial GP; model influence kernel as spatiotemporal GP; adopt separable product kernels; represent GPs on structured grids to induce Kronecker covariance; use Kronecker algebra for scalable computation; use tensor-product Gauss-Legendre quadrature to evaluate intractable likelihood integrals.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the framework's effectiveness in capturing diverse interaction patterns (excitation, inhibition, neutrality, time-varying effects) and scalable performance.
AI.conclusion: KSTPP provides transparent event-wise relationship discovery with high modeling flexibility, aided by Kronecker-structured computations and quadrature to enable scalability to large event sets.

title: AdvSplat: Adversarial Attacks on Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting Models
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) is increasingly recognized as a powerful paradigm for real-time, high-fidelity 3D reconstruction. However, its per-scene optimization pipeline limits scalability and generalization, and prevents efficient inference. Recently emerged feed-forward 3DGS models address these limitations by enabling fast reconstruction from a few input views after large-scale pretraining, without scene-specific optimization. Despite their advantages and strong potential for commercial deployment, the use of neural networks as the backbone also amplifies the risk of adversarial manipulation. In this paper, we introduce AdvSplat, the first systematic study of adversarial attacks on feed-forward 3DGS. We first employ white-box attacks to reveal fundamental vulnerabilities of this model family. We then develop two improved, practically relevant, query-efficient black-box algorithms that optimize pixel-space perturbations via a frequency-domain parameterization: one based on gradient estimation and the other gradient-free, without requiring any access to model internals. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that AdvSplat can significantly disrupt reconstruction results by injecting imperceptible perturbations into the input images. Our findings surface an overlooked yet urgent problem in this domain, and we hope to draw the community's attention to this emerging security and robustness challenge.

AI.tldr: AdvSplat reveals vulnerabilities of feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting models to adversarial perturbations; introduces two black-box, frequency-domain attacks that are query-efficient and can disrupt reconstructions with imperceptible changes.
AI.motivation: Security/robustness risk in fast, pre-trained 3DGS pipelines; lack of adversarial studies in this domain; need for evaluation and defense.
AI.method: White-box attacks; two black-box perturbation methods in frequency domain: (1) gradient-estimation-based, (2) gradient-free; both optimize pixel-space perturbations with limited queries without model internals; evaluate across multiple datasets.
AI.result: AdvSplat can significantly degrade reconstruction quality with imperceptible perturbations; demonstrates practicality of attacks; reveals robustness issues.
AI.conclusion: Calls attention to security/robustness challenges in 3DGS feed-forward models; motivates development of defenses and robust training, better attack modeling, and standard benchmarks.

title: Inspection and Control of Self-Generated-Text Recognition Ability in Llama3-8b-Instruct
comment: 10 pages, 13 figs, 2 tables, accepted as conference paper to ICLR 2025
summary: It has been reported that LLMs can recognize their own writing. As this has potential implications for AI safety, yet is relatively understudied, we investigate the phenomenon, seeking to establish whether it robustly occurs at the behavioral level, how the observed behavior is achieved, and whether it can be controlled. First, we find that the Llama3-8b-Instruct chat model - but not the base Llama3-8b model - can reliably distinguish its own outputs from those of humans, and present evidence that the chat model is likely using its experience with its own outputs, acquired during post-training, to succeed at the writing recognition task. Second, we identify a vector in the residual stream of the model that is differentially activated when the model makes a correct self-written-text recognition judgment, show that the vector activates in response to information relevant to self-authorship, present evidence that the vector is related to the concept of "self" in the model, and demonstrate that the vector is causally related to the model's ability to perceive and assert self-authorship. Finally, we show that the vector can be used to control both the model's behavior and its perception, steering the model to claim or disclaim authorship by applying the vector to the model's output as it generates it, and steering the model to believe or disbelieve it wrote arbitrary texts by applying the vector to them as the model reads them.

AI.tldr: The Llama3-8b-Instruct chat model can reliably distinguish its own outputs from humans; a residual-vector encodes self-authorship, enabling causal control over both perception and claims of authorship.
AI.motivation: To assess whether LLMs robustly recognize their own writing, to understand the mechanisms in the model's internal representations that enable this, and to evaluate the feasibility and safety implications of controlling such self-authorship judgments.
AI.method: Empirical comparison between the Llama3-8b-Instruct chat model and the base Llama3-8b model on a self-writing recognition task; residual-stream analysis to identify a vector differentially activated during correct self-written-text judgments; evaluation of the vector's relation to self-authorship concepts; causal manipulation by applying the vector to outputs during generation and to reading streams to steer authorship beliefs.
AI.result: The chat model reliably detects when it wrote text, unlike the base model. A residual-stream vector is selectively activated for correct self-authorship judgments and aligns with self-related information; this vector appears to encode a notion of 'self' and causally affects both perception and assertion of authorship. Applying the vector to the model’s outputs or reads can steer the model to claim or disclaim authorship and to believe or disbelieve texts.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates an internal, manipulable self-authorship signal within an LLM, with clear safety and governance implications for how models might be steered to claim or deny authorship, and for understanding how internally represented self-concepts can be leveraged or misused.

title: Decentralized End-to-End Multi-AAV Pursuit Using Predictive Spatio-Temporal Observation via Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Decentralized cooperative pursuit in cluttered environments is challenging for autonomous aerial swarms, especially under partial and noisy perception. Existing methods often rely on abstracted geometric features or privileged ground-truth states, and therefore sidestep perceptual uncertainty in real-world settings. We propose a decentralized end-to-end multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework that maps raw LiDAR observations directly to continuous control commands. Central to the framework is the Predictive Spatio-Temporal Observation (PSTO), an egocentric grid representation that aligns obstacle geometry with predictive adversarial intent and teammate motion in a unified, fixed-resolution projection. Built on PSTO, a single decentralized policy enables agents to navigate static obstacles, intercept dynamic targets, and maintain cooperative encirclement. Simulations demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior capture efficiency and competitive success rates compared to state-of-the-art learning-based approaches relying on privileged obstacle information. Furthermore, the unified policy scales seamlessly across different team sizes without retraining. Finally, fully autonomous outdoor experiments validate the framework on a quadrotor swarm relying on only onboard sensing and computing.

AI.tldr: A decentralized end-to-end MARL framework using Predictive Spatio-Temporal Observation (PSTO) with LiDAR, mapping observations to control for cooperative pursuit in cluttered environments. It achieves efficient capture, scales to different team sizes without retraining, and is validated on outdoor quadrotor experiments using onboard sensing.
AI.motivation: Perception uncertainty in cluttered, partially observable environments makes reliance on privileged ground-truth or abstract geometric features problematic. Need a decentralized policy that leverages raw sensing and remains robust to noise.
AI.method: Introduce PSTO, an egocentric fixed-resolution grid that aligns obstacle geometry with predictive adversarial intent and teammate motion. Train a single decentralized MARL policy that maps raw LiDAR observations to continuous controls. The approach enables static obstacle avoidance, dynamic target interception, and cooperative encirclement without centralized coordination.
AI.result: In simulations, the PSTO-based method achieves superior capture efficiency and competitive success rates compared to state-of-the-art methods that assume privileged obstacle information. The policy scales to varying team sizes without retraining. Outdoor quadrotor experiments validate autonomous operation with onboard sensing and computing.
AI.conclusion: PSTO enables robust, scalable decentralized MARL for aerial swarms in cluttered environments, delivering real-world viability and reducing dependence on privileged perception data.

title: Self Paced Gaussian Contextual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Curriculum learning improves reinforcement learning (RL) efficiency by sequencing tasks from simple to complex. However, many self-paced curriculum methods rely on computationally expensive inner-loop optimizations, limiting their scalability in high-dimensional context spaces. In this paper, we propose Self-Paced Gaussian Curriculum Learning (SPGL), a novel approach that avoids costly numerical procedures by leveraging a closed-form update rule for Gaussian context distributions. SPGL maintains the sample efficiency and adaptability of traditional self-paced methods while substantially reducing computational overhead. We provide theoretical guarantees on convergence and validate our method across several contextual RL benchmarks, including the Point Mass, Lunar Lander, and Ball Catching environments. Experimental results show that SPGL matches or outperforms existing curriculum methods, especially in hidden context scenarios, and achieves more stable context distribution convergence. Our method offers a scalable, principled alternative for curriculum generation in challenging continuous and partially observable domains.

AI.tldr: A method SPGL for curriculum learning in RL uses a closed-form Gaussian context update to avoid inner-loop optimization, achieving scalable, sample-efficient, convergent self-paced curriculum with competitive performance on contextual RL benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational burden of self-paced curriculum methods in high-dimensional context spaces while preserving efficiency and adaptability.
AI.method: Introduce Self-Paced Gaussian Curriculum Learning (SPGL) with closed-form update for Gaussian context distributions; bypass inner-loop optimization; maintain self-paced learning.
AI.result: Theoretical convergence guarantees; empirical validation on Point Mass, Lunar Lander, Ball Catching; SPGL matches or exceeds existing methods, particularly in hidden-context settings, with stable context distribution convergence.
AI.conclusion: SPGL provides scalable, principled curriculum generation for challenging continuous and partially observable RL domains.

title: CoRe: Joint Optimization with Contrastive Learning for Medical Image Registration
summary: Medical image registration is a fundamental task in medical image analysis, enabling the alignment of images from different modalities or time points. However, intensity inconsistencies and nonlinear tissue deformations pose significant challenges to the robustness of registration methods. Recent approaches leveraging self-supervised representation learning show promise by pre-training feature extractors to generate robust anatomical embeddings, that farther used for the registration. In this work, we propose a novel framework that integrates equivariant contrastive learning directly into the registration model. Our approach leverages the power of contrastive learning to learn robust feature representations that are invariant to tissue deformations. By jointly optimizing the contrastive and registration objectives, we ensure that the learned representations are not only informative but also suitable for the registration task. We evaluate our method on abdominal and thoracic image registration tasks, including both intra-patient and inter-patient scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that the integration of contrastive learning directly into the registration framework significantly improves performance, surpassing strong baseline methods.

AI.tldr: Integrating equivariant contrastive learning into a registration model improves robustness and accuracy for cross-modality and longitudinal abdominal/thoracic registrations, outperforming strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Intensity inconsistencies and nonlinear tissue deformations challenge registration; self-supervised robust representations via equivariant/contrastive learning can enhance alignment.
AI.method: End-to-end framework that jointly optimizes registration loss and contrastive loss within an equivariant contrastive learning setup, producing deformation-invariant anatomical embeddings; training uses augmented data to enforce equivariance.
AI.result: On abdominal and thoracic datasets, including intra-patient and inter-patient scenarios, the method significantly surpasses strong baseline registrations.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end integration of equivariant contrastive learning with registration yields more robust and accurate registrations, generalizing across modalities and patient variability.

title: Mitigating Many-Shot Jailbreaking
summary: Many-shot jailbreaking (MSJ) is an adversarial technique that exploits the long context windows of modern LLMs to circumvent model safety training by including in the prompt many examples of a "fake" assistant responding inappropriately before the final request. With enough examples, the model's in-context learning abilities override its safety training, and it responds as if it were the "fake" assistant. In this work, we probe the effectiveness of different fine-tuning and input sanitization approaches on mitigating MSJ attacks, alone and in combination. We find incremental mitigation effectiveness for each, and show that the combined techniques significantly reduce the effectiveness of MSJ attacks, while retaining model performance in benign in-context learning and conversational tasks. We suggest that our approach could meaningfully ameliorate this vulnerability if incorporated into model safety post-training.

AI.tldr: Sequential fine-tuning and input sanitization yield incremental defenses against many-shot jailbreaking in LLMs; combining these defenses significantly curtails MSJ while preserving in-context task performance.
AI.motivation: MSJ exploits in-context learning to bypass safety training; robust, practical defenses are needed to protect LLM safety without harming benign use.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation of multiple defenses: varying fine-tuning strategies and input sanitization techniques, tested alone and in combination, assessing MSJ susceptibility and benign performance.
AI.result: Each defense provides incremental mitigation; their combination markedly reduces MSJ effectiveness and maintains performance on benign tasks.
AI.conclusion: Integrating these defenses into post-training safety pipelines could meaningfully reduce MSJ vulnerability in production models.

title: Toward Generalist Neural Motion Planners for Robotic Manipulators: Challenges and Opportunities
summary: State-of-the-art generalist manipulation policies have enabled the deployment of robotic manipulators in unstructured human environments. However, these frameworks struggle in cluttered environments primarily because they utilize auxiliary modules for low-level motion planning and control. Motion planning remains challenging due to the high dimensionality of the robot's configuration space and the presence of workspace obstacles. Neural motion planners have enhanced motion planning efficiency by offering fast inference and effectively handling the inherent multi-modality of the motion planning problem. Despite such benefits, current neural motion planners often struggle to generalize to unseen, out-of-distribution planning settings. This paper reviews and analyzes the state-of-the-art neural motion planners, highlighting both their benefits and limitations. It also outlines a path toward establishing generalist neural motion planners capable of handling domain-specific challenges. For a list of the reviewed papers, please refer to https://davoodsz.github.io/planning-manip-survey.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Survey of neural motion planners for robotic manipulation; finds benefits in fast inference and handling multi-modality but weak generalization to unseen planning settings; outlines a roadmap toward generalist planners and provides a curated bibliography.
AI.motivation: Robust manipulation in unstructured, cluttered human environments is hindered by high-dimensional planning and obstacles; neural planners promise efficiency but must generalize beyond training distributions to be practical.
AI.method: Qualitative literature review and synthesis of state-of-the-art neural motion planners; categorize benefits/limitations; identify gaps; outline a roadmap for generalist planners; includes a curated list of referenced works.
AI.result: Identifies strengths of neural motion planners (fast inference, multi-modality handling) and a dominant limitation (poor generalization to out-of-distribution tasks); highlights domain-specific challenges; proposes a pathway toward generalist planners and provides a bibliography.
AI.conclusion: Advocates developing generalist neural motion planners capable of handling diverse, domain-specific challenges; emphasizes addressing generalization and robustness; directs readers to an online bibliography for reviewed papers.

title: Lightweight Fairness for LLM-Based Recommendations via Kernelized Projection and Gated Adapters
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have introduced new capabilities to recommender systems, enabling dynamic, context-aware, and conversational recommendations. However, LLM-based recommender systems inherit and may amplify social biases embedded in their pre-training data, especially when demographic cues are present. Existing fairness solutions either require extra parameters fine-tuning, or suffer from optimization instability. We propose a lightweight and scalable bias mitigation method that combines a kernelized Iterative Null-space Projection (INLP) with a gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) adapter. Our approach estimates a closed-form projection that removes single or multiple sensitive attributes from LLM representations with no additional trainable parameters. To preserve task utility, we introduce a two-level MoE adapter that selectively restores useful signals without reintroducing bias. Experiments on two public datasets show that our method reduces attribute leakage across multiple protected variables while maintaining competitive recommendation accuracy.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, parameter-free bias mitigation framework for LLM-based recommender systems that combines a kernelized INLP projection to remove sensitive attributes from representations with a two-level gated MoE adapter to preserve task utility, achieving reduced attribute leakage with competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLMs in recommender systems can propagate and amplify social biases from pre-training data, especially when demographic cues are available. Existing fairness methods often require extra trainable parameters or struggle with optimization stability.
AI.method: Kernelized Iterative Null-space Projection (INLP) yields a closed-form projection that removes one or multiple sensitive attributes from LLM representations without additional trainable parameters. A two-level Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) adapter selectively restores useful signals via gating to maintain performance while avoiding bias reintroduction.
AI.result: Empirical results on two public datasets show reduced attribute leakage across multiple protected variables while maintaining competitive recommendation accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a scalable, parameter-efficient solution for debiasing LLM-based recommender systems, balancing bias mitigation with task utility and offering potential for broader applicability to other LLM-enabled tasks.

title: Mind the Hitch: Dynamic Calibration and Articulated Perception for Autonomous Trucks
summary: Autonomous trucking poses unique challenges due to articulated tractor-trailer geometry, and time-varying sensor poses caused by the fifth-wheel joint and trailer flex. Existing perception and calibration methods assume static baselines or rely on high-parallax and texture-rich scenes, limiting their reliability under real-world settings. We propose dCAP (dynamic Calibration and Articulated Perception), a vision-based framework that continuously estimates the 6-DoF (degree of freedom) relative pose between tractor and trailer cameras. dCAP employs a transformer with cross-view and temporal attention to robustly aggregate spatial cues while maintaining temporal consistency, enabling accurate perception under rapid articulation and occlusion. Integrated with BEVFormer, dCAP improves 3D object detection by replacing static calibration with dynamically predicted extrinsics. To facilitate evaluation, we introduce STT4AT, a CARLA-based benchmark simulating semi-trailer trucks with synchronized multi-sensor suites and time-varying inter-rig geometry across diverse environments. Experiments demonstrate that dCAP achieves stable, accurate perception while addressing the limitations of static calibration in autonomous trucking. The dataset, development kit, and source code will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: Introduce dCAP, a transformer-based dynamic calibration framework that estimates 6-DoF tractor–trailer camera pose in real-time to improve BEV-based perception for articulated trucks. It addresses static-baseline limitations under articulation and occlusion, using cross-view and temporal attention; evaluated on a CARLA-based STT4AT benchmark; dataset/code to be released.
AI.motivation: Static calibration struggles with time-varying baselines due to fifth-wheel articulation and trailer flex, causing degraded perception in real-world autonomous trucking scenarios. A dynamic, vision-based extrinsic estimation promises robustness to articulation and occlusion.
AI.method: A vision-based framework (dCAP) that jointly estimates 6-DoF extrinsics between tractor and trailer cameras using a transformer with cross-view and temporal attention to fuse spatial cues and enforce temporal consistency. Integrated with BEVFormer to replace static extrinsics with dynamic predictions. Evaluation on a CARLA-based STT4AT benchmark with synchronized multi-sensor data and varying inter-rig geometry.
AI.result: Experimental results show stable, accurate perception under articulation and occlusion, outperforming static calibration baselines in BEV-based 3D object detection. The work provides a dataset, development kit, and source code for public release.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic extrinsic estimation via dCAP enables robust perception in autonomous trucking where rigid, static calibration fails due to articulation and time-varying geometry; the approach is validated on a CARLA-based benchmark and will be accompanied by public release.

title: Evidence for Limited Metacognition in LLMs
summary: The possibility of LLM self-awareness and even sentience is gaining increasing public attention and has major safety and policy implications, but the science of measuring them is still in a nascent state. Here we introduce a novel methodology for quantitatively evaluating metacognitive abilities in LLMs. Taking inspiration from research on metacognition in nonhuman animals, our approach eschews model self-reports and instead tests to what degree models can strategically deploy knowledge of internal states. Using two experimental paradigms, we demonstrate that frontier LLMs introduced since early 2024 show increasingly strong evidence of certain metacognitive abilities, specifically the ability to assess and utilize their own confidence in their ability to answer factual and reasoning questions correctly and the ability to anticipate what answers they would give and utilize that information appropriately. We buttress these behavioral findings with an analysis of the token probabilities returned by the models, which suggests the presence of an upstream internal signal that could provide the basis for metacognition. We further find that these abilities 1) are limited in resolution, 2) emerge in context-dependent manners, and 3) seem to be qualitatively different from those of humans. We also report intriguing differences across models of similar capabilities, suggesting that LLM post-training may have a role in developing metacognitive abilities.

AI.tldr: Proposes a quantitative, animal-metacognition–inspired framework to measure metacognitive abilities in LLMs, and reports initial evidence that frontier models (post-early 2024) exhibit confidence assessment and anticipatory use of internal states, with a possible upstream signal in token probabilities; findings indicate limited resolution, context dependence, and non–humancaliber differences, plus model-to-model variation and possible post-training effects.
AI.motivation: Public concern about LLM self-awareness/sentience and the safety/policy implications; a rigorous, scalable method to quantify metacognition in AI is needed beyond self-report prompts.
AI.method: Two experimental paradigms that test strategic use of knowledge about internal states without requiring model self-report; analysis relies on external behavior (confidence judgments, reliance on own anticipated answers) and token-probability signals as proxies for internal state signals; comparisons across frontier LLMs introduced since early 2024; examination of how confidence relates to factual/reasoning tasks and how anticipated answers influence behavior.
AI.result: Evidence of metacognitive abilities in recent LLMs: ability to assess and utilize confidence, and to anticipate their own answers and use that information; an upstream signal in token probabilities suggesting a metacognitive basis; these abilities are limited in resolution, context-dependent, and qualitatively different from human metacognition; notable differences across models of similar capabilities, hinting at post-training effects.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can exhibit measurable metacognitive tendencies that are not necessarily human-like and are influenced by training and context; the work provides a framework and initial empirical support, but broader validation and deeper mechanistic understanding are required.

title: Evidence of an Emergent "Self" in Continual Robot Learning
summary: A key challenge to understanding self-awareness has been a principled way of quantifying whether an intelligent system has a concept of a "self," and if so how to differentiate the "self" from other cognitive structures. We propose that the "self" can be isolated by seeking the invariant portion of cognitive process that changes relatively little compared to more rapidly acquired cognitive knowledge and skills, because our self is the most persistent aspect of our experiences. We used this principle to analyze the cognitive structure of robots under two conditions: One robot learns a constant task, while a second robot is subjected to continual learning under variable tasks. We find that robots subjected to continual learning develop an invariant subnetwork that is significantly more stable (p < 0.001) compared to the control. We suggest that this principle can offer a window into exploring selfhood in other cognitive AI systems.

AI.tldr: Invariant subnetworks as a proxy for 'self' in AI; continual learning yields a more stable invariant subnetwork than constant-task learning, suggesting a mechanism for selfhood.
AI.motivation: Quantify self-awareness by isolating invariant cognitive processes that persist across learning; differentiate 'self' from other cognitive structures.
AI.method: Compare two robot learning regimes (constant task vs continual learning with variable tasks); identify invariant subnetworks and assess stability; statistical test indicates p<0.001.
AI.result: Continual learning yields an invariant subnetwork with significantly higher stability than control; p<0.001; supports the hypothesis that a persistent self-like structure emerges.
AI.conclusion: The invariant-cognition principle offers a window into studying selfhood in AI; applicable to other cognitive systems; motivates further investigation.

title: Probabilistic Geometric Alignment via Bayesian Latent Transport for Domain-Adaptive Foundation Models
comment: 11 pages, 8 Figures, 25 Equations, 5 Tables and 3 Theorems
summary: Adapting large-scale foundation models to new domains with limited supervision remains a fundamental challenge due to latent distribution mismatch, unstable optimization dynamics, and miscalibrated uncertainty propagation. This paper introduces an uncertainty-aware probabilistic latent transport framework that formulates domain adaptation as a stochastic geometric alignment problem in representation space. A Bayesian transport operator is proposed to redistribute latent probability mass along Wasserstein-type geodesic trajectories, while a PAC-Bayesian regularization mechanism constrains posterior model complexity to mitigate catastrophic overfitting. The proposed formulation yields theoretical guarantees on convergence stability, loss landscape smoothness, and sample efficiency under distributional shift. Empirical analyses demonstrate substantial reduction in latent manifold discrepancy, accelerated transport energy decay, and improved covariance calibration compared with deterministic fine-tuning and adversarial domain adaptation baselines.
