Furthermore, bounded posterior uncertainty evolution indicates enhanced probabilistic reliability during cross-domain transfer. By establishing a principled connection between stochastic optimal transport geometry and statistical generalization theory, the proposed framework provides new insights into robust adaptation of modern foundation architectures operating in heterogeneous environments. These findings suggest that uncertainty-aware probabilistic alignment constitutes a promising paradigm for reliable transfer learning in next-generation deep representation systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes an uncertainty-aware probabilistic latent transport approach for domain adaptation, combining Bayesian transport on Wasserstein geodesics with PAC-Bayesian regularization to improve stability, sample efficiency, and uncertainty calibration during cross-domain transfer.
AI.motivation: Address latent distribution mismatch, unstable optimization, and miscalibrated uncertainty in adapting large-scale foundation models to new domains with limited supervision.
AI.method: Formulates domain adaptation as stochastic geometric alignment in representation space. Introduces a Bayesian transport operator that redistributes latent probability mass along Wasserstein-type geodesics and applies PAC-Bayesian regularization to bound posterior complexity, aiming for convergence stability, smooth loss landscapes, and better sample efficiency.
AI.result: The framework yields theoretical guarantees on convergence stability, loss landscape smoothness, and sample efficiency under distributional shift; empirically reduces latent manifold discrepancy, accelerates transport energy decay, and improves covariance calibration compared with deterministic fine-tuning and adversarial domain adaptation baselines; posterior uncertainty evolution is bounded, indicating reliable cross-domain transfer.
AI.conclusion: Bridges stochastic optimal transport with statistical generalization theory to enable robust transfer learning for modern foundation models in heterogeneous environments. Uncertainty-aware probabilistic alignment is presented as a promising paradigm for reliable adaptation across domains.

title: Bi-CRCL: Bidirectional Conservative-Radical Complementary Learning with Pre-trained Foundation Models for Class-incremental Medical Image Analysis
summary: Class-incremental learning (CIL) in medical image-guided diagnosis requires retaining prior diagnostic knowledge while adapting to newly emerging disease categories, which is critical for scalable clinical deployment. This problem is particularly challenging due to heterogeneous data and privacy constraints that prevent memory replay. Although pretrained foundation models (PFMs) have advanced general-domain CIL, their potential in medical imaging remains underexplored, where domain-specific adaptation is essential yet difficult due to anatomical complexity and inter-institutional heterogeneity. To address this gap, we conduct a systematic benchmark of recent PFM-based CIL methods and propose Bidirectional Conservative-Radical Complementary Learning (Bi-CRCL), a dual-learner framework inspired by complementary learning systems. Bi-CRCL integrates a conservative learner that preserves prior knowledge through stability-oriented updates and a radical learner that rapidly adapts to new categories via plasticity-oriented learning. A bidirectional interaction mechanism enables forward transfer and backward consolidation, allowing continual integration of new knowledge while mitigating catastrophic forgetting. During inference, outputs from both learners are adaptively fused for robust predictions. Experiments on five medical imaging datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods under diverse settings, including cross-dataset shifts and varying task configurations.

AI.tldr: Bi-CRCL: a dual-learner continual learning framework for medical image CIL that combines a conservative (stability) and radical (plasticity) learner with bidirectional interaction and adaptive inference fusion, achieving improvements over SOTA across five medical datasets under cross-dataset shifts.
AI.motivation: Overcome catastrophic forgetting and privacy constraints in class-incremental medical imaging by leveraging pre-trained foundation models and a complementary learning systems strategy to retain old knowledge while adapting to new disease categories without memory replay.
AI.method: Bi-CRCL comprises two interacting learners: a conservative learner that emphasizes stability to preserve prior knowledge, and a radical learner that rapidly adapts to new categories through plasticity. These learners engage in bidirectional interaction to enable forward transfer and backward consolidation. Inference fuses outputs from both learners for robust predictions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five medical imaging datasets shows consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods under diverse CIL settings, including cross-dataset shifts and varying task configurations.
AI.conclusion: Bi-CRCL demonstrates that a dual-learner, bidirectionally interacting continual learning framework can effectively mitigate forgetting and facilitate adaptation in medical imaging CIL, highlighting the value of combining stability-plasticity dynamics with adaptive inference fusion.

title: A Sensorless, Inherently Compliant Anthropomorphic Musculoskeletal Hand Driven by Electrohydraulic Actuators
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Robotic manipulation in unstructured environments requires end-effectors that combine high kinematic dexterity with physical compliance. While traditional rigid hands rely on complex external sensors for safe interaction, electrohydraulic actuators offer a promising alternative. This paper presents the design, control, and evaluation of a novel musculoskeletal robotic hand architecture powered entirely by remote Peano-HASEL actuators, specifically optimized for safe manipulation. By relocating the actuators to the forearm, we functionally isolate the grasping interface from electrical hazards while maintaining a slim, human-like profile. To address the inherently limited linear contraction of these soft actuators, we integrate a 1:2 pulley routing mechanism that mechanically amplifies tendon displacement. The resulting system prioritizes compliant interaction over high payload capacity, leveraging the intrinsic force-limiting characteristics of the actuators to provide a high level of inherent safety. Furthermore, this physical safety is augmented by the self-sensing nature of the HASEL actuators. By simply monitoring the operating current, we achieve real-time grasp detection and closed-loop contact-aware control without relying on external force transducers or encoders. Experimental results validate the system's dexterity and inherent safety, demonstrating the successful execution of various grasp taxonomies and the non-destructive grasping of highly fragile objects, such as a paper balloon. These findings highlight a significant step toward simplified, inherently compliant soft robotic manipulation.

AI.tldr: A remote-actuated musculoskeletal hand using forearm-mounted Peano-HASEL actuators with a 1:2 pulley to amplify tendon displacement enables safe, dexterous manipulation; self-sensing via actuator current supports real-time grasp detection and closed-loop control without external sensors, validated on diverse grasps including fragile objects like a paper balloon.
AI.motivation: Need for end-effectors that combine high dexterity with intrinsic safety for manipulation in unstructured environments; traditional rigid hands rely on external sensors and pose safety risks. Relocating actuators to the forearm with soft, inherently safe Peano-HASEL actuators reduces electrical hazards and enhances safety while maintaining a slim, human-like form.
AI.method: Design and control of a musculoskeletal hand powered entirely by remote Peano-HASEL actuators placed in the forearm. A 1:2 pulley routing mechanically amplifies the limited linear contraction of the soft actuators, enabling effective tendon displacement. Grasp state is monitored via actuator current for real-time grasp detection and closed-loop contact-aware control without external force sensors or encoders.
AI.result: Experimental validation showing dexterous manipulation across multiple grasp taxonomies and non-destructive handling of fragile objects (e.g., a paper balloon). Demonstrates inherent safety due to force-limiting actuators and current-based sensing, enabling safe interaction with delicate items.
AI.conclusion: The work represents a significant step toward simplified, inherently compliant soft robotic manipulation by decoupling actuation from sensing and safety hazards, achieving dexterity and safe manipulation in unstructured environments.

title: Latent Algorithmic Structure Precedes Grokking: A Mechanistic Study of ReLU MLPs on Modular Arithmetic
summary: Grokking-the phenomenon where validation accuracy of neural networks on modular addition of two integers rises long after training data has been memorized-has been characterized in previous works as producing sinusoidal input weight distributions in transformers and multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs). We find empirically that ReLU MLPs in our experimental setting instead learn near-binary square wave input weights, where intermediate-valued weights appear exclusively near sign-change boundaries, alongside output weight distributions whose dominant Fourier phases satisfy a phase-sum relation $φ_{\mathrm{out}} = φ_a + φ_b$; this relation holds even when the model is trained on noisy data and fails to grok. We extract the frequency and phase of each neuron's weights via DFT and construct an idealized MLP: Input weights are replaced by perfect binary square waves and output weights by cosines, both parametrized by the frequencies, phases, and amplitudes extracted from the dominant Fourier components of the real model weights. This idealized model achieves 95.5% accuracy when the frequencies and phases are extracted from the weights of a model trained on noisy data that itself achieves only 0.23% accuracy. This suggests that grokking does not discover the correct algorithm, but rather sharpens an algorithm substantially encoded during memorization, progressively binarizing the input weights into cleaner square waves and aligning the output weights, until generalization becomes possible.

AI.tldr: Grokking is explained as sharpening memorization: input weights become binary square waves, outputs become phase-aligned cosines; an idealized model using these components generalizes well, implying grokking leverages memorization-encoded structure rather than discovering a new algorithm.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanistic basis of grokking in neural nets by examining weight distributions and Fourier structure, and to test whether grokking reflects a genuine algorithm or memorization.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of ReLU MLPs on modular addition; use DFT to extract dominant frequencies/phases of input/output weights; create an idealized MLP with binary square-wave input weights and cosine output weights; evaluate on datasets with noisy labels.
AI.result: Findings: weights are near-binary square waves with intermediate weights at sign-change boundaries; φ_out ≈ φ_a + φ_b; the idealized model with extracted frequencies/phases achieves 95.5% accuracy on noisy-data-derived parameters, despite the real model achieving only 0.23%.
AI.conclusion: Grokking may sharpen an algorithm already encoded during memorization rather than discovering the true algorithm; weight binarization and phase alignment drive delayed generalization.

title: An Adapter-free Fine-tuning Approach for Tuning 3D Foundation Models
comment: Accepted at The Fifth International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (ICPRAI 2026)
summary: Point cloud foundation models demonstrate strong generalization, yet adapting them to downstream tasks remains challenging in low-data regimes. Full fine-tuning often leads to overfitting and significant drift from pre-trained representations, while existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods mitigate this issue by introducing additional trainable components at the cost of increased inference-time latency. We propose Momentum-Consistency Fine-Tuning (MCFT), an adapter-free approach that bridges the gap between full and parameter-efficient fine-tuning. MCFT selectively fine-tunes a portion of the pre-trained encoder while enforcing a momentum-based consistency constraint to preserve task-agnostic representations. Unlike PEFT methods, MCFT introduces no additional representation learning parameters beyond a standard task head, maintaining the original model's parameter count and inference efficiency. We further extend MCFT with two variants: a semi-supervised framework that leverages abundant unlabeled data to enhance few-shot performance, and a pruning-based variant that improves computational efficiency through structured layer removal. Extensive experiments on object recognition and part segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that MCFT consistently outperforms prior methods, achieving a 3.30% gain in 5-shot settings and up to a 6.13% improvement with semi-supervised learning, while remaining well-suited for resource-constrained deployment.

AI.tldr: MCFT is an adapter-free fine-tuning method for point cloud models that selects a subset of the encoder to train while enforcing a momentum-based consistency constraint, preserving pre-trained representations and avoiding extra parameters. It outperforms PEFT methods in low-data regimes, and is extended with semi-supervised and pruning variants for further gains and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Adapt point cloud foundation models to downstream tasks when data is scarce. Full fine-tuning causes overfitting and drift from pre-trained representations; PEFT reduces parameter drift but adds trainable components and latency. A middle-ground method that preserves original parameter count while achieving strong adaptation is needed.
AI.method: MCFT selectively fine-tunes a portion of the pre-trained encoder (adapter-free) and imposes a momentum-based consistency constraint to maintain task-agnostic representations. Two variants are introduced: (1) semi-supervised framework leveraging unlabeled data to boost few-shot performance, and (2) pruning-based variant that removes structured layers to improve computational efficiency.
AI.result: Experiments on object recognition and part segmentation show MCFT outperforms prior methods, with up to 3.30 percentage points gain in 5-shot settings and up to 6.13 percentage points improvement when combined with semi-supervised learning. The method maintains the original parameter count and inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: MCFT provides a principled middle ground between full fine-tuning and PEFT by fine-tuning only parts of the encoder while preserving representations through momentum-consistency. The semi-supervised and pruning variants further enhance performance and efficiency, making MCFT well-suited for resource-constrained deployment.

title: LATS: Large Language Model Assisted Teacher-Student Framework for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning in Traffic Signal Control
summary: Adaptive Traffic Signal Control (ATSC) aims to optimize traffic flow and minimize delays by adjusting traffic lights in real time. Recent advances in Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) have shown promise for ATSC, yet existing approaches still suffer from limited representational capacity, often leading to suboptimal performance and poor generalization in complex and dynamic traffic environments. On the other hand, Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at semantic representation, reasoning, and analysis, yet their propensity for hallucination and slow inference speeds often hinder their direct application to decision-making tasks. To address these challenges, we propose a novel learning paradigm named LATS that integrates LLMs and MARL, leveraging the former's strong prior knowledge and inductive abilities to enhance the latter's decision-making process. Specifically, we introduce a plug-and-play teacher-student learning module, where a trained embedding LLM serves as a teacher to generate rich semantic features that capture each intersection's topology structures and traffic dynamics. A much simpler (student) neural network then learns to emulate these features through knowledge distillation in the latent space, enabling the final model to operate independently from the LLM for downstream use in the RL decision-making process. This integration significantly enhances the overall model's representational capacity across diverse traffic scenarios, thus leading to more efficient and generalizable control strategies. Extensive experiments across diverse traffic datasets empirically demonstrate that our method enhances the representation learning capability of RL models, thereby leading to improved overall performance and generalization over both traditional RL and LLM-only approaches. [...]

AI.tldr: A novel LATS framework combines LLMs and MARL for adaptive traffic signal control via a plug-and-play teacher-student distillation, enriching RL representations with semantic features while maintaining real-time decision-making efficiency.
AI.motivation: MARL-based ATSC often suffers from limited representational capacity and generalization in complex, dynamic traffic; LLMs offer strong semantic priors but are expensive and prone to hallucinations; integrating them aims to leverage semantic awareness without sacrificing efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce a teacher-student distillation where a trained embedding LLM generates rich semantic features of intersection topology and traffic dynamics. A lightweight student neural network learns to emulate these features in latent space, enabling MARL to use distilled representations without depending on the LLM at inference time. The RL controller operates on the distilled latent features for decision-making.
AI.result: Empirical experiments across diverse traffic datasets show improved representation learning and overall performance compared to traditional MARL and LLM-only baselines, with better generalization to varying traffic conditions.
AI.conclusion: LATS demonstrates that leveraging LLM-based semantic priors through latent-space distillation can substantially enhance MARL-based ATSC, yielding more robust and generalizable control strategies while maintaining efficiency for downstream RL decision-making.

title: Manifold Generalization Provably Proceeds Memorization in Diffusion Models
comment: The first two authors contributed equally
summary: Diffusion models often generate novel samples even when the learned score is only \emph{coarse} -- a phenomenon not accounted for by the standard view of diffusion training as density estimation. In this paper, we show that, under the \emph{manifold hypothesis}, this behavior can instead be explained by coarse scores capturing the \emph{geometry} of the data while discarding the fine-scale distributional structure of the population measure~$μ_{\scriptscriptstyle\mathrm{data}}$. Concretely, whereas estimating the full data distribution $μ_{\scriptscriptstyle\mathrm{data}}$ supported on a $k$-dimensional manifold is known to require the classical minimax rate $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(N^{-1/k})$, we prove that diffusion models trained with coarse scores can exploit the \emph{regularity of the manifold support} and attain a near-parametric rate toward a \emph{different} target distribution. This target distribution has density uniformly comparable to that of~$μ_{\scriptscriptstyle\mathrm{data}}$ throughout any $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\bigl(N^{-β/(4k)}\bigr)$-neighborhood of the manifold, where $β$ denotes the manifold regularity. Our guarantees therefore depend only on the smoothness of the underlying support, and are especially favorable when the data density itself is irregular, for instance non-differentiable. In particular, when the manifold is sufficiently smooth, we obtain that \emph{generalization} -- formalized as the ability to generate novel, high-fidelity samples -- occurs at a statistical rate strictly faster than that required to estimate the full population distribution~$μ_{\scriptscriptstyle\mathrm{data}}$.

AI.tldr: Diffusion models with coarse scores leverage manifold geometry to generalize faster than full density estimation, achieving near-parametric rates in a neighborhood of the manifold.
AI.motivation: Explain why diffusion models can generate novel samples with coarse scores and reconcile this with diffusion training as density estimation by showing the role of geometry under the manifold hypothesis.
AI.method: Theoretical minimax analysis under the manifold setting: compare the minimax rate for estimating the full data distribution on a k-dimensional manifold (~N^{-1/k}) with the rate achieved by coarse-score diffusion toward a target distribution. Introduce manifold regularity β and show a near-parametric rate N^{-β/(4k)} to a target density that is uniformly comparable to μ_data within an ~N^{-β/(4k)}-neighborhood of the manifold.
AI.result: Under coarse scores, diffusion models can exploit manifold regularity to attain a near-parametric rate toward a target distribution whose density is uniformly comparable to μ_data within a neighborhood of the manifold. The guarantees depend on the smoothness of the manifold; the rate improves as β increases and can surpass the rate needed to estimate μ_data when the manifold is sufficiently smooth.
AI.conclusion: Coarse-score diffusion leverages the geometry of the data manifold rather than the full distribution, enabling faster generalization (novel, high-fidelity samples) under favorable manifold regularity, sometimes beating the traditional density-estimation rate.

title: Detection and Classification of (Pre)Cancerous Cells in Pap Smears: An Ensemble Strategy for the RIVA Cervical Cytology Challenge
comment: Accepted for Poster Presentation at the RIVA Cervical Cytology Challenge, IEEE ISBI 2026. 4 pages, 2 figures
summary: Automated detection and classification of cervical cells in conventional Pap smear images can strengthen cervical cancer screening at scale by reducing manual workload, improving triage, and increasing consistency across readers. However, it is challenged by severe class imbalance and frequent nuclear overlap. We present our approach to the RIVA Cervical Cytology Challenge (ISBI 2026), which requires multi-class detection of eight Bethesda cell categories under these conditions. Using YOLOv11m as the base architecture, we systematically evaluate three strategies to improve detection performance: loss reweighting, data resampling and transfer learning. We build an ensemble by combining models trained under each strategy, promoting complementary detection behavior and combining them through Weighted Boxes Fusion (WBF). The ensemble achieves a mAP50-95 of 0.201 on the preliminary test set and 0.147 on the final test set, representing a 29% improvement over the best individual model on the final test set and demonstrating the effectiveness of combining complementary imbalance mitigation strategies.

AI.tldr: YOLOv11m-based multi-strategy ensemble for eight-class cervical cytology detection in the ISBI 2026 RIVA challenge; uses loss reweighting, data resampling, and transfer learning; combines models via Weighted Boxes Fusion; achieves mAP50-95 0.201 (prelim) and 0.147 (final), 29% improvement over best single model on final test.
AI.motivation: Mitigate severe class imbalance and nuclear overlap in Pap smear detection to enable scalable, consistent cervical cancer screening.
AI.method: Base architecture YOLOv11m; implement three strategies (loss reweighting, data resampling, transfer learning); train models under each; ensemble via Weighted Boxes Fusion; evaluate on preliminary and final test sets.
AI.result: mAP50-95 scores: 0.201 (prelim), 0.147 (final); ensemble yields 29% improvement over the best single model on final test; demonstrates that combining complementary imbalance mitigation strategies is effective.
AI.conclusion: An effective approach to multi-class cervical cytology detection under imbalance and overlap; ensemble leveraging diverse mitigation strategies improves detection performance over individual models.

title: 3D-Mix for VLA: A Plug-and-Play Module for Integrating VGGT-based 3D Information into Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models leverage Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for robotic control, but recent studies reveal that MLLMs exhibit limited spatial intelligence due to training predominantly on 2D data, resulting in inadequate 3D perception for manipulation tasks. While recent approaches incorporate specialized 3D vision models such as VGGT to enhance spatial understanding, they employ diverse integration mechanisms without systematic investigation, leaving the optimal fusion strategy unclear. We conduct a comprehensive pilot study comparing nine VGGT integration schemes on standardized benchmarks and find that semantic-conditioned gated fusion, which adaptively balances 2D semantic and 3D geometric features based on task context, achieved the strongest performance among all nine evaluated fusion schemes in our pilot study. We present 3D-Mix, a plug-and-play module that integrates into diverse VLA architectures (GR00T-style and $π$-style) without modifying existing MLLM or action expert components. Experiments across six MLLM series (nine model variants, 2B--8B parameters) on SIMPLER and LIBERO show that 3D-Mix delivers consistent performance gains, averaging +7.0% on the out-of-domain (OOD) SIMPLER benchmark across all nine GR00T-style variants, establishing a principled approach for enhancing spatial intelligence in VLA systems.

AI.tldr: Systematically evaluated nine VGGT fusion schemes for Vision-Language-Action models and found semantic-conditioned gated fusion to be the strongest. Introduced 3D-Mix, a plug-and-play module that enhances 3D spatial reasoning across diverse VLA architectures and MLLMs, achieving ~+7.0% average gains on OOD SIMPLER benchmarks for GR00T-style variants.
AI.motivation: MLLMs trained predominantly on 2D data exhibit limited 3D spatial perception, hindering manipulation tasks. Existing VGGT fusion methods are varied and lack systematic comparison, making it unclear which fusion strategy is most effective for integrating 3D geometry with 2D semantics.
AI.method: Conducted a comprehensive pilot study evaluating nine VGGT integration schemes on standardized benchmarks. Identified semantic-conditioned gated fusion as the best. Proposed 3D-Mix, a plug-and-play module that integrates with GR00T-style and π-style VLA architectures without changing MLLMs or action experts. Evaluated across six MLLM series (nine variants, 2B–8B) on SIMPLER and LIBERO.
AI.result: 3D-Mix yields consistent performance gains, averaging +7.0% on the OOD SIMPLER benchmark across all nine GR00T-style variants.
AI.conclusion: A principled approach to fusing 2D semantic and 3D geometric cues via semantic-conditioned gated fusion is effective for VLA spatial understanding. 3D-Mix provides a versatile, device-agnostic integration that enhances spatial intelligence across diverse VLA systems without modifying core components.

title: Resolving gradient pathology in physics-informed epidemiological models
comment: 16 pages, 4 figures. Submitted to Neural Networks
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) are increasingly used in mathematical epidemiology to bridge the gap between noisy clinical data and compartmental models, such as the susceptible-exposed-infected-removed (SEIR) model. However, training these hybrid networks is often unstable due to competing optimization objectives. As established in recent literature on ``gradient pathology," the gradient vectors derived from the data loss and the physical residual often point in conflicting directions, leading to slow convergence or optimization deadlock. While existing methods attempt to resolve this by balancing gradient magnitudes or projecting conflicting vectors, we propose a novel method, conflict-gated gradient scaling (CGGS), to address gradient conflicts in physics-informed neural networks for epidemiological modelling, ensuring stable and efficient training and a computationally efficient alternative. This method utilizes the cosine similarity between the data and physics gradients to dynamically modulate the penalty weight. Unlike standard annealing schemes that only normalize scales, CGGS acts as a geometric gate: it suppresses the physical constraint when directional conflict is high, allowing the optimizer to prioritize data fidelity, and restores the constraint when gradients align. We prove that this gating mechanism preserves the standard $O(1/T)$ convergence rate for smooth non-convex objectives, a guarantee that fails under fixed-weight or magnitude-balanced training when gradients conflict. We demonstrate that this mechanism autonomously induces a curriculum learning effect, improving parameter estimation in stiff epidemiological systems compared to magnitude-based baselines. Our empirical results show improved peak recovery and convergence over magnitude-based methods.

AI.tldr: Conflict-gated gradient scaling (CGGS) mitigates gradient conflicts in PINNs for SEIR-based epidemiological modelling by modulating the physics penalty via cosine similarity between data and physics gradients, preserving O(1/T) convergence and enabling curriculum-like learning, with improved convergence vs magnitude-based baselines.
AI.motivation: PINNs trained on epidemiological models suffer from gradient pathology due to competing objectives (data vs physics). Existing balancing strategies are insufficient for stable training; a dynamic gating mechanism is needed.
AI.method: Compute cosine similarity between data gradient and physics gradient; gate the physics residual penalty accordingly: suppress when gradients conflict (low alignment) and restore when aligned; this creates a geometric gate rather than simple magnitude scaling. Prove convergence rate O(1/T) for smooth non-convex objectives; empirical demonstration on stiff SEIR-like systems; yields curriculum learning effect.
AI.result: CGGS yields improved peak recovery and faster convergence compared to magnitude-based baselines; minor computational overhead; autonomous curriculum effect; stable training.
AI.conclusion: CGGS provides a theoretically grounded, computationally efficient method to stabilize PINN training in epidemiological modelling, preserving convergence guarantees and improving practical performance.

title: IJmond Industrial Smoke Segmentation Dataset
summary: This report describes a dataset for industrial smoke segmentation, published on a figshare repository (https://doi.org/10.21942/uva.31847188). The dataset is licensed under CC BY 4.0.

AI.tldr: A dataset for industrial smoke segmentation is introduced, hosted on figshare and licensed CC BY 4.0.
AI.motivation: Facilitates research on detecting and segmenting industrial smoke; provides open, citable data to promote reproducibility and model evaluation.
AI.method: Compilation and annotation of industrial smoke imagery with segmentation masks; deposition on figshare; licensing CC BY 4.0 to enable reuse; likely data collection and annotation pipeline.
AI.result: A publicly available, openly licensed dataset enabling training/evaluation of smoke segmentation methods.
AI.conclusion: The dataset supports advancement in industrial smoke analysis and can foster cross-study comparability due to open licensing.

title: Enhancing Drone Light Shows Performances: Optimal Allocation and Trajectories for Swarm Drone Formations
summary: Drone light shows (DLShows) represent a rapidly growing application of swarm robotics, creating captivating aerial displays through the synchronized flight of hundreds or thousands of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) as environmentally friendly and reusable alternatives to traditional pyrotechnics. This domain presents unique challenges in optimally assigning drones to visual waypoints and generating smooth, collision-free trajectories at a very large scale. This article introduces the Unified Assignment and Trajectory Generation (UATG) framework. The proposed approach concurrently solves two core problems: the optimal assignment of drones to designated goal locations and the generation of dynamically feasible, collision-free, time-parameterized trajectories. The UATG framework is specifically designed for DLShows, ensuring minimal transition times between formations and guaranteeing inter-drone collision avoidance. A key innovation is its exceptional computational efficiency, enabling the coordination of large-scale in real-time; for instance, it computes the optimal assignment and trajectories for 1008 drones in approximately one second on a standard laptop. Extensive simulations in realistic environments validate the framework's performance, demonstrating its capability to orchestrate complex formations, from alphanumeric characters to intricate 3D shapes, with precision and visual smoothness. This work provides a critical advancement for the DLShow industry, offering a practical and scalable solution for generating complex aerial choreography and establishing a valuable benchmark for ground control station software designed for the efficient coordination of multiple UAVs. A supplemental animated simulation of this work is available at https://youtu.be/-Fjrhw03594.

AI.tldr: Unified Assignment and Trajectory Generation (UATG) enables real-time, scalable coordination of DLShows by jointly solving drone-to-goal assignment and collision-free trajectory generation for large fleets (e.g., 1008 drones in ~1s on a laptop).
AI.motivation: The need for safe, efficient, real-time planning of large-scale drone shows that can form complex 2D/3D visuals while minimizing transition times and avoiding collisions, addressing the computational bottlenecks of naive decoupled approaches.
AI.method: A unified optimization framework that concurrently solves (i) optimal assignment of drones to designated goal locations and (ii) generation of dynamically feasible, collision-free, time-parameterized trajectories. Emphasizes scalability to hundreds to thousands of UAVs to support real-time performance; demonstrates capabilities for complex formations (alphabets, 3D shapes) in simulations.
AI.result: Demonstrates real-time coordination for large drone fleets (e.g., 1008 drones in ~1s on a standard laptop); validated via extensive simulations showing precise, smooth trajectories and capabilities to form diverse formations; provides a practical benchmark for ground control software in multi-UAV shows.
AI.conclusion: UATG constitutes a significant practical advance for DLShows, delivering a scalable, real-time solution for multi-UAV choreography with guaranteed collision avoidance and smooth transitions, and establishing benchmarks for future ground control systems.

title: Deep Neural Regression Collapse
comment: Accepted to CPAL 2026; Code will be available at https://github.com/altayunal/neural-collapse-regression
summary: Neural Collapse is a phenomenon that helps identify sparse and low rank structures in deep classifiers. Recent work has extended the definition of neural collapse to regression problems, albeit only measuring the phenomenon at the last layer. In this paper, we establish that Neural Regression Collapse (NRC) also occurs below the last layer across different types of models. We show that in the collapsed layers of neural regression models, features lie in a subspace that corresponds to the target dimension, the feature covariance aligns with the target covariance, the input subspace of the layer weights aligns with the feature subspace, and the linear prediction error of the features is close to the overall prediction error of the model. In addition to establishing Deep NRC, we also show that models that exhibit Deep NRC learn the intrinsic dimension of low rank targets and explore the necessity of weight decay in inducing Deep NRC. This paper provides a more complete picture of the simple structure learned by deep networks in the context of regression.

AI.tldr: Neural Regression Collapse (NRC) extends neural collapse to regression, showing deep NRC across layers with subspace alignment and intrinsic-dimension learning, and exploring the role of weight decay.
AI.motivation: To generalize neural collapse to regression beyond the last layer, and to characterize the structure and mechanisms (subspace alignment, covariance alignment, intrinsic dimension) that govern deep regression networks.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of regression models across layers, deriving conditions under which features reside in a target-dimension subspace, feature covariance matches target covariance, input subspace aligns with feature subspace, and layer predictions' linear error tracks overall error; empirical/analytical demonstration of deep NRC and the impact of weight decay.
AI.result: Proof that NRC occurs below the last layer (Deep NRC) for various models; identification of subspace and covariance alignments; discovery that networks learn the intrinsic dimension of low-rank targets; analysis of weight decay necessity in inducing Deep NRC.
AI.conclusion: Deep NRC provides a fuller understanding of the simple structure learned by deep networks in regression, extending the collapse paradigm beyond the final layer and highlighting dimensionality and regularization roles.

title: Learning Cross-Joint Attention for Generalizable Video-Based Seizure Detection
summary: Automated seizure detection from long-term clinical videos can substantially reduce manual review time and enable real-time monitoring. However, existing video-based methods often struggle to generalize to unseen subjects due to background bias and reliance on subject-specific appearance cues. We propose a joint-centric attention model that focuses exclusively on body dynamics to improve cross-subject generalization. For each video segment, body joints are detected and joint-centered clips are extracted, suppressing background context. These joint-centered clips are tokenized using a Video Vision Transformer (ViViT), and cross-joint attention is learned to model spatial and temporal interactions between body parts, capturing coordinated movement patterns characteristic of seizure semiology. Extensive cross-subject experiments show that the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art CNN-, graph-, and transformer-based approaches on unseen subjects.

AI.tldr: A joint-centric attention framework for video-based seizure detection isolates body dynamics via joint-centered clips and ViViT, improving cross-subject generalization over prior CNN/graph/transformer methods.
AI.motivation: Generalization to unseen subjects is hampered by background bias and reliance on subject-specific cues; a focus on body dynamics could yield more robust seizure semiology recognition.
AI.method: For each video segment, detect body joints and extract joint-centered clips, suppressing background context. These clips are tokenized with a Video Vision Transformer (ViViT), and cross-joint attention is learned to model spatial and temporal interactions among joints to capture coordinated movement patterns.
AI.result: Extensive cross-subject experiments show the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art CNN-, graph-, and transformer-based approaches on unseen subjects.
AI.conclusion: Focusing on body dynamics through joint-centered clips and cross-joint attention using ViViT improves cross-subject generalization for video-based seizure detection.

title: Design, Modelling and Characterisation of a Miniature Fibre-Reinforced Soft Bending Actuator for Endoluminal Interventions
summary: Miniaturised soft pneumatic actuators are crucial for robotic intervention within highly constrained anatomical pathways. This work presents the design and validation of a fibre-reinforced soft actuator at the centimetre scale for inte- gration into an endoluminal robotic platform for natural-orifice interventional and diagnostic applications. A single-chamber geometry reinforced with embedded Kevlar fibre was de- signed to maximise curvature while preserving sealing integrity, fabricated using a multi-stage multi-stiffness silicone casting process, and validated against a high-fidelity Abaqus FEM using experimentally parametrised hyperelastic material models and embedded beam reinforcement. The semi-cylindrical actuator has an outer diameter of 18,mm and a length of 37.5,mm. Single and double helix winding configurations, fibre pitch, and fibre density were investigated. The optimal 100 SH configuration achieved a bending angle of 202.9° experimentally and 297.6° in simulation, with structural robustness maintained up to 100,kPa and radial expansion effectively constrained by the fibre reinforcement. Workspace evaluation confirmed suitability for integration into the target device envelope, demonstrating that fibre-reinforcement strategies can be effectively translated to the centimetre regime while retaining actuator performance.

AI.tldr: Centimetre-scale fibre-reinforced soft pneumatic actuator for endoluminal robotics; validated by FEM against experiments; demonstrates large bending with restrained radial expansion, enabling integration into constrained anatomical pathways.
AI.motivation: Enable navigation and diagnostic/interventional capabilities within natural orifices using compact, sealed soft actuators; validate performance with experiments and high-fidelity finite element modeling.
AI.method: Design of a single-chamber, Kevlar-reinforced fibre-reinforced soft actuator fabricated by multi-stage silicone casting; embedded beam reinforcement; Abaqus FEM with experimentally parametrised hyperelastic material models; explored single/double helix winding, fibre pitch and density; dimensions: semi-cylindrical actuator Ø≈18 mm, length≈37.5 mm; tested configurations, optimized 100 SH.
AI.result: Optimal 100 SH configuration yielded experimental bending of 202.9° and FEM-predicted 297.6°; radial expansion constrained by reinforcement; robust up to 100 kPa; workspace adequate for target device envelope; validates transfer of fibre-reinforcement strategies to centimetre-scale actuators.
AI.conclusion: Fibre-reinforcement strategies can be effectively translated to centimetre-scale soft actuators for endoluminal platforms, achieving high bending with sealing integrity and structural robustness, suitable for integration into clinical device envelopes.

title: Circuit Complexity of Hierarchical Knowledge Tracing and Implications for Log-Precision Transformers
summary: Knowledge tracing models mastery over interconnected concepts, often organized by prerequisites. We analyze hierarchical prerequisite propagation through a circuit-complexity lens to clarify what is provable about transformer-style computation on deep concept hierarchies. Using recent results that log-precision transformers lie in logspace-uniform $\mathsf{TC}^0$, we formalize prerequisite-tree tasks including recursive-majority mastery propagation. Unconditionally, recursive-majority propagation lies in $\mathsf{NC}^1$ via $O(\log n)$-depth bounded-fanin circuits, while separating it from uniform $\mathsf{TC}^0$ would require major progress on open lower bounds. Under a monotonicity restriction, we obtain an unconditional barrier: alternating ALL/ANY prerequisite trees yield a strict depth hierarchy for \emph{monotone} threshold circuits. Empirically, transformer encoders trained on recursive-majority trees converge to permutation-invariant shortcuts; explicit structure alone does not prevent this, but auxiliary supervision on intermediate subtrees elicits structure-dependent computation and achieves near-perfect accuracy at depths 3--4. These findings motivate structure-aware objectives and iterative mechanisms for prerequisite-sensitive knowledge tracing on deep hierarchies.

AI.tldr: The paper links transformer-like knowledge tracing on deep prerequisite trees to circuit complexity, proving NC^1 upper bounds for recursive-majority propagation, shows logspace-uniform TC^0 containment for log-precision transformers, and demonstrates empirical benefits of structure-aware supervision on deep hierarchies.
AI.motivation: Clarify provable capabilities and limits of transformer-type computation for hierarchical knowledge tracing.
AI.method: Circuit-complexity analysis; formalization of prerequisite-tree tasks; proofs that recursive-majority propagation lies in NC^1; discussion of logspace-uniform TC^0 for log-precision transformers; monotone-threshold hierarchy under monotonicity; empirical training of transformer encoders on recursive-majority trees with and without intermediate supervision.
AI.result: Unconditionally, recursive-majority propagation is in NC^1; separating it from uniform TC^0 would require major breakthroughs in lower bounds; under monotonicity, a strict depth hierarchy appears for monotone threshold circuits; empirically, transformers develop permutation-invariant shortcuts on recursive-majority trees; auxiliary supervision on intermediate subtrees elicits structure-dependent computation and yields near-perfect accuracy at depths 3–4.
AI.conclusion: Motivates structure-aware objectives and iterative mechanisms for prerequisite-sensitive knowledge tracing on deep hierarchies.

title: Semantic Iterative Reconstruction: One-Shot Universal Anomaly Detection
summary:
Unsupervised medical anomaly detection is severely limited by the scarcity of normal training samples. Existing methods typically train dedicated models for each dataset or disease, requiring hundreds of normal images per task and lacking cross-modality generalization.
  We propose Semantic Iterative Reconstruction (SIR), a framework that enables a single universal model to detect anomalies across diverse medical domains using extremely few normal samples. SIR leverages a pretrained teacher encoder to extract multi-scale deep features and employs a compact up-then-down decoder with multi-loop iterative refinement to enforce robust normality priors in deep feature space. The framework adopts a one-shot universal design: a single model is trained by mixing exactly one normal sample from each of nine heterogeneous datasets, enabling effective anomaly detection on all corresponding test sets without task-specific retraining.
  Extensive experiments on nine medical benchmarks demonstrate that SIR achieves state-of-the-art under all four settings -- one-shot universal, full-shot universal, one-shot specialized, and full-shot specialized -- consistently outperforming previous methods. SIR offers an efficient and scalable solution for multi-domain clinical anomaly detection. Code is available at https://github.com/jusufzn212427/sir4ad.

AI.tldr: SIR provides a universal, one-shot trained model for cross-domain medical anomaly detection using a multi-loop iterative reconstruction with a teacher encoder, achieving state-of-the-art results on nine benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Medical anomaly detection is hindered by scarce normal data and lack of cross-modality generalization; existing methods require dataset- or task-specific models with many normal images. A universal approach with minimal supervision is highly desirable.
AI.method: Semantic Iterative Reconstruction (SIR) uses a pretrained teacher encoder to extract multi-scale features and a compact up-then-down decoder with multi-loop iterative refinement to enforce robust normal priors in deep feature space. Training is one-shot and universal: mix exactly one normal sample from each of nine heterogeneous datasets to train a single model for all tasks.
AI.result: On nine medical benchmarks, SIR achieves state-of-the-art performance across four settings (one-shot universal, full-shot universal, one-shot specialized, full-shot specialized), consistently outperforming prior methods and offering an efficient, scalable solution for multi-domain clinical anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: SIR demonstrates that a single, minimally supervised model can robustly detect anomalies across diverse medical domains, enabling scalable cross-modality anomaly detection with strong performance and practical code availability.

title: Chameleon: Episodic Memory for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation
summary: Robotic manipulation often requires memory: occlusion and state changes can make decision-time observations perceptually aliased, making action selection non-Markovian at the observation level because the same observation may arise from different interaction histories. Most embodied agents implement memory via semantically compressed traces and similarity-based retrieval, which discards disambiguating fine-grained perceptual cues and can return perceptually similar but decision-irrelevant episodes. Inspired by human episodic memory, we propose Chameleon, which writes geometry-grounded multimodal tokens to preserve disambiguating context and produces goal-directed recall through a differentiable memory stack. We also introduce Camo-Dataset, a real-robot UR5e dataset spanning episodic recall, spatial tracking, and sequential manipulation under perceptual aliasing. Across tasks, Chameleon consistently improves decision reliability and long-horizon control over strong baselines in perceptually confusable settings.

AI.tldr: A memory-augmented robotic manipulation framework (Chameleon) with geometry-grounded multimodal tokens and a differentiable memory stack; introduces the Camo-Dataset for real-robot episodic recall; shows improved decision reliability and long-horizon control under perceptual aliasing.
AI.motivation: Perceptual aliasing and state non-Markovianity in manipulation tasks arise because occlusions and history can collapse observations. Conventional semantic-memory traces discard fine-grained cues, hindering disambiguation; there is a need for memory that preserves geometry-grounded context for robust decision-making.
AI.method: Introduce Chameleon, which writes geometry-grounded multimodal tokens to a differentiable memory stack to preserve disambiguating context and support goal-directed recall. Build Camo-Dataset (real-robot UR5e) spanning episodic recall, spatial tracking, and sequential manipulation under perceptual aliasing, to evaluate memory-based decision-making.
AI.result: Across tasks, Chameleon consistently improves decision reliability and long-horizon control over strong baselines in perceptually confusable settings.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-grounded, differentiable episodic memory architecture (Chameleon) plus a realistic UR5e dataset (Camo-Dataset) enable robust, recall-driven manipulation under perceptual aliasing, addressing a key challenge in memory-enabled robotics.

title: Unveiling Hidden Convexity in Deep Learning: a Sparse Signal Processing Perspective
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs), particularly those using Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation functions, have achieved remarkable success across diverse machine learning tasks, including image recognition, audio processing, and language modeling. Despite this success, the non-convex nature of DNN loss functions complicates optimization and limits theoretical understanding. In this paper, we highlight how recently developed convex equivalences of ReLU NNs and their connections to sparse signal processing models can address the challenges of training and understanding NNs. Recent research has uncovered several hidden convexities in the loss landscapes of certain NN architectures, notably two-layer ReLU networks and other deeper or varied architectures. This paper seeks to provide an accessible and educational overview that bridges recent advances in the mathematics of deep learning with traditional signal processing, encouraging broader signal processing applications.

AI.tldr: An accessible overview of convex reformulations of ReLU neural networks and their links to sparse signal processing, highlighting hidden convexities in loss landscapes (especially in two-layer networks) and offering a bridge between deep learning theory and traditional signal processing to broaden applications.
AI.motivation: Non-convex loss surfaces in deep neural networks impede optimization and theory. Convex equivalences and connections to sparse modeling promise improved understanding, training guarantees, and interpretability, motivating a unified treatment with signal processing concepts.
AI.method: Survey and synthesis of recent theoretical results on convex reformulations of ReLU networks, analysis of loss landscape convexities (notably for two-layer networks and extensions), and pedagogical exposition to connect deep learning mathematics with sparse signal processing.
AI.result: Consolidates and clarifies key convexity results and their implications, explains when and how convex reformulations arise, and presents a coherent framework for using these ideas to analyze and guide training and interpretation of ReLU networks.
AI.conclusion: Provides an educational resource that bridges deep learning mathematics with traditional signal processing, and advocates applying convexity insights to broader signal processing tasks and applications.

title: Retinal Disease Classification from Fundus Images using CNN Transfer Learning
summary: Retinal diseases remain among the leading preventable causes of visual impairment worldwide. Automated screening based on fundus image analysis has the potential to expand access to early detection, particularly in underserved populations. This paper presents a reproducible deep learning pipeline for binary retinal disease risk classification from publicly available fundus photographs. We implement and compare a baseline convolutional neural network with a transfer learning approach using a pretrained VGG16 backbone and evaluate generalization on held-out data. To address class imbalance, we apply class weighting and report standard classification metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrices, and ROC-AUC. The VGG16 transfer learning model achieves 90.8% test accuracy with a weighted F1-score of 0.90, substantially outperforming the baseline CNN (83.1% accuracy). Results indicate that transfer learning improves discrimination compared to a baseline CNN, while also revealing remaining challenges in sensitivity to minority disease cases. We discuss practical limitations related to dataset characteristics, class imbalance, and threshold selection, and provide guidance for reproducibility and future improvements for clinically reliable screening

AI.tldr: A reproducible deep learning pipeline for binary retinal disease risk classification on fundus photographs, showing transfer learning with VGG16 outperforms a baseline CNN (90.8% vs 83.1% accuracy; weighted F1 0.90).
AI.motivation: Retinal diseases are major preventable causes of visual impairment; automated, scalable screening from fundus images can increase early detection in underserved populations.
AI.method: Comparison of a baseline CNN and a transfer learning model using a pretrained VGG16 backbone. Class weighting used to address label imbalance. Evaluation on held-out data with metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1, confusion matrices, ROC-AUC.
AI.result: VGG16 transfer learning achieved 90.8% test accuracy and weighted F1-score of 0.90, substantially better than the baseline CNN (83.1% accuracy). Indicates improved discrimination with transfer learning; however, sensitivity to minority disease cases remains challenging.
AI.conclusion: Transfer learning with VGG16 enhances binary retinal disease risk classification from fundus images, but dataset characteristics, class imbalance, and threshold selection limit clinical reliability; results underscore the need for reproducibility guidance and future improvements for robust screening.

title: Symbolic--KAN: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks with Discrete Symbolic Structure for Interpretable Learning
summary: Symbolic discovery of governing equations is a long-standing goal in scientific machine learning, yet a fundamental trade-off persists between interpretability and scalable learning. Classical symbolic regression methods yield explicit analytic expressions but rely on combinatorial search, whereas neural networks scale efficiently with data and dimensionality but produce opaque representations. In this work, we introduce Symbolic Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (Symbolic-KANs), a neural architecture that bridges this gap by embedding discrete symbolic structure directly within a trainable deep network. Symbolic-KANs represent multivariate functions as compositions of learned univariate primitives applied to learned scalar projections, guided by a library of analytic primitives, hierarchical gating, and symbolic regularization that progressively sharpens continuous mixtures into one-hot selections. After gated training and discretization, each active unit selects a single primitive and projection direction, yielding compact closed-form expressions without post-hoc symbolic fitting. Symbolic-KANs further act as scalable primitive discovery mechanisms, identifying the most relevant analytic components that can subsequently inform candidate libraries for sparse equation-learning methods. We demonstrate that Symbolic-KAN reliably recovers correct primitive terms and governing structures in data-driven regression and inverse dynamical systems. Moreover, the framework extends to forward and inverse physics-informed learning of partial differential equations, producing accurate solutions directly from governing constraints while constructing compact symbolic representations whose selected primitives reflect the true analytical structure of the underlying equations. These results position Symbolic-KAN as a step toward scalable, interpretable, and mechanistically grounded learning of governing laws.

AI.tldr: Symbolic-KANs fuse symbolic regression interpretability with neural scalability by embedding discrete symbolic structure inside a trainable network, yielding interpretable closed-form expressions after discretization and enabling primitive discovery for equation learning and PDEs.
AI.motivation: Bridge the interpretability–scalability gap in learning governing equations by combining neural predictive power with symbolic structure discovery and automatic primitive selection.
AI.method: A neural architecture that represents multivariate functions as compositions of learned univariate primitives applied to learned scalar projections, guided by a library of analytic primitives, hierarchical gating, and symbolic regularization that sharpens continuous mixtures into one-hot selections; after gated training and discretization, each active unit selects a single primitive and projection direction, yielding compact closed-form expressions without post-hoc symbolic fitting.
AI.result: Symbolic-KANs reliably recover correct primitive terms and governing structures in data-driven regression and inverse dynamical systems; extend to forward and inverse physics-informed learning of PDEs, producing accurate solutions directly from governing constraints while constructing compact symbolic representations reflecting the true analytical structure.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic-KANs offer a scalable, interpretable, and mechanistically grounded approach for learning governing laws, serving as a primitive discovery mechanism for sparse equation-learning pipelines and aiding interpretable PDE modeling.

title: Re-Prompting SAM 3 via Object Retrieval: 3rd of the 5th PVUW MOSE Track
summary: This technical report explores the MOSEv2 track of the PVUW 2026 Challenge, which targets complex semi-supervised video object segmentation. Built on SAM~3, we develop an automatic re-prompting framework to improve robustness under target disappearance and reappearance, severe transformation, and strong same-category distractors. Our method first applies the SAM~3 detector to later frames to identify same-category object candidates, and then performs DINOv3-based object-level matching with a transformation-aware target feature pool to retrieve reliable target anchors. These anchors are injected back into the SAM~3 tracker together with the first-frame mask, enabling multi-anchor propagation rather than relying solely on the initial prompt. This simple directly benefits several core challenges of MOSEv2. Our solution achieves a J&F of 51.17% on the test set, ranking 3rd in the MOSEv2 track.

AI.tldr: A semi-supervised video object segmentation method for MOSEv2 using SAM-3 detector and DINOv3-based matching to inject multiple anchors into the tracker; achieves 51.17% J&F and 3rd place.
AI.motivation: Address robust tracking under target disappearance/reappearance, severe transformation, and strong same-category distractors in MOSEv2; improve SAM-3-based tracking by leveraging multi-anchor prompts.
AI.method: 1) Apply the SAM-3 detector on later frames to propose same-category object candidates. 2) Use a DINOv3-based object-level matching with a transformation-aware target feature pool to retrieve reliable target anchors. 3) Inject these anchors into the SAM-3 tracker together with the first-frame mask to enable multi-anchor propagation rather than relying solely on the initial prompt.
AI.result: J&F of 51.17% on the test set; ranked 3rd in the MOSEv2 track.
AI.conclusion: A simple yet effective multi-anchor propagation approach directly addresses core MOSEv2 challenges, yielding robustness to appearance changes and distractors.

title: Why the Maximum Second Derivative of Activations Matters for Adversarial Robustness
summary: This work investigates the critical role of activation function curvature -- quantified by the maximum second derivative $\max|σ''|$ -- in adversarial robustness. Using the Recursive Curvature-Tunable Activation Family (RCT-AF), which enables precise control over curvature through parameters $α$ and $β$, we systematically analyze this relationship. Our study reveals a fundamental trade-off: insufficient curvature limits model expressivity, while excessive curvature amplifies the normalized Hessian diagonal norm of the loss, leading to sharper minima that hinder robust generalization. This results in a non-monotonic relationship where optimal adversarial robustness consistently occurs when $\max|σ''|$ falls within 4 to 10, a finding that holds across diverse network architectures, datasets, and adversarial training methods. We provide theoretical insights into how activation curvature affects the diagonal elements of the hessian matrix of the loss, and experimentally demonstrate that the normalized Hessian diagonal norm exhibits a U-shaped dependence on $\max|σ''|$, with its minimum within the optimal robustness range, thereby validating the proposed mechanism.

AI.tldr: Activation function curvature critically influences adversarial robustness; an optimal curvature range exists; too little or too much curvature degrades robustness.
AI.motivation: To understand how activation curvature, measured by max|σ''|, affects robustness, and to reconcile expressivity with robust generalization via Hessian analysis.
AI.method: Introduce Recursive Curvature-Tunable Activation Family (RCT-AF) with parameters α, β to precisely control curvature. The study combines theoretical analysis of Hessian diagonal elements of the loss with extensive empirical experiments across architectures, datasets, and adversarial training methods.
AI.result: Revealed a non-monotonic relationship: optimal robustness occurs when max|σ''| is within 4–10. The normalized Hessian diagonal norm exhibits a U-shaped dependence on max|σ''|, with a minimum in the optimal range. This holds across diverse networks and training regimes.
AI.conclusion: Activation curvature is a key knob for adversarial robustness. There exists a practical curvature window (4–10 for max|σ''|) that balances expressivity and robust generalization; beyond this window, robustness degrades due to sharper minima.

title: Sparse Autoencoders for Interpretable Medical Image Representation Learning
summary: Vision foundation models (FMs) achieve state-of-the-art performance in medical imaging. However, they encode information in abstract latent representations that clinicians cannot interrogate or verify. The goal of this study is to investigate Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) for replacing opaque FM image representations with human-interpretable, sparse features. We train SAEs on embeddings from BiomedParse (biomedical) and DINOv3 (general-purpose) using 909,873 CT and MRI 2D image slices from the TotalSegmentator dataset. We find that learned sparse features: (a) reconstruct original embeddings with high fidelity (R2 up to 0.941) and recover up to 87.8% of downstream performance using only 10 features (99.4% dimensionality reduction), (b) preserve semantic fidelity in image retrieval tasks, (c) correspond to specific concepts that can be expressed in language using large language model (LLM)-based auto-interpretation. (d) bridge clinical language and abstract latent representations in zero-shot language-driven image retrieval. Our work indicates SAEs are a promising pathway towards interpretable, concept-driven medical vision systems. Code repository: https://github.com/pwesp/sail.

AI.tldr: Sparse Autoencoders can translate opaque foundation-model embeddings into sparse, human-interpretable features that preserve most downstream performance in medical imaging.
AI.motivation: Foundation-model embeddings in medical imaging are abstract and not directly interpretable by clinicians; there is a need for interpretable, concept-driven representations to enable verification, retrieval, and clinical trust.
AI.method: Train SAEs on embeddings from BiomedParse (biomedical) and DINOv3 (general-purpose) using 909,873 CT/MRI 2D slices from the TotalSegmentator dataset. Evaluate: (1) reconstruction fidelity of embeddings (R^2 up to 0.941), (2) downstream performance recuperation with only 10 features (87.8% recovery; 99.4% dimensionality reduction), (3) semantic fidelity in image retrieval, (4) correspondence to language-expressible concepts via LLM-based auto-interpretation, (5) zero-shot language-driven image retrieval bridging clinical language and latent space.
AI.result: SAEs achieve high-fidelity reconstruction of embeddings, recover substantial downstream performance with a 99.4% dimensionality reduction using just 10 features, preserve semantic fidelity in image retrieval, and map features to language-expressible concepts via LLM interpretation, enabling zero-shot language-driven retrieval.
AI.conclusion: SAEs offer a promising path toward interpretable, concept-driven medical vision systems; code is available (GitHub: sail).

title: An Invariant Compiler for Neural ODEs in AI-Accelerated Scientific Simulation
summary: Neural ODEs are increasingly used as continuous-time models for scientific and sensor data, but unconstrained neural ODEs can drift and violate domain invariants (e.g., conservation laws), yielding physically implausible solutions. In turn, this can compound error in long-horizon prediction and surrogate simulation. Existing solutions typically aim to enforce invariance by soft penalties or other forms of regularization, which can reduce overall error but do not guarantee that trajectories will not leave the constraint manifold. We introduce the invariant compiler, a framework that enforces invariants by construction: it treats invariants as first-class types and uses an LLM-driven compilation workflow to translate a generic neural ODE specification into a structure-preserving architecture whose trajectories remain on the admissible manifold in continuous time (and up to numerical integration error in practice). This compiler view cleanly separates what must be preserved (scientific structure) from what is learned from data (dynamics within that structure). It provides a systematic design pattern for invariant-respecting neural surrogates across scientific domains.

AI.tldr: Introduces the invariant compiler to enforce invariants in Neural ODEs by construction, using an LLM-driven workflow to map generic ODE specs into structure-preserving architectures that keep trajectories on the admissible manifold (within numerical error).
AI.motivation: To prevent unbounded drift of unconstrained Neural ODEs and violation of domain invariants (e.g., conservation laws) which degrade long-horizon predictions and simulation accuracy.
AI.method: Treat invariants as first-class types and use an LLM-driven compilation workflow to translate a generic neural ODE into a structure-preserving architecture. This separates preserved scientific structure from learned dynamics, yielding trajectories that stay on the invariant manifold in continuous time (up to numerical error).
AI.result: A systematic design pattern (the invariant compiler) that guarantees invariant preservation by construction, yielding structure-respecting neural surrogates across scientific domains and a clean separation between physics-based constraints and learned dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Invariant-respecting neural surrogates can be systematically synthesized by compiling ODE specifications into structure-preserving architectures, enabling robust long-horizon predictions within invariant manifolds.

title: 3D-LLDM: Label-Guided 3D Latent Diffusion Model for Improving High-Resolution Synthetic MR Imaging in Hepatic Structure Segmentation
summary: Deep learning and generative models are advancing rapidly, with synthetic data increasingly being integrated into training pipelines for downstream analysis tasks. However, in medical imaging, their adoption remains constrained by the scarcity of reliable annotated datasets. To address this limitation, we propose 3D-LLDM, a label-guided 3D latent diffusion model that generates high-quality synthetic magnetic resonance (MR) volumes with corresponding anatomical segmentation masks. Our approach uses hepatobiliary phase MR images enhanced with the Gd-EOB-DTPA contrast agent to derive structural masks for the liver, portal vein, hepatic vein, and hepatocellular carcinoma, which then guide volumetric synthesis through a ControlNet-based architecture. Trained on 720 real clinical hepatobiliary phase MR scans from Samsung Medical Center, 3D-LLDM achieves a Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) of 28.31, improving over GANs by 70.9% and over state-of-the-art diffusion baselines by 26.7%. When used for data augmentation, the synthetic volumes improve hepatocellular carcinoma segmentation by up to 11.153% Dice score across five CNN architectures.

AI.tldr: A label-guided 3D latent diffusion model, 3D-LLDM, generates synthetic multi-label hepatobiliary MR volumes with corresponding segmentation masks using ControlNet-guided synthesis based on masked liver and vessels from Gd-EOB-DTPA-enhanced MRI; trained on 720 scans, yielding FID 28.31 and improving segmentation Dice by up to 11.153% across five CNNs; demonstrates efficacy for data augmentation but evaluated on a single-center dataset.
AI.motivation: Medical imaging suffers from scarce annotated data. Generating labeled 3D MR volumes can augment training for downstream tasks like segmentation, provided synthetic data preserves anatomical structure and segmentation masks.
AI.method: 3D-LLDM uses hepatobiliary phase MR images with Gd-EOB-DTPA to derive structural masks for liver, portal vein, hepatic vein, and hepatocellular carcinoma. These masks guide volumetric synthesis through a ControlNet-based 3D latent diffusion model, trained on 720 real scans.
AI.result: Achieves Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) of 28.31, outperforming GANs by 70.9% and state-of-the-art diffusion baselines by 26.7%. When used for augmentation, synthetic volumes improve hepatocellular carcinoma segmentation Dice by up to 11.153% across five CNN architectures.
AI.conclusion: Label-guided 3D diffusion is effective for producing high-quality, annotated synthetic MRI volumes, enabling meaningful data augmentation for segmentation; results indicate strong performance gains but are based on a single-institution dataset and may require broader validation.

title: Off-Policy Safe Reinforcement Learning with Constrained Optimistic Exploration
comment: 21 pages, 9 figures, accepted by ICLR 2026 poster
summary: When safety is formulated as a limit of cumulative cost, safe reinforcement learning (RL) aims to learn policies that maximize return subject to the cost constraint in data collection and deployment. Off-policy safe RL methods, although offering high sample efficiency, suffer from constraint violations due to cost-agnostic exploration and estimation bias in cumulative cost. To address this issue, we propose Constrained Optimistic eXploration Q-learning (COX-Q), an off-policy safe RL algorithm that integrates cost-bounded online exploration and conservative offline distributional value learning. First, we introduce a novel cost-constrained optimistic exploration strategy that resolves gradient conflicts between reward and cost in the action space and adaptively adjusts the trust region to control the training cost. Second, we adopt truncated quantile critics to stabilize the cost value learning. Quantile critics also quantify epistemic uncertainty to guide exploration. Experiments on safe velocity, safe navigation, and autonomous driving tasks demonstrate that COX-Q achieves high sample efficiency, competitive test safety performance, and controlled data collection cost. The results highlight COX-Q as a promising RL method for safety-critical applications.

AI.tldr: COX-Q is an off-policy safe RL algorithm that combines cost-bounded optimistic exploration with truncated distributional value learning to balance reward maximization with cost constraints, achieving high sample efficiency and safety in safety-critical tasks.
AI.motivation: To address constraint violations in safe RL caused by cost-agnostic exploration and estimation bias in cumulative cost, aiming for efficient learning under safety constraints.
AI.method: Constrained Optimistic eXploration Q-learning (COX-Q) integrates cost-bounded online exploration with conservative offline distributional value learning. It features (1) a cost-constrained optimistic exploration strategy that resolves gradient conflicts between reward and cost and adaptively adjusts the trust region to manage training cost, and (2) truncated quantile critics to stabilize cost-value learning and quantify epistemic uncertainty to guide exploration.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on safe velocity, safe navigation, and autonomous driving tasks shows COX-Q achieves high sample efficiency, competitive test safety, and controlled data collection cost.
AI.conclusion: COX-Q is a promising RL method for safety-critical applications due to its combination of optimistic exploration under cost constraints and distributional, uncertainty-aware value learning.

title: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for predicting highest priority functional group in organic molecules
summary: Our work addresses the problem of predicting the highest priority functional group present in an organic molecule. Functional Groups are groups of bound atoms that determine the physical and chemical properties of organic molecules. In the presence of multiple functional groups, the dominant functional group determines the compound's properties. Fourier-transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) is a commonly used spectroscopic method for identifying the presence or absence of functional groups within a compound. We propose the use of a Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to predict the highest priority functional group from the Fourier-transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of the organic molecule. We have compared our model with other previously applied Machine Learning (ML) method Support Vector Machine (SVM) and reasoned why CNN outperforms it.

AI.tldr: A CNN-based approach on FTIR spectra predicts the highest-priority functional group, outperforming SVM baselines.
AI.motivation: To automate and improve accuracy in identifying dominant functional groups from FTIR spectra, since the dominant FG governs molecular properties.
AI.method: Train a deep Convolutional Neural Network on FTIR spectral data to classify the highest-priority functional group; compare performance with a Support Vector Machine baseline.
AI.result: CNN achieves superior performance relative to SVM; provides justification for why deep learning is advantageous for FTIR-based FG classification.
AI.conclusion: Deep CNN on FTIR spectra is effective for determining the dominant functional group, supporting the use of deep learning for spectroscopic FG identification.

title: See, Remember, Explore: A Benchmark and Baselines for Streaming Spatial Reasoning
summary: Spatial understanding is fundamental for embodied agents, yet most spatial VLMs and benchmarks remain offline-evaluating post-hoc QA over pre-recorded inputs and overlooking two crucial deployment-critical requirements: long-horizon streaming inference and active perception when the current view is insufficient. To address this gap, we introduce S3-Bench, a benchmark suite for streaming spatial question answering with active exploration, where queries are temporally grounded to specific timestamps and must be answered using only observations available up to that moment. S3-Bench adopts a dual-domain design, combining a scalable simulator with controllable trajectories and exploration actions, and real-world streaming videos that capture practical sensing artifacts for rigorous generalization evaluation. Overall, it spans 10K+ scenes and 26K+ trajectories, with dedicated training (S3-Train) and evaluation (S3-Eval) splits. We further propose AMF-VLM, which supports streaming spatial reasoning under bounded computing via (i) memory folding, which compresses long-horizon observations into compact structured memory, and (ii) active exploration, which outputs explicit actions (e.g. move/rotate/scan) to acquire missing evidence before answering. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, compared to models using identical training data, our approach yields improvements of 8.8% and 13.3% on the simulated and real splits of S3-Eval, respectively, while maintaining competitive transferability to standard spatial benchmarks.

AI.tldr: S3-Bench introduces streaming spatial QA with active exploration and AMF-VLM for bounded computing, improving streaming performance and enabling long-horizon online inference.
AI.motivation: Addresses lack of deployment-critical streaming and active perception in existing spatial VLM benchmarks that rely on offline, post-hoc QA over pre-recorded inputs.
AI.method: Dual-domain benchmark (simulator with controllable trajectories and real-world streaming videos) plus AMF-VLM with memory folding and explicit action planning for information gathering; 10K+ scenes, 26K+ trajectories; separate S3-Train and S3-Eval splits.
AI.result: 8.8% improvement on simulated S3-Eval and 13.3% on real S3-Eval, outperforming baseline models with identical training data; maintains competitive transferability to standard spatial benchmarks.
