AI.conclusion: S3-Bench and AMF-VLM advance streaming spatial reasoning under resource constraints, enabling long-horizon, active perception in both synthetic and real data, with strong generalization potential.

title: SLAT-Phys: Fast Material Property Field Prediction from Structured 3D Latents
summary: Estimating the material property field of 3D assets is critical for physics-based simulation, robotics, and digital twin generation. Existing vision-based approaches are either too expensive and slow or rely on 3D information. We present SLAT-Phys, an end-to-end method that predicts spatially varying material property fields of 3D assets directly from a single RGB image without explicit 3D reconstruction. Our approach leverages spatially organised latent features from a pretrained 3D asset generation model that encodes rich geometry and semantic prior, and trains a lightweight neural decoder to estimate Young's modulus, density, and Poisson's ratio. The coarse volumetric layout and semantic cues of the latent representation about object geometry and appearance enable accurate material estimation. Our experiments demonstrate that our method provides competitive accuracy in predicting continuous material parameters when compared against prior approaches, while significantly reducing computation time. In particular, SLAT-Phys requires only 9.9 seconds per object on an NVIDIA RTXA5000 GPU and avoids reconstruction and voxelization preprocessing. This results in 120x speedup compared to prior methods and enables faster material property estimation from a single image.

AI.tldr: SLAT-Phys estimates spatially varying material properties of 3D assets from a single RGB image without 3D reconstruction by leveraging latent geometry/semantic priors from a pretrained 3D asset generator and a lightweight decoder; achieves competitive accuracy with ~9.9s per object on RTX A5000 and ~120x speedup over prior methods, removing reconstruction/voxelization preprocessing.
AI.motivation: Material-property fields are crucial for physics-based tasks; current vision-based methods are either computationally expensive or require explicit 3D information, hindering rapid material estimation from images.
AI.method: Use spatially organized latent features from a pretrained 3D asset generation model that captures geometry and semantic priors. Train a lightweight neural decoder to map these features to continuous material parameters (Young's modulus, density, Poisson's ratio). Rely on the latent's coarse volumetric layout and semantic cues to guide estimation, enabling end-to-end inference from a single RGB image without explicit 3D reconstruction or voxelization.
AI.result: The method achieves competitive accuracy compared with prior approaches for predicting continuous material parameters and significantly reduces computation time, delivering ~9.9 seconds per object on an RTX A5000 and ~120x speedup over previous methods by avoiding reconstruction and voxelization preprocessing.
AI.conclusion: Leaning on latent priors from a 3D asset generator enables fast, accurate material-property estimation from a 2D image without 3D reconstruction, highlighting the effectiveness of geometry-semantic priors in accelerating material inference for digital twins, robotics, and simulations.

title: Can VLMs Reason Robustly? A Neuro-Symbolic Investigation
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have been applied to a wide range of reasoning tasks, yet it remains unclear whether they can reason robustly under distribution shifts. In this paper, we study covariate shifts in which the perceptual input distribution changes while the underlying prediction rules do not. To investigate this question, we consider visual deductive reasoning tasks, where a model is required to answer a query given an image and logical rules defined over the object concepts in the image. Empirically, we find that VLMs fine-tuned through gradient-based end-to-end training can achieve high in-distribution accuracy but fail to generalize under such shifts, suggesting that fine-tuning does not reliably induce the underlying reasoning function. This motivates a neuro-symbolic perspective that decouples perception from reasoning. However, we further observe that recent neuro-symbolic approaches that rely on black-box components for reasoning can still exhibit inconsistent robustness across tasks. To address this issue, we propose VLC, a neuro-symbolic method that combines VLM-based concept recognition with circuit-based symbolic reasoning. In particular, task rules are compiled into a symbolic program, specifically a circuit, which executes the rules exactly over the object concepts recognized by the VLM. Experiments on three visual deductive reasoning tasks with distinct rule sets show that VLC consistently achieves strong performance under covariate shifts, highlighting its ability to support robust reasoning.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic approach (VLC) that couples VLM-based concept recognition with circuit-based symbolic reasoning improves robustness to covariate shifts in visual deductive reasoning tasks, outperforming end-to-end fine-tuned VLMs and other black-box neuro-symbolic methods.
AI.motivation: Assess whether VLMs' in-distribution success generalizes under perceptual distribution shifts; reveal limitations of end-to-end fine-tuning for reasoning, motivate decoupling perception from reasoning; address robustness concerns in existing neuro-symbolic methods.
AI.method: Propose VLC: recognize object concepts with VLM; compile task rules into a symbolic circuit; execute circuit over recognized concepts; evaluate on three visual deductive reasoning tasks with distinct rule sets; compare with end-to-end fine-tuned VLMs and black-box neuro-symbolic approaches.
AI.result: VLC consistently achieves strong performance under covariate shifts across all three tasks, showing robust reasoning when perception changes; outperforms baseline end-to-end fine-tuned models and other neuro-symbolic methods in these settings.
AI.conclusion: Grounding reasoning in explicit, circuit-based symbolic computation over perceptual concepts yields robust performance under distribution shifts; supports a neuro-symbolic paradigm that separates perception and symbolic reasoning for reliable visual reasoning.

title: MLE-UVAD: Minimal Latent Entropy Autoencoder for Fully Unsupervised Video Anomaly Detection
comment: Submitted to ECCV 2026. 18 pages, 8 figures. Includes supplementary material
summary: In this paper, we address the challenging problem of single-scene, fully unsupervised video anomaly detection (VAD), where raw videos containing both normal and abnormal events are used directly for training and testing without any labels. This differs sharply from prior work that either requires extensive labeling (fully or weakly supervised) or depends on normal-only videos (one-class classification), which are vulnerable to distribution shifts and contamination. We propose an entropy-guided autoencoder that detects anomalies through reconstruction error by reconstructing normal frames well while making anomalies reconstruct poorly. The key idea is to combine the standard reconstruction loss with a novel Minimal Latent Entropy (MLE) loss in the autoencoder. Reconstruction loss alone maps normal and abnormal inputs to distinct latent clusters due to their inherent differences, but also risks reconstructing anomalies too well to detect. Therefore, MLE loss addresses this by minimizing the entropy of latent embeddings, encouraging them to concentrate around high-density regions. Since normal frames dominate the raw video, sparse anomalous embeddings are pulled into the normal cluster, so the decoder emphasizes normal patterns and produces poor reconstructions for anomalies. This dual-loss design produces a clear reconstruction gap that enables effective anomaly detection. Extensive experiments on two widely used benchmarks and a challenging self-collected driving dataset demonstrate that our method achieves robust and superior performance over baselines.

AI.tldr: A fully unsupervised single-scene VAD method based on an entropy-guided autoencoder with a dual loss (reconstruction plus Minimal Latent Entropy) to separate normal and anomalous frames, pulling anomalies toward normal latent regions due to normal-frame dominance; achieves robust, superior performance on standard benchmarks and a driving dataset.
AI.motivation: Tackle video anomaly detection without labels and without relying on normal-only training data. Conventional reconstruction-based methods may still reconstruct anomalies well or fail under distribution shifts/contamination. Introducing latent entropy minimization aims to pull anomalous latent embeddings toward high-density normal regions, leveraging the predominance of normal frames to improve detection.
AI.method: Train an autoencoder with two losses: (1) standard reconstruction loss to ensure normal frames are well reconstructed; (2) Minimal Latent Entropy (MLE) loss that minimizes entropy of latent embeddings, encouraging clustering around high-density regions (normal patterns). This draws anomalous embeddings toward the normal cluster, causing poor reconstructions for anomalies and creating a reconstruction gap that enables detection. The approach is fully unsupervised and applicable to single-scene videos.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on two standard benchmarks plus a self-collected driving dataset shows robust and superior anomaly detection performance compared with baselines, indicating effective unsupervised VAD under single-scene conditions.
AI.conclusion: The dual-loss framework combines reconstruction fidelity with latent entropy minimization to align latent representations toward normal patterns in a fully unsupervised setting, producing a clear reconstruction gap that improves anomaly detection in single-scene VAD.

title: CoordLight: Learning Decentralized Coordination for Network-Wide Traffic Signal Control
comment: \c{opyright} 20XX IEEE. Personal use of this material is permitted. Permission from IEEE must be obtained for all other uses, in any current or future media, including reprinting/republishing this material for advertising or promotional purposes, creating new collective works, for resale or redistribution to servers or lists, or reuse of any copyrighted component of this work in other works
summary: Adaptive traffic signal control (ATSC) is crucial in alleviating congestion, maximizing throughput and promoting sustainable mobility in ever-expanding cities. Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) has recently shown significant potential in addressing complex traffic dynamics, but the intricacies of partial observability and coordination in decentralized environments still remain key challenges in formulating scalable and efficient control strategies. To address these challenges, we present CoordLight, a MARL-based framework designed to improve intra-neighborhood traffic by enhancing decision-making at individual junctions (agents), as well as coordination with neighboring agents, thereby scaling up to network-level traffic optimization. Specifically, we introduce the Queue Dynamic State Encoding (QDSE), a novel state representation based on vehicle queuing models, which strengthens the agents' capability to analyze, predict, and respond to local traffic dynamics. We further propose an advanced MARL algorithm, named Neighbor-aware Policy Optimization (NAPO). It integrates an attention mechanism that discerns the state and action dependencies among adjacent agents, aiming to facilitate more coordinated decision-making, and to improve policy learning updates through robust advantage calculation. This enables agents to identify and prioritize crucial interactions with influential neighbors, thus enhancing the targeted coordination and collaboration among agents. Through comprehensive evaluations against state-of-the-art traffic signal control methods over three real-world traffic datasets composed of up to 196 intersections, we empirically show that CoordLight consistently exhibits superior performance across diverse traffic networks with varying traffic flows. The code is available at https://github.com/marmotlab/CoordLight

AI.tldr: CoordLight is a MARL-based traffic signal control framework that combines Queue Dynamic State Encoding (QDSE) and a Neighbor-aware Policy Optimization (NAPO) with attention to neighboring intersections, achieving superior network-level performance on large real-world datasets (up to 196 intersections).
AI.motivation: Address partial observability and coordination challenges in decentralized MARL for adaptive traffic signal control, aiming to improve both per-intersection decisions and coordination with neighboring junctions to enable scalable network-wide optimization.
AI.method: Introduce QDSE, a state representation based on vehicle queuing models, to better capture local traffic dynamics; develop NAPO, an attention-based MARL algorithm that models state/action dependencies among adjacent agents to enhance coordinated policy learning and robust advantage estimation; evaluate on three real-world datasets with up to 196 intersections; provide open-source code.
AI.result: CoordLight consistently outperforms state-of-the-art ATSC methods across networks with varying traffic flows on real-world data, demonstrating scalability and effectiveness in large urban networks.
AI.conclusion: A scalable MARL framework for adaptive traffic signal control that combines a queue-aware state encoding with neighbor-aware attention-based coordination, delivering improved network-level performance; code is publicly available.

title: HDPO: Hybrid Distillation Policy Optimization via Privileged Self-Distillation
summary: Large language models trained with reinforcement learning (RL) for mathematical reasoning face a fundamental challenge: on problems the model cannot solve at all - "cliff" prompts - the RL gradient vanishes entirely, preventing any learning signal from reaching these failure modes. We introduce Hybrid Distillation Policy Optimization (HDPO), which augments standard RL with privileged self-distillation targeting cliff prompts. On each training step, HDPO identifies prompts where all rollouts fail, generates privileged rollouts by providing the model with ground-truth information, filters for correct solutions, and distills the teacher's token-level distribution into the student. Because teacher and student share the same weights - differing only in their input - the realizability gap is provably bounded, unlike cross-model distillation. We prove that R=1 filtered privileged generation recovers the optimal KL-regularized RL policy in the hard-threshold limit. Experiments on OpenMathInstruct-2 with Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B-Instruct show that HDPO consistently improves coverage metrics (pass@4 by +0.8-1.1%, pass@8 by +0.4-1.7%) while maintaining greedy accuracy, with the distillation weight lambda providing direct control over the exploration-exploitation tradeoff.

AI.tldr: HDPO enhances RL-based mathematical reasoning by privileged self-distillation on cliff prompts, sharing weights to bound realizability gap; improves coverage metrics with controlled exploration via lambda.
AI.motivation: RL for math reasoning suffers gradient vanishing on cliff prompts where no solution is found, hindering learning of failure modes.
AI.method: HDPO identifies fully failing prompts, creates privileged rollouts using ground-truth, selects correct solutions, and distills the teacher's token distribution into the student; teacher and student share weights; theoretical result with R=1; hyperparameter lambda controls exploration-exploitation.
AI.result: On OpenMathInstruct-2 with Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B-Instruct, HDPO improves pass@4 by 0.8–1.1% and pass@8 by 0.4–1.7% without sacrificing greedy accuracy; provides a controlled mechanism for balancing exploration via lambda.
AI.conclusion: Privileged self-distillation in HDPO addresses the cliff prompt problem in RL for mathematical reasoning, offering both theoretical bound and empirical gains; the approach leverages identical model weights to bound realizability gap and uses lambda to tune the tradeoff.

title: EnvSocial-Diff: A Diffusion-Based Crowd Simulation Model with Environmental Conditioning and Individual-Group Interaction
summary: Modeling realistic pedestrian trajectories requires accounting for both social interactions and environmental context, yet most existing approaches largely emphasize social dynamics. We propose \textbf{EnvSocial-Diff}: a diffusion-based crowd simulation model informed by social physics and augmented with environmental conditioning and individual--group interaction. Our structured environmental conditioning module explicitly encodes obstacles, objects of interest, and lighting levels, providing interpretable signals that capture scene constraints and attractors. In parallel, the individual--group interaction module goes beyond individual-level modeling by capturing both fine-grained interpersonal relations and group-level conformity through a graph-based design. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that EnvSocial-Diff outperforms the latest state-of-the-art methods, underscoring the importance of explicit environmental conditioning and multi-level social interaction for realistic crowd simulation. Code is here: https://github.com/zqyq/EnvSocial-Diff.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based crowd simulator (EnvSocial-Diff) that integrates environmental conditioning and multi-level social interactions to produce realistic pedestrian trajectories, achieving state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: Modeling realistic pedestrian trajectories requires accounting for both social dynamics and environmental context; existing methods often emphasize social interactions and neglect scene constraints and individual-group dynamics.
AI.method: EnvSocial-Diff combines two modules: (1) a structured environmental conditioning module that encodes obstacles, objects of interest, and lighting levels to provide interpretable scene signals; (2) a graph-based individual–group interaction module that captures fine-grained interpersonal relations and group-level conformity; the diffusion-based generation framework integrates these cues for crowd simulation.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple benchmark datasets show EnvSocial-Diff outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, highlighting the benefits of explicit environmental conditioning and multi-level social interaction.
AI.conclusion: Explicit environmental conditioning and multi-level social interactions are crucial for realistic crowd simulation; the approach achieves superior performance and provides interpretable, controllable signals. Code is publicly available.

title: Towards Safe Learning-Based Non-Linear Model Predictive Control through Recurrent Neural Network Modeling
summary: The practical deployment of nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC) is often limited by online computation: solving a nonlinear program at high control rates can be expensive on embedded hardware, especially when models are complex or horizons are long. Learning-based NMPC approximations shift this computation offline but typically demand large expert datasets and costly training. We propose Sequential-AMPC, a sequential neural policy that generates MPC candidate control sequences by sharing parameters across the prediction horizon. For deployment, we wrap the policy in a safety-augmented online evaluation and fallback mechanism, yielding Safe Sequential-AMPC. Compared to a naive feedforward policy baseline across several benchmarks, Sequential-AMPC requires substantially fewer expert MPC rollouts and yields candidate sequences with higher feasibility rates and improved closed-loop safety. On high-dimensional systems, it also exhibits better learning dynamics and performance in fewer epochs while maintaining stable validation improvement where the feedforward baseline can stagnate.

AI.tldr: Sequential-AMPC is a sequential neural policy that shares parameters across the MPC horizon to generate candidate control sequences, with a safety-augmented online evaluation and fallback (Safe Sequential-AMPC). It reduces required expert rollouts, improves feasibility and closed-loop safety, and demonstrates better learning dynamics on high-dimensional systems compared to a naive feedforward baseline.
AI.motivation: NMPC is powerful but computationally intensive for online control, especially on embedded hardware. Learning-based approximations reduce online computation but require extensive expert data and training. The paper aims to reduce data requirements and improve practical deployment by exploiting horizon-wide parameter sharing and a safety wrapper.
AI.method: Introduce Sequential-AMPC: a neural policy that sequentially outputs MPC candidate sequences with shared parameters across the horizon. Train using expert MPC rollouts (offline). At deployment, use a safety-augmented online evaluation with a fallback mechanism to form Safe Sequential-AMPC. Compare against a naive feedforward baseline across benchmarks, including high-dimensional systems.
AI.result: Sequential-AMPC uses substantially fewer expert MPC rollouts. It yields candidate sequences with higher feasibility rates and better closed-loop safety. On high-dimensional systems, it shows faster learning (fewer epochs) and more stable validation improvements where feedforward baselines stagnate.
AI.conclusion: Sequential-AMPC provides data-efficient learning of MPC sequences via horizon-shared policy, with a safety wrapper enabling reliable deployment. It improves feasibility and safety over baselines and scales favorably to high-dimensional problems, suggesting broad applicability for practical NMPC acceleration.

title: The Luna Bound Propagator for Formal Analysis of Neural Networks
summary: The parameterized CROWN analysis, a.k.a., alpha-CROWN, has emerged as a practically successful bound propagation method for neural network verification. However, existing implementations of alpha-CROWN are limited to Python, which complicates integration into existing DNN verifiers and long-term production-level systems. We introduce Luna, a new bound propagator implemented in C++. Luna supports Interval Bound Propagation, the CROWN analysis, and the alpha-CROWN analysis over a general computational graph. We describe the architecture of Luna and show that it is competitive with the state-of-the-art alpha-CROWN implementation in terms of both bound tightness and computational efficiency on benchmarks from VNN-COMP 2025.

AI.tldr: Luna is a C++ bound propagator implementing Interval Bound Propagation (IBP), CROWN, and alpha-CROWN for general computational graphs, achieving competitive bound tightness and computational efficiency compared with the Python alpha-CROWN implementation on VNN-COMP 2025 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of Python-only bound propagation tools by delivering a fast, reusable, and integrable C++ implementation that can be deployed within verifiers and production systems.
AI.method: Develop Luna to support IBP, CROWN, and alpha-CROWN over a general computational graph; describe its architecture; benchmark against the state-of-the-art Python alpha-CROWN on VNN-COMP 2025 datasets to assess bound tightness and performance.
AI.result: Luna demonstrates competitive bound tightness and computational efficiency relative to the Python alpha-CROWN implementation on the evaluated benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A practical, high-performance C++ bound propagator that facilitates integration into DNN verifiers and production pipelines, enabling broader adoption of alpha-CROWN-style bound propagation.

title: BioVITA: Biological Dataset, Model, and Benchmark for Visual-Textual-Acoustic Alignment
summary: Understanding animal species from multimodal data poses an emerging challenge at the intersection of computer vision and ecology. While recent biological models, such as BioCLIP, have demonstrated strong alignment between images and textual taxonomic information for species identification, the integration of the audio modality remains an open problem. We propose BioVITA, a novel visual-textual-acoustic alignment framework for biological applications. BioVITA involves (i) a training dataset, (ii) a representation model, and (iii) a retrieval benchmark. First, we construct a large-scale training dataset comprising 1.3 million audio clips and 2.3 million images, covering 14,133 species annotated with 34 ecological trait labels. Second, building upon BioCLIP2, we introduce a two-stage training framework to effectively align audio representations with visual and textual representations. Third, we develop a cross-modal retrieval benchmark that covers all possible directional retrieval across the three modalities (i.e., image-to-audio, audio-to-text, text-to-image, and their reverse directions), with three taxonomic levels: Family, Genus, and Species. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model learns a unified representation space that captures species-level semantics beyond taxonomy, advancing multimodal biodiversity understanding. The project page is available at: https://dahlian00.github.io/BioVITA_Page/

AI.tldr: A multimodal framework (BioVITA) that adds audio to the BioCLIP visual-text alignment for species identification, with a large-scale audio-visual-text dataset, a two-stage training strategy, and a cross-modal retrieval benchmark across three modalities at multiple taxonomic levels, achieving a unified species-level representation.
AI.motivation: Extend multimodal biodiversity modeling beyond image and text by incorporating audio, enabling robust cross-modal retrieval and richer species semantics.
AI.method: (1) Dataset: 1.3M audio clips, 2.3M images, covering 14,133 species and 34 ecological trait labels. (2) Representation model: build upon BioCLIP2 with a two-stage training framework to align audio representations with visual and textual modalities. (3) Benchmark: develop a cross-modal retrieval benchmark enabling image↔audio↔text retrieval in all directions, across Family, Genus, Species levels.
AI.result: Experiments show a unified multimodal representation space that captures species-level semantics beyond taxonomic information, advancing multimodal biodiversity understanding.
AI.conclusion: BioVITA provides a comprehensive framework—including dataset, model, and benchmark—for unified audio-visual-textual species understanding, demonstrating effective cross-modal alignment and retrieval across taxonomic levels.

title: Latent-WAM: Latent World Action Modeling for End-to-End Autonomous Driving
summary: We introduce Latent-WAM, an efficient end-to-end autonomous driving framework that achieves strong trajectory planning through spatially-aware and dynamics-informed latent world representations. Existing world-model-based planners suffer from inadequately compressed representations, limited spatial understanding, and underutilized temporal dynamics, resulting in sub-optimal planning under constrained data and compute budgets. Latent-WAM addresses these limitations with two core modules: a Spatial-Aware Compressive World Encoder (SCWE) that distills geometric knowledge from a foundation model and compresses multi-view images into compact scene tokens via learnable queries, and a Dynamic Latent World Model (DLWM) that employs a causal Transformer to autoregressively predict future world status conditioned on historical visual and motion representations. Extensive experiments on NAVSIM v2 and HUGSIM demonstrate new state-of-the-art results: 89.3 EPDMS on NAVSIM v2 and 28.9 HD-Score on HUGSIM, surpassing the best prior perception-free method by 3.2 EPDMS with significantly less training data and a compact 104M-parameter model.

AI.tldr: Latent-WAM is an end-to-end autonomous driving framework that uses a spatially-aware latent world representation for efficient trajectory planning, featuring a Spatial-Aware Compressive World Encoder (SCWE) and a Dynamic Latent World Model (DLWM) with a causal Transformer; achieves state-of-the-art results on NAVSIM v2 and HUGSIM with a compact 104M model and less training data.
AI.motivation: Current world-model-based planners struggle due to inadequately compressed representations, limited spatial understanding, and underutilized temporal dynamics, especially under constrained data and compute budgets. There is a need for compact, spatially-aware, and temporally informed latent representations to improve planning performance.
AI.method: SCWE distills geometric knowledge from a foundation model and compresses multi-view images into compact scene tokens via learnable queries. DLWM employs a causal Transformer to autoregressively predict future world status conditioned on historical visual and motion representations, enabling end-to-end planning.
AI.result: On NAVSIM v2 and HUGSIM, Latent-WAM achieves 89.3 EPDMS and 28.9 HD-Score, respectively, outperforming the best prior perception-free method by 3.2 EPDMS, with significantly less training data and a compact 104M-parameter model.
AI.conclusion: Latent-WAM demonstrates that combining spatially-aware latent representations with a dynamics-informed latent world model yields strong trajectory planning under limited data and compute, achieving state-of-the-art results on two benchmarks with a lightweight model.

title: Towards Real-World Document Parsing via Realistic Scene Synthesis and Document-Aware Training
summary: Document parsing has recently advanced with multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that directly map document images to structured outputs. Traditional cascaded pipelines depend on precise layout analysis and often fail under casually captured or non-standard conditions. Although end-to-end approaches mitigate this dependency, they still exhibit repetitive, hallucinated, and structurally inconsistent predictions - primarily due to the scarcity of large-scale, high-quality full-page (document-level) end-to-end parsing data and the lack of structure-aware training strategies. To address these challenges, we propose a data-training co-design framework for robust end-to-end document parsing. A Realistic Scene Synthesis strategy constructs large-scale, structurally diverse full-page end-to-end supervision by composing layout templates with rich document elements, while a Document-Aware Training Recipe introduces progressive learning and structure-token optimization to enhance structural fidelity and decoding stability. We further build Wild-OmniDocBench, a benchmark derived from real-world captured documents for robustness evaluation. Integrated into a 1B-parameter MLLM, our method achieves superior accuracy and robustness across both scanned/digital and real-world captured scenarios. All models, data synthesis pipelines, and benchmarks will be publicly released to advance future research in document understanding.

AI.tldr: A data-training co-design framework for robust end-to-end document parsing, combining realistic scene synthesis for scale and a document-aware learning recipe for structural fidelity; introduces Wild-OmniDocBench and validates on a 1B-parameter MLLM.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of large-scale, high-quality full-page end-to-end parsing data and absence of structure-aware training strategies, which cause hallucinations and instability in end-to-end document parsing.
AI.method: 1) Realistic Scene Synthesis: compose layout templates with rich document elements to generate diverse, full-page supervision. 2) Document-Aware Training Recipe: progressive learning and structure-token optimization to improve structural fidelity and decoding stability. 3) Wild-OmniDocBench: robustness benchmark from real-world captured documents. 4) Integration into a 1B-param MLLM to validate performance.
AI.result: Outperforms cascaded pipelines and prior end-to-end approaches in accuracy and robustness across scanned/digital and real-world captures; demonstrates improved structural fidelity and decoding stability. Public release of models, data pipelines, and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed co-design framework advances robust end-to-end document parsing and provides resources (models, data pipelines, benchmarks) to foster future research in document understanding.

title: TAG: Target-Agnostic Guidance for Stable Object-Centric Inference in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision--Language--Action (VLA) policies have shown strong progress in mapping language instructions and visual observations to robotic actions, yet their reliability degrades in cluttered scenes with distractors. By analyzing failure cases, we find that many errors do not arise from infeasible motions, but from instance-level grounding failures: the policy often produces a plausible grasp trajectory that lands slightly off-target or even on the wrong object instance. To address this issue, we propose TAG (Target-Agnostic Guidance), a simple inference-time guidance mechanism that explicitly reduces distractor- and appearance-induced bias in VLA policies. Inspired by classifier-free guidance (CFG), TAG contrasts policy predictions under the original observation and an object-erased observation, and uses their difference as a residual steering signal that strengthens the influence of object evidence in the decision process. TAG does not require modifying the policy architecture and can be integrated with existing VLA policies with minimal training and inference changes. We evaluate TAG on standard manipulation benchmarks, including LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and VLABench, where it consistently improves robustness under clutter and reduces near-miss and wrong-object executions.

AI.tldr: TAG is an inference-time, CFG-inspired residual guidance for VLA policies that reduces distractor/appearance bias by comparing original vs object-erased observations, improving robustness to clutter without changing policy architecture.
AI.motivation: Instance-level grounding failures in cluttered scenes degrade VLA policy reliability; near-miss and wrong-object errors arise from misgrounding rather than infeasible motions, necessitating bias reduction toward correct object evidence.
AI.method: Introduce Target-Agnostic Guidance (TAG): at inference, run the policy on the original observation and on an object-erased observation; compute the difference as a residual steering signal (in the spirit of classifier-free guidance) and apply it to action selection to emphasize object evidence. No architecture changes required; integrates with existing VLA policies with minimal training/inference overhead.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and VLABench shows TAG consistently improves robustness in clutter, reducing near-miss and wrong-object executions across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: TAG offers a simple, plug-in inference-time technique to mitigate appearance-based distractor bias in VLA policies, enhancing grounding fidelity without retraining; its effectiveness across multiple benchmarks supports broader applicability, with opportunities for exploring erasure strategies and extension to more complex tasks.

title: Optimal Variance-Dependent Regret Bounds for Infinite-Horizon MDPs
summary:
Online reinforcement learning in infinite-horizon Markov decision processes (MDPs) remains less theoretically and algorithmically developed than its episodic counterpart, with many algorithms suffering from high ``burn-in'' costs and failing to adapt to benign instance-specific complexity. In this work, we address these shortcomings for two infinite-horizon objectives: the classical average-reward regret and the $γ$-regret. We develop a single tractable UCB-style algorithm applicable to both settings, which achieves the first optimal variance-dependent regret guarantees. Our regret bounds in both settings take the form $\tilde{O}( \sqrt{SA\,\text{Var}} + \text{lower-order terms})$, where $S,A$ are the state and action space sizes, and $\text{Var}$ captures cumulative transition variance. This implies minimax-optimal average-reward and $γ$-regret bounds in the worst case but also adapts to easier problem instances, for example yielding nearly constant regret in deterministic MDPs. Furthermore, our algorithm enjoys significantly improved lower-order terms for the average-reward setting. With prior knowledge of the optimal bias span $\Vert h^\star\Vert_\text{sp}$, our algorithm obtains lower-order terms scaling as $\Vert h^\star\Vert_\text{sp} S^2 A$, which we prove is optimal in both $\Vert h^\star\Vert_\text{sp}$ and $A$.
  Without prior knowledge, we prove that no algorithm can have lower-order terms smaller than $\Vert h^\star \Vert_\text{sp}^2 S A$, and we provide a prior-free algorithm whose lower-order terms scale as $\Vert h^\star\Vert_\text{sp}^2 S^3 A$, nearly matching this lower bound. Taken together, these results completely characterize the optimal dependence on $\Vert h^\star\Vert_\text{sp}$ in both leading and lower-order terms, and reveal a fundamental gap in what is achievable with and without prior knowledge.

AI.tldr: A single UCB-style algorithm for infinite-horizon MDPs achieving variance-dependent regret bounds O~( sqrt(S A Var) ) for both average-reward and γ-regret, with optimal leading terms and instance-adaptive lower-order terms tied to the optimal bias span ||h*||_sp. It reveals a gap between settings with and without prior knowledge of ||h*||_sp and provides near-optimal bounds in both cases.
AI.motivation: Address slow burn-in and lack of adaptivity in online RL for infinite-horizon MDPs. Unify the average-reward and γ-regret objectives and derive variance-dependent, minimax-optimal regret bounds that adapt to problem structure, particularly the bias span of the optimal value function.
AI.method: Propose a single tractable UCB-style algorithm applicable to both average-reward and γ-regret; derive variance-dependent regret bounds of Õ(√(S A Var)) with lower-order terms; establish matching lower bounds and characterize dependence on the optimal bias span ||h*||_sp; develop prior-free and prior-knowledge-dependent variants with corresponding lower-order terms.
AI.result: Achieves first optimal variance-dependent regret guarantees in both objectives with leading term Õ(√(S A Var)). Regret bounds are minimax-optimal in the worst case and adapt to easier instances (e.g., near-constant regret in deterministic MDPs). Improved lower-order terms for the average-reward setting; with prior knowledge of ||h*||_sp, lower-order terms scale as ||h*||_sp S^2 A (optimal in both ||h*||_sp and A). Without prior knowledge, any algorithm has lower-order term at least ||h*||_sp^2 S A; a prior-free algorithm achieves ||h*||_sp^2 S^3 A, nearly matching the bound.
AI.conclusion: The results fully characterize the optimal dependence on the bias-span ||h*||_sp in both leading and lower-order terms and highlight a fundamental gap between what is achievable with and without prior knowledge. The work provides a unified, variance-aware approach for infinite-horizon RL with strong theoretical guarantees.

title: FilterGS: Traversal-Free Parallel Filtering and Adaptive Shrinking for Large-Scale LoD 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting has revolutionized neural rendering with real-time performance. However, scaling this approach to large scenes using Level-of-Detail methods faces critical challenges: inefficient serial traversal consuming over 60\% of rendering time, and redundant Gaussian-tile pairs that incur unnecessary processing overhead. To address these limitations, we introduce FilterGS, featuring a parallel filtering mechanism with two complementary filters that select Gaussian elements efficiently without tree traversal. Additionally, we propose a novel GTC metric that quantifies the redundancy of Gaussian-tile key-value pairs. Based on this metric, we introduce a scene-adaptive Gaussian shrinking strategy that effectively reduces redundant pairs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FilterGS achieves state-of-the-art rendering speeds while maintaining competitive visual quality across multiple large-scale datasets. Project page: https://github.com/xenon-w/FilterGS

AI.tldr: FilterGS introduces parallel Gaussian filtering and a redundancy metric (GTC) to remove redundant Gaussian-tile pairs in large-scale 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling scene-adaptive shrinking. It achieves state-of-the-art rendering speeds with competitive visual quality across large datasets.
AI.motivation: Address the main bottlenecks in large-scale 3D Gaussian Splatting: (1) inefficient serial traversal that dominates rendering time, and (2) redundant Gaussian-tile key-value pairs causing unnecessary computation.
AI.method: Two complementary, tree-traversal-free filters perform parallel selection of Gaussian elements. A novel GTC (Gaussian-Tile Redundancy) metric quantifies redundancy of Gaussian-tile key-value pairs. Based on GTC, implement a scene-adaptive Gaussian shrinking strategy to remove or reduce redundant pairs. Evaluation on multiple large-scale datasets with a project page reference.
AI.result: FilterGS achieves state-of-the-art rendering speeds while maintaining competitive visual quality on several large-scale datasets, outperforming prior methods in speed while keeping quality on par.
AI.conclusion: FilterGS demonstrates that parallel filtering combined with an GTC-driven shrinking strategy effectively reduces redundant Gaussian-tile pairs, yielding fast and scalable neural rendering for large scenes. The approach is ready for broader adoption, as supported by a project page.

title: DreamerAD: Efficient Reinforcement Learning via Latent World Model for Autonomous Driving
summary: We introduce DreamerAD, the first latent world model framework that enables efficient reinforcement learning for autonomous driving by compressing diffusion sampling from 100 steps to 1 - achieving 80x speedup while maintaining visual interpretability. Training RL policies on real-world driving data incurs prohibitive costs and safety risks. While existing pixel-level diffusion world models enable safe imagination-based training, they suffer from multi-step diffusion inference latency (2s/frame) that prevents high-frequency RL interaction. Our approach leverages denoised latent features from video generation models through three key mechanisms: (1) shortcut forcing that reduces sampling complexity via recursive multi-resolution step compression, (2) an autoregressive dense reward model operating directly on latent representations for fine-grained credit assignment, and (3) Gaussian vocabulary sampling for GRPO that constrains exploration to physically plausible trajectories. DreamerAD achieves 87.7 EPDMS on NavSim v2, establishing state-of-the-art performance and demonstrating that latent-space RL is effective for autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: DreamerAD proposes a latent world-model framework to enable efficient RL for autonomous driving by compressing diffusion sampling from 100 steps to 1, achieving 80x speedup and maintaining interpretability.
AI.motivation: Training RL policies on real-world driving data is costly and unsafe; pixel-level diffusion world models incur high inference latency, hindering high-frequency interaction. A latent-space approach with safe imagination is pursued.
AI.method: Leverages denoised latent features from video-generation models and introduces three mechanisms: (1) shortcut forcing for recursive multi-resolution step compression; (2) autoregressive dense reward model operating directly on latent representations for fine-grained credit assignment; (3) Gaussian vocabulary sampling for GRPO to constrain exploration to physically plausible trajectories.
AI.result: Achieves 87.7 EPDMS on NavSim v2, claiming state-of-the-art performance and an 80x speedup over 100-step diffusion; demonstrates effectiveness of latent-space RL for autonomous driving.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space reinforcement learning with a latent diffusion model can deliver efficient, safe, and interpretable RL for autonomous driving, establishing DreamerAD as a leading framework in this domain.

title: GRMLR: Knowledge-Enhanced Small-Data Learning for Deep-Sea Cold Seep Stage Inference
summary: Deep-sea cold seep stage assessment has traditionally relied on costly, high-risk manned submersible operations and visual surveys of macrofauna. Although microbial communities provide a promising and more cost-effective alternative, reliable inference remains challenging because the available deep-sea dataset is extremely small ($n = 13$) relative to the microbial feature dimension ($p = 26$), making purely data-driven models highly prone to overfitting. To address this, we propose a knowledge-enhanced classification framework that incorporates an ecological knowledge graph as a structural prior. By fusing macro-microbe coupling and microbial co-occurrence patterns, the framework internalizes established ecological logic into a \underline{\textbf{G}}raph-\underline{\textbf{R}}egularized \underline{\textbf{M}}ultinomial \underline{\textbf{L}}ogistic \underline{\textbf{R}}egression (GRMLR) model, effectively constraining the feature space through a manifold penalty to ensure biologically consistent classification. Importantly, the framework removes the need for macrofauna observations at inference time: macro-microbe associations are used only to guide training, whereas prediction relies solely on microbial abundance profiles. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms standard baselines, highlighting its potential as a robust and scalable framework for deep-sea ecological assessment.

AI.tldr: A knowledge-augmented GRMLR classifier uses an ecological knowledge graph to regularize microbial features for deep-sea stage classification, enabling training with small samples and inference from microbial data alone, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Address overfitting in a high-dimensional, small-sample deep-sea microbiome dataset and remove the need for macrofauna data at inference by leveraging ecological knowledge as a prior.
AI.method: Build an ecological knowledge graph encoding macro–microbe couplings and microbial co-occurrence. Train a Graph-Regularized Multinomial Logistic Regression (GRMLR) with a manifold penalty guided by the graph. Macro–microbe associations guide training; predictions rely solely on microbial abundance.
AI.result: Significant improvement over standard baselines, demonstrating a robust, scalable framework for deep-sea ecological assessment.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge-augmented graph regularization effectively enables microbiome-based classification in data-scarce deep-sea contexts and can be extended to broader ecological assessments.

title: MMTIT-Bench: A Multilingual and Multi-Scenario Benchmark with Cognition-Perception-Reasoning Guided Text-Image Machine Translation
summary: End-to-end text-image machine translation (TIMT), which directly translates textual content in images across languages, is crucial for real-world multilingual scene understanding. Despite advances in vision-language large models (VLLMs), robustness across diverse visual scenes and low-resource languages remains underexplored due to limited evaluation resources. We present MMTIT-Bench, a human-verified multilingual and multi-scenario benchmark with 1,400 images spanning fourteen non-English and non-Chinese languages and diverse settings such as documents, scenes, and web images, enabling rigorous assessment of end-to-end TIMT. Beyond benchmarking, we study how reasoning-oriented data design improves translation. Although recent VLLMs have begun to incorporate long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, effective thinking paradigms for TIMT are still immature: existing designs either cascade parsing and translation in a sequential manner or focus on language-only reasoning, overlooking the visual cognition central to VLLMs. We propose Cognition-Perception-Reasoning for Translation (CPR-Trans), a data paradigm that integrates scene cognition, text perception, and translation reasoning within a unified reasoning process. Using a VLLM-driven data generation pipeline, CPR-Trans provides structured, interpretable supervision that aligns perception with reasoning. Experiments on 3B and 7B models show consistent gains in accuracy and interpretability. We will release MMTIT-Bench to promote the multilingual and multi-scenario TIMT research upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A multilingual TIMT benchmark (MMTIT-Bench) with 1,400 images across 14 languages and diverse settings, plus CPR-Trans, a cognition-perception-reasoning data paradigm for end-to-end TIMT using VLLMs; shows improved accuracy and interpretability on 3B/7B models; benchmark release planned.
AI.motivation: Address robustness gaps in end-to-end TIMT across diverse visual scenes and low-resource languages, and the lack of comprehensive, interpretable, reasoning-focused data designs for TIMT.
AI.method: Introduce MMTIT-Bench: a human-verified multilingual/multi-scenario image dataset (1,400 images; 14 languages; documents, scenes, web images). Propose CPR-Trans: a unified data paradigm integrating scene cognition, text perception, and translation reasoning; use a VLLM-driven data generation pipeline for structured supervision. Empirical evaluation on 3B and 7B VLLMs.
AI.result: CPR-Trans yields consistent gains in translation accuracy and interpretability across models; MMTIT-Bench enables rigorous TIMT evaluation across multiple languages and scenarios; dataset to be released publicly after acceptance.
AI.conclusion: MMTIT-Bench and CPR-Trans advance end-to-end TIMT by providing a robust multilingual, multi-scenario benchmark and a reasoning-oriented data design that aligns perception with translation, with demonstrated improvements on large language-vision models.

title: Wireless communication empowers online scheduling of partially-observable transportation multi-robot systems in a smart factory
summary: Achieving agile and reconfigurable production flows in smart factories depends on online multi-robot task assignment (MRTA), which requires online collision-free and congestion-free route scheduling of transportation multi-robot systems (T-MRS), e.g., collaborative automatic guided vehicles (AGVs). Due to the real-time operational requirements and dynamic interactions between T-MRS and production MRS, online scheduling under partial observability in dynamic factory environments remains a significant and under-explored challenge. This paper proposes a novel communication-enabled online scheduling framework that explicitly couples wireless machine-to-machine (M2M) networking with route scheduling, enabling AGVs to exchange intention information, e.g., planned routes, to overcome partial observations and assist complex computation of online scheduling. Specifically, we determine intelligent AGVs' intention and sensor data as new M2M traffic and tailor the retransmission-free multi-link transmission networking to meet real-time operation demands. This scheduling-oriented networking is then integrated with a simulated annealing-based MRTA scheme and a congestion-aware A*-based route scheduling method. The integrated communication and scheduling scheme allows AGVs to dynamically adjust collision-free and congestion-free routes with reduced computational overhead. Numerical experiments shows the impacts from wireless communication on the performance of T-MRS and suggest that the proposed integrated scheme significantly enhances scheduling efficiency compared to other baselines, even under high AGV load conditions and limited channel resources. Moreover, the results reveal that the scheduling-oriented wireless M2M communication design fundamentally differs from human-to-human communications, implying new technological opportunities in a wireless networked smart factory.

AI.tldr: Integrated wireless M2M-enabled online scheduling for MRTA in smart factories; coupling AGV intention sharing with route planning using simulated annealing MRTA and congestion-aware A*; improves real-time, collision- and congestion-free routing under partial observability.
AI.motivation: Online MRTA under partial observability and dynamic factory environments; real-time requirements and interactions between T-MRS and production MRS make scheduling challenging; existing approaches underperform due to separate handling of communication and scheduling.
AI.method: Treat AGV intention and sensor data as new M2M traffic and design a retransmission-free multi-link network to meet real-time demands; integrate this scheduling-oriented networking with a simulated annealing-based MRTA and a congestion-aware A*-based route scheduler; enables dynamic, collision-free and congestion-free route updates.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show that introducing wireless M2M impacts improves T-MRS performance; the integrated scheme significantly enhances scheduling efficiency compared with baselines, even at high AGV load and limited channel resources.
AI.conclusion: Scheduling-oriented wireless M2M design fundamentally differs from human-to-human communications, suggesting new opportunities for wireless-networked smart factories and enabling more efficient online MRTA under dynamics and partial observability.

title: Knowledge-Refined Dual Context-Aware Network for Partially Relevant Video Retrieval
summary: Retrieving partially relevant segments from untrimmed videos remains difficult due to two persistent challenges: the mismatch in information density between text and video segments, and limited attention mechanisms that overlook semantic focus and event correlations. We present KDC-Net, a Knowledge-Refined Dual Context-Aware Network that tackles these issues from both textual and visual perspectives. On the text side, a Hierarchical Semantic Aggregation module captures and adaptively fuses multi-scale phrase cues to enrich query semantics. On the video side, a Dynamic Temporal Attention mechanism employs relative positional encoding and adaptive temporal windows to highlight key events with local temporal coherence. Additionally, a dynamic CLIP-based distillation strategy, enhanced with temporal-continuity-aware refinement, ensures segment-aware and objective-aligned knowledge transfer. Experiments on PRVR benchmarks show that KDC-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, especially under low moment-to-video ratios.

AI.tldr: KDC-Net improves untrimmed video retrieval by aligning text and video via a knowledge-refined dual-context architecture including hierarchical text aggregation, dynamic temporal video attention, and CLIP-based distillation with temporal continuity.
AI.motivation: Retrieving partially relevant video segments is hampered by (1) mismatch in information density between text queries and video segments and (2) limited attention mechanisms that miss semantic focus and event correlations.
AI.method: Text: Hierarchical Semantic Aggregation to fuse multi-scale phrase cues for richer query semantics. Video: Dynamic Temporal Attention with relative positional encoding and adaptive temporal windows to emphasize key events with local coherence. Cross-modal: dynamic CLIP-based distillation with temporal-continuity-aware refinement for segment-aware knowledge transfer.
AI.result: On PRVR benchmarks, KDC-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance, especially under low moment-to-video ratios.
AI.conclusion: KDC-Net effectively addresses cross-modal retrieval challenges by refining textual and visual representations and leveraging robust, temporally aware distillation, yielding improved robustness when moment density is low.

title: Kirchhoff-Inspired Neural Networks for Evolving High-Order Perception
summary: Deep learning architectures are fundamentally inspired by neuroscience, particularly the structure of the brain's sensory pathways, and have achieved remarkable success in learning informative data representations. Although these architectures mimic the communication mechanisms of biological neurons, their strategies for information encoding and transmission are fundamentally distinct. Biological systems depend on dynamic fluctuations in membrane potential; by contrast, conventional deep networks optimize weights and biases by adjusting the strengths of inter-neural connections, lacking a systematic mechanism to jointly characterize the interplay among signal intensity, coupling structure, and state evolution. To tackle this limitation, we propose the Kirchhoff-Inspired Neural Network (KINN), a state-variable-based network architecture constructed based on Kirchhoff's current law. KINN derives numerically stable state updates from fundamental ordinary differential equations, enabling the explicit decoupling and encoding of higher-order evolutionary components within a single layer while preserving physical consistency, interpretability, and end-to-end trainability. Extensive experiments on partial differential equation (PDE) solving and ImageNet image classification validate that KINN outperforms state-of-the-art existing methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces Kirchhoff-Inspired Neural Network (KINN): a state-variable network using Kirchhoff's current law for numerically stable, interpretable layer updates that decouple signal intensity, coupling structure, and state evolution; shows improvements in PDE solving and ImageNet classification.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between biology-inspired neural dynamics and conventional weight-based networks by explicitly modeling dynamic state evolution and inter-component interactions within a layer for physical consistency and interpretability.
AI.method: Proposes a state-variable network (KINN) derived from Kirchhoff's current law, yielding numerically stable ODE-based state updates that decouple higher-order evolutionary components; ensures end-to-end trainability and physical consistency; validates on PDE solving and ImageNet classification.
AI.result: KINN outperforms state-of-the-art methods on both PDE solving tasks and ImageNet image classification.
AI.conclusion: KINN provides a physically coherent, interpretable, and trainable architecture that explicitly decouples signal intensity, coupling structure, and state evolution within each layer, with strong empirical performance and potential broad applicability.

title: Latent Bias Alignment for High-Fidelity Diffusion Inversion in Real-World Image Reconstruction and Manipulation
summary: Recent research has shown that text-to-image diffusion models are capable of generating high-quality images guided by text prompts. But can they be used to generate or approximate real-world images from the seed noise? This is known as the diffusion inversion problem, which serves as a fundamental building block for bridging diffusion models and real-world scenarios. However, existing diffusion inversion methods often suffer from low reconstruction quality or weak robustness. Two major challenges need to be carefully addressed: (1) the misalignment between the inversion and generation trajectories during the diffusion process, and (2) the mismatch between the diffusion inversion process and the VQ autoencoder (VQAE) reconstruction. To address these challenges, we introduce a latent bias vector at each inversion step, which is learned to reduce the misalignment between inversion and generation trajectories. We refer to this strategy as Latent Bias Optimization (LBO). Furthermore, we perform an approximate joint optimization of the diffusion inversion and VQAE reconstruction processes by learning to adjust the image latent representation, which serves as the connecting interface between them. We refer to this technique as Image Latent Boosting (ILB). Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves the image reconstruction quality of the diffusion model, as well as the performance of downstream tasks, including image editing and rare concept generation.

AI.tldr: Introduces Latent Bias Optimization (LBO) and Image Latent Boosting (ILB) to improve diffusion inversion by aligning inversion/generation trajectories and reconciling with VQAE reconstructions, yielding better image reconstruction and enabling effective downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Bridging diffusion models with real-world imagery requires accurate inversion. Current inversion methods suffer from trajectory misalignment and VQAE reconstruction mismatch, hindering reconstruction quality and downstream tasks.
AI.method: LBO adds a learnable latent bias vector at each inversion step to align inversion and synthesis trajectories. ILB performs approximate joint optimization of diffusion inversion and VQAE reconstruction by learning to adjust the image latent representation that connects the two processes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show significant gains in reconstruction quality and improved performance on downstream tasks such as image editing and rare concept generation.
AI.conclusion: The combination of LBO and ILB substantially improves diffusion inversion quality and enhances downstream capabilities, enabling more faithful reconstructions and versatile image editing with diffusion models.

title: Transcending Classical Neural Network Boundaries: A Quantum-Classical Synergistic Paradigm for Seismic Data Processing
summary: In recent years, a number of neural-network (NN) methods have exhibited good performance in seismic data processing, such as denoising, interpolation, and frequency-band extension. However, these methods rely on stacked perceptrons and standard activation functions, which imposes a bottleneck on the representational capacity of deep-learning models, making it difficult to capture the complex and non-stationary dynamics of seismic wavefields. Different from the classical perceptron-stacked NNs which are fundamentally confined to real-valued Euclidean spaces, the quantum NNs leverage the exponential state space of quantum mechanics to map the features into high-dimensional Hilbert spaces, transcending the representational boundary of classical NNs. Based on this insight, we propose a quantum-classical synergistic generative adversarial network (QC-GAN) for seismic data processing, serving as the first application of quantum NNs in seismic exploration. In QC-GAN, a quantum pathway is used to exploit the high-order feature correlations, while the convolutional pathway specializes in extracting the waveform structures of seismic wavefields. Furthermore, we design a QC feature complementarity loss to enforce the feature orthogonality in the proposed QC-GAN. This novel loss function can ensure that the two pathways encode non-overlapping information to enrich the capacity of feature representation. On the whole, by synergistically integrating the quantum and convolutional pathways, the proposed QC-GAN breaks the representational bottleneck inherent in classical GAN. Experimental results on denoising and interpolation tasks demonstrate that QC-GAN preserves wavefield continuity and amplitude-phase information under complex noise conditions.

AI.tldr: A quantum-classical GAN for seismic data denoising/interpolation that integrates a quantum pathway with a convolutional pathway and a feature complementarity loss to overcome classical NN bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: Classical NN denoising/interpolation in seismic data is limited by representational capacity and real-valued Euclidean spaces; quantum NNs offer exponential Hilbert-space representations to capture non-stationary, high-order correlations in seismic wavefields; apply QC-NN to seismic exploration.
AI.method: Proposed QC-GAN with a quantum pathway to capture high-order feature correlations and a convolutional pathway for waveform structure; introduces QC feature complementarity loss to enforce feature orthogonality and non-overlap between pathways.
AI.result: Experimental results on denoising and interpolation show preserved wavefield continuity and amplitude-phase information under complex noise, indicating the QC-GAN overcomes the representational bottleneck of classical GANs.
AI.conclusion: QC-GAN demonstrates the feasibility and potential of quantum-classical hybrids for seismic data processing and may inspire further integration of quantum NNs in geophysical workflows.

title: GenMask: Adapting DiT for Segmentation via Direct Mask
summary: Recent approaches for segmentation have leveraged pretrained generative models as feature extractors, treating segmentation as a downstream adaptation task via indirect feature retrieval. This implicit use suffers from a fundamental misalignment in representation. It also depends heavily on indirect feature extraction pipelines, which complicate the workflow and limit adaptation. In this paper, we argue that instead of indirect adaptation, segmentation tasks should be trained directly in a generative manner. We identify a key obstacle to this unified formulation: VAE latents of binary masks are sharply distributed, noise robust, and linearly separable, distinct from natural image latents. To bridge this gap, we introduce timesteps sampling strategy for binary masks that emphasizes extreme noise levels for segmentation and moderate noise for image generation, enabling harmonious joint training. We present GenMask, a DiT trains to generate black-and-white segmentation masks as well as colorful images in RGB space under the original generative objective. GenMask preserves the original DiT architecture while removing the need of feature extraction pipelines tailored for segmentation tasks. Empirically, GenMask attains state-of-the-art performance on referring and reasoning segmentation benchmarks and ablations quantify the contribution of each component.

AI.tldr: GenMask enables direct generative segmentation by training a DiT to produce binary masks and RGB images under the same generative objective, using a timestep sampling strategy to bridge binary mask latents and natural image latents, achieving state-of-the-art on referring and reasoning segmentation.
AI.motivation: Address misalignment and reliance on indirect feature extraction when using pretrained generative models for segmentation; reduce pipeline complexity; enable direct task-specific generative modeling.
AI.method: Propose timestep sampling strategy emphasizing extreme noise for masks and moderate noise for images; modify DiT to output both binary masks and RGB images under original generative objective; preserve architecture; remove feature extraction pipelines.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on referring and reasoning segmentation benchmarks; ablations show contributions of components.
AI.conclusion: Direct generative segmentation via GenMask bridges latent spaces and simplifies workflow; validates unified generative training approach for segmentation.

title: Diet Your LLM: Dimension-wise Global Pruning of LLMs via Merging Task-specific Importance Score
comment: 14 pages, 10 figures. Code available at https://github.com/Jimmy145123/DIET
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities, but their massive scale poses significant challenges for practical deployment. Structured pruning offers a promising solution by removing entire dimensions or layers, yet existing methods face critical trade-offs: task-agnostic approaches cannot adapt to task-specific requirements, while task-aware methods require costly training to learn task adaptability. We propose DIET (Dimension-wise global pruning of LLMs via merging Task-wise importance scores), a training-free structured pruning method that combines dimension-level granularity with task-aware selection. DIET profiles activation magnitudes across tasks using only 100 samples per task, then applies majority voting to construct a single global mask. DIET does not require large costs from pre-computation or training. Experiments on seven zero-shot benchmarks using Gemma-2 2B and 9B models demonstrate the effectiveness of DIET; for example, at 20% sparsity on Gemma-2 2B, DIET achieves near 10% average accuracy improvement, compared to previous state-of-the-art structured pruning methods. This advantage persists across various sparsity levels and model scales, positioning DIET as a practical and robust choice for structured LLM pruning.

AI.tldr: DIET is a training-free structured pruning method for LLMs that merges task-wise importance into a single global dimension-level mask by profiling activations with 100 samples per task and majority voting; it achieves substantial accuracy gains at 20% sparsity on Gemma-2 2B compared to prior structured pruning methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome the trade-off between task-agnostic pruning (cheap but suboptimal per-task performance) and task-aware pruning (better but costly due to fine-tuning); provide a scalable, training-free approach.
AI.method: Profile activation magnitudes per task using ~100 samples per task, apply majority voting to construct a single global dimension-wise pruning mask, enabling structured pruning without training cost.
AI.result: On seven zero-shot benchmarks with Gemma-2 2B and 9B models, DIET at 20% sparsity yields ~10% average accuracy improvement over previous SOTA structured pruning methods; effectiveness persists across sparsity levels and scales.
AI.conclusion: DIET is a practical, robust, training-free approach for structured pruning of LLMs, combining dimension-level granularity with task awareness to deliver improved performance under pruning.

title: Attention-aware Inference Optimizations for Large Vision-Language Models with Memory-efficient Decoding
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success in multi-modal reasoning, but their inference time efficiency remains a significant challenge due to the memory overhead during decoding, especially when the query and answer of VLMs consist of long sequences of visual and text tokens. This paper presents AttentionPack, an adaptive and attention-aware optimization framework tailored for large vision-language models with improving memory-efficiency during decoding, focusing on addressing the challenges due to the increased high number of visual inputs and interactions, particularly in long-context tasks with multiple high-resolution images or videos. AttentionPack is novel in two aspects: (i) We introduce a multi-head attention compaction method for economically storing key and value matrices by exploiting the implicit low-rank structure, and (ii) we develop a token-specific attention-aware decompression mechanism to reduce latency overhead. Experimental results on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that AttentionPack improves memory efficiency by up to 8x, enabling higher batch sizes and faster batch inference while preserving the model output quality or longer context lengths for superior retrieval performance. We also report the effectiveness of AttentionPack combined with eviction, quantization and kernel fusion, showing further efficiency gains for resource-limited environments.

AI.tldr: AttentionPack introduces adaptive multi-head attention compaction and token-specific decompression to dramatically reduce memory usage during decoding in large vision-language models, achieving up to 8x memory savings and enabling larger batches and longer contexts.
AI.motivation: Memory bottlenecks during decoding for VLMs with long sequences and high visual input counts; need to improve memory efficiency without sacrificing output quality.
AI.method: Twofold: (i) multi-head attention compaction exploiting implicit low-rank structure to compress key/value matrices; (ii) token-specific attention-aware decompression per token to reduce latency; additional integration with eviction, quantization, and kernel fusion.
AI.result: Empirical results show memory efficiency gains up to 8x; improved batch size and faster batch inference; preserved model output quality or support for longer context; compatible with other optimization techniques.
AI.conclusion: AttentionPack provides a practical approach to memory-efficient decoding in large VLMs, enabling longer contexts and higher throughput with limited resources; potential applicability to various multi-modal models.

title: Can we generate portable representations for clinical time series data using LLMs?
comment: Accepted to the 14th International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026)
summary: Deploying clinical ML is slow and brittle: models that work at one hospital often degrade under distribution shifts at the next. In this work, we study a simple question -- can large language models (LLMs) create portable patient embeddings i.e. representations of patients enable a downstream predictor built on one hospital to be used elsewhere with minimal-to-no retraining and fine-tuning. To do so, we map from irregular ICU time series onto concise natural language summaries using a frozen LLM, then embed each summary with a frozen text embedding model to obtain a fixed length vector capable of serving as input to a variety of downstream predictors. Across three cohorts (MIMIC-IV, HIRID, PPICU), on multiple clinically grounded forecasting and classification tasks, we find that our approach is simple, easy to use and competitive with in-distribution with grid imputation, self-supervised representation learning, and time series foundation models, while exhibiting smaller relative performance drops when transferring to new hospitals. We study the variation in performance across prompt design, with structured prompts being crucial to reducing the variance of the predictive models without altering mean accuracy. We find that using these portable representations improves few-shot learning and does not increase demographic recoverability of age or sex relative to baselines, suggesting little additional privacy risk. Our work points to the potential that LLMs hold as tools to enable the scalable deployment of production grade predictive models by reducing the engineering overhead.

AI.tldr: LLMs can generate portable patient embeddings from ICU time series by converting to concise natural language summaries and embedding them, enabling downstream predictors to transfer across hospitals with minimal retraining. This approach is competitive with in-distribution baselines and shows smaller performance drops under distribution shifts, with structured prompts reducing variance and minimal added privacy risk.
AI.motivation: Clinical ML deployments are slow and brittle due to distribution shifts across hospitals. The work investigates whether portable representations derived from LLMs can bridge cross-institution generalization without extensive retraining.
AI.method: Convert irregular ICU time-series into concise natural language summaries using a frozen LLM, then obtain fixed-length embeddings via a frozen text embedding model. Use these embeddings as input to downstream predictors. Evaluate across three cohorts (MIMIC-IV, HIRID, PPICU) on forecasting and classification tasks. Explore prompt design, finding structured prompts reduce predictive variance without harming mean accuracy.
AI.result: The portable embeddings perform competitively with on-distribution baselines (grid imputation, self-supervised representation learning, time-series foundation models) and exhibit smaller drops when transferring to new hospitals. Structured prompts reduce variance across models. The approach improves few-shot learning and does not increase demographic recoverability of age or sex relative to baselines, suggesting limited additional privacy risk.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can reduce engineering overhead and enable scalable deployment of production-grade predictive models by providing portable patient representations that transfer across institutions with minimal retraining.

title: DecepGPT: Schema-Driven Deception Detection with Multicultural Datasets and Robust Multimodal Learning
summary: Multimodal deception detection aims to identify deceptive behavior by analyzing audiovisual cues for forensics and security. In these high-stakes settings, investigators need verifiable evidence connecting audiovisual cues to final decisions, along with reliable generalization across domains and cultural contexts. However, existing benchmarks provide only binary labels without intermediate reasoning cues. Datasets are also small with limited scenario coverage, leading to shortcut learning. We address these issues through three contributions. First, we construct reasoning datasets by augmenting existing benchmarks with structured cue-level descriptions and reasoning chains, enabling model output auditable reports. Second, we release T4-Deception, a multicultural dataset based on the unified ``To Tell The Truth'' television format implemented across four countries. With 1695 samples, it is the largest non-laboratory deception detection dataset. Third, we propose two modules for robust learning under small-data conditions. Stabilized Individuality-Commonality Synergy (SICS) refines multimodal representations by synergizing learnable global priors with sample-adaptive residuals, followed by a polarity-aware adjustment that bi-directionally recalibrates representations. Distilled Modality Consistency (DMC) aligns modality-specific predictions with the fused multimodal predictions via knowledge distillation to prevent unimodal shortcut learning. Experiments on three established benchmarks and our novel dataset demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both in-domain and cross-domain scenarios, while exhibiting superior transferability across diverse cultural contexts. The datasets and codes will be released.

AI.tldr: Proposes reasoning-augmented multimodal deception detection with a new multicultural dataset (T4-Deception) and two robust learning modules (SICS and DMC) to mitigate small-data issues and reduce unimodal shortcuts, achieving state-of-the-art in-domain and cross-domain performance and cross-cultural transferability.
AI.motivation: Need for verifiable, auditable evidence linking audiovisual cues to decisions in deception detection, along with robust generalization across domains and cultures. Existing benchmarks are binary-labeled, small, and prone to shortcut learning, lacking intermediate reasoning cues.
AI.method: 1) Construct cue-level reasoning datasets with structured descriptions and reasoning chains; 2) Create T4-Deception, a 1695-sample multicultural deception dataset across four countries using the To Tell The Truth format; 3) Propose Stabilized Individuality-Commonality Synergy (SICS) to refine multimodal representations via global priors, sample-adaptive residuals, and polarity-aware recalibration; 4) Propose Distilled Modality Consistency (DMC) to align modality-specific and fused predictions via knowledge distillation to prevent unimodal shortcuts.
AI.result: Experiments on three established benchmarks and the new T4-Deception dataset show state-of-the-art performance in both in-domain and cross-domain settings, with superior transferability across diverse cultural contexts.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers (i) a scalable, auditable reasoning dataset framework for deception detection, (ii) a large multicultural benchmark (T4-Deception), and (iii) two robust learning modules (SICS and DMC) that improve performance under small-data regimes and enhance cross-cultural generalization; datasets and code will be released.

title: Understanding the Challenges in Iterative Generative Optimization with LLMs
