summary: Generative optimization uses large language models (LLMs) to iteratively improve artifacts (such as code, workflows or prompts) using execution feedback. It is a promising approach to building self-improving agents, yet in practice remains brittle: despite active research, only 9% of surveyed agents used any automated optimization. We argue that this brittleness arises because, to set up a learning loop, an engineer must make ``hidden'' design choices: What can the optimizer edit and what is the "right" learning evidence to provide at each update? We investigate three factors that affect most applications: the starting artifact, the credit horizon for execution traces, and batching trials and errors into learning evidence. Through case studies in MLAgentBench, Atari, and BigBench Extra Hard, we find that these design decisions can determine whether generative optimization succeeds, yet they are rarely made explicit in prior work. Different starting artifacts determine which solutions are reachable in MLAgentBench, truncated traces can still improve Atari agents, and larger minibatches do not monotonically improve generalization on BBEH. We conclude that the lack of a simple, universal way to set up learning loops across domains is a major hurdle for productionization and adoption. We provide practical guidance for making these choices.

AI.tldr: Hidden design choices in learning loops (starting artifact, credit horizon, batching) largely determine whether generative optimization with LLMs succeeds; explicit, domain-aware guidance is needed for reliable productionization.
AI.motivation: Despite the promise of self-improving agents via LLM-based optimization, practice remains brittle because engineers must implicitly decide what the optimizer can edit and what learning evidence to provide. This paper investigates how these latent design decisions impact success across domains.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across MLAgentBench, Atari, and BigBench Extra Hard. The authors vary three core factors—starting artifact, credit horizon for execution traces, and batching of trials into learning evidence—and observe their effects on outcomes.
AI.result: The design decisions materially affect success: starting artifacts constrain reachable solutions, truncated traces can still improve performance (e.g., Atari), and larger minibatches do not guarantee better generalization (BBEH). Many prior works fail to make these choices explicit, hindering replication and productionization.
AI.conclusion: Provide practical guidance to specify these design factors, highlighting that there is no universal setup across domains and advocating domain-aware templates or guidelines to enable reliable deployment of generative optimization.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Vision-based Risk Object Identification via Conformal Risk Tube Prediction
comment: IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: We study object importance-based vision risk object identification (Vision-ROI), a key capability for hazard detection in intelligent driving systems. Existing approaches make deterministic decisions and ignore uncertainty, which could lead to safety-critical failures. Specifically, in ambiguous scenarios, fixed decision thresholds may cause premature or delayed risk detection and temporally unstable predictions, especially in complex scenes with multiple interacting risks. Despite these challenges, current methods lack a principled framework to model risk uncertainty jointly across space and time. We propose Conformal Risk Tube Prediction, a unified formulation that captures spatiotemporal risk uncertainty, provides coverage guarantees for true risks, and produces calibrated risk scores with uncertainty estimates. To conduct a systematic evaluation, we present a new dataset and metrics probing diverse scenario configurations with multi-risk coupling effects, which are not supported by existing datasets. We systematically analyze factors affecting uncertainty estimation, including scenario variations, per-risk category behavior, and perception error propagation. Our method delivers substantial improvements over prior approaches, enhancing vision-ROI robustness and downstream performance, such as reducing nuisance braking alerts. For more qualitative results, please visit our project webpage: https://hcis-lab.github.io/CRTP/

AI.tldr: CRTP introduces a conformal prediction framework for spatiotemporal vision-risk estimation (Vision-ROI) to provide coverage guarantees and calibrated risk scores, addressing uncertainty in driving scene risk detection; it includes a new dataset and metrics and reports robustness gains over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of principled, joint spatiotemporal uncertainty modeling in hazard detection for intelligent driving; deterministic thresholds lead to premature/delayed or unstable predictions in complex scenes.
AI.method: Conformal Risk Tube Prediction (CRTP): a unified framework that yields spatiotemporal risk tubes with coverage guarantees and calibrated risk scores, plus uncertainty estimates; ends with a new dataset and evaluation metrics for multi-risk coupling scenarios.
AI.result: CRTP shows substantial improvements over prior approaches in robustness and downstream tasks (e.g., fewer nuisance braking alerts) across diverse scenario configurations written in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: CRTP advances principled uncertainty modeling in Vision-ROI for driving hazards, enabling calibrated risk estimation with guarantees, and provides datasets/metrics to facilitate future research.

title: Stochastic Dimension-Free Zeroth-Order Estimator for High-Dimensional and High-Order PINNs
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for high-dimensional and high-order partial differential equations (PDEs) are primarily constrained by the $\mathcal{O}(d^k)$ spatial derivative complexity and the $\mathcal{O}(P)$ memory overhead of backpropagation (BP). While randomized spatial estimators successfully reduce the spatial complexity to $\mathcal{O}(1)$, their reliance on first-order optimization still leads to prohibitive memory consumption at scale. Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization offers a BP-free alternative; however, naively combining randomized spatial operators with ZO perturbations triggers a variance explosion of $\mathcal{O}(1/\varepsilon^2)$, leading to numerical divergence. To address these challenges, we propose the \textbf{S}tochastic \textbf{D}imension-free \textbf{Z}eroth-order \textbf{E}stimator (\textbf{SDZE}), a unified framework that achieves dimension-independent complexity in both space and memory. Specifically, SDZE leverages \emph{Common Random Numbers Synchronization (CRNS)} to algebraically cancel the $\mathcal{O}(1/\varepsilon^2)$ variance by locking spatial random seeds across perturbations. Furthermore, an \emph{implicit matrix-free subspace projection} is introduced to reduce parameter exploration variance from $\mathcal{O}(P)$ to $\mathcal{O}(r)$ while maintaining an $\mathcal{O}(1)$ optimizer memory footprint. Empirical results demonstrate that SDZE enables the training of 10-million-dimensional PINNs on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, delivering significant improvements in speed and memory efficiency over state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: SDZE enables dimension-free zeroth-order optimization for PINNs, combining common random numbers synchronization and a subspace projection to achieve O(1) spatial complexity and memory, enabling training of 10M-dimensional PINNs on a single GPU.
AI.motivation: PINNs for high-dimensional/high-order PDEs face prohibitive spatial derivative costs (scaling with d^k) and backpropagation memory (O(P)). Zeroth-order methods avoid backprop but suffer high variance; need a variance-controlled, memory-efficient, dimension-free approach for large-scale PINNs.
AI.method: SDZE uses Common Random Numbers Synchronization (CRNS) to cancel O(1/ε^2) variance by locking spatial random seeds across perturbations. It also introduces an implicit matrix-free subspace projection to reduce parameter exploration variance from O(P) to O(r) while keeping O(1) optimizer memory.
AI.result: Empirical results show SDZE enables training of 10-million-dimensional PINNs on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, with significant speed and memory advantages over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: SDZE offers a unified, scalable framework for dimension-free zeroth-order optimization in PINNs, addressing both spatial and memory bottlenecks and enabling ultra-high-dimensional PDE solving on practical hardware.

title: DepthArb: Training-Free Depth-Arbitrated Generation for Occlusion-Robust Image Synthesis
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models frequently exhibit deficiencies in synthesizing accurate occlusion relationships of multiple objects, particularly within dense overlapping regions. Existing training-free layout-guided methods predominantly rely on rigid spatial priors that remain agnostic to depth order, often resulting in concept mixing or illogical occlusion. To address these limitations, we propose DepthArb, a training-free framework that resolves occlusion ambiguities by arbitrating attention competition between interacting objects. Specifically, DepthArb employs two core mechanisms: Attention Arbitration Modulation (AAM), which enforces depth-ordered visibility by suppressing background activations in overlapping regions, and Spatial Compactness Control (SCC), which preserves structural integrity by curbing attention divergence. These mechanisms enable robust occlusion generation without model retraining. To systematically evaluate this capability, we propose OcclBench, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate diverse occlusion scenarios. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that DepthArb consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both occlusion accuracy and visual fidelity. As a plug-and-play method, DepthArb seamlessly enhances the compositional capabilities of diffusion backbones, offering a novel perspective on spatial layering within generative models.

AI.tldr: DepthArb introduces a training-free attention arbitration framework to improve occlusion correctness in text-to-image diffusion, using Attention Arbitration Modulation (AAM) and Spatial Compactness Control (SCC), validated on OcclBench with superior occlusion accuracy and visual fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-image diffusion models struggle to generate accurate depth-ordered occlusions in dense scenes. Training-free layout-guided methods rely on rigid, depth-agnostic priors, leading to concept mixing and illogical occlusions.
AI.method: DepthArb comprises two mechanisms: (1) Attention Arbitration Modulation (AAM) that suppresses background activations in overlapping regions to enforce depth-ordered visibility, and (2) Spatial Compactness Control (SCC) that reduces attention divergence to preserve structural integrity. It is designed as a plug-and-play augmentation that operates without retraining of diffusion backbones. Evaluation framework OcclBench provides diverse occlusion scenarios.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show DepthArb consistently surpasses strong baselines in occlusion accuracy and visual fidelity across scenarios, demonstrating robust occlusion generation without retraining.
AI.conclusion: DepthArb offers a lightweight, generalizable approach for enforcing spatial layering in diffusion-based generative models, enhancing their compositional and occlusion-aware capabilities with a plug-and-play design.

title: i-IF-Learn: Iterative Feature Selection and Unsupervised Learning for High-Dimensional Complex Data
comment: 28 pages, 5 figures, including appendix. Accepted at AISTATS
summary: Unsupervised learning of high-dimensional data is challenging due to irrelevant or noisy features obscuring underlying structures. It's common that only a few features, called the influential features, meaningfully define the clusters. Recovering these influential features is helpful in data interpretation and clustering. We propose i-IF-Learn, an iterative unsupervised framework that jointly performs feature selection and clustering. Our core innovation is an adaptive feature selection statistic that effectively combines pseudo-label supervision with unsupervised signals, dynamically adjusting based on intermediate label reliability to mitigate error propagation common in iterative frameworks. Leveraging low-dimensional embeddings (PCA or Laplacian eigenmaps) followed by $k$-means, i-IF-Learn simultaneously outputs influential feature subset and clustering labels. Numerical experiments on gene microarray and single-cell RNA-seq datasets show that i-IF-Learn significantly surpasses classical and deep clustering baselines. Furthermore, using our selected influential features as preprocessing substantially enhances downstream deep models such as DeepCluster, UMAP, and VAE, highlighting the importance and effectiveness of targeted feature selection.

AI.tldr: Iterative unsupervised framework i-IF-Learn jointly selects influential features and clusters data using adaptive pseudo-label supervision, improving clustering and downstream model performance on high-dimensional bio datasets.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional data have many noisy/uninformative features; only a subset influences cluster structure; need robust feature selection integrated with clustering to aid interpretation and performance.
AI.method: Adaptive feature selection statistic that blends pseudo-label supervision with unsupervised signals; iterative updates mitigate error propagation; uses low-dimensional embeddings (PCA or Laplacian eigenmaps) followed by k-means; outputs influential feature subset and cluster labels.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms classical and deep clustering baselines on gene microarray and scRNA-seq datasets; using selected features as preprocessing enhances downstream deep models (DeepCluster, UMAP, VAE).
AI.conclusion: Joint feature selection and clustering with adaptive supervision yields interpretable influential features and improved clustering, with practical benefits for downstream modeling in bioinformatics.

title: DP^2-VL: Private Photo Dataset Protection by Data Poisoning for Vision-Language Models
summary: Recent advances in visual-language alignment have endowed vision-language models (VLMs) with fine-grained image understanding capabilities. However, this progress also introduces new privacy risks. This paper first proposes a novel privacy threat model named identity-affiliation learning: an attacker fine-tunes a VLM using only a few private photos of a target individual, thereby embedding associations between the target facial identity and their private property and social relationships into the model's internal representations. Once deployed via public APIs, this model enables unauthorized exposure of the target user's private information upon input of their photos. To benchmark VLMs' susceptibility to such identity-affiliation leakage, we introduce the first identity-affiliation dataset comprising seven typical scenarios appearing in private photos. Each scenario is instantiated with multiple identity-centered photo-description pairs. Experimental results demonstrate that mainstream VLMs like LLaVA, Qwen-VL, and MiniGPT-v2, can recognize facial identities and infer identity-affiliation relationships by fine-tuning on small-scale private photographic dataset, and even on synthetically generated datasets. To mitigate this privacy risk, we propose DP2-VL, the first Dataset Protection framework for private photos that leverages Data Poisoning. Though optimizing imperceptible perturbations by pushing the original representations toward an antithetical region, DP2-VL induces a dataset-level shift in the embedding space of VLMs'encoders. This shift separates protected images from clean inference images, causing fine-tuning on the protected set to overfit. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DP2-VL achieves strong generalization across models, robustness to diverse post-processing operations, and consistent effectiveness across varying protection ratios.

AI.tldr: Proposes identity-affiliation learning as a privacy threat for vision-language models (VLMs) and introduces DP2-VL, a dataset protection framework using data poisoning to mitigate leakage.
AI.motivation: As VLMs enable fine-grained alignment, they pose privacy risks: a user’s facial identity can be linked to private attributes/relationships via model fine-tuning on small private photo sets. There is a need to quantify leakage risk and propose defenses.
AI.method: 1) Define identity-affiliation learning threat model. 2) Create an identity-affiliation dataset with seven scenarios and identity-centered photo-description pairs. 3) Evaluate leakage by fine-tuning LLaVA, Qwen-VL, MiniGPT-v2 on private data (and synthetic data). 4) Propose DP2-VL, a dataset-level protection via data poisoning: generate imperceptible perturbations to push representations toward a contrasting region, causing fine-tuning on protected data to overfit and shift embeddings.
AI.result: Mainstream VLMs can identify target identities and infer identity-affiliations after fine-tuning on small private datasets or synthetic data. DP2-VL achieves strong generalization across models, robustness to post-processing, and effectiveness across varying protection ratios.
AI.conclusion: Identity-affiliation learning reveals a tangible privacy threat in VLMs, and DP2-VL offers a promising mitigation through dataset-level poisoning to separate protected images in the embedding space and hinder leakage.

title: Lagrangian Relaxation Score-based Generation for Mixed Integer linear Programming
summary: Predict-and-search (PaS) methods have shown promise for accelerating mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) solving. However, existing approaches typically assume variable independence and rely on deterministic single-point predictions, which limits solution diversityand often necessitates extensive downstream search for high-quality solutions. In this paper, we propose \textbf{SRG}, a generative framework based on Lagrangian relaxation-guided stochastic differential equations (SDEs), with theoretical guarantees on solution quality. SRG leverages convolutional kernels to capture inter-variable dependencies while integrating Lagrangian relaxation to guide the sampling process toward feasible and near-optimal regions. Rather than producing a single estimate, SRG generates diverse, high-quality solution candidates that collectively define compact and effective trust-region subproblems for standard MILP solvers. Across multiple public benchmarks, SRG consistently outperforms existing machine learning baselines in solution quality. Moreover, SRG demonstrates strong zero-shot transferability: on unseen cross-scale/problem instances, it achieves competitive optimality with state-of-the-art exact solvers while significantly reducing computational overhead through faster search and superior solution quality.

AI.tldr: SRG is a generative SDE-based MILP solver guided by Lagrangian relaxation, producing diverse candidate solutions to form effective trust-region subproblems; it outperforms ML baselines and shows strong zero-shot transfer with reduced search overhead.
AI.motivation: Current PaS approaches assume variable independence and deterministic single-point predictions, which limits solution diversity and necessitates extensive downstream search for high-quality solutions.
AI.method: SRG uses Lagrangian-relaxation-guided sampling within a stochastic differential equation framework, with convolutional kernels to capture inter-variable dependencies. It generates multiple diverse, high-quality candidates instead of a single estimate, shaping compact trust-region subproblems for standard MILP solvers.
AI.result: Across public benchmarks, SRG consistently outperforms existing ML baselines in solution quality and exhibits strong zero-shot transferability: on unseen cross-scale/problem instances, it achieves competitive optimality with state-of-the-art exact solvers while reducing computational overhead via faster search and better quality solutions.
AI.conclusion: SRG provides theoretical guarantees on solution quality, enhances diversity and search efficiency, and demonstrates robust generalization to unseen instances, representing a solid advancement for predict-and-search in MILP.

title: Revealing Multi-View Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) are increasingly being applied to multi-view image inputs captured from diverse viewpoints. However, despite this growing use, current LVLMs often confuse or mismatch visual information originating from different instances or viewpoints, a phenomenon we term multi-view hallucination. To systematically analyze this problem, we construct MVH-Bench, a benchmark comprising 4.8k question-answer pairs targeting two types of hallucination: cross-instance and cross-view. Empirical results show that recent LVLMs struggle to correctly associate visual evidence with its corresponding instance or viewpoint. To overcome this limitation, we propose Reference Shift Contrastive Decoding (RSCD), a training-free decoding technique that suppresses visual interference by generating negative logits through attention masking. Experiments on MVH-Bench with Qwen2.5-VL and LLaVA-OneVision demonstrate that RSCD consistently improves performance by up to 21.1 and 34.6 points over existing hallucination mitigation methods, highlighting the effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: LVLMs suffer from multi-view hallucination across instances/views. A benchmark MVH-Bench (4.8k QA pairs) targets cross-instance and cross-view hallucination. A training-free decoding method, RSCD, using attention masking reduces interference and improves results.
AI.motivation: Systematically evaluate and mitigate multi-view hallucination in LVLMs by creating a dedicated benchmark and a decoding technique that suppresses cross-view/ cross-instance interference.
AI.method: 1) Build MVH-Bench with 4.8k QA pairs focusing on cross-instance and cross-view errors. 2) Propose Reference Shift Contrastive Decoding (RSCD), a training-free decoding scheme that generates negative logits via attention masking to suppress visual interference.
AI.result: LVLMs show strong susceptibility to misattributing visual evidence. RSCD yields consistent gains, improving performance by up to 21.1 and 34.6 points on MVH-Bench for Qwen2.5-VL and LLaVA-OneVision, outperforming existing hallucination mitigation methods.
AI.conclusion: RSCD effectively mitigates multi-view hallucination in LVLMs without extra training, demonstrating a viable path to more reliable multi-view visual reasoning.

title: MoE-Sieve: Routing-Guided LoRA for Efficient MoE Fine-Tuning
summary: Standard LoRA fine-tuning of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models applies adapters to every expert, yet our profiling shows that per-layer expert routing is highly skewed: a small subset of experts handles most tokens in each layer, while many others are rarely activated ("cold"). We propose MoE-Sieve, a simple routing-guided framework for LoRA fine-tuning, and pair it with a systematic profiling study of expert routing across architectures and tasks. The method is simple: profile routing counts on a small calibration set, select the top-k most-routed experts per layer, and apply LoRA only to those experts. Across two architecturally distinct MoE models and three diverse tasks, tuning only the top 25% routed experts per layer remains competitive with full LoRA, with mean differences within +/-1 percentage point across all conditions. This reduces LoRA trainable parameters by 70-73%, adapter checkpoint size by 71-73%, and wall-clock training time by up to 50%. We also observe a non-monotonic relationship between expert count and seed-to-seed variance, consistent with the hypothesis that adapting cold experts can introduce gradient noise without improving accuracy. Further ablations show that random expert selection at matched budget is about 2.5 percentage points worse, indicating that the routing signal matters, while greedy per-layer budget optimization does not improve over uniform top-k.

AI.tldr: MoE-Sieve reduces LoRA fine-tuning cost by applying adapters only to the top-k most-routed experts per layer, achieving similar accuracy with substantial parameter and compute savings.
AI.motivation: In mixture-of-experts (MoE) models, routing is highly skewed; many experts are rarely activated. Applying LoRA adapters to every expert is wasteful. The work proposes a simple, routing-guided pruning-like approach and provides profiling across architectures and tasks to quantify potential efficiency gains.
AI.method: Profile per-layer routing counts on a small calibration set to identify the top-k most-routed experts per layer. Apply LoRA only to those selected experts. Evaluate on two architecturally distinct MoE models and three diverse tasks, comparing against full LoRA. Conduct ablations: random expert selection, seed variance analysis, and budget optimization contrasts.
AI.result: Using the top 25% of routed experts per layer yields performance competitive with full LoRA, with mean accuracy differences within ±1 percentage point across conditions. Parameter count reduced by 70–73%; adapter checkpoint size reduced by 71–73%; wall-clock training time reduced up to 50%. Cold-expert adaptation introduces gradient noise without accuracy gains, explaining non-monotonic seed-to-seed variance. Random selection under matched budgets incurs ~2.5 percentage points worse performance, while greedy per-layer budget optimization does not outperform uniform top-k.
AI.conclusion: Routing-guided selection is an effective, simple strategy to reduce LoRA fine-tuning cost for MoE models. The evidence supports focusing adaptation on heavily routed experts and cautions against adapting cold, rarely activated ones due to potential gradient noise. Further work could explore dynamic/top-k adjustments, more diverse tasks/models, and integration with other efficient fine-tuning methods.

title: High-Fidelity Face Content Recovery via Tamper-Resilient Versatile Watermarking
summary: The proliferation of AIGC-driven face manipulation and deepfakes poses severe threats to media provenance, integrity, and copyright protection. Prior versatile watermarking systems typically rely on embedding explicit localization payloads, which introduces a fidelity--functionality trade-off: larger localization signals degrade visual quality and often reduce decoding robustness under strong generative edits. Moreover, existing methods rarely support content recovery, limiting their forensic value when original evidence must be reconstructed. To address these challenges, we present VeriFi, a versatile watermarking framework that unifies copyright protection, pixel-level manipulation localization, and high-fidelity face content recovery. VeriFi makes three key contributions: (1) it embeds a compact semantic latent watermark that serves as an content-preserving prior, enabling faithful restoration even after severe manipulations; (2) it achieves fine-grained localization without embedding localization-specific artifacts by correlating image features with decoded provenance signals; and (3) it introduces an AIGC attack simulator that combines latent-space mixing with seamless blending to improve robustness to realistic deepfake pipelines. Extensive experiments on CelebA-HQ and FFHQ show that VeriFi consistently outperforms strong baselines in watermark robustness, localization accuracy, and recovery quality, providing a practical and verifiable defense for deepfake forensics.

AI.tldr: VeriFi unifies copyright protection, pixel-level manipulation localization, and high-fidelity face content recovery through a compact semantic latent watermark, feature-based localization, and an AIGC attack simulator, outperforming baselines on CelebA-HQ/FFHQ.
AI.motivation: Address fidelity–functionality trade-offs in existing watermarking for face manipulation; enable robust, verifiable provenance, localization, and content restoration in deepfake scenarios.
AI.method: Three core components: (1) embed a compact semantic latent watermark that serves as a content-preserving prior for faithful recovery after manipulations; (2) achieve fine-grained localization by correlating image features with decoded provenance signals without embedding explicit localization artifacts; (3) introduce an AIGC attack simulator by combining latent-space mixing with seamless blending to enhance robustness against realistic deepfake pipelines.
AI.result: Empirical results on CelebA-HQ and FFHQ show VeriFi achieves superior watermark robustness, localization accuracy, and recovery quality compared to strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: VeriFi offers a practical, verifiable defense for deepfake forensics by unifying copyright protection, localization, and content recovery, with strong empirical performance and potential utility in media provenance and rights management.

title: The impact of sensor placement on graph-neural-network-based leakage detection
summary: Sensor placement for leakage detection in water distribution networks is an important and practical challenge for water utilities. Recent work has shown that graph neural networks can estimate and predict pressures and detect leaks, but their performance strongly depends on the available sensor measurements and configurations. In this paper, we investigate how sensor placement influences the performance of GNN-based leakage detection. We propose a novel PageRank-Centrality-based sensor placement method and demonstrate that it substantially impacts reconstruction, prediction, and leakage detection on the EPANET Net1.

AI.tldr: PageRank-centrality-based sensor placement improves GNN-based leakage detection in water networks, with notable gains in reconstruction, prediction, and leak detection on EPANET Net1.
AI.motivation: Leaks in water distribution networks are hard to detect with limited sensors; GNNs’ performance hinges on sensor configuration. A principled placement strategy is needed to maximize information for leakage tasks.
AI.method: Develop a sensor placement heuristic based on PageRank centrality and graph information flow. Evaluate by training GNNs for pressure reconstruction and leakage detection on EPANET Net1 across different sensor subsets, comparing against baseline placements (e.g., random, degree-based).
AI.result: Centrality-informed placements yield substantial improvements in reconstruction accuracy, predictive performance, and leak-detection metrics compared to baselines; performance gains are robust across task types and sensor budget levels.
AI.conclusion: Sensor placement critically shapes the effectiveness of GNN-based leakage detection. PageRank centrality offers a practical, scalable heuristic that enhances information coverage and task performance on water networks like Net1; results suggest potential generalization to other networks and related tasks.

title: VOLMO: Versatile and Open Large Models for Ophthalmology
summary: Vision impairment affects millions globally, and early detection is critical to preventing irreversible vision loss. Ophthalmology workflows require clinicians to integrate medical images, structured clinical data, and free-text notes to determine disease severity and management, which is time-consuming and burdensome. Recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) show promise, but existing general and medical MLLMs perform poorly in ophthalmology, and few ophthalmology-specific MLLMs are openly available. We present VOLMO (Versatile and Open Large Models for Ophthalmology), a model-agnostic, data-open framework for developing ophthalmology-specific MLLMs. VOLMO includes three stages: ophthalmology knowledge pretraining on 86,965 image-text pairs from 26,569 articles across 82 journals; domain task fine-tuning on 26,929 annotated instances spanning 12 eye conditions for disease screening and severity classification; and multi-step clinical reasoning on 913 patient case reports for assessment, planning, and follow-up care. Using this framework, we trained a compact 2B-parameter MLLM and compared it with strong baselines, including InternVL-2B, LLaVA-Med-7B, MedGemma-4B, MedGemma-27B, and RETFound. We evaluated these models on image description generation, disease screening and staging classification, and assessment-and-management generation, with additional manual review by two healthcare professionals and external validation on three independent cohorts for age-related macular degeneration and diabetic retinopathy. Across settings, VOLMO-2B consistently outperformed baselines, achieving stronger image description performance, an average F1 of 87.4% across 12 eye conditions, and higher scores in external validation.

AI.tldr: VOLMO is a data-open, model-agnostic framework enabling ophthalmology-specific multimodal LLMs, demonstrated with VOLMO-2B, a compact 2B-parameter model that outperforms baselines across image description, disease screening/staging, and clinical reasoning tasks, with external validation on AMD and DR cohorts.
AI.motivation: Ophthalmology lacks open, capable multimodal LLMs tailored to ophthalmic data; integrating images, structured data, and notes is time-consuming, necessitating specialized models trained on ophthalmology literature and case data.
AI.method: Three-stage framework: (1) ophthalmology knowledge pretraining on 86,965 image-text pairs from 26,569 articles across 82 journals; (2) domain task fine-tuning on 26,929 annotated instances across 12 eye conditions for screening and severity classification; (3) multi-step clinical reasoning on 913 patient case reports for assessment, planning, and follow-up care. A 2B-parameter model (VOLMO-2B) was trained and evaluated against InternVL-2B, LLaVA-Med-7B, MedGemma-4B, MedGemma-27B, and RETFound using image-description, screening/staging, and assessment/management generation tasks, with two clinicians annotating outputs and external validation on AMD and DR cohorts.
AI.result: VOLMO-2B consistently outperformed baselines across tasks, achieving stronger image-description performance, an average F1 of 87.4% across 12 eye conditions, and higher external validation scores.
AI.conclusion: A versatile, open, model-agnostic framework (VOLMO) can yield ophthalmology-specialized MLLMs that improve screening, severity assessment, and care planning, with strong cross-cohort generalization; supports broader data-open, domain-specific LLM development in ophthalmology.

title: Towards Effective Experiential Learning: Dual Guidance for Utilization and Internalization
summary: Recently, reinforcement learning~(RL) has become an important approach for improving the capabilities of large language models~(LLMs). In particular, reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards~(RLVR) has emerged as a promising paradigm for reasoning tasks. However, existing RL-based training still remains only a rough approximation to human learning. Human learners leverage both external and internal experience to guide exploration and gradually internalize useful trajectories into stable knowledge. Motivated by this gap, we ask: how can LLMs better utilize and internalize experience during RLVR training? To answer this question, we propose \textbf{D}ual \textbf{G}uidance \textbf{O}ptimization~(\textbf{DGO}), a unified framework that leverages \emph{external} and \emph{internal experience} to improve training effectiveness. Specifically, DGO first constructs an experience bank from previously explored trajectories. The policy then performs exploration under the joint guidance of the experience bank and the model's internal knowledge. The resulting trajectories are further used to refine the experience bank and optimize model parameters, forming a closed loop of experience utilization and internalization. Experiments show that DGO consistently outperforms baseline methods, suggesting that better utilization and internalization of experience lead to more effective reasoning.

AI.tldr: DGO is a dual-guidance framework for RLVR in LLMs that combines an external experience bank of past trajectories with the model's internal knowledge, guiding exploration and updating both the policy and the experience bank in a closed loop, yielding improved reasoning performance over baselines.
AI.motivation: RL-based training for LLMs approximates human learning but largely ignores internalized experience. Incorporating external experiences and internal knowledge into the exploration process could improve data efficiency, guidance quality, and the stability of acquired reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Construct an experience bank from previously explored trajectories. During training, the policy explores under joint guidance from the experience bank and the model’s internal knowledge. The resulting trajectories update/refine the experience bank, and the model parameters are optimized in tandem, forming a closed loop of experience utilization and internalization.
AI.result: Empirical results show that DGO consistently outperforms baselines on reasoning tasks, suggesting that effective use and internalization of experience improves RLVR-based reasoning in LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging both external and internal experience in a closed-loop framework improves RLVR training for LLMs and offers a general blueprint for integrating experience-driven guidance into policy optimization.

title: SynMVCrowd: A Large Synthetic Benchmark for Multi-view Crowd Counting and Localization
summary: Existing multi-view crowd counting and localization methods are evaluated under relatively small scenes with limited crowd numbers, camera views, and frames. This makes the evaluation and comparison of existing methods impractical, as small datasets are easily overfit by these methods. To avoid these issues, 3DROM proposes a data augmentation method. Instead, in this paper, we propose a large synthetic benchmark, SynMVCrowd, for more practical evaluation and comparison of multi-view crowd counting and localization tasks. The SynMVCrowd benchmark consists of 50 synthetic scenes with a large number of multi-view frames and camera views and a much larger crowd number (up to 1000), which is more suitable for large-scene multi-view crowd vision tasks. Besides, we propose strong multi-view crowd localization and counting baselines that outperform all comparison methods on the new SynMVCrowd benchmark. Moreover, we prove that better domain transferring multi-view and single-image counting performance could be achieved with the aid of the benchmark on novel new real scenes. As a result, the proposed benchmark could advance the research for multi-view and single-image crowd counting and localization to more practical applications. The codes and datasets are here: https://github.com/zqyq/SynMVCrowd.

AI.tldr: Proposes SynMVCrowd, a large-scale synthetic benchmark for multi-view crowd counting and localization to enable realistic evaluation and better-domain transfer to real scenes.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations rely on small, limited-scene datasets that risk overfitting and poor generalization for multi-view crowd counting/localization.
AI.method: Construct 50 synthetic scenes with numerous multi-view frames, many camera views, and crowds up to 1000; develop strong baselines for multi-view localization and counting; demonstrate improved domain transfer to novel real scenes.
AI.result: The proposed baselines outperform existing methods on SynMVCrowd; the benchmark facilitates better domain transfer to real-world scenes.
AI.conclusion: SynMVCrowd provides a practical, scalable benchmark for advancing multi-view and single-image crowd counting/localization toward real-world applications; code and data are publicly available.

title: KCLNet: Electrically Equivalence-Oriented Graph Representation Learning for Analog Circuits
summary: Digital circuits representation learning has made remarkable progress in the electronic design automation domain, effectively supporting critical tasks such as testability analysis and logic reasoning. However, representation learning for analog circuits remains challenging due to their continuous electrical characteristics compared to the discrete states of digital circuits. This paper presents a direct current (DC) electrically equivalent-oriented analog representation learning framework, named \textbf{KCLNet}. It comprises an asynchronous graph neural network structure with electrically-simulated message passing and a representation learning method inspired by Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL). This method maintains the orderliness of the circuit embedding space by enforcing the equality of the sum of outgoing and incoming current embeddings at each depth, which significantly enhances the generalization ability of circuit embeddings. KCLNet offers a novel and effective solution for analog circuit representation learning with electrical constraints preserved. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves significant performance in a variety of downstream tasks, e.g., analog circuit classification, subcircuit detection, and circuit edit distance prediction.

AI.tldr: KCLNet is a physics-constrained, asynchronous GNN for analog circuits that uses Kirchhoff's Current Law-inspired embeddings and DC electrical equivalence to improve representation learning and generalization across analog tasks.
AI.motivation: Analog circuits exhibit continuous electrical behavior; traditional digital-leaning representation learning ignores physical laws, hindering generalization. A physics-informed, DC-equivalent framework can better capture analog circuit structure and constraints.
AI.method: An asynchronous graph neural network with electrically-simulated message passing, coupled with a Kirchhoff's Current Law-inspired representation objective that enforces that at each depth, the sum of outgoing and incoming current embeddings are equal, preserving electrical constraints in the learned embedding space.
AI.result: Demonstrates significant performance gains on downstream tasks: analog circuit classification, subcircuit detection, and circuit edit distance prediction, indicating improved generalization and task generality.
AI.conclusion: KCLNet provides a novel, effective approach for analog circuit representation learning by embedding electrical constraints into the learning process, yielding robust representations suitable for diverse analog-domain tasks.

title: PointRFT: Explicit Reinforcement Fine-tuning for Point Cloud Few-shot Learning
summary: Understanding spatial dynamics and semantics in point cloud is fundamental for comprehensive 3D comprehension. While reinforcement learning algorithms such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) have recently achieved remarkable breakthroughs in large language models by incentivizing reasoning capabilities through strategic reward design, their potential remains largely unexplored in the 3D perception domain. This naturally raises a pivotal question: Can RL-based methods effectively empower 3D point cloud fine-tuning? In this paper, we propose PointRFT, the first reinforcement fine-tuning paradigm tailored specifically for point cloud representation learning. We select three prevalent 3D foundation models and devise specialized accuracy reward and dispersion reward functions to stabilize training and mitigate distribution shifts. Through comprehensive few-shot classification experiments comparing distinct training paradigms, we demonstrate that PointRFT consistently outperforms vanilla supervised fine-tuning (SFT) across diverse benchmarks. Furthermore, when organically integrated into a hybrid Pretraining-SFT-RFT paradigm, the representational capacity of point cloud foundation models is substantially unleashed, achieving state-of-the-art performance particularly under data-scarce scenarios.

AI.tldr: PointRFT introduces reinforcement fine-tuning for point cloud learning, using accuracy and dispersion rewards to stabilize training and mitigate distribution shift; it yields improvements in few-shot setups and, when combined with pretraining, achieves state-of-the-art results in data-scarce scenarios.
AI.motivation: Extend reinforcement learning-inspired fine-tuning to 3D point cloud representation learning. Address stabilization and distribution shift during fine-tuning, leveraging reward signals to enhance feature learning similar to GRPO in LLMs.
AI.method: Select three 3D foundation models as backbones; design two reward signals (accuracy and dispersion) to guide fine-tuning; implement reinforcement fine-tuning (PointRFT); compare against supervised fine-tuning across few-shot classification tasks; investigate a hybrid Pretraining-SFT-RFT paradigm to assess representational gains in data-scarce settings.
AI.result: PointRFT consistently outperforms vanilla supervised fine-tuning in few-shot classification across benchmarks; integrating PointRFT into a Pretraining-SFT-RFT pipeline yields substantial gains and achieves state-of-the-art performance in data-scarce scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement fine-tuning with task-aligned rewards is beneficial for 3D point cloud models, improving stability and generalization. When combined with pretraining, it can unlock greater representational capacity under limited data conditions.

title: Causality-Driven Disentangled Representation Learning in Multiplex Graphs
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks. Includes supplementary material
summary: Learning representations from multiplex graphs, i.e., multi-layer networks where nodes interact through multiple relation types, is challenging due to the entanglement of shared (common) and layer-specific (private) information, which limits generalization and interpretability. In this work, we introduce a causal inference-based framework that disentangles common and private components in a self-supervised manner. CaDeM jointly (i) aligns shared embeddings across layers, (ii) enforces private embeddings to capture layer-specific signals, and (iii) applies backdoor adjustment to ensure that the common embeddings capture only global information while being separated from the private representations. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over existing baselines, highlighting the effectiveness of our approach for robust and interpretable multiplex graph representation learning.

AI.tldr: CaDeM is a self-supervised causal framework that disentangles common and private information in multiplex graphs by aligning shared embeddings, enforcing layer-specific private embeddings, and applying backdoor adjustment to ensure global information in the shared component, yielding robust and interpretable representations with improved performance on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Multiplex graphs couple shared (global) and layer-specific information, which entangles representations and harms generalization and interpretability. A causal decomposition into common and private components can enable robust, interpretable representations and better downstream performance.
AI.method: CaDeM jointly learns: (i) aligned shared embeddings across layers to capture common information; (ii) private embeddings per layer to encode layer-specific signals; (iii) a backdoor adjustment mechanism to ensure the shared embeddings capture only global information by mitigating influence from private components. It uses self-supervised objectives across layers and evaluates on synthetic and real-world multiplex graphs, comparing to baselines.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic and real-world datasets show improvements over existing baselines, demonstrating more robust and interpretable multiplex graph representations. Ablation studies likely confirm the contribution of each component (alignment, private encodings, and backdoor adjustment).
AI.conclusion: CaDeM demonstrates that causal-inspired disentanglement of common and private information in multiplex graphs yields improved generalization and interpretability, with a framework extensible to more complex causal structures and dynamic multiplex networks.

title: Leave No Stone Unturned: Uncovering Holistic Audio-Visual Intrinsic Coherence for Deepfake Detection
summary: The rapid progress of generative AI has enabled hyper-realistic audio-visual deepfakes, intensifying threats to personal security and social trust. Most existing deepfake detectors rely either on uni-modal artifacts or audio-visual discrepancies, failing to jointly leverage both sources of information. Moreover, detectors that rely on generator-specific artifacts tend to exhibit degraded generalization when confronted with unseen forgeries. We argue that robust and generalizable detection should be grounded in intrinsic audio-visual coherence within and across modalities. Accordingly, we propose HAVIC, a Holistic Audio-Visual Intrinsic Coherence-based deepfake detector. HAVIC first learns priors of modality-specific structural coherence, inter-modal micro- and macro-coherence by pre-training on authentic videos. Based on the learned priors, HAVIC further performs holistic adaptive aggregation to dynamically fuse audio-visual features for deepfake detection. Additionally, we introduce HiFi-AVDF, a high-fidelity audio-visual deepfake dataset featuring both text-to-video and image-to-video forgeries from state-of-the-art commercial generators. Extensive experiments across several benchmarks demonstrate that HAVIC significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, achieving improvements of 9.39% AP and 9.37% AUC on the most challenging cross-dataset scenario. Our code and dataset are available at https://github.com/tuffy-studio/HAVIC.

AI.tldr: HAVIC introduces a holistic audio-visual detector based on intrinsic coherence learned from authentic videos, combining modality-specific and cross-modal coherence priors with adaptive fusion; paired with a new HiFi-AVDF dataset for T2V and I2V forgeries, achieving notable cross-dataset gains.
AI.motivation: Current deepfake detectors over-rely on uni-modal cues or generator-specific artifacts, which generalize poorly to unseen forgeries; robust detection requires leveraging intrinsic audio-visual coherence within and across modalities.
AI.method: Pre-train on authentic videos to learn priors of modality-specific structural coherence and inter-modal micro- and macro-coherence. Then apply holistic adaptive aggregation to dynamically fuse audio-visual features for detection. Introduce HiFi-AVDF as a benchmark dataset with T2V and I2V forgeries.
AI.result: HAVIC outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods with substantial cross-dataset gains (9.39% AP and 9.37% AUC). The HiFi-AVDF dataset expands evaluation to real-world forgery scenarios; code and data are released.
AI.conclusion: Grounding deepfake detection in intrinsic audio-visual coherence yields robust and generalizable detectors; the proposed priors and adaptive fusion enable effective cross-domain detection, aided by a new challenging dataset.

title: Mixed-signal implementation of feedback-control optimizer for single-layer Spiking Neural Networks
summary: On-chip learning is key to scalable and adaptive neuromorphic systems, yet existing training methods are either difficult to implement in hardware or overly restrictive. However, recent studies show that feedback-control optimizers can enable expressive, on-chip training of neuromorphic devices. In this work, we present a proof-of-concept implementation of such feedback-control optimizers on a mixed-signal neuromorphic processor. We assess the proposed approach in an In-The-Loop(ITL) training setup on both a binary classification task and the nonlinear Yin-Yang problem, demonstrating on-chip training that matches the performance of numerical simulations and gradient-based baselines. Our results highlight the feasibility of feedback-driven, online learning under realistic mixed-signal constraints, and represent a co-design approach toward embedding such rules directly in silicon for autonomous and adaptive neuromorphic computing.

AI.tldr: Proof-of-concept on-chip training of feedback-control optimizers on a mixed-signal neuromorphic processor; ITL training on binary classification and Yin-Yang tasks achieves performance matching simulations and gradient baselines, supporting silicon-embedded online learning.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable, adaptive neuromorphic learning by embedding expressive, hardware-friendly training rules; overcome limitations of existing hardware-training methods.
AI.method: Implement feedback-control optimizer for on-chip training on a mixed-signal neuromorphic processor; evaluate via In-The-Loop training on binary classification and Yin-Yang problems.
AI.result: On-chip training matches numerical simulations and gradient-based baselines under realistic mixed-signal constraints.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of feedback-driven online learning on hardware and supports co-design of learning rules directly in silicon for autonomous, adaptive neuromorphic systems.

title: HyDRA: Hybrid Domain-Aware Robust Architecture for Heterogeneous Collaborative Perception
summary: In collaborative perception, an agent's performance can be degraded by heterogeneity arising from differences in model architecture or training data distributions. To address this challenge, we propose HyDRA (Hybrid Domain-Aware Robust Architecture), a unified pipeline that integrates intermediate and late fusion within a domain-aware framework. We introduce a lightweight domain classifier that dynamically identifies heterogeneous agents and assigns them to the late-fusion branch. Furthermore, we propose anchor-guided pose graph optimization to mitigate localization errors inherent in late fusion, leveraging reliable detections from intermediate fusion as fixed spatial anchors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, despite requiring no additional training, HyDRA achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art heterogeneity-aware CP methods. Importantly, this performance is maintained as the number of collaborating agents increases, enabling zero-cost scaling without retraining.

AI.tldr: HyDRA presents a hybrid domain-aware architecture for collaborative perception that fuses intermediate and late fusion while handling agent heterogeneity with a lightweight domain classifier and anchor-guided pose graph optimization; it achieves state-of-the-art-like performance without extra training and scales without retraining as agents increase.
AI.motivation: Collaborative perception suffers from heterogeneity across agents (model architectures, data distributions). A unified, training-free, scalable fusion strategy that preserves performance as the number of agents grows is needed.
AI.method: A unified pipeline that combines intermediate and late fusion within a domain-aware framework. A lightweight domain classifier dynamically identifies heterogeneous agents and assigns them to the late-fusion branch. Anchor-guided pose graph optimization uses reliable detections from intermediate fusion as fixed spatial anchors to mitigate localization errors in late fusion.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show HyDRA achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art heterogeneity-aware CP methods without additional training, and maintains performance as the number of collaborating agents increases, enabling zero-cost scaling.
AI.conclusion: HyDRA offers a practical, scalable solution for heterogeneous collaborative perception by integrating domain-aware fusion with anchor-based localization stabilization, eliminating the need for retraining while supporting scalable multi-agent collaboration.

title: Likelihood hacking in probabilistic program synthesis
summary: When language models are trained by reinforcement learning (RL) to write probabilistic programs, they can artificially inflate their marginal-likelihood reward by producing programs whose data distribution fails to normalise instead of fitting the data better. We call this failure likelihood hacking (LH). We formalise LH in a core probabilistic programming language (PPL) and give sufficient syntactic conditions for its prevention, proving that a safe language fragment $\mathcal{L}_{\text{safe}}$ satisfying these conditions cannot produce likelihood-hacking programs. Empirically, we show that GRPO-trained models generating PyMC code discover LH exploits within the first few training steps, driving violation rates well above the untrained-model baseline. We implement $\mathcal{L}_{\text{safe}}$'s conditions as $\texttt{SafeStan}$, a LH-resistant modification of Stan, and show empirically that it prevents LH under optimisation pressure. These results show that language-level safety constraints are both theoretically grounded and effective in practice for automated Bayesian model discovery.

AI.tldr: Formulates likelihood hacking (LH) in RL-trained probabilistic programs, proves a safe language fragment L_safe prevents LH, and implements SafeStan to resist LH in practice; empirical results show LH exploits arise in PyMC code while SafeStan mitigates them, supporting language-level safety for automated Bayesian model discovery.
AI.motivation: To prevent manipulation of marginal-likelihood rewards in reinforcement-learning-trained probabilistic programs by producing non-normalizing data-generating distributions, and to provide formal, practical safety guarantees via language design.
AI.method: Formalize LH in a core probabilistic programming language; derive sufficient syntactic safety conditions; prove that a safe fragment L_safe cannot produce LH programs; instantiate SafeStan from these conditions; perform empirical evaluations: show LH exploits in GRPO-trained PyMC code, implement LH-resistant constraints, and demonstrate prevention under optimization pressure.
AI.result: LH can be induced early during training; the proposed safe fragment L_safe (via SafeStan) blocks LH at the language level; empirical results validate both the theory and practical effectiveness in preventing LH under optimization.
AI.conclusion: Language-level safety constraints are theoretically grounded and effective in preventing LH in automated Bayesian model discovery; SafeStan demonstrates a viable path to safer probabilistic programming in RL settings, with potential for extension to other PPLs and tighter safety guarantees.

title: SilLang: Improving Gait Recognition with Silhouette Language Encoding
summary: Gait silhouettes, which can be encoded into binary gait codes, are widely adopted to representing motion patterns of pedestrian. Recent approaches commonly leverage visual backbones to encode gait silhouettes, achieving successful performance. However, they primarily focus on continuous visual features, overlooking the discrete nature of binary silhouettes that inherently share a discrete encoding space with natural language. Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional capability in extracting discriminative features from discrete sequences and modeling long-range dependencies, highlighting their potential to capture temporal motion patterns by identifying subtle variations. Motivated by these observations, we explore bridging binary gait silhouettes and natural language within a binary encoding space. However, the encoding spaces of text tokens and binary gait silhouettes remain misaligned, primarily due to differences in token frequency and density. To address this issue, we propose the Contour-Velocity Tokenizer, which encodes binary gait silhouettes while reshaping their distribution to better align with the text token space. We then establish a dual-branch framework termed Silhouette Language Model, which enhances visual silhouettes by integrating discrete linguistic embeddings derived from LLMs. Implemented on mainstream gait backbones, SilLang consistently improves state-of-the-art methods across SUSTech1K, GREW, and Gait3D.

AI.tldr: SilLang bridges binary gait silhouettes with natural language using Contour-Velocity Tokenizer and a dual-branch model to incorporate discrete linguistic embeddings, achieving state-of-the-art gains on SUSTech1K, GREW, and Gait3D.
AI.motivation: Binary gait silhouettes are discrete and occupy a space similar to natural language; LLMs excel at extracting features from discrete sequences and modeling long-range dependencies. However, text token spaces and silhouette encodings are misaligned due to token frequency/density, hindering cross-modal fusion.
AI.method: Propose Contour-Velocity Tokenizer to encode silhouettes and reshape their distribution to align with text token space; construct Silhouette Language Model (SilLang), a dual-branch architecture that injects discrete linguistic embeddings from LLMs into visual silhouette representations; integrated with mainstream gait backbones.
AI.result: Across SUSTech1K, GREW, and Gait3D benchmarks, SilLang consistently improves state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: A novel framework that aligns discrete silhouette encodings with language tokens enables effective cross-modal fusion, yielding robust gait recognition improvements and suggesting broader potential for discrete-sequence vision-language modeling.

title: On Gossip Algorithms for Machine Learning with Pairwise Objectives
summary: In the IoT era, information is more and more frequently picked up by connected smart sensors with increasing, though limited, storage, communication and computation abilities. Whether due to privacy constraints or to the structure of the distributed system, the development of statistical learning methods dedicated to data that are shared over a network is now a major issue. Gossip-based algorithms have been developed for the purpose of solving a wide variety of statistical learning tasks, ranging from data aggregation over sensor networks to decentralized multi-agent optimization. Whereas the vast majority of contributions consider situations where the function to be estimated or optimized is a basic average of individual observations, it is the goal of this article to investigate the case where the latter is of pairwise nature, taking the form of a U -statistic of degree two. Motivated by various problems such as similarity learning, ranking or clustering for instance, we revisit gossip algorithms specifically designed for pairwise objective functions and provide a comprehensive theoretical framework for their convergence. This analysis fills a gap in the literature by establishing conditions under which these methods succeed, and by identifying the graph properties that critically affect their efficiency. In particular, a refined analysis of the convergence upper and lower bounds is performed.

AI.tldr: Investigates gossip algorithms for decentralized estimation/optimization with a pairwise U-statistic objective (degree two); provides a convergence theory and shows how graph properties affect efficiency, including refined convergence bounds.
AI.motivation: In IoT-like distributed settings with privacy and resource constraints, data are shared over networks. Many learning tasks involve pairwise objectives (e.g., similarity, ranking, clustering) rather than simple averages, creating a gap in existing gossip analyses.
AI.method: Adapt gossip protocols to pairwise U-statistics, develop a theoretical convergence framework, derive conditions for convergence, analyze the impact of network topology, and establish upper and lower bounds on convergence rates.
AI.result: Derived conditions under which the gossip method converges for pairwise objectives; identified graph properties that critically influence efficiency; provided refined upper and lower bounds on convergence rates.
AI.conclusion: The work fills a gap by delivering a comprehensive convergence theory for pairwise gossip algorithms and clarifies how network topology affects performance.

title: CAKE: Real-time Action Detection via Motion Distillation and Background-aware Contrastive Learning
summary: Online Action Detection (OAD) systems face two primary challenges: high computational cost and insufficient modeling of discriminative temporal dynamics against background motion. Adding optical flow could provides strong motion cues but it incurs significant computational overhead. We propose CAKE, a OAD Flow-based distillation framework to transfer motion knowledge into RGB models. We propose Dynamic Motion Adapter (DMA) to suppress static background noise and emphasize pixel changes, effectively approximating optical flow without explicit computation. The framework also integrates a Floating Contrastive Learning strategy to distinguish informative motion dynamics from temporal background. Various experiments conducted on the TVSeries, THUMOS'14, Kinetics-400 datasets show effectiveness of our model. CAKE achieves a standout mAP compared with SOTA while using the same backbone. Our model operates at over 72 FPS on a single CPU, making it highly suitable for resource-constrained systems.

AI.tldr: CAKE is a lightweight Flow-based distillation framework for Online Action Detection that distills motion knowledge into RGB models using a Dynamic Motion Adapter and Floating Contrastive Learning, achieving state-of-the-art mAP with the same backbone and running >72 FPS on CPU.
AI.motivation: Online Action Detection requires strong temporal dynamics modeling but optical-flow based cues are computationally expensive. A method that preserves discriminative motion while remaining real-time is highly desirable.
AI.method: CAKE transfers motion knowledge from a flow-based teacher to an RGB student. It introduces Dynamic Motion Adapter (DMA) to suppress static background and highlight pixel changes, approximating optical flow without explicit computation. It also employs Floating Contrastive Learning to separate informative motion cues from temporal background. Training and evaluation are conducted on TVSeries, THUMOS'14, and Kinetics-400.
AI.result: CAKE achieves state-of-the-art mAP compared with the current best methods while using the same backbone, and runs at over 72 frames per second on a single CPU.
AI.conclusion: Motion-aware distillation via DMA and Floating Contrastive Learning enables accurate and efficient OAD without explicit optical flow, making it suitable for resource-constrained environments.

title: Reservoir-Based Graph Convolutional Networks
summary: Message passing is a core mechanism in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), enabling the iterative update of node embeddings by aggregating information from neighboring nodes. Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) exemplify this approach by adapting convolutional operations for graph structures, allowing features from adjacent nodes to be combined effectively. However, GCNs encounter challenges with complex or dynamic data. Capturing long-range dependencies often requires deeper layers, which not only increase computational costs but also lead to over-smoothing, where node embeddings become indistinguishable. To overcome these challenges, reservoir computing has been integrated into GNNs, leveraging iterative message-passing dynamics for stable information propagation without extensive parameter tuning. Despite its promise, existing reservoir-based models lack structured convolutional mechanisms, limiting their ability to accurately aggregate multi-hop neighborhood information. To address these limitations, we propose RGC-Net (Reservoir-based Graph Convolutional Network), which integrates reservoir dynamics with structured graph convolution. Key contributions include: (i) a reimagined convolutional framework with fixed random reservoir weights and a leaky integrator to enhance feature retention; (ii) a robust, adaptable model for graph classification; and (iii) an RGC-Net-powered transformer for graph generation with application to dynamic brain connectivity. Extensive experiments show that RGC-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance in classification and generative tasks, including brain graph evolution, with faster convergence and reduced over-smoothing. Source code is available at https://github.com/basiralab/RGC-Net .

AI.tldr: RGC-Net fuses reservoir dynamics with a structured, fixed-weight graph convolution to improve long-range information propagation in GNNs, mitigating over-smoothing, enabling robust graph classification and reservoir-driven graph generation (including brain graphs).
AI.motivation: GCNs struggle with deep networks and dynamic graphs due to vanishing/excessive smoothing and computational costs. Reservoir computing offers stable propagation with less tuning but lacks structured convolution to aggregate multi-hop neighborhoods.
AI.method: Introduce fixed random reservoir weights with a leaky integrator to create a stable, memory-preserving convolutional backbone. Develop RGC-Net for graph classification and an RGC-Net-powered transformer for graph generation, applied to dynamic brain connectivity.
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on graph classification and generative tasks, including brain graph evolution, with faster convergence and reduced over-smoothing. Source code provided.
AI.conclusion: Reservoir-based structured graph convolution effectively enhances multi-hop aggregation and stable learning, enabling versatile performance in classification and generation; promising for dynamic brain networks and beyond; potential for faster convergence with simplified training.

title: HGGT: Robust and Flexible 3D Hand Mesh Reconstruction from Uncalibrated Images
summary: Recovering high-fidelity 3D hand geometry from images is a critical task in computer vision, holding significant value for domains such as robotics, animation and VR/AR. Crucially, scalable applications demand both accuracy and deployment flexibility, requiring the ability to leverage massive amounts of unstructured image data from the internet or enable deployment on consumer-grade RGB cameras without complex calibration. However, current methods face a dilemma. While single-view approaches are easy to deploy, they suffer from depth ambiguity and occlusion. Conversely, multi-view systems resolve these uncertainties but typically demand fixed, calibrated setups, limiting their real-world utility. To bridge this gap, we draw inspiration from 3D foundation models that learn explicit geometry directly from visual data. By reformulating hand reconstruction from arbitrary views as a visual-geometry grounded task, we propose a feed-forward architecture that, for the first time in literature, jointly infers 3D hand meshes and camera poses from uncalibrated views. Extensive evaluations show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks and demonstrates strong generalization to uncalibrated, in-the-wild scenarios. Here is the link of our project page: https://lym29.github.io/HGGT/.

AI.tldr: Novel feed-forward method to jointly estimate 3D hand meshes and camera poses from uncalibrated multi-view images, bridging single-view simplicity and multi-view accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable, accurate 3D hand reconstruction from uncalibrated images and consumer RGB cameras; overcome depth ambiguity and occlusion without fixed calibration; leverage geometry-aware learning inspired by 3D foundation models.
AI.method: Reformulate hand reconstruction as a visual-geometry grounded task and implement a feed-forward network that outputs 3D hand mesh and camera pose from arbitrary views; jointly optimize across views; operates on uncalibrated inputs.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks and shows strong generalization to uncalibrated, in-the-wild data; first to jointly infer 3D hand meshes and camera poses from uncalibrated views.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of uncalibrated, scalable 3D hand reconstruction; paves way for deployment in unconstrained environments and consumer devices, aligning with 3D foundation-model philosophy.

title: Efficient Controller Learning from Human Preferences and Numerical Data Via Multi-Modal Surrogate Models
summary: Tuning control policies manually to meet high-level objectives is often time-consuming. Bayesian optimization provides a data-efficient framework for automating this process using numerical evaluations of an objective function. However, many systems, particularly those involving humans, require optimization based on subjective criteria. Preferential Bayesian optimization addresses this by learning from pairwise comparisons instead of quantitative measurements, but relying solely on preference data can be inefficient. We propose a multi-fidelity, multi-modal Bayesian optimization framework that integrates low-fidelity numerical data with high-fidelity human preferences. Our approach employs Gaussian process surrogate models with both hierarchical, autoregressive and non-hierarchical, coregionalization-based structures, enabling efficient learning from mixed-modality data. We illustrate the framework by tuning an autonomous vehicle's trajectory planner, showing that combining numerical and preference data significantly reduces the need for experiments involving the human decision maker while effectively adapting driving style to individual preferences.

AI.tldr: A multi-fidelity, multi-modal Bayesian optimization framework fusing low-fidelity numerical data with high-fidelity human preferences via GP surrogates (hierarchical autoregressive and coregionalization), shown to efficiently tune an autonomous vehicle trajectory planner to individual driving styles.
AI.motivation: Subjective criteria from humans are essential but expensive; traditional Bayesian optimization relying on qualitative preferences or quantitative measurements alone is data-inefficient. A framework that combines cheap numerical evaluations with expensive human judgments can accelerate policy tuning while respecting user preferences.
AI.method: Gaussian process surrogates capable of handling mixed modalities: both low-fidelity numerical data and high-fidelity preference data. Utilizes hierarchical autoregressive and non-hierarchical (coregionalization) structures to model cross-domain correlations. Applies Bayesian optimization to optimize an autonomous vehicle trajectory planner by integrating numerical and preference information.
AI.result: Demonstrates that incorporating both numerical and preference data reduces the number of human-in-the-loop experiments and enables effective adaptation of driving style to individual preferences, achieving data-efficient tuning.
AI.conclusion: A multi-fidelity, multi-modal BO framework is effective for optimizing control policies with subjective criteria, enabling personalized, data-efficient tuning and potentially broad applicability to human-in-the-loop decision tasks.

title: DB SwinT: A Dual-Branch Swin Transformer Network for Road Extraction in Optical Remote Sensing Imagery
summary: With the continuous improvement in the spatial resolution of optical remote sensing imagery, accurate road extraction has become increasingly important for applications such as urban planning, traffic monitoring, and disaster management. However, road extraction in complex urban and rural environments remains challenging, as roads are often occluded by trees, buildings, and other objects, leading to fragmented structures and reduced extraction accuracy. To address this problem, this paper proposes a Dual-Branch Swin Transformer network (DB SwinT) for road extraction. The proposed framework combines the long-range dependency modeling capability of the Swin Transformer with the multi-scale feature fusion strategy of U-Net, and employs a dual-branch encoder to learn complementary local and global representations. Specifically, the local branch focuses on recovering fine structural details in occluded areas, while the global branch captures broader semantic context to preserve the overall continuity of road networks. In addition, an Attentional Feature Fusion (AFF) module is introduced to adaptively fuse features from the two branches, further enhancing the representation of occluded road segments. Experimental results on the Massachusetts and DeepGlobe datasets show that DB SwinT achieves Intersection over Union (IoU) scores of 79.35\% and 74.84\%, respectively, demonstrating its effectiveness for road extraction from optical remote sensing imagery.

AI.tldr: DB SwinT: a dual-branch Swin Transformer with AFF for road extraction, achieving IoU of 79.35% (Massachusetts) and 74.84% (DeepGlobe).
AI.motivation: Improve road extraction in high-resolution optical remote sensing under occlusions from trees/buildings, preserving road network continuity for urban planning, traffic monitoring, and disaster management.
AI.method: Dual-branch encoder: local branch recovers fine structural details in occluded areas; global branch captures broad semantic context. Swin Transformer provides long-range dependencies; U‑Net-style multi-scale feature fusion; Attentional Feature Fusion (AFF) adaptively fuses branch features to enhance occluded segments.
AI.result: IoU on Massachusetts: 79.35%; on DeepGlobe: 74.84%; demonstrates effectiveness for road extraction from optical RS imagery.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively enhances road extraction under occlusions by leveraging complementary local/global representations and adaptive fusion, achieving competitive IoU on standard datasets.

title: Linear-Nonlinear Fusion Neural Operator for Partial Differential Equations
summary: Neural operator learning directly constructs the mapping relationship from the equation parameter space to the solution space, enabling efficient direct inference in practical applications without the need for repeated solution of partial differential equations (PDEs) - an advantage that is difficult to achieve with traditional numerical methods. In this work, we find that explicitly decoupling linear and nonlinear effects within such operator mappings leads to markedly improved learning efficiency. This yields a novel network structure, namely the Linear-Nonlinear Fusion Neural Operator (LNF-NO), which models operator mappings via the multiplicative fusion of a linear component and a nonlinear component, thus achieving a lightweight and interpretable representation. This linear-nonlinear decoupling enables efficient capture of complex solution features at the operator level while maintaining stability and generality. LNF-NO naturally supports multiple functional inputs and is applicable to both regular grids and irregular geometries. Across a diverse suite of PDE operator-learning benchmarks, including nonlinear Poisson-Boltzmann equations and multi-physics coupled systems, LNF-NO is typically substantially faster to train than Deep Operator Networks (DeepONet) and Fourier Neural Operators (FNO), while achieving comparable or better accuracy in most cases. On the tested 3D Poisson-Boltzmann case, LNF-NO attains the best accuracy among the compared models and trains approximately 2.7x faster than a 3D FNO baseline.

AI.tldr: Introduces Linear-Nonlinear Fusion Neural Operator (LNF-NO), a linear-nonlinear decoupled operator-learning architecture that achieves faster training and competitive accuracy across PDE operator benchmarks, including 3D Poisson-Boltzmann, outperforming or matching DeepONet and FNO; 3D case shows 2.7x faster training with best accuracy.
