AI.motivation: To improve learning efficiency and generalization of neural operators by decoupling linear and nonlinear effects, enabling lightweight, interpretable representations that can handle multiple inputs and irregular geometries.
AI.method: Multiplicative fusion of linear and nonlinear operator components; supports multiple functional inputs; designed to be lightweight; applicable to regular grids and irregular domains; evaluated on nonlinear Poisson-Boltzmann and multi-physics systems.
AI.result: Typically faster to train than DeepONet and FNO; comparable or better accuracy; 3D Poisson-Boltzmann case yields best accuracy among tested models and ~2.7x training speed-up over 3D FNO.
AI.conclusion: LNF-NO offers an efficient, stable, and generalizable operator-learning framework; linear-nonlinear decoupling yields improved learning efficiency; suitable for broad PDE operator-learning tasks.

title: UW-VOS: A Large-Scale Dataset for Underwater Video Object Segmentation
summary: Underwater Video Object Segmentation (VOS) is essential for marine exploration, yet open-air methods suffer significant degradation due to color distortion, low contrast, and prevalent camouflage. A primary hurdle is the lack of high-quality training data. To bridge this gap, we introduce $\textbf{UW-VOS}$, the first large-scale underwater VOS benchmark comprising 1,431 video sequences across 409 categories with 309,295 mask annotations, constructed via a semi-automatic data engine with rigorous human verification. We further propose $\textbf{SAM-U}$, a parameter-efficient framework that adapts SAM2 to the underwater domain. By inserting lightweight adapters into the image encoder, SAM-U achieves state-of-the-art performance with only $\sim$2$\%$ trainable parameters. Extensive experiments reveal that existing methods experience an average 13-point $\mathcal{J}\&\mathcal{F}$ drop on UW-VOS, while SAM-U effectively bridges this domain gap. Detailed attribute-based analysis further identifies small targets, camouflage, and exit-re-entry as critical bottlenecks, providing a roadmap for future research in robust underwater perception.

AI.tldr: Introduces UW-VOS, the first large-scale underwater VOS benchmark, and SAM-U, a parameter-efficient SAM2-based framework for underwater segmentation using adapters; results show improved robustness and a gap closure for underwater data compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity and domain gap of underwater video object segmentation data, compounded by underwater imaging challenges (color distortion, low contrast, camouflage), to enable robust marine perception.
AI.method: UW-VOS: build a semi-automatic, human-verified dataset with 1,431 video sequences, 409 categories, and 309,295 mask annotations. SAM-U: integrate lightweight adapters into the SAM2 image encoder to adapt it to underwater data, achieving ~2% trainable parameters.
AI.result: Existing VOS methods experience an average 13-point drop in J&F on UW-VOS; SAM-U narrows the domain gap and achieves state-of-the-art performance for underwater VOS with low trainable parameter count; attribute-based analysis highlights small targets, camouflage, and exit-re-entry as bottlenecks.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes UW-VOS as a challenging underwater benchmark and demonstrates that efficient adaptor-based adaptation of SAM2 can substantially improve underwater VOS performance, guiding future research towards robust underwater perception.

title: TsetlinWiSARD: On-Chip Training of Weightless Neural Networks using Tsetlin Automata on FPGAs
comment: Accepted at the 63rd Design Automation Conference (DAC 2026)
summary:
Increasing demands for adaptability, privacy, and security at the edge have persistently pushed the frontiers for a new generation of machine learning (ML) algorithms with training and inference capabilities on-chip. Weightless Neural Network (WNN) is such an algorithm that is principled on lookup table based simple neuron structures. As a result, it offers architectural benefits, such as low-latency, low-complexity inference, compared to deep neural networks that depend heavily on multiply-accumulate operations. However, traditional WNNs rely on memorization-based one-shot training, which either leads to overfitting and reduced accuracy or requires tedious post-training adjustments, limiting their effectiveness for efficient on chip training.
  In this work, we propose TsetlinWiSARD, a training approach for WNNs that leverages Tsetlin Automata (TAs) to enable probabilistic, feedback-driven learning. It overcomes the overfitting of WiSARD's one-shot training with iterative optimization, while maintaining simple, continuous binary feedback for efficient on-chip training. Central to our approach is a field programmable gate array (FPGA)-based training architecture that delivers state-of-the-art accuracy while significantly improving hardware efficiency. Our approach provides over 1000x faster training when compared with the traditional WiSARD implementation of WNNs. Further, we demonstrate 22% reduced resource usage, 93.3% lower latency, and 64.2% lower power consumption compared to FPGA-based training accelerators implementing other ML algorithms.

AI.tldr: Proposes TsetlinWiSARD, a TA-based training method for Weightless Neural Networks, enabling probabilistic, feedback-driven on-chip learning with FPGA support; achieves large training speedups and improved hardware efficiency over traditional WNN/WiSARD and other FPGA accelerators.
AI.motivation: Edge AI demands on-chip, privacy-preserving training with low latency and low power. Weightless Neural Networks offer simple, low-complexity inference but suffer from one-shot memorization training that causes overfitting or tedious adjustments. A lightweight, trainable WNN variant is needed for practical on-chip learning.
AI.method: Integrates Tsetlin Automata to provide probabilistic, binary-feedback guided learning for WNNs (WiSARD-like) to overcome one-shot memorization. Uses iterative optimization to reduce overfitting and enable on-chip training. Implements FPGA-based training architecture to deliver efficient hardware performance.
AI.result: Achieves >1000x faster training than traditional WiSARD. Shows 22% reduction in resource usage, 93.3% lower latency, and 64.2% lower power consumption relative to FPGA-based accelerators for other ML algorithms, while maintaining state-of-the-art accuracy for WNNs.
AI.conclusion: TA-based training on FPGA makes WNNs practical for on-chip, edge ML by delivering rapid training and improved hardware efficiency, addressing overfitting of one-shot WNN training and enabling scalable, low-power edge inference and learning.

title: COVTrack++: Learning Open-Vocabulary Multi-Object Tracking from Continuous Videos via a Synergistic Paradigm
summary: Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) has traditionally focused on a few specific categories, restricting its applicability to real-world scenarios involving diverse objects. Open-Vocabulary Multi-Object Tracking (OVMOT) addresses this by enabling tracking of arbitrary categories, including novel objects unseen during training. However, current progress is constrained by two challenges: the lack of continuously annotated video data for training, and the lack of a customized OVMOT framework to synergistically handle detection and association. We address the data bottleneck by constructing C-TAO, the first continuously annotated training set for OVMOT, which increases annotation density by 26x over the original TAO and captures smooth motion dynamics and intermediate object states. For the framework bottleneck, we propose COVTrack++, a synergistic framework that achieves a bidirectional reciprocal mechanism between detection and association through three modules: (1) Multi-Cue Adaptive Fusion (MCF) dynamically balances appearance, motion, and semantic cues for association feature learning; (2) Multi-Granularity Hierarchical Aggregation (MGA) exploits hierarchical spatial relationships in dense detections, where visible child nodes (e.g., object parts) assist occluded parent objects (e.g., whole body) for association feature enhancement; (3) Temporal Confidence Propagation (TCP) recovers flickering detections through high-confidence tracked objects boosting low-confidence candidates across frames, stabilizing trajectories. Extensive experiments on TAO demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with novel TETA reaching 35.4% and 30.5% on validation and test sets, improving novel AssocA by 4.8% and novel LocA by 5.8% over previous methods, and show strong zero-shot generalization on BDD100K. The code and dataset will be publicly available.

AI.tldr: Introduces C-TAO, the first continuously annotated training set for Open-Vocabulary MOT (OVMOT), and COVTrack++, a synergistic framework unifying detection and association via three modules (MCF, MGA, TCP) to boost open-category tracking. Demonstrates state-of-the-art results on TAO (TETA: 35.4%/30.5%), improved novel AssocA and LocA, plus strong zero-shot generalization on BDD100K; code and data to be released.
AI.motivation: Two bottlenecks limit OVMOT progress: (1) lack of continuously annotated video data for training and (2) absence of an integrated OVMOT framework that jointly optimizes detection and association. The work proposes a continuously annotated dataset (C-TAO) to capture smooth motion and intermediate states, and a synergistic framework (COVTrack++) to enable bidirectional learning between detection and association.
AI.method: Dataset contribution: C-TAO, the first continuously annotated training set for OVMOT, with 26x higher annotation density than TAO, capturing smooth motion and intermediate states. Framework contribution: COVTrack++ with three modules that enable bidirectional synergy between detection and association: (1) Multi-Cue Adaptive Fusion (MCF) to balance appearance, motion, and semantic cues for association features; (2) Multi-Granularity Hierarchical Aggregation (MGA) to exploit hierarchical spatial relations in dense detections, using visible child nodes to assist occluded parent objects for improved association; (3) Temporal Confidence Propagation (TCP) to propagate high-confidence tracklets to boost low-confidence detections across frames, stabilizing trajectories.
AI.result: On TAO, achieves state-of-the-art performance with novel TETA of 35.4% (validation) and 30.5% (test). It improves novel AssocA by 4.8% and novel LocA by 5.8% over prior methods and demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization on BDD100K.
AI.conclusion: The authors will release code and C-TAO dataset publicly, enabling reproducibility and broader evaluation in OVMOT.

title: Mitigating Object Hallucinations in LVLMs via Attention Imbalance Rectification
summary: Object hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) severely compromises their reliability in real-world applications, posing a critical barrier to their deployment in high-stakes scenarios such as autonomous driving and medical image analysis. Through systematic empirical investigation, we identify that the imbalanced attention allocation, both across modalities (i.e., vision and language) and within modalities (among individual tokens), exhibits a strong causal correlation with the occurrence of object hallucination. Leveraging this insight, we introduce a novel concept termed attention imbalance, which not only quantifies the degree of attention disparity but also visually delineates the underlying patterns (e.g., over-attentiveness to irrelevant language tokens or under-attentiveness to discriminative visual features) that drive object hallucination. To mitigate object hallucination, we further propose Attention Imbalance Rectification (AIR), a lightweight decoding-time intervention method that reallocates attention weights and adjusts attention distributions to rectify modality-wise and token-wise imbalances. Extensive evaluations on four mainstream LVLMs and three benchmarks (CHAIR, POPE, and MM-Vet) with seven baselines demonstrate that AIR consistently reduces object hallucination rates, achieving up to a 35.1% reduction compared to the baselines, while improving up to 15.9% of LVLMs' general capability across diverse vision-language tasks.

AI.tldr: Identifies attention imbalance as a root cause of object hallucination in LVLMs; proposes AIR to rectify decoding-time attention distribution, reducing hallucinations and boosting general capability.
AI.motivation: Object hallucination undermines reliability in high-stakes vision-language tasks; previous work lacks a systematic diagnostic of attention allocation; quantify attention imbalance to link to hallucinations and provide a corrective method.
AI.method: Define attention imbalance across modalities (vision vs language) and tokens; introduce visualizations of attention patterns; develop AIR as a lightweight decoding-time intervention that reallocates attention weights and normalizes distributions to correct imbalances.
AI.result: Evaluated on four LVLMs and three benchmarks (CHAIR, POPE, MM-Vet) against seven baselines; AIR reduces object hallucination rates by up to 35.1% and improves general capability by up to 15.9% across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Attention imbalance is a practical diagnostic and mitigation target; AIR offers an effective, lightweight decoding-time solution enhancing reliability and performance in LVLMs.

title: A Deep Dive into Scaling RL for Code Generation with Synthetic Data and Curricula
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for improving large language models beyond supervised fine-tuning, yet sustaining performance gains at scale remains an open challenge, as data diversity and structure, rather than volume alone, become the limiting factor. We address this by introducing a scalable multi-turn synthetic data generation pipeline in which a teacher model iteratively refines problems based on in-context student performance summaries, producing structured difficulty progressions without any teacher fine-tuning. Compared to single-turn generation, this multi-turn approach substantially improves the yield of valid synthetic problems and naturally produces stepping stones, i.e. easier and harder variants of the same core task, that support curriculum-based training. We systematically study how task difficulty, curriculum scheduling, and environment diversity interact during RL training across the Llama3.1-8B Instruct and Qwen3-8B Base model families, with additional scaling experiments on Qwen2.5-32B. Our results show that synthetic augmentation consistently improves in-domain code and in most cases out-of-domain math performance, and we provide empirical insights into how curriculum design and data diversity jointly shape RL training dynamics.

AI.tldr: A scalable multi-turn synthetic data pipeline for RL with LLMs that uses a teacher to refine problems based on in-context student performance, producing curriculum-friendly stepping stones and improving task yield across multiple model families and domains.
AI.motivation: Data diversity and structure, not volume alone, limit scaling in RL for LLMs; need scalable, structured synthetic data generation that supports curriculum-based training without teacher fine-tuning.
AI.method: A multi-turn generation loop where a teacher uses in-context student performance summaries to iteratively refine problems, creating difficulty progressions and stepping stones; study across Llama3.1-8B Instruct and Qwen3-8B Base, with scaling to Qwen2.5-32B; analyze task difficulty, curriculum scheduling, and environment diversity.
AI.result: Synthetic augmentation improves in-domain code and often out-of-domain math performance; higher yield of valid synthetic problems; naturally produces stepping stones that support curriculum-based RL training; insights into how curriculum design and data diversity shape training dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Curriculum design and data diversity jointly shape RL training dynamics; the proposed multi-turn synthetic data generation pipeline is effective for scaling RL with LLMs and does not require teacher fine-tuning.

title: Decompose and Transfer: CoT-Prompting Enhanced Alignment for Open-Vocabulary Temporal Action Detection
summary: Open-Vocabulary Temporal Action Detection (OV-TAD) aims to classify and localize action segments in untrimmed videos for unseen categories. Previous methods rely solely on global alignment between label-level semantics and visual features, which is insufficient to transfer temporal consistent visual knowledge from seen to unseen classes. To address this, we propose a Phase-wise Decomposition and Alignment (PDA) framework, which enables fine-grained action pattern learning for effective prior knowledge transfer. Specifically, we first introduce the CoT-Prompting Semantic Decomposition (CSD) module, which leverages the chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning ability of large language models to automatically decompose action labels into coherent phase-level descriptions, emulating human cognitive processes. Then, Text-infused Foreground Filtering (TIF) module is introduced to adaptively filter action-relevant segments for each phase leveraging phase-wise semantic cues, producing semantically aligned visual representations. Furthermore, we propose the Adaptive Phase-wise Alignment (APA) module to perform phase-level visual-textual matching, and adaptively aggregates alignment results across phases for final prediction. This adaptive phase-wise alignment facilitates the capture of transferable action patterns and significantly enhances generalization to unseen actions. Extensive experiments on two OV-TAD benchmarks demonstrated the superiority of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: PDA: phase-wise decomposition and alignment for open-vocabulary temporal action detection using CoT-based semantic decomposition, phase-aware filtering, and adaptive phase-wise alignment to improve generalization to unseen actions.
AI.motivation: Global label-semantics alignment with visual features is insufficient for transferring temporally consistent knowledge from seen to unseen action categories; phase-level patterns are needed.
AI.method: 1) CoT-Prompting Semantic Decomposition (CSD) uses chain-of-thought prompting to decompose action labels into phase-level descriptions. 2) Text-infused Foreground Filtering (TIF) adaptively filters action-relevant segments per phase using phase-wise semantic cues to produce semantically aligned visuals. 3) Adaptive Phase-wise Alignment (APA) performs phase-level visual-textual matching and adaptively aggregates results across phases for final prediction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two OV-TAD benchmarks show the method achieves superior generalization to unseen actions and overall performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Phase-wise decomposition and alignment enables transferable action patterns and enhances open-vocabulary action detection generalization.

title: When Understanding Becomes a Risk: Authenticity and Safety Risks in the Emerging Image Generation Paradigm
summary: Recently, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have emerged as a unified paradigm for language and image generation. Compared with diffusion models, MLLMs possess a much stronger capability for semantic understanding, enabling them to process more complex textual inputs and comprehend richer contextual meanings. However, this enhanced semantic ability may also introduce new and potentially greater safety risks. Taking diffusion models as a reference point, we systematically analyze and compare the safety risks of emerging MLLMs along two dimensions: unsafe content generation and fake image synthesis. Across multiple unsafe generation benchmark datasets, we observe that MLLMs tend to generate more unsafe images than diffusion models. This difference partly arises because diffusion models often fail to interpret abstract prompts, producing corrupted outputs, whereas MLLMs can comprehend these prompts and generate unsafe content. For current advanced fake image detectors, MLLM-generated images are also notably harder to identify. Even when detectors are retrained with MLLMs-specific data, they can still be bypassed by simply providing MLLMs with longer and more descriptive inputs. Our measurements indicate that the emerging safety risks of the cutting-edge generative paradigm, MLLMs, have not been sufficiently recognized, posing new challenges to real-world safety.

AI.tldr: MLLMs show higher safety risks than diffusion models in unsafe content generation and fake image detection; current detectors are fragile to descriptive prompts; richer semantics and prompt-following capabilities introduce new safety challenges.
AI.motivation: To systematically compare safety risks of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) versus diffusion models across unsafe content generation and fake image synthesis, and to reveal how enhanced semantic understanding affects safety.
AI.method: Benchmark analysis comparing MLLMs and diffusion models on multiple unsafe content generation datasets; evaluation of state-of-the-art fake image detectors on MLLM-generated images, including retraining with MLLM-specific data and testing with longer, more descriptive prompts.
AI.result: MLLMs generate more unsafe images than diffusion models on several benchmarks; diffusion models may fail on abstract prompts leading to corrupted outputs; fake-image detectors are harder to detect MLLM-generated images and can be bypassed by longer/descriptive prompts even after retraining.
AI.conclusion: The safety risks of cutting-edge MLLMs are under-recognized; there is a need for improved detection and safety strategies that account for richer semantic understanding and prompt-following behavior in MLLMs.

title: IPatch: A Multi-Resolution Transformer Architecture for Robust Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Accurate forecasting of multivariate time series remains challenging due to the need to capture both short-term fluctuations and long-range temporal dependencies. Transformer-based models have emerged as a powerful approach, but their performance depends critically on the representation of temporal data. Traditional point-wise representations preserve individual time-step information, enabling fine-grained modeling, yet they tend to be computationally expensive and less effective at modeling broader contextual dependencies, limiting their scalability to long sequences. Patch-wise representations aggregate consecutive steps into compact tokens to improve efficiency and model local temporal dynamics, but they often discard fine-grained temporal details that are critical for accurate predictions in volatile or complex time series. We propose IPatch, a multi-resolution Transformer architecture that integrates both point-wise and patch-wise tokens, modeling temporal information at multiple resolutions. Experiments on 7 benchmark datasets demonstrate that IPatch consistently improves forecasting accuracy, robustness to noise, and generalization across various prediction horizons compared to single-representation baselines.

AI.tldr: IPatch uses a multi-resolution Transformer that fuses point-wise and patch-wise tokens for multivariate time-series forecasting, improving accuracy, noise robustness, and horizon generalization over single-representation baselines.
AI.motivation: To overcome the trade-off between fine-grained temporal detail (point-wise) and computational efficiency (patch-wise) in Transformer-based time-series forecasting, enabling scalable modeling of long sequences without sacrificing local dynamics.
AI.method: A hybrid architecture that jointly learns from point-wise and patch-wise tokens across multiple temporal resolutions (multi-resolution Transformer). Evaluation on 7 benchmark datasets against single-representation baselines.
AI.result: IPatch consistently achieves higher forecasting accuracy, better robustness to noise, and improved generalization across different forecast horizons.
AI.conclusion: Multi-resolution temporal representations via IPatch offer a robust and scalable improvement for multivariate time-series forecasting compared to single-representation baselines.

title: SpectralSplats: Robust Differentiable Tracking via Spectral Moment Supervision
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) enables real-time, photorealistic novel view synthesis, making it a highly attractive representation for model-based video tracking. However, leveraging the differentiability of the 3DGS renderer "in the wild" remains notoriously fragile. A fundamental bottleneck lies in the compact, local support of the Gaussian primitives. Standard photometric objectives implicitly rely on spatial overlap; if severe camera misalignment places the rendered object outside the target's local footprint, gradients strictly vanish, leaving the optimizer stranded. We introduce SpectralSplats, a robust tracking framework that resolves this "vanishing gradient" problem by shifting the optimization objective from the spatial to the frequency domain. By supervising the rendered image via a set of global complex sinusoidal features (Spectral Moments), we construct a global basin of attraction, ensuring that a valid, directional gradient toward the target exists across the entire image domain, even when pixel overlap is completely nonexistent. To harness this global basin without introducing periodic local minima associated with high frequencies, we derive a principled Frequency Annealing schedule from first principles, gracefully transitioning the optimizer from global convexity to precise spatial alignment. We demonstrate that SpectralSplats acts as a seamless, drop-in replacement for spatial losses across diverse deformation parameterizations (from MLPs to sparse control points), successfully recovering complex deformations even from severely misaligned initializations where standard appearance-based tracking catastrophically fails.

AI.tldr: SpectralSplats introduces a frequency-domain supervision framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting tracking, using global Spectral Moments and a Frequency Annealing schedule to overcome vanishing gradients and enable robust alignment from severely misaligned initializations.
AI.motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) primitives have compact local support, causing gradient vanishing when target and render do not spatially overlap. A global, robust optimization signal is needed to reliably track deformations in the wild.
AI.method: Replace spatial losses with global spectral supervision via complex sinusoidal features (Spectral Moments). Develop a Frequency Annealing schedule to gradually shift from global convexity to precise spatial alignment. The approach is compatible with various deformation parameterizations (MLPs, sparse control points) and serves as a drop-in replacement for existing spatial losses.
AI.result: SpectralSplats yields a robust tracking signal that preserves a global basin of attraction, enabling recovery of complex deformations from severely misaligned starts where appearance-based tracking fails. It acts as a seamless drop-in replacement for spatial losses.
AI.conclusion: Frequency-domain supervision provides a robust, global optimization signal for 3DGS tracking, mitigating vanishing gradients and enabling reliable alignment across diverse deformations; the method is broadly applicable and can complement or replace spatial losses in practice.

title: Uncovering Memorization in Timeseries Imputation models: LBRM Membership Inference and its link to attribute Leakage
summary: Deep learning models for time series imputation are now essential in fields such as healthcare, the Internet of Things (IoT), and finance. However, their deployment raises critical privacy concerns. Beyond the well-known issue of unintended memorization, which has been extensively studied in generative models, we demonstrate that time series models are vulnerable to inference attacks in a black-box setting. In this work, we introduce a two-stage attack framework comprising: (1) a novel membership inference attack based on a reference model that improves detection accuracy, even for models robust to overfitting-based attacks, and (2) the first attribute inference attack that predicts sensitive characteristics of the training data for timeseries imputation model. We evaluate these attacks on attention-based and autoencoder architectures in two scenarios: models that are trained from scratch, and fine-tuned models where the adversary has access to the initial weights. Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed membership attack retrieves a significant portion of the training data with a tpr@top25% score significantly higher than a naive attack baseline. We show that our membership attack also provides a good insight of whether attribute inference will work (with a precision of 90% instead of 78% in the genral case).

AI.tldr: Two-stage black-box privacy attacks on time series imputation models: a reference-model-based membership inference attack and an attribute inference attack; evaluated on attention-based and autoencoder architectures in from-scratch and fine-tuned settings; demonstrates significant leakage (high tpr@top25%) and high attribute-inference precision (90%).
AI.motivation: Privacy risks in DL-based time series imputation extend beyond memorization; black-box attacks can reveal training data and sensitive attributes, challenging deployment in healthcare, IoT, and finance.
AI.method: Propose a two-stage attack: (1) membership inference using a reference model to boost detection even against models robust to overfitting; (2) first attribute inference attack predicting sensitive training-data attributes. Evaluate on attention-based and autoencoder models under two training regimes: scratch and fine-tuned with known initial weights.
AI.result: Membership attack retrieves a significant portion of training data with higher-than-naive tpr@top25%; the attack's usefulness extends to predicting whether attribute inference will succeed, with precision 90% vs 78% in general.
AI.conclusion: Time series imputation models are vulnerable to black-box privacy attacks; the proposed framework enhances leakage through a two-stage approach and highlights need for defense strategies against membership and attribute inference; future work should explore defenses like differential privacy, robust training, and model auditing.

title: A^3: Towards Advertising Aesthetic Assessment
summary: Advertising images significantly impact commercial conversion rates and brand equity, yet current evaluation methods rely on subjective judgments, lacking scalability, standardized criteria, and interpretability. To address these challenges, we present A^3 (Advertising Aesthetic Assessment), a comprehensive framework encompassing four components: a paradigm (A^3-Law), a dataset (A^3-Dataset), a multimodal large language model (A^3-Align), and a benchmark (A^3-Bench). Central to A^3 is a theory-driven paradigm, A^3-Law, comprising three hierarchical stages: (1) Perceptual Attention, evaluating perceptual image signals for their ability to attract attention; (2) Formal Interest, assessing formal composition of image color and spatial layout in evoking interest; and (3) Desire Impact, measuring desire evocation from images and their persuasive impact. Building on A^3-Law, we construct A^3-Dataset with 120K instruction-response pairs from 30K advertising images, each richly annotated with multi-dimensional labels and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) rationales. We further develop A^3-Align, trained under A^3-Law with CoT-guided learning on A^3-Dataset. Extensive experiments on A^3-Bench demonstrate that A^3-Align achieves superior alignment with A^3-Law compared to existing models, and this alignment generalizes well to quality advertisement selection and prescriptive advertisement critique, indicating its potential for broader deployment. Dataset, code, and models can be found at: https://github.com/euleryuan/A3-Align.

AI.tldr: A^3 introduces a theory-driven framework for advertising aesthetic assessment (A^3-Law, A^3-Dataset, A^3-Align, A^3-Bench) with a 120K-instruction dataset, CoT rationales, and strong alignment/generalization to ad quality selection and critique.
AI.motivation: Current ad image evaluation relies on subjective judgments with limited scalability, standardized criteria, or interpretability. A theory-guided, scalable, interpretable framework is needed to reliably assess ad aesthetics and persuasive impact.
AI.method: Proposes A^3-Law (three stages: Perceptual Attention, Formal Interest, Desire Impact). Builds A^3-Dataset (120K instruction-response pairs from 30K images with multi-dimensional labels and CoT). Develops A^3-Align (multimodal model trained under A^3-Law with CoT-guided learning) and A^3-Bench for evaluation.
AI.result: A^3-Align achieves superior alignment with A^3-Law vs. existing models and generalizes to high-quality ad selection and prescriptive critique; indicates potential for broader deployment.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a scalable, interpretable, theory-grounded approach to advertising aesthetics, with resources (dataset, code, models) available for broader adoption.

authors: Peter G. M. van der Heijden
title: Identification of NMF by choosing maximum-volume basis vectors
summary: In nonnegative matrix factorization (NMF), minimum-volume-constrained NMF is a widely used framework for identifying the solution of NMF by making basis vectors as similar as possible. This typically induces sparsity in the coefficient matrix, with each row containing zero entries. Consequently, minimum-volume-constrained NMF may fail for highly mixed data, where such sparsity does not hold. Moreover, the estimated basis vectors in minimum-volume-constrained NMF may be difficult to interpret as they may be mixtures of the ground truth basis vectors. To address these limitations, in this paper we propose a new NMF framework, called maximum-volume-constrained NMF, which makes the basis vectors as distinct as possible. We further establish an identifiability theorem for maximum-volume-constrained NMF and provide an algorithm to estimate it. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: Introduces maximum-volume-constrained NMF to maximize distinctness among basis vectors to improve identifiability and interpretability, with a provable identifiability theorem, an estimation algorithm, and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Existing minimum-volume-constrained NMF enforces sparsity in the coefficient matrix and can fail on highly mixed data; basis vectors may become mixtures of ground truth elements, hindering interpretability. A framework that maximizes basis-vector distinctness aims to improve identifiability and interpretation.
AI.method: Propose a maximum-volume constraint in NMF, derive an identifiability theorem, and develop an algorithm to estimate the maximum-volume NMF solution.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of maximum-volume-constrained NMF, indicating improved identifiability and interpretability in the tested scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Maximum-volume-constrained NMF provides a viable alternative to minimum-volume-constrained NMF for highly mixed data by promoting distinct, interpretable basis vectors and offering theoretical identifiability guarantees.

title: SemLayer: Semantic-aware Generative Segmentation and Layer Construction for Abstract Icons
summary: Graphic icons are a cornerstone of modern design workflows, yet they are often distributed as flattened single-path or compound-path graphics, where the original semantic layering is lost. This absence of semantic decomposition hinders downstream tasks such as editing, restyling, and animation. We formalize this problem as semantic layer construction for flattened vector art and introduce SemLayer, a visual generation empowered pipeline that restores editable layered structures. Given an abstract icon, SemLayer first generates a chromatically differentiated representation in which distinct semantic components become visually separable. To recover the complete geometry of each part, including occluded regions, we then perform a semantic completion step that reconstructs coherent object-level shapes. Finally, the recovered parts are assembled into a layered vector representation with inferred occlusion relationships. Extensive qualitative comparisons and quantitative evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of SemLayer, enabling editing workflows previously inapplicable to flattened vector graphics and establishing semantic layer reconstruction as a practical and valuable task. Project page: https://xxuhaiyang.github.io/SemLayer/

AI.tldr: SemLayer recovers editable semantic layers from flattened vector icons by (1) creating a chromatically separated intermediate representation to distinguish components, (2) performing semantic completion to recover full geometry including occluded parts, and (3) assembling parts into an occlusion-aware layered vector. It is validated with qualitative and quantitative assessments, enabling editing/restyling/animation workflows that flattened graphics previously could not support.
AI.motivation: Flattened vector graphics lose semantic layering, hindering downstream tasks such as editing, restyling, and animation. There is a need to reconstruct semantic layers and occlusion-aware geometry from single-path representations to enable editable vector workflows.
AI.method: 1) Generate a chromatically differentiated visualization where distinct semantic components become visually separable. 2) Semantic completion to reconstruct coherent object-level shapes, including occluded regions. 3) Assemble recovered parts into a layered vector representation with inferred occlusion relationships.
AI.result: Qualitative comparisons and quantitative evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of SemLayer in recovering editable layered structures from flattened vector art and enabling editing workflows previously inapplicable to flattened graphics.
AI.conclusion: Semantic layer reconstruction is a practical and valuable task; SemLayer provides a pipeline that makes flattened vector graphics editable by recovering semantic layers with occlusion-aware geometry.

title: HAM: A Training-Free Style Transfer Approach via Heterogeneous Attention Modulation for Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable performance in image generation, particularly within the domain of style transfer. Prevailing style transfer approaches typically leverage pre-trained diffusion models' robust feature extraction capabilities alongside external modular control pathways to explicitly impose style guidance signals. However, these methods often fail to capture complex style reference or retain the identity of user-provided content images, thus falling into the trap of style-content balance. Thus, we propose a training-free style transfer approach via $\textbf{h}$eterogeneous $\textbf{a}$ttention $\textbf{m}$odulation ($\textbf{HAM}$) to protect identity information during image/text-guided style reference transfer, thereby addressing the style-content trade-off challenge. Specifically, we first introduces style noise initialization to initialize latent noise for diffusion. Then, during the diffusion process, it innovatively employs HAM for different attention mechanisms, including Global Attention Regulation (GAR) and Local Attention Transplantation (LAT), which better preserving the details of the content image while capturing complex style references. Our approach is validated through a series of qualitative and quantitative experiments, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple quantitative metrics.

AI.tldr: Training-free style transfer for diffusion models using heterogeneous attention modulation (HAM) to preserve content identity while transferring complex styles, via Global Attention Regulation (GAR) and Local Attention Transplantation (LAT).
AI.motivation: Current style transfer with diffusion models relies on pre-trained features and external control paths, often failing to capture complex style references and preserving content identity, leading to a style-content imbalance.
AI.method: Introduce style noise initialization for diffusion latent input. Apply HAM across attention mechanisms, using GAR to regulate global style features and LAT to transplant local attention cues, enabling better content detail preservation and richer style transfer without training.
AI.result: Qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on multiple metrics, validating the approach.
AI.conclusion: HAM provides a training-free, effective mechanism to balance style and content in diffusion-based style transfer, improving identity preservation while capturing complex style references.

title: Embracing Heteroscedasticity for Probabilistic Time Series Forecasting
summary: Probabilistic time series forecasting (PTSF) aims to model the full predictive distribution of future observations, enabling both accurate forecasting and principled uncertainty quantification. A central requirement of PTSF is to embrace heteroscedasticity, as real-world time series exhibit time-varying conditional variances induced by nonstationary dynamics, regime changes, and evolving external conditions. However, most existing non-autoregressive generative approaches to PTSF, such as TimeVAE and $K^2$VAE, rely on MSE-based training objectives that implicitly impose a homoscedastic assumption, thereby fundamentally limiting their ability to model temporal heteroscedasticity. To address this limitation, we propose the Location-Scale Gaussian VAE (LSG-VAE), a simple but effective framework that explicitly parameterizes both the predictive mean and time-dependent variance through a location-scale likelihood formulation. This design enables LSG-VAE to faithfully capture heteroscedastic aleatoric uncertainty and introduces an adaptive attenuation mechanism that automatically down-weights highly volatile observations during training, leading to improved robustness in trend prediction. Extensive experiments on nine benchmark datasets demonstrate that LSG-VAE consistently outperforms fifteen strong generative baselines while maintaining high computational efficiency suitable for real-time deployment.

AI.tldr: LSG-VAE: a location-scale Gaussian VAE that models both predictive mean and time-varying variance to capture heteroscedasticity in probabilistic time series forecasting; shows robustness and efficiency, outperforming many baselines.
AI.motivation: Current non-autoregressive PTSF methods (e.g., TimeVAE, K^2VAE) train with MSE objectives that enforce homoscedasticity, limiting modeling of time-varying uncertainty in nonstationary or regime-changing time series.
AI.method: Introduce a Location-Scale Gaussian VAE (LSG-VAE) that explicitly parameterizes time-varying variance via a location-scale likelihood. The model down-weights highly volatile observations during training (adaptive attenuation) to improve robustness in trend prediction.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on nine benchmark datasets shows LSG-VAE consistently outperforms fifteen strong generative baselines while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for real-time deployment.
AI.conclusion: LSG-VAE offers a simple yet effective framework for heteroscedastic PTSF by jointly modeling mean and time-dependent variance, improving calibration and robustness without sacrificing efficiency.

title: LGEST: Dynamic Spatial-Spectral Expert Routing for Hyperspectral Image Classification
summary: Deep learning methods, including Convolutional Neural Networks, Transformers and Mamba, have achieved remarkable success in hyperspectral image (HSI) classification. Nevertheless, existing methods exhibit inflexible integration of local-global representations, inadequate handling of spectral-spatial scale disparities across heterogeneous bands, and susceptibility to the Hughes phenomenon under high-dimensional sample heterogeneity. To address these challenges, we propose Local-Global Expert Spatial-Spectral Transformer (LGEST), a novel framework that synergistically combines three key innovations. The LGEST first employs a Deep Spatial-Spectral Autoencoder (DSAE) to generate compact yet discriminative embeddings through hierarchical nonlinear compression, preserving 3D neighborhood coherence while mitigating information loss in high-dimensional spaces. Secondly, a Cross-Interactive Mixed Expert Feature Pyramid (CIEM-FPN) leverages cross-attention mechanisms and residual mixture-of-experts layers to dynamically fuse multi-scale features, adaptively weighting spectral discriminability and spatial saliency through learnable gating functions. Finally, a Local-Global Expert System (LGES) processes decomposed features via sparsely activated expert pairs: convolutional sub-experts capture fine-grained textures, while transformer sub-experts model long-range contextual dependencies, with a routing controller dynamically selecting experts based on real-time feature saliency. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that LGEST consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: LGEST introduces a hierarchical local-global hyperspectral classifier using a Deep Spatial-Spectral Autoencoder, a Cross-Interactive Mixed Expert Feature Pyramid, and a Local-Global Expert System with dynamic routing, achieving state-of-the-art results on four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address inflexible integration of local-global representations, spectral-spatial scale disparities across heterogeneous bands, and the Hughes phenomenon under high-dimensional sample heterogeneity in hyperspectral image classification.
AI.method: 1) Deep Spatial-Spectral Autoencoder (DSAE) for hierarchical nonlinear compression preserving 3D neighborhood coherence; 2) Cross-Interactive Mixed Expert Feature Pyramid (CIEM-FPN) using cross-attention and residual mixture-of-experts with learnable gating to fuse multi-scale features, balancing spectral discriminability and spatial saliency; 3) Local-Global Expert System (LGES) with sparsely activated expert pairs: convolutional sub-experts for fine textures and transformer sub-experts for long-range context, guided by a routing controller that selects experts based on real-time feature saliency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets show LGEST consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: LGEST effectively integrates local-global representations and scale-aware fusion with dynamic expert routing, yielding robust hyperspectral classification and improved accuracy across multiple datasets.

title: Forecasting with Guidance: Representation-Level Supervision for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Nowadays, time series forecasting is predominantly approached through the end-to-end training of deep learning architectures using error-based objectives. While this is effective at minimizing average loss, it encourages the encoder to discard informative yet extreme patterns. This results in smooth predictions and temporal representations that poorly capture salient dynamics. To address this issue, we propose ReGuider, a plug-in method that can be seamlessly integrated into any forecasting architecture. ReGuider leverages pretrained time series foundation models as semantic teachers. During training, the input sequence is processed together by the target forecasting model and the pretrained model. Rather than using the pretrained model's outputs directly, we extract its intermediate embeddings, which are rich in temporal and semantic information, and align them with the target model's encoder embeddings through representation-level supervision. This alignment process enables the encoder to learn more expressive temporal representations, thereby improving the accuracy of downstream forecasting. Extensive experimentation across diverse datasets and architectures demonstrates that our ReGuider consistently improves forecasting performance, confirming its effectiveness and versatility.

AI.tldr: Plug-in ReGuider uses pretrained time series foundation models as semantic teachers, aligning their intermediate embeddings with a forecast model's encoder to learn richer temporal representations and improve accuracy across architectures.
AI.motivation: End-to-end forecasting with error-based objectives often smooths extreme patterns, losing salient dynamics; richer temporal representations are needed for better forecasting.
AI.method: During training, the input sequence is processed by both the target forecast model and a pretrained time series foundation model. Instead of copying outputs, ReGuider extracts intermediate embeddings from the pretrained model and enforces representation-level alignment with the target model's encoder embeddings, providing supervision that enriches the encoder.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse datasets and architectures show that ReGuider consistently improves forecasting performance, validating its effectiveness and versatility.
AI.conclusion: ReGuider is a versatile, architecture-agnostic plug-in that enhances time-series forecasting by leveraging semantic information from pretrained foundation models through intermediate-representation alignment.

title: Beyond Semantic Priors: Mitigating Optimization Collapse for Generalizable Visual Forensics
summary: While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like CLIP have emerged as a dominant paradigm for generalizable deepfake detection, a representational disconnect remains: their semantic-centric pre-training is ill-suited for capturing non-semantic artifacts inherent to hyper-realistic synthesis. In this work, we identify a failure mode termed Optimization Collapse, where detectors trained with Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) degenerate to random guessing on non-semantic forgeries once the perturbation radius exceeds a narrow threshold. To theoretically formalize this collapse, we propose the Critical Optimization Radius (COR) to quantify the geometric stability of the optimization landscape, and leverage the Gradient Signal-to-Noise Ratio (GSNR) to measure generalization potential. We establish a theorem proving that COR increases monotonically with GSNR, thereby revealing that the geometric instability of SAM optimization originates from degraded intrinsic generalization potential. This result identifies the layer-wise attenuation of GSNR as the root cause of Optimization Collapse in detecting non-semantic forgeries. Although naively reducing perturbation radius yields stable convergence under SAM, it merely treats the symptom without mitigating the intrinsic generalization degradation, necessitating enhanced gradient fidelity. Building on this insight, we propose the Contrastive Regional Injection Transformer (CoRIT), which integrates a computationally efficient Contrastive Gradient Proxy (CGP) with three training-free strategies: Region Refinement Mask to suppress CGP variance, Regional Signal Injection to preserve CGP magnitude, and Hierarchical Representation Integration to attain more generalizable representations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoRIT mitigates optimization collapse and achieves state-of-the-art generalization across cross-domain and universal forgery benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Identifies Optimization Collapse in SAM-trained detectors for non-semantic forgeries, introduces COR and GSNR to quantify optimization geometry and generalization; proves COR rises with GSNR, tracing collapse to attenuated GSNR; proposes CoRIT with CGP and three training-free strategies to stabilize optimization and improve cross-domain/universal forgery detection.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) pretrained on semantic cues struggle to capture non-semantic artifacts in hyper-realistic forgeries. Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) can cause optimization collapse under perturbations, hurting generalization. A formal framework is needed to diagnose and remedy this failure.
AI.method: Theoretically define Critical Optimization Radius (COR) to quantify landscape stability and use Gradient Signal-to-Noise Ratio (GSNR) as a proxy for intrinsic generalization. Prove COR increases with GSNR, linking instability to degraded generalization. Diagnose as layer-wise GSNR attenuation. Propose Contrastive Regional Injection Transformer (CoRIT) integrating a Contrastive Gradient Proxy (CGP) with three training-free strategies: Region Refinement Mask, Regional Signal Injection, Hierarchical Representation Integration.
AI.result: CoRIT mitigates optimization collapse and achieves state-of-the-art generalization on cross-domain and universal forgery benchmarks against non-semantic forgeries.
AI.conclusion: Enhancing gradient fidelity is essential; the intrinsic generalization potential governed by GSNR under SAM explains optimization collapse. CoRIT provides a practical, training-efficient approach to obtain more robust, generalizable representations for non-semantic forgery detection.

title: Powerful Teachers Matter: Text-Guided Multi-view Knowledge Distillation with Visual Prior Enhancement
summary: Knowledge distillation transfers knowledge from large teacher models to smaller students for efficient inference. While existing methods primarily focus on distillation strategies, they often overlook the importance of enhancing teacher knowledge quality. In this paper, we propose Text-guided Multi-view Knowledge Distillation (TMKD), which leverages dual-modality teachers, a visual teacher and a text teacher (CLIP), to provide richer supervisory signals. Specifically, we enhance the visual teacher with multi-view inputs incorporating visual priors (edge and high-frequency features), while the text teacher generates semantic weights through prior-aware prompts to guide adaptive feature fusion. Additionally, we introduce vision-language contrastive regularization to strengthen semantic knowledge in the student model. Extensive experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate that TMKD consistently improves knowledge distillation performance by up to 4.49\%, validating the effectiveness of our dual-teacher multi-view enhancement strategy. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TMKD-main-44D1.

AI.tldr: A dual-teacher, multi-view KD framework (TMKD) uses a visual teacher with multi-view priors and a CLIP-based text teacher to guide adaptive feature fusion, plus vision-language contrastive regularization, achieving up to 4.49 percentage points gains on five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Improve knowledge distillation quality by enriching teacher knowledge with richer, multi-modal signals and priors rather than relying solely on student-focused distillation strategies.
AI.method: 1) Enhance the visual teacher with multi-view inputs incorporating visual priors (edge and high-frequency features). 2) Use a text teacher (CLIP) to generate semantic weights through prior-aware prompts to guide adaptive feature fusion. 3) Apply vision-language contrastive regularization to strengthen semantic knowledge in the student.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five benchmarks shows consistent improvements, with gains up to 4.49 percentage points. Code is released.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of a dual-teacher, multi-view enhancement strategy for knowledge distillation and the benefit of semantic guidance and contrastive regularization in improving student performance.

title: DeepDTF: Dual-Branch Transformer Fusion for Multi-Omics Anticancer Drug Response Prediction
summary: Cancer drug response varies widely across tumors due to multi-layer molecular heterogeneity, motivating computational decision support for precision oncology. Despite recent progress in deep CDR models, robust alignment between high-dimensional multi-omics and chemically structured drugs remains challenging due to cross-modal misalignment and limited inductive bias. We present DeepDTF, an end-to-end dual-branch Transformer fusion framework for joint log(IC50) regression and drug sensitivity classification. The cell-line branch uses modality-specific encoders for multi-omics profiles with Transformer blocks to capture long-range dependencies, while the drug branch represents compounds as molecular graphs and encodes them with a GNN-Transformer to integrate local topology with global context. Omics and drug representations are fused by a Transformer-based module that models cross-modal interactions and mitigates feature misalignment. On public pharmacogenomic benchmarks under 5-fold cold-start cell-line evaluation, DeepDTF consistently outperforms strong baselines across omics settings, achieving up to RMSE=1.248, R^2=0.875, and AUC=0.987 with full multi-omics inputs, while reducing classification error (1-ACC) by 9.5%. Beyond accuracy, DeepDTF provides biologically grounded explanations via SHAP-based gene attributions and pathway enrichment with pre-ranked GSEA.

AI.tldr: A dual-branch Transformer fusion framework (DeepDTF) aligns multi-omics cell-line data with drug graphs for joint regression (log(IC50)) and classification in precision oncology, delivering strong predictive performance and SHAP-based interpretability.
AI.motivation: Cancer drug response is highly heterogeneous due to multi-omics variation, causing cross-modal misalignment between cell-line profiles and chemically structured drugs. Existing deep CDR models lack robust multi-modal fusion and inductive bias across modalities.
AI.method: DeepDTF is an end-to-end dual-branch Transformer fusion model: (1) a cell-line branch with modality-specific encoders for multi-omics data and Transformer blocks to capture long-range dependencies; (2) a drug branch encoding molecular graphs with a GNN-Transformer to combine local topology with global context; (3) a Transformer-based cross-modal fusion module to model cross-modal interactions and reduce misalignment; (4) joint optimization for log(IC50) regression and drug sensitivity classification; (5) evaluation on public pharmacogenomic benchmarks with 5-fold cold-start cell-line evaluation; (6) interpretability via SHAP-based gene attributions and pre-ranked GSEA.
AI.result: DeepDTF consistently outperforms strong baselines across different omics settings, achieving RMSE up to 1.248, R^2 of 0.875, and AUC of 0.987 with full multi-omics inputs; classification error (1-ACC) is reduced by 9.5%.
AI.conclusion: Besides high predictive accuracy, the framework provides biologically meaningful explanations through SHAP-based gene attributions and pathway enrichment via pre-ranked GSEA.

title: Language-Assisted Image Clustering Guided by Discriminative Relational Signals and Adaptive Semantic Centers
summary: Language-Assisted Image Clustering (LAIC) augments the input images with additional texts with the help of vision-language models (VLMs) to promote clustering performance. Despite recent progress, existing LAIC methods often overlook two issues: (i) textual features constructed for each image are highly similar, leading to weak inter-class discriminability; (ii) the clustering step is restricted to pre-built image-text alignments, limiting the potential for better utilization of the text modality. To address these issues, we propose a new LAIC framework with two complementary components. First, we exploit cross-modal relations to produce more discriminative self-supervision signals for clustering, as it compatible with most VLMs training mechanisms. Second, we learn category-wise continuous semantic centers via prompt learning to produce the final clustering assignments. Extensive experiments on eight benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method achieves an average improvement of 2.6% over state-of-the-art methods, and the learned semantic centers exhibit strong interpretability. Code is available in the supplementary material.

AI.tldr: A LAIC method that addresses two issues: non-discriminative image textual features and reliance on pre-built alignments by introducing cross-modal supervision signals and category-wise semantic centers via prompt learning; claims 2.6% avg improvement over SOTA on 8 datasets; interpretable semantic centers; code in supplementary.
AI.motivation: Existing LAIC methods suffer from highly similar textual features across images, hurting inter-class discriminability, and are constrained by pre-built image-text alignments, which limit effective use of the text modality.
AI.method: Two-component framework: (1) leverage cross-modal relations to generate more discriminative self-supervision signals for clustering, compatible with common VLM training pipelines; (2) learn category-wise continuous semantic centers through prompt learning to derive final clustering assignments.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on eight benchmark datasets shows an average improvement of 2.6% over state-of-the-art methods; the learned semantic centers are highly interpretable; code is provided in supplementary material.
AI.conclusion: The framework improves clustering performance and interpretability by combining cross-modal relational signals with prompt-learning-based semantic centers, demonstrating robustness across multiple datasets.

title: AD-Reasoning: Multimodal Guideline-Guided Reasoning for Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) diagnosis requires integrating neuroimaging with heterogeneous clinical evidence and reasoning under established criteria, yet most multimodal models remain opaque and weakly guideline-aligned. We present AD-Reasoning, a multimodal framework that couples structural MRI with six clinical modalities and a rule-based verifier to generate structured, NIA-AA-consistent diagnoses. AD-Reasoning combines modality-specific encoders, bidirectional cross-attention fusion, and reinforcement fine-tuning with verifiable rewards that enforce output format, guideline evidence coverage, and reasoning--decision consistency. We also release AD-MultiSense, a 10,378-visit multimodal QA dataset with guideline-validated rationales built from ADNI/AIBL. On AD-MultiSense, AD-Reasoning achieves state-of-the-art diagnostic accuracy and produces structured rationales that improve transparency over recent baselines, while providing transparent rationales.

AI.tldr: Multimodal AD-Reasoning framework integrates MRI with six clinical modalities and a rule-based verifier to deliver NIA-AA-consistent diagnoses; accompanied by AD-MultiSense QA dataset; achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and provides transparent, structured rationales.
AI.motivation: Tackle opacity and misalignment with clinical guidelines in multimodal AD diagnosis by combining interpretable reasoning with verifiable outputs.
AI.method: Modality-specific encoders; bidirectional cross-attention fusion; reinforcement fine-tuning with reward signals that enforce output format, guideline evidence coverage, and reasoning-decision consistency; rule-based verifier; release of AD-MultiSense dataset from ADNI/AIBL.
AI.result: State-of-the-art diagnostic accuracy on AD-MultiSense; generates structured rationales that enhance transparency relative to recent baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that combining multimodal encoding with verifiable, guideline-aligned reasoning yields accurate and interpretable AD diagnoses; dataset enables evaluation of rationale quality.

title: Cost-Sensitive Neighborhood Aggregation for Heterophilous Graphs: When Does Per-Edge Routing Help?
summary: Recent work distinguishes two heterophily regimes: adversarial, where cross-class edges dilute class signal and harm classification, and informative, where the heterophilous structure itself carries useful signal. We ask: when does per-edge message routing help, and when is a uniform spectral channel sufficient? To operationalize this question we introduce Cost-Sensitive Neighborhood Aggregation (CSNA), a GNN layer that computes pairwise distance in a learned projection and uses it to soft-route each message through concordant and discordant channels with independent transformations. Under a contextual stochastic block model we show that cost-sensitive weighting preserves class-discriminative signal where mean aggregation provably attenuates it, provided $w_+/w_- > q/p$. On six benchmarks with uniform tuning, CSNA is competitive with state-of-the-art methods on adversarial-heterophily datasets (Texas, Wisconsin, Cornell, Actor) but underperforms on informative-heterophily datasets (Chameleon, Squirrel) -- precisely the regime where per-edge routing has no useful decomposition to exploit. The pattern is itself the finding: the cost function's ability to separate edge types serves as a diagnostic for the heterophily regime, revealing when fine-grained routing adds value over uniform channels and when it does not. Code is available at https://github.com/eyal-weiss/CSNA-public .

AI.tldr: CSNA is a GNN layer that uses cost-sensitive, learned routing to separate concordant and discordant messages, preserving class signal when heterophily is adversarial, while not overfitting on informative heterophily; serves as a diagnostic for heterophily regime; code available.
AI.motivation: Different heterophily regimes affect whether per-edge routing improves GNN performance. A method to diagnose and adapt to the regime can guide model design and avoid unnecessary routing complexity.
AI.method: Introduce Cost-Sensitive Neighborhood Aggregation (CSNA): a GNN layer that computes pairwise distances in a learned projection to soft-route messages through concordant and discordant channels with independent transformations. Use a cost-sensitive weighting w+ vs w- to control routing. Theoretical analysis under a contextual stochastic block model shows discrimination preserved if w+/w- > q/p. Empirical evaluation on six benchmarks with uniform tuning compares CSNA to state-of-the-art methods on adversarial and informative heterophily datasets.
AI.result: Theoretically, CSNA preserves class-discriminative signal when the specified ratio condition holds; empirically, CSNA matches or exceeds baselines on adversarial-heterophily datasets (Texas, Wisconsin, Cornell, Actor) but underperforms on informative-heterophily datasets (Chameleon, Squirrel). The cost function acts as a diagnostic for heterophily regime, indicating when fine-grained routing adds real value.
AI.conclusion: Cost-sensitive routing adds value in adversarial heterophily and serves as a diagnostic for the regime; uniform channels suffice in informative heterophily. The authors provide code for reproducibility.

title: PosterIQ: A Design Perspective Benchmark for Poster Understanding and Generation
summary: We present PosterIQ, a design-driven benchmark for poster understanding and generation, annotated across composition structure, typographic hierarchy, and semantic intent. It includes 7,765 image-annotation instances and 822 generation prompts spanning real, professional, and synthetic cases. To bridge visual design cognition and generative modeling, we define tasks for layout parsing, text-image correspondence, typography/readability and font perception, design quality assessment, and controllable, composition-aware generation with metaphor. We evaluate state-of-the-art MLLMs and diffusion-based generators, finding persistent gaps in visual hierarchy, typographic semantics, saliency control, and intention communication; commercial models lead on high-level reasoning but act as insensitive automatic raters, while generators render text well yet struggle with composition-aware synthesis. Extensive analyses show PosterIQ is both a quantitative benchmark and a diagnostic tool for design reasoning, offering reproducible, task-specific metrics. We aim to catalyze models' creativity and integrate human-centred design principles into generative vision-language systems.

AI.tldr: PosterIQ is a comprehensive design-driven benchmark for poster understanding and generation, with 7,765 image-annotation instances and 822 generation prompts across real, professional, and synthetic cases. It defines tasks for layout parsing, text-image correspondence, typography/readability and font perception, design quality assessment, and controllable, composition-aware generation with metaphor. It reveals persistent gaps in visual hierarchy, typographic semantics, saliency control, and intention communication; commercial models excel in high-level reasoning but act as insensitive automatic raters, while generators render text well yet struggle with composition-aware synthesis. PosterIQ serves as a quantitative benchmark and diagnostic tool with reproducible, task-specific metrics, aiming to catalyze design-aware AI.
AI.motivation: To bridge visual design cognition and generative modeling, providing a formal benchmark to evaluate and improve design-aware AI systems across poster-level tasks, addressing gaps in layout, typography, saliency, and communicative intention.
AI.method: Assemble a design-centric poster dataset annotated along composition structure, typographic hierarchy, and semantic intent; define tasks including layout parsing, text-image correspondence, typography/readability and font perception, design quality assessment, and controllable, composition-aware generation with metaphor; evaluate state-of-the-art multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and diffusion-based generators on these tasks.
AI.result: Reveals persistent gaps in visual hierarchy, typographic semantics, saliency control, and intention communication. Commercial models lead on high-level reasoning but are insensitive automatic raters; generators render text well yet struggle with composition-aware synthesis. PosterIQ provides quantitative metrics and diagnostic tooling for design reasoning, with reproducible, task-specific evaluations.
AI.conclusion: PosterIQ functions as both a quantitative benchmark and a diagnostic tool to assess design reasoning in AI, and it aims to catalyze models' creativity while integrating human-centered design principles into generative vision–language systems.

title: Large Language Model Guided Incentive Aware Reward Design for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Designing effective auxiliary rewards for cooperative multi-agent systems remains a precarious task; misaligned incentives risk inducing suboptimal coordination, especially where sparse task feedback fails to provide sufficient grounding. This study introduces an automated reward design framework that leverages large language models to synthesize executable reward programs from environment instrumentation. The procedure constrains candidate programs within a formal validity envelope and evaluates their efficacy by training policies from scratch under a fixed computational budget; selection depends exclusively on the sparse task return. The framework is evaluated across four distinct Overcooked-AI layouts characterized by varied corridor congestion, handoff dependencies, and structural asymmetries. Iterative search generations consistently yield superior task returns and delivery counts, with the most pronounced gains occurring in environments dominated by interaction bottlenecks. Diagnostic analysis of the synthesized shaping components indicates increased interdependence in action selection and improved signal alignment in coordination-intensive tasks. These results demonstrate that the search for objectivegrounded reward programs can mitigate the burden of manual engineering while producing shaping signals compatible with cooperative learning under finite budgets.

AI.tldr: Automated reward design for cooperative MARL via LLM-synthesized executable reward programs; constrained by a validity envelope; evaluated under fixed budget on Overcooked-AI layouts; iterative search improves task returns and coordination, especially in bottleneck settings.
AI.motivation: Reward design for cooperative multi-agent systems is fragile and often manual; sparse feedback makes grounding difficult, creating a need for automated, objective-grounded shaping signals.
AI.method: Use large language models to synthesize executable reward programs from environment instrumentation; constrain candidate programs within a formal validity envelope; evaluate by training policies from scratch under a fixed compute budget; select based on sparse task return.
AI.result: Iterative search generations yield higher task returns and delivery counts; strongest gains in interaction bottlenecks; diagnostic analysis shows increased interdependence in action selection and better signal alignment in coordination-intensive tasks.
AI.conclusion: Automated, objective-grounded reward program search can reduce manual engineering burden and produce shaping signals compatible with cooperative learning under finite budgets.

title: Accelerating Diffusion-based Video Editing via Heterogeneous Caching: Beyond Full Computing at Sampled Denoising Timestep
summary: Diffusion-based video editing has emerged as an important paradigm for high-quality and flexible content generation. However, despite their generality and strong modeling capacity, Diffusion Transformers (DiT) remain computationally expensive due to the iterative denoising process, posing challenges for practical deployment. Existing video diffusion acceleration methods primarily exploit denoising timestep-level feature reuse, which mitigates the redundancy in denoising process, but overlooks the architectural redundancy within the DiT that many attention operations over spatio-temporal tokens are redundantly executed, offering little to no incremental contribution to the model output. This work introduces HetCache, a training-free diffusion acceleration framework designed to exploit the inherent heterogeneity in diffusion-based masked video-to-video (MV2V) generation and editing. Instead of uniformly reuse or randomly sampling tokens, HetCache assesses the contextual relevance and interaction strength among various types of tokens in designated computing steps. Guided by spatial priors, it divides the spatial-temporal tokens in DiT model into context and generative tokens, and selectively caches the context tokens that exhibit the strongest correlation and most representative semantics with generative ones. This strategy reduces redundant attention operations while maintaining editing consistency and fidelity. Experiments show that HetCache achieves a noticeable acceleration, including a 2.67$\times$ latency speedup and FLOPs reduction over commonly used foundation models, with negligible degradation in editing quality.

AI.tldr: HetCache is a training-free diffusion acceleration framework for diffusion-based video editing that caches context tokens based on their relevance to generative tokens, reducing attention computations and yielding 2.67x latency speedup with minimal quality loss.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Transformers are powerful but computationally heavy due to iterative denoising. Existing acceleration methods reuse features at the denoising timestep but ignore intra-model architectural redundancy in self-attention over spatio-temporal tokens; this limits speedups.
AI.method: Leverage spatial priors to split tokens into context and generative tokens; assess contextual relevance and interaction strength among token types during designated computing steps; selectively cache the most semantically representative context tokens correlated with generative tokens; training-free and compatible with MV2V diffusion.
AI.result: Achieves 2.67x latency speedup and reduced FLOPs over baseline foundation models; editing quality largely preserved.
AI.conclusion: HetCache demonstrates that exploiting heterogeneity in token interactions and spatial priors can substantially accelerate diffusion-based video editing without heavy training, offering practical deployment advantages.

title: LGTM: Training-Free Light-Guided Text-to-Image Diffusion Model via Initial Noise Manipulation
summary: Diffusion models have demonstrated high-quality performance in conditional text-to-image generation, particularly with structural cues such as edges, layouts, and depth. However, lighting conditions have received limited attention and remain difficult to control within the generative process. Existing methods handle lighting through a two-stage pipeline that relights images after generation, which is inefficient. Moreover, they rely on fine-tuning with large datasets and heavy computation, limiting their adaptability to new models and tasks. To address this, we propose a novel Training-Free Light-Guided Text-to-Image Diffusion Model via Initial Noise Manipulation (LGTM), which manipulates the initial latent noise of the diffusion process to guide image generation with text prompts and user-specified light directions. Through a channel-wise analysis of the latent space, we find that selectively manipulating latent channels enables fine-grained lighting control without fine-tuning or modifying the pre-trained model. Extensive experiments show that our method surpasses prompt-based baselines in lighting consistency, while preserving image quality and text alignment. This approach introduces new possibilities for dynamic, user-guided light control. Furthermore, it integrates seamlessly with models like ControlNet, demonstrating adaptability across diverse scenarios.

AI.tldr: LGTM is a training-free method that guides diffusion-based text-to-image generation by manipulating the initial latent noise to control lighting. It achieves fine-grained lighting control via channel-wise manipulation without fine-tuning, works with models like ControlNet, and outperforms prompt-based baselines in lighting consistency while preserving quality and text alignment.
AI.motivation: Lighting control in diffusion-based T2I is underexplored and inefficient with two-stage relighting pipelines. There is a need for a lightweight, adaptable, training-free approach that allows intuitive lighting control without retraining.
AI.method: Perform a channel-wise analysis of the diffusion latent space to identify channels associated with lighting; manipulate the initial noise (latent) to steer lighting directions during generation, without modifying or retraining the pre-trained model; compatible with ControlNet to extend adaptability across tasks.
AI.result: empirical results show improved lighting consistency over prompt-based baselines, while maintaining image fidelity and text alignment; demonstrates dynamic, user-guided light control and seamless integration with ControlNet across diverse scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a training-free framework for user-guided lighting in diffusion-based T2I, enabling fine-grained light control without fine-tuning, and broad compatibility with existing diffusion pipelines and models.

title: MolEvolve: LLM-Guided Evolutionary Search for Interpretable Molecular Optimization
summary: Despite deep learning's success in chemistry, its impact is hindered by a lack of interpretability and an inability to resolve activity cliffs, where minor structural nuances trigger drastic property shifts. Current representation learning, bound by the similarity principle, often fails to capture these structural-activity discontinuities. To address this, we introduce MolEvolve, an evolutionary framework that reformulates molecular discovery as an autonomous, look-ahead planning problem.
