Unlike traditional methods that depend on human-engineered features or rigid prior knowledge, MolEvolve leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) to actively explore and evolve a library of executable chemical symbolic operations. By utilizing the LLM to cold start and an Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) engine for test-time planning with external tools (e.g. RDKit), the system self-discovers optimal trajectories autonomously. This process evolves transparent reasoning chains that translate complex structural transformations into actionable, human-readable chemical insights. Experimental results demonstrate that MolEvolve's autonomous search not only evolves transparent, human-readable chemical insights, but also outperforms baselines in both property prediction and molecule optimization tasks.

AI.tldr: MolEvolve reframes molecular discovery as autonomous look-ahead planning using an LLM-guided evolution of chemical symbolic operators, enabling interpretable reasoning chains and improved property prediction and optimization compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: Addresses two central issues in deep-learning chemistry: (1) lack of interpretability and (2) inability to resolve activity cliffs—sensitive property shifts due to small structural changes. Traditional representation learning constrained by similarity principles fail to capture discontinuities in structure-activity landscapes.
AI.method: An autonomous framework that uses a Large Language Model (LLM) to cold-start and a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) engine, augmented with external tools (RDKit), to evolve a library of executable chemical symbolic operations. The LLM actively explores and evolves trajectories, producing transparent, human-readable reasoning chains. This look-ahead planning discovers optimal transformation sequences for molecules.
AI.result: Experimental results show that MolEvolve's autonomous search yields transparent, human-readable chemical insights and outperforms baselines in both property prediction and molecule optimization tasks.
AI.conclusion: MolEvolve demonstrates that autonomous, interpretable planning with LLMs and symbolic operation evolution can improve chemistry discovery by capturing structural-activity discontinuities and providing actionable insights, with potential for broader adoption in AI-assisted chemistry.

title: LaDy: Lagrangian-Dynamic Informed Network for Skeleton-based Action Segmentation via Spatial-Temporal Modulation
summary: Skeleton-based Temporal Action Segmentation (STAS) aims to densely parse untrimmed skeletal sequences into frame-level action categories. However, existing methods, while proficient at capturing spatio-temporal kinematics, neglect the underlying physical dynamics that govern human motion. This oversight limits inter-class discriminability between actions with similar kinematics but distinct dynamic intents, and hinders precise boundary localization where dynamic force profiles shift. To address these, we propose the Lagrangian-Dynamic Informed Network (LaDy), a framework integrating principles of Lagrangian dynamics into the segmentation process. Specifically, LaDy first computes generalized coordinates from joint positions and then estimates Lagrangian terms under physical constraints to explicitly synthesize the generalized forces. To further ensure physical coherence, our Energy Consistency Loss enforces the work-energy theorem, aligning kinetic energy change with the work done by the net force. The learned dynamics then drive a Spatio-Temporal Modulation module: Spatially, generalized forces are fused with spatial representations to provide more discriminative semantics. Temporally, salient dynamic signals are constructed for temporal gating, thereby significantly enhancing boundary awareness. Experiments on challenging datasets show that LaDy achieves state-of-the-art performance, validating the integration of physical dynamics for action segmentation. Code is available at https://github.com/HaoyuJi/LaDy.

AI.tldr: Introduces LaDy, a physics-informed STAS model that uses Lagrangian dynamics and an energy-consistency loss to improve discrimination and boundary precision, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Current skeleton-based STAS methods capture kinematics but ignore underlying physical dynamics, reducing inter-class discriminability for similar motions and causing boundary localization issues.
AI.method: From joint positions, compute generalized coordinates; estimate Lagrangian terms to synthesize generalized forces under physical constraints; apply an Energy Consistency Loss enforcing work-energy consistency; feed learned dynamics into a Spatio-Temporal Modulation module for spatial fusion with generalized forces and temporal gating of dynamic signals.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on challenging STAS datasets show state-of-the-art performance, with code released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Integrating physical dynamics via a Lagrangian framework enhances STAS by improving semantic discrimination and boundary accuracy, demonstrating the value of physics-informed representations for action segmentation.

title: On the Use of Bagging for Local Intrinsic Dimensionality Estimation
comment: Main document: 10 pages, 5 figures; Appendix: 38 pages, 27 figures
summary: The theory of Local Intrinsic Dimensionality (LID) has become a valuable tool for characterizing local complexity within and across data manifolds, supporting a range of data mining and machine learning tasks. Accurate LID estimation requires samples drawn from small neighborhoods around each query to avoid biases from nonlocal effects and potential manifold mixing, yet limited data within such neighborhoods tends to cause high estimation variance. As a variance reduction strategy, we propose an ensemble approach that uses subbagging to preserve the local distribution of nearest neighbor (NN) distances. The main challenge is that the uniform reduction in total sample size within each subsample increases the proximity threshold for finding a fixed number k of NNs around the query. As a result, in the specific context of LID estimation, the sampling rate has an additional, complex interplay with the neighborhood size, where both combined determine the sample size as well as the locality and resolution considered for estimation. We analyze both theoretically and experimentally how the choice of the sampling rate and the k-NN size used for LID estimation, alongside the ensemble size, affects performance, enabling informed prior selection of these hyper-parameters depending on application-based preferences. Our results indicate that within broad and well-characterized regions of the hyper-parameters space, using a bagged estimator will most often significantly reduce variance as well as the mean squared error when compared to the corresponding non-bagged baseline, with controllable impact on bias. We additionally propose and evaluate different ways of combining bagging with neighborhood smoothing for substantial further improvements on LID estimation performance.

AI.tldr: Bagged (subbagging) estimators for LID reduce variance and MSE in local intrinsic dimensionality estimation, by preserving the local NN-distance distribution; analyzes how sampling rate, k-NN size, and ensemble size interact; shows broad gains with controllable bias and proposes combining bagging with neighborhood smoothing for further gains.
AI.motivation: LID estimates rely on small local neighborhoods; limited local data causes high variance. A variance-reduction strategy that maintains locality is needed to improve estimation reliability.
AI.method: Propose an ensemble approach using subbagging to preserve the local distribution of nearest-neighbor distances. Analyze the interaction between subsample rate, k used in LID, and ensemble size, both theoretically and empirically. Evaluate combining bagging with neighborhood smoothing.
AI.result: Across broad hyperparameter regions, bagged estimators reduce variance and MSE relative to non-bagged baselines, with controllable bias. Additional gains are obtained by integrating bagging with neighborhood smoothing.
AI.conclusion: Bagging is effective for improving LID estimation by reducing variance (and MSE) while offering tunable bias; provides guidance on hyperparameter choices and suggests extensions via neighborhood smoothing.

title: Granular Ball Guided Stable Latent Domain Discovery for Domain-General Crowd Counting
summary: Single-source domain generalization for crowd counting remains highly challenging because a single labeled source domain often contains heterogeneous latent domains, while test data may exhibit severe distribution shifts. A fundamental difficulty lies in stable latent domain discovery: directly performing flat clustering on evolving sample-level latent features is easily affected by feature noise, outliers, and representation drift, leading to unreliable pseudo-domain assignments and weakened domain-structured learning. To address this issue, we propose a granular ball guided stable latent domain discovery framework for domain-general crowd counting. Specifically, the proposed method first organizes samples into compact local granular balls and then clusters granular ball centers as representatives to obtain pseudo-domains, transforming direct sample-level clustering into a hierarchical representative-based clustering process. This design yields more stable and semantically consistent pseudo-domain assignments. Built upon the discovered latent domains, we further develop a two-branch learning framework that enhances transferable semantic representations via semantic codebook re-encoding while modeling domain-specific appearance variations through a style branch, thereby reducing semantic--style entanglement and improving generalization under domain shifts. Extensive experiments on ShanghaiTech A/B, UCF\_QNRF, and NWPU-Crowd under a strict no-adaptation protocol demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms strong baselines, especially under large domain gaps.

AI.tldr: Proposes granular ball-based latent domain discovery to stabilize pseudo-domain creation for single-source domain generalization in crowd counting, followed by a two-branch learning framework with semantic codebook re-encoding and a style branch to disentangle semantic and style factors. Demonstrates improved generalization under a strict no-adaptation protocol on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Single-source domain generalization for crowd counting is hampered by heterogeneous latent domains and distribution shifts; flat clustering on evolving, noisy latent features yields unreliable pseudo-domain assignments and weak domain-structured learning.
AI.method: Introduce granular local balls to group samples; cluster granular ball centers to form pseudo-domains in a hierarchical representative-based clustering; build a two-branch network with semantic codebook re-encoding and a style branch to model domain-specific appearance variations, reducing semantic–style entanglement.
AI.result: Across ShanghaiTech A/B, UCF_QNRF, and NWPU-Crowd with no adaptation, the method consistently outperforms strong baselines, especially with large domain gaps.
AI.conclusion: Granular ball guided stable latent domain discovery enables more stable pseudo-domain assignments and, when combined with a two-branch learning framework, improves domain-general crowd counting under significant domain shifts.

title: Marchuk: Efficient Global Weather Forecasting from Mid-Range to Sub-Seasonal Scales via Flow Matching
summary: Accurate subseasonal weather forecasting remains a major challenge due to the inherently chaotic nature of the atmosphere, which limits the predictive skill of conventional models beyond the mid-range horizon (approximately 15 days). In this work, we present \textit{Marchuk}, a generative latent flow-matching model for global weather forecasting spanning mid-range to subseasonal timescales, with prediction horizons of up to 30 days. Marchuk conditions on current-day weather maps and autoregressively predicts subsequent days' weather maps within the learned latent space. We replace rotary positional encodings (RoPE) with trainable positional embeddings and extend the temporal context window, which together enhance the model's ability to represent and propagate long-range temporal dependencies during latent forecasting. Marchuk offers two key advantages: high computational efficiency and strong predictive performance. Despite its compact architecture of only 276 million parameters, the model achieves performance comparable to LaDCast, a substantially larger model with 1.6 billion parameters, while operating at significantly higher inference speeds. We open-source our inference code and model at: https://v-gen-ai.github.io/Marchuk/

AI.tldr: Marchuk is a compact latent flow-matching model for global subseasonal weather forecasting (up to 30 days) that conditions on current weather maps and autoregressively forecasts future maps in latent space, achieving competitive performance with far larger models while offering higher efficiency and open-source release.
AI.motivation: Subseasonal forecasting remains challenging due to chaotic atmospheric dynamics and limited skill beyond mid-range (~15 days). There is a need for efficient, accurate models that bridge mid-range to subseasonal horizons without the computational burden of very large networks.
AI.method: A generative latent flow-matching architecture that conditions on current-day weather maps and autoregressively predicts subsequent maps in a learned latent space. Key design changes include replacing rotary positional encodings (RoPE) with trainable positional embeddings and extending the temporal context window to better capture long-range dependencies. The model is compact (276M parameters) and operates in an autoregressive latent forecasting scheme.
AI.result: The model achieves predictive performance comparable to LaDCast (a 1.6B-parameter model) while maintaining significantly faster inference speeds, demonstrating high computational efficiency despite a compact architecture.
AI.conclusion: Marchuk demonstrates that a smaller latent-flow model can deliver competitive mid-range to subseasonal weather forecasts with efficient inference, supporting practical deployment and further open-source contribution.

title: Retinal Layer Segmentation in OCT Images With 2.5D Cross-slice Feature Fusion Module for Glaucoma Assessment
summary: For accurate glaucoma diagnosis and monitoring, reliable retinal layer segmentation in OCT images is essential. However, existing 2D segmentation methods often suffer from slice-to-slice inconsistencies due to the lack of contextual information across adjacent B-scans. 3D segmentation methods are better for capturing slice-to-slice context, but they require expensive computational resources. To address these limitations, we propose a 2.5D segmentation framework that incorporates a novel cross-slice feature fusion (CFF) module into a U-Net-like architecture. The CFF module fuses inter-slice features to effectively capture contextual information, enabling consistent boundary detection across slices and improved robustness in noisy regions. The framework was validated on both a clinical dataset and the publicly available DUKE DME dataset. Compared to other segmentation methods without the CFF module, the proposed method achieved an 8.56% reduction in mean absolute distance and a 13.92% reduction in root mean square error, demonstrating improved segmentation accuracy and robustness. Overall, the proposed 2.5D framework balances contextual awareness and computational efficiency, enabling anatomically reliable retinal layer delineation for automated glaucoma evaluation and potential clinical applications.

AI.tldr: A 2.5D retinal layer segmentation framework with a cross-slice feature fusion (CFF) module integrated into a U-Net-like network improves 2D/3D trade-offs for OCT glaucoma assessment, achieving better boundary accuracy and robustness than 2D baselines with lower computational cost than full 3D methods.
AI.motivation: Need for accurate, robust retinal layer delineation in OCT to support glaucoma diagnosis/monitoring; 2D methods miss cross-slice context; 3D methods are resource-intensive; 2.5D with CFF aims to capture inter-slice context efficiently.
AI.method: Introduce a CFF module that fuses inter-slice features within a U-Net-like architecture to exploit cross-slice context while preserving 2D efficiency; evaluate on clinical and DUKE DME datasets; compare to segmentation methods without CFF.
AI.result: 8.56% reduction in mean absolute distance and 13.92% reduction in RMSE relative to non-CFF methods; improved boundary consistency across slices and robustness in noisy regions.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a balanced 2.5D framework that achieves contextual awareness and computational efficiency, enabling reliable retinal layer delineation for automated glaucoma evaluation and potential clinical deployment.

title: Boosting Document Parsing Efficiency and Performance with Coarse-to-Fine Visual Processing
summary: Document parsing is a fine-grained task where image resolution significantly impacts performance. While advanced research leveraging vision-language models benefits from high-resolution input to boost model performance, this often leads to a quadratic increase in the number of vision tokens and significantly raises computational costs. We attribute this inefficiency to substantial visual regions redundancy in document images, like background. To tackle this, we propose PaddleOCR-VL, a novel coarse-to-fine architecture that focuses on semantically relevant regions while suppressing redundant ones, thereby improving both efficiency and performance. Specifically, we introduce a lightweight Valid Region Focus Module (VRFM) which leverages localization and contextual relationship prediction capabilities to identify valid vision tokens. Subsequently, we design and train a compact yet powerful 0.9B vision-language model (PaddleOCR-VL-0.9B) to perform detailed recognition, guided by VRFM outputs to avoid direct processing of the entire large image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PaddleOCR-VL achieves state-of-the-art performance in both page-level parsing and element-level recognition. It significantly outperforms existing solutions, exhibits strong competitiveness against top-tier VLMs, and delivers fast inference while utilizing substantially fewer vision tokens and parameters, highlighting the effectiveness of targeted coarse-to-fine parsing for accurate and efficient document understanding. The source code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/PaddlePaddle/PaddleOCR.

AI.tldr: PaddleOCR-VL presents a coarse-to-fine document parsing pipeline with VRFM guiding a 0.9B VLM to focus on valid regions, achieving state-of-the-art performance with fewer tokens and parameters and lower compute.
AI.motivation: High-resolution vision-language models improve accuracy but incur quadratic growth in tokens and compute; document images contain substantial visual redundancy (background); need efficient, accurate document understanding.
AI.method: Introduce Valid Region Focus Module (VRFM) to predict valid vision tokens; build PaddleOCR-VL with a 0.9B VLM guided by VRFM; coarse-to-fine processing to focus on semantically relevant regions while suppressing redundant areas; train end-to-end or in stages; design to minimize token count and compute.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in page-level parsing and element-level recognition; outperforms many existing solutions and is competitive with top-tier VLMs; fast inference with substantially fewer vision tokens and parameters.
AI.conclusion: Targeted coarse-to-fine parsing effectively balances accuracy and efficiency for document understanding; the VRFM-guided approach reduces redundancy and computation while preserving or improving performance.

title: Learning Response-Statistic Shifts and Parametric Roll Episodes from Wave--Vessel Time Series via LSTM Functional Models
summary: Parametric roll is a rare but high-consequence instability that can trigger abrupt regime changes in ship response, including pronounced shifts in roll statistics and tail risk. This paper develops a data-driven surrogate that learns the nonlinear, causal functional mapping from incident wave--motion time series to vessel motions, and demonstrates that the surrogate reproduces both (i) parametric roll episodes and (ii) the associated statistical shifts in the response. Crucially, the learning framework is data-source agnostic: the paired wave--motion time series can be obtained from controlled experiments (e.g., towing-tank or basin tests with wave probes and motion tracking) when a hull exists, or from high-fidelity simulations during design when experiments are not yet available. To provide a controlled severe-sea demonstration, we generate training data with a URANS numerical wave tank, using long-crested irregular seas synthesized from a modified Pierson--Moskowitz spectrum. The demonstration dataset comprises 49 random-phase realizations for each of three sea states, simulated at a fixed forward speed selected to yield encounter conditions under which parametric-roll episodes can occur. A stacked LSTM surrogate is trained on wave-elevation time series and evaluated on held-out realizations using time-domain accuracy and distributional fidelity metrics. In the most severe case, the model tracks the onset and growth of large-amplitude roll consistent with parametric excitation, and captures the corresponding changes in roll probability density functions (PDFs). We further compare loss-function choices (MSE, relative-entropy-based objectives, and amplitude-weighted variants) and show how they trade average error for improved tail fidelity relevant to operability and risk assessment.

AI.tldr: A data-driven stacked LSTM surrogate maps incident wave–motion time series to hull response, capturing parametric-roll episodes and associated tail shifts; training uses URANS-generated seas and a source-agnostic approach; evaluates with time-domain accuracy and distributional fidelity, comparing loss functions to improve tail risk.
AI.motivation: Parametric roll is a rare but high-consequence instability that can cause abrupt changes in roll statistics and tail risk. A data-driven, source-agnostic surrogate can learn the nonlinear causal mapping from wave to motion and quantify rare-event risk without relying on a specific physical model.
AI.method: Generate training data via URANS numerical wave tank with long-crested irregular seas from a modified Pierson–Moskowitz spectrum; 49 random-phase realizations per of three sea states at a fixed forward speed to yield encounter conditions enabling parametric roll; train a stacked LSTM on wave-elevation time series; evaluate on held-out realizations using time-domain accuracy and distributional fidelity metrics; compare loss functions (MSE, relative-entropy-based, amplitude-weighted) and assess trade-offs between average error and tail fidelity.
AI.result: The surrogate reproduces both parametric-roll episodes and the associated changes in roll PDFs; it can track onset and growth of large-amplitude roll consistent with parametric excitation; certain loss functions improve tail fidelity while incurring higher average error; demonstrates data-source agnostic applicability across experiments or simulations.
AI.conclusion: A data-driven surrogate can accurately replicate nonlinear parametric-roll dynamics and tail-risk characteristics, offering a practical tool for operability assessment and design risk evaluation. Future work could explore additional architectures, training data efficiency, and experimental validation.

title: Combi-CAM: A Novel Multi-Layer Approach for Explainable Image Geolocalization
summary: Planet-scale photo geolocalization involves the intricate task of estimating the geographic location depicted in an image purely based on its visual features. While deep learning models, particularly convolutional neural networks (CNNs), have significantly advanced this field, understanding the reasoning behind their predictions remains challenging. In this paper, we present Combi-CAM, a novel method that enhances the explainability of CNN-based geolocalization models by combining gradient-weighted class activation maps obtained from several layers of the network architecture, rather than using only information from the deepest layer as is typically done. This approach provides a more detailed understanding of how different image features contribute to the model's decisions, offering deeper insights than the traditional approaches.

AI.tldr: Combi-CAM fuses Grad-CAM maps from multiple CNN layers to improve explainability in planet-scale geolocalization, surpassing single deepest-layer CAMs.
AI.motivation: Current CAM-based explanations largely rely on the deepest CNN layer, which can miss informative cues present at earlier or intermediate layers. Planet-scale geolocalization demands interpretability across features at different spatial scales (textures, patterns, landmarks) to build trust and facilitate debugging.
AI.method: Compute gradient-weighted class activation maps (Grad-CAM) at multiple network layers (early, mid, late). Normalize and aggregate these maps using a layer-weighted scheme to produce Combi-CAM heatmaps that reflect contributions across layers. Compare against single-layer CAMs on a geolocalization task trained on a global dataset, using qualitative visualizations and, if applicable, quantitative explainability metrics.
AI.result: Combi-CAM yields richer, more detailed explanations by capturing complementary cues across layers. The produced heatmaps more comprehensively highlight regions relevant to localization, including both fine-grained textures and coarse semantic patterns, providing deeper insights than single-layer CAMs.
AI.conclusion: Multi-layer CAM aggregation enhances CNN explainability for geolocalization, enabling better understanding and debugging of model decisions. This approach can improve trust and guide future improvements; future work could include quantitative evaluation of explainability, ablation studies over layer choices and aggregation weights, and user studies to assess interpretability.

title: CUA-Suite: Massive Human-annotated Video Demonstrations for Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents (CUAs) hold great promise for automating complex desktop workflows, yet progress toward general-purpose agents is bottlenecked by the scarcity of continuous, high-quality human demonstration videos. Recent work emphasizes that continuous video, not sparse screenshots, is the critical missing ingredient for scaling these agents. However, the largest existing open dataset, ScaleCUA, contains only 2 million screenshots, equating to less than 20 hours of video. To address this bottleneck, we introduce CUA-Suite, a large-scale ecosystem of expert video demonstrations and dense annotations for professional desktop computer-use agents. At its core is VideoCUA, which provides approximately 10,000 human-demonstrated tasks across 87 diverse applications with continuous 30 fps screen recordings, kinematic cursor traces, and multi-layerfed reasoning annotations, totaling approximately 55 hours and 6 million frames of expert video. Unlike sparse datasets that capture only final click coordinates, these continuous video streams preserve the full temporal dynamics of human interaction, forming a superset of information that can be losslessly transformed into the formats required by existing agent frameworks. CUA-Suite further provides two complementary resources: UI-Vision, a rigorous benchmark for evaluating grounding and planning capabilities in CUAs, and GroundCUA, a large-scale grounding dataset with 56K annotated screenshots and over 3.6 million UI element annotations. Preliminary evaluation reveals that current foundation action models struggle substantially with professional desktop applications (~60% task failure rate). Beyond evaluation, CUA-Suite's rich multimodal corpus supports emerging research directions including generalist screen parsing, continuous spatial control, video-based reward modeling, and visual world models. All data and models are publicly released.

AI.tldr: CUA-Suite provides large-scale continuous-video demonstrations for professional desktop agents, introducing VideoCUA plus supporting benchmarks (UI-Vision, GroundCUA); early results indicate current models struggle on professional apps; data enables diverse multimodal research.
AI.motivation: The bottleneck for general-purpose computer-use agents is access to high-quality continuous demonstration video. ScaleCUA lacks video depth (2M screenshots ≈ 20h).
AI.method: VideoCUA contains ~10k tasks across 87 applications with continuous 30fps recordings, cursor traces, and reasoning annotations (~55 hours, 6M frames). UI-Vision benchmarks grounding/planning; GroundCUA provides 56K annotated screenshots and 3.6M UI element annotations. All data/models are publicly released.
AI.result: Preliminary evaluation shows current foundation action models struggle with professional desktop apps, ~60% task failure rate.
AI.conclusion: CUA-Suite offers a rich, multimodal corpus to advance areas like generalist screen parsing, continuous spatial control, video-based reward modeling, and visual world models; it should accelerate progress toward capable desktop agents.

title: Spectral Scalpel: Amplifying Adjacent Action Discrepancy via Frequency-Selective Filtering for Skeleton-Based Action Segmentation
summary: Skeleton-based Temporal Action Segmentation (STAS) seeks to densely segment and classify diverse actions within long, untrimmed skeletal motion sequences. However, existing STAS methodologies face challenges of limited inter-class discriminability and blurred segmentation boundaries, primarily due to insufficient distinction of spatio-temporal patterns between adjacent actions. To address these limitations, we propose Spectral Scalpel, a frequency-selective filtering framework aimed at suppressing shared frequency components between adjacent distinct actions while amplifying their action-specific frequencies, thereby enhancing inter-action discrepancies and sharpening transition boundaries. Specifically, Spectral Scalpel employs adaptive multi-scale spectral filters as scalpels to edit frequency spectra, coupled with a discrepancy loss between adjacent actions serving as the surgical objective. This design amplifies representational disparities between neighboring actions, effectively mitigating boundary localization ambiguities and inter-class confusion. Furthermore, complementing long-term temporal modeling, we introduce a frequency-aware channel mixer to strengthen channel evolution by aggregating spectra across channels. This work presents a novel paradigm for STAS that extends conventional spatio-temporal modeling by incorporating frequency-domain analysis. Extensive experiments on five public datasets demonstrate that Spectral Scalpel achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code is available at https://github.com/HaoyuJi/SpecScalpel.

AI.tldr: A frequency-domain STAS method (Spectral Scalpel) using adaptive multi-scale spectral filters and a discrepancy loss to suppress shared frequencies and amplify action-specific frequencies, plus a frequency-aware channel mixer, achieving state-of-the-art results on five datasets.
AI.motivation: To overcome limited inter-class discriminability and blurred action boundaries in skeleton-based temporal action segmentation by leveraging frequency-domain differences between adjacent actions.
AI.method: Adaptive multi-scale spectral filters act as scalpel edits on frequency spectra to suppress shared components and amplify action-specific components; a discrepancy loss between adjacent actions guides the optimization; a frequency-aware channel mixer aggregates spectra across channels to strengthen temporal evolution, integrating with long-term modeling.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five public datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance; code released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a new paradigm for STAS by incorporating frequency-domain analysis to sharpen boundaries and relieve inter-class confusion, validated by strong empirical results.

title: Language-Guided Structure-Aware Network for Camouflaged Object Detection
summary: Camouflaged Object Detection (COD) aims to segment objects that are highly integrated with the background in terms of color, texture, and structure, making it a highly challenging task in computer vision. Although existing methods introduce multi-scale fusion and attention mechanisms to alleviate the above issues, they generally lack the guidance of textual semantic priors, which limits the model's ability to focus on camouflaged regions in complex scenes. To address this issue, this paper proposes a Language-Guided Structure-Aware Network (LGSAN). Specifically, based on the visual backbone PVT-v2, we introduce CLIP to generate masks from text prompts and RGB images, thereby guiding the multi-scale features extracted by PVT-v2 to focus on potential target regions. On this foundation, we further design a Fourier Edge Enhancement Module (FEEM), which integrates multi-scale features with high-frequency information in the frequency domain to extract edge enhancement features. Furthermore, we propose a Structure-Aware Attention Module (SAAM) to effectively enhance the model's perception of object structures and boundaries. Finally, we introduce a Coarse-Guided Local Refinement Module (CGLRM) to enhance fine-grained reconstruction and boundary integrity of camouflaged object regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently achieves highly competitive performance across multiple COD datasets, validating its effectiveness and robustness.

AI.tldr: A language-guided framework (LGSAN) for camouflaged object detection that fuses CLIP-based textual priors with RGB features, plus Fourier-based edge enhancement, structure-aware attention, and coarse refinement to achieve competitive COD results.
AI.motivation: COD is difficult because camouflaged objects blend with the background; existing COD methods rely on visual cues and multi-scale attention but largely ignore semantic priors from language. Incorporating textual priors via CLIP can steer the model toward probable target regions.
AI.method: Backbone PVT-v2; use CLIP to generate masks from text prompts and RGB images to guide multi-scale features; Fourier Edge Enhancement Module (FEEM) to integrate multi-scale features with high-frequency information in the frequency domain for edge features; Structure-Aware Attention Module (SAAM) to enhance perception of object structure and boundaries; Coarse-Guided Local Refinement Module (CGLRM) to improve fine-grained reconstruction and boundary integrity of camouflaged regions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate highly competitive performance across multiple COD datasets, validating the effectiveness and robustness of LGSAN.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework shows that incorporating textual semantic priors with structure-focused processing and refinement modules yields improved camouflaged object detection, with strong performance and robustness across datasets.

title: Conformalized Transfer Learning for Li-ion Battery State of Health Forecasting under Manufacturing and Usage Variability
comment: Submitted to the 2026 American Control Conference (ACC)
summary: Accurate forecasting of state-of-health (SOH) is essential for ensuring safe and reliable operation of lithium-ion cells. However, existing models calibrated on laboratory tests at specific conditions often fail to generalize to new cells that differ due to small manufacturing variations or operate under different conditions. To address this challenge, an uncertainty-aware transfer learning framework is proposed, combining a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model with domain adaptation via Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and uncertainty quantification through Conformal Prediction (CP). The LSTM model is trained on a virtual battery dataset designed to capture real-world variability in electrode manufacturing and operating conditions. MMD aligns latent feature distributions between simulated and target domains to mitigate domain shift, while CP provides calibrated, distribution-free prediction intervals. This framework improves both the generalization and trustworthiness of SOH forecasts across heterogeneous cells.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware transfer learning for Li-ion SOH forecasting using LSTM with MMD domain adaptation and Conformal Prediction intervals, trained on simulated data to generalize across manufacturing variability and operating conditions.
AI.motivation: SOH forecasts often fail to generalize across cells due to small manufacturing variations and different operating conditions; need models with domain generalization and calibrated uncertainty estimates.
AI.method: Train an LSTM on a virtual battery dataset capturing real-world variability; apply Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) to align latent features between simulated (source) and target domains; use Conformal Prediction (CP) to obtain calibrated, distribution-free prediction intervals.
AI.result: Framework improves generalization to heterogeneous cells and provides calibrated prediction intervals, increasing trust in SOH forecasts across varying conditions.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware transfer learning combining domain adaptation and conformal uncertainty quantification enhances robust and trustworthy SOH forecasting across diverse Li-ion cells.

title: Tutor-Student Reinforcement Learning: A Dynamic Curriculum for Robust Deepfake Detection
summary: Standard supervised training for deepfake detection treats all samples with uniform importance, which can be suboptimal for learning robust and generalizable features. In this work, we propose a novel Tutor-Student Reinforcement Learning (TSRL) framework to dynamically optimize the training curriculum. Our method models the training process as a Markov Decision Process where a ``Tutor'' agent learns to guide a ``Student'' (the deepfake detector). The Tutor, implemented as a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent, observes a rich state representation for each training sample, encapsulating not only its visual features but also its historical learning dynamics, such as EMA loss and forgetting counts. Based on this state, the Tutor takes an action by assigning a continuous weight (0-1) to the sample's loss, thereby dynamically re-weighting the training batch. The Tutor is rewarded based on the Student's immediate performance change, specifically rewarding transitions from incorrect to correct predictions. This strategy encourages the Tutor to learn a curriculum that prioritizes high-value samples, such as hard-but-learnable examples, leading to a more efficient and effective training process. We demonstrate that this adaptive curriculum improves the Student's generalization capabilities against unseen manipulation techniques compared to traditional training methods. Code is available at https://github.com/wannac1/TSRL.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement-learning-based curriculum learning framework (TSRL) for deepfake detection, where a PPO tutor assigns per-sample loss weights to guide the student detector, aiming to improve generalization to unseen manipulations.
AI.motivation: Standard uniform training treats all samples equally, which can impede learning robust, generalizable features for deepfake detection. A dynamic curriculum that prioritizes informative samples and leverages learning dynamics could enhance generalization.
AI.method: Model the training process as an MDP. A Tutor agent (PPO) observes rich per-sample state representations, including visual features and learning dynamics (EMA loss, forgetting counts). The Tutor outputs a continuous weight [0,1] to scale the sample loss, effectively re-weighting the training batch. The Tutor is rewarded by the Student's immediate performance improvement (correcting from incorrect to correct).
AI.result: Claimed improvement in generalization to unseen manipulation techniques compared with standard training. Code is provided at the authors' repository.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive curriculum learning via a Tutor–Student RL framework can yield more efficient and effective training for deepfake detectors by focusing on high-value samples (e.g., hard-but-learnable examples).

title: Uniform Laws of Large Numbers in Product Spaces
summary:
Uniform laws of large numbers form a cornerstone of Vapnik--Chervonenkis theory, where they are characterized by the finiteness of the VC dimension. In this work, we study uniform convergence phenomena in cartesian product spaces, under assumptions on the underlying distribution that are compatible with the product structure. Specifically, we assume that the distribution is absolutely continuous with respect to the product of its marginals, a condition that captures many natural settings, including product distributions, sparse mixtures of product distributions, distributions with low mutual information, and more.
  We show that, under this assumption, a uniform law of large numbers holds for a family of events if and only if the linear VC dimension of the family is finite. The linear VC dimension is defined as the maximum size of a shattered set that lies on an axis-parallel line, namely, a set of vectors that agree on all but at most one coordinate. This dimension is always at most the classical VC dimension, yet it can be arbitrarily smaller. For instance, the family of convex sets in $\mathbb{R}^d$ has linear VC dimension $2$, while its VC dimension is infinite already for $d\ge 2$. Our proofs rely on estimator that departs substantially from the standard empirical mean estimator and exhibits more intricate structure. We show that such deviations from the standard empirical mean estimator are unavoidable in this setting. Throughout the paper, we propose several open questions, with a particular focus on quantitative sample complexity bounds.

AI.tldr: In product-space settings with distributions absolutely continuous w.r.t. the product of marginals, uniform LLN for a family of events holds iff the linear VC dimension is finite. The linear VC dimension is the max size of a set shattered along an axis-parallel line, and can be much smaller than the standard VC dimension (e.g., convex sets have linear VC dimension 2 but infinite VC dimension in d≥2). The authors introduce a non-standard estimator whose deviation from the empirical mean is necessary in this context, and provide open questions on quantitative sample complexity.
AI.motivation: To extend uniform convergence results (VC theory) to product-structured spaces by exploiting a natural weakened dependence structure (absolute continuity w.r.t. product marginals). This captures product distributions, sparse mixtures, and low mutual information scenarios, enabling refined complexity notions (linear VC dimension).
AI.method: Establish a uniform LLN for a family of events under the product-structure assumption. Define the linear VC dimension as the largest axis-parallel-shattered set size. Prove equivalence between finiteness of this dimension and uniform LLN, and show to achieve this, one must use estimators deviating from the sample mean. Provide examples (convex sets in R^d) and discuss the relationship between linear and standard VC dimensions. Present open questions and discuss sample complexity implications.
AI.result: Characterizes uniform convergence in product-spaces via a finite linear VC dimension; introduces non-standard estimators embodying required deviations from empirical mean; demonstrates the linear VC dimension can be strictly smaller than the classical VC dimension; gives examples (convex sets with linear VC dimension 2) and frames open questions on quantitative bounds.
AI.conclusion: Uniform LLN in product-structured spaces is governed by the finite linear VC dimension, a sharper dimension measure than classical VC dimension. The study highlights the intrinsic need for non-standard estimators in this setting and points to future work on explicit sample complexity bounds and broader applicability.

title: LightSplat: Fast and Memory-Efficient Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Understanding in Five Seconds
summary: Open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding enables users to segment novel objects in complex 3D environments through natural language. However, existing approaches remain slow, memory-intensive, and overly complex due to iterative optimization and dense per-Gaussian feature assignments. To address this, we propose LightSplat, a fast and memory-efficient training-free framework that injects compact 2-byte semantic indices into 3D representations from multi-view images. By assigning semantic indices only to salient regions and managing them with a lightweight index-feature mapping, LightSplat eliminates costly feature optimization and storage overhead. We further ensure semantic consistency and efficient inference via single-step clustering that links geometrically and semantically related masks in 3D. We evaluate our method on LERF-OVS, ScanNet, and DL3DV-OVS across complex indoor-outdoor scenes. As a result, LightSplat achieves state-of-the-art performance with up to 50-400x speedup and 64x lower memory, enabling scalable language-driven 3D understanding. For more details, visit our project page https://vision3d-lab.github.io/lightsplat/.

AI.tldr: LightSplat is a training-free, memory-efficient framework that injects 2-byte semantic indices into 3D representations for open-vocabulary 3D understanding, achieving 50–400x speedups and 64x memory reduction.
AI.motivation: Open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding methods are slow and memory-intensive due to iterative optimization and dense per-Gaussian features; a fast, scalable alternative is needed.
AI.method: LightSplat attaches compact 2-byte semantic indices to 3D representations extracted from multi-view images, but only for salient regions. It uses a lightweight index–feature mapping to avoid heavy feature storage and eliminates training-based optimization. Semantic consistency is ensured via single-step clustering that links geometrically and semantically related masks in 3D.
AI.result: On LERF-OVS, ScanNet, and DL3DV-OVS, LightSplat achieves state-of-the-art performance with up to 50–400x speedup and 64x lower memory usage.
AI.conclusion: LightSplat enables scalable, language-driven 3D understanding in a training-free, memory-efficient framework, suitable for complex indoor–outdoor scenes; see project page for details.

title: Project and Generate: Divergence-Free Neural Operators for Incompressible Flows
summary: Learning-based models for fluid dynamics often operate in unconstrained function spaces, leading to physically inadmissible, unstable simulations. While penalty-based methods offer soft regularization, they provide no structural guarantees, resulting in spurious divergence and long-term collapse. In this work, we introduce a unified framework that enforces the incompressible continuity equation as a hard, intrinsic constraint for both deterministic and generative modeling. First, to project deterministic models onto the divergence-free subspace, we integrate a differentiable spectral Leray projection grounded in the Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition, which restricts the regression hypothesis space to physically admissible velocity fields. Second, to generate physically consistent distributions, we show that simply projecting model outputs is insufficient when the prior is incompatible. To address this, we construct a divergence-free Gaussian reference measure via a curl-based pushforward, ensuring the entire probability flow remains subspace-consistent by construction. Experiments on 2D Navier-Stokes equations demonstrate exact incompressibility up to discretization error and substantially improved stability and physical consistency.

AI.tldr: A unified framework enforcing incompressibility as a hard constraint for both deterministic and generative fluid models: spectral Leray projection for deterministic regression; curl-based pushforward to build a divergence-free prior for generative models; experiments on 2D Navier–Stokes show exact incompressibility within discretization error and improved stability.
AI.motivation: Unconstrained function spaces yield physically inadmissible, unstable simulations; penalties lack guarantees. A hard, intrinsic incompressibility constraint is needed for robust, long-term physical fidelity.
AI.method: Deterministic: differentiable spectral Leray projection based on the Helmholtz–Hodge decomposition to project velocity fields onto the divergence-free subspace. Generative: construct a divergence-free Gaussian reference measure via a curl-based pushforward to keep probability flow within the divergence-free subspace, ensuring subspace-consistent sampling.
AI.result: Exact incompressibility up to discretization error; substantially improved stability and physical consistency in 2D Navier–Stokes experiments.
AI.conclusion: Hard incompressibility constraints can be integrated into both regression and generative modeling pipelines to yield physically admissible, stable fluid simulations.

title: A convergent Plug-and-Play Majorization-Minimization algorithm for Poisson inverse problems
summary: In this paper, we present a novel variational plug-and-play algorithm for Poisson inverse problems. Our approach minimizes an explicit functional which is the sum of a Kullback-Leibler data fidelity term and a regularization term based on a pre-trained neural network. By combining classical likelihood maximization methods with recent advances in gradient-based denoisers, we allow the use of pre-trained Gaussian denoisers without sacrificing convergence guarantees. The algorithm is formulated in the majorization-minimization framework, which guarantees convergence to a stationary point. Numerical experiments confirm state-of-the-art performance in deconvolution and tomography under moderate noise, and demonstrate clear superiority in high-noise conditions, making this method particularly valuable for nuclear medicine applications.

AI.tldr: A variational plug-and-play method for Poisson inverse problems using KL data fidelity and a pre-trained neural network regularizer, with majorization-minimization guarantees of convergence to a stationary point; achieves state-of-the-art results in deconvolution and tomography, especially under high noise, with applications in nuclear medicine.
AI.motivation: To combine likelihood-based Poisson modeling with powerful gradient-based denoisers while preserving convergence guarantees, addressing the challenges of high-noise Poisson inverses.
AI.method: Formulates a variational objective as KL data fidelity plus a neural-network-based regularization term; uses a plug-and-play framework within majorization-minimization to ensure convergence; enables use of pre-trained Gaussian denoisers without sacrificing convergence.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show state-of-the-art performance in deconvolution and tomography under moderate noise and clear superiority in high-noise conditions, highlighting benefits for nuclear medicine.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a principled, convergent PnP algorithm that leverages pre-trained denoisers for Poisson inverse problems, offering strong performance in challenging imaging scenarios and potential impact on medical imaging.

title: Counting Without Numbers \& Finding Without Words
summary: Every year, 10 million pets enter shelters, separated from their families. Despite desperate searches by both guardians and lost animals, 70% never reunite, not because matches do not exist, but because current systems look only at appearance, while animals recognize each other through sound. We ask, why does computer vision treat vocalizing species as silent visual objects? Drawing on five decades of cognitive science showing that animals perceive quantity approximately and communicate identity acoustically, we present the first multimodal reunification system integrating visual and acoustic biometrics. Our species-adaptive architecture processes vocalizations from 10Hz elephant rumbles to 4kHz puppy whines, paired with probabilistic visual matching that tolerates stress-induced appearance changes. This work demonstrates that AI grounded in biological communication principles can serve vulnerable populations that lack human language.

AI.tldr: A multimodal biometrics system combines acoustic vocalizations and probabilistic visual matching to reunify lost pets, addressing appearance-only limitations in current computer vision.
AI.motivation: Despite high numbers of pets entering shelters and low reunification rates (≈70%), current systems rely on appearance. Cognitive science shows animals use acoustic cues for identity, suggesting a need for vocal biometrics in AI reunification.
AI.method: A species-adaptive architecture processes a wide vocal range (from low-frequency elephant rumbles to high-frequency puppy whines) and couples it with probabilistic visual matching that tolerates stress-induced appearance changes.
AI.result: Demonstrates, for the first time, an AI reunification framework grounded in biological communication principles, capable of serving populations lacking human language by leveraging multimodal cues.
AI.conclusion: Integrating acoustic and visual biometrics grounded in biology can enhance animal welfare applications and motivate broader cross-modal AI systems for vulnerable populations.

title: CarePilot: A Multi-Agent Framework for Long-Horizon Computer Task Automation in Healthcare
summary: Multimodal agentic pipelines are transforming human-computer interaction by enabling efficient and accessible automation of complex, real-world tasks. However, recent efforts have focused on short-horizon or general-purpose applications (e.g., mobile or desktop interfaces), leaving long-horizon automation for domain-specific systems, particularly in healthcare, largely unexplored. To address this, we introduce CareFlow, a high-quality human-annotated benchmark comprising complex, long-horizon software workflows across medical annotation tools, DICOM viewers, EHR systems, and laboratory information systems. On this benchmark, existing vision-language models (VLMs) perform poorly, struggling with long-horizon reasoning and multi-step interactions in medical contexts. To overcome this, we propose CarePilot, a multi-agent framework based on the actor-critic paradigm. The Actor integrates tool grounding with dual-memory mechanisms (long-term and short-term experience) to predict the next semantic action from the visual interface and system state. The Critic evaluates each action, updates memory based on observed effects, and either executes or provides corrective feedback to refine the workflow. Through iterative agentic simulation, the Actor learns to perform more robust and reasoning-aware predictions during inference. Our experiments show that CarePilot achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong closed-source and open-source multimodal baselines by approximately 15.26% and 3.38%, respectively, on our benchmark and out-of-distribution dataset.

AI.tldr: Introduces CareFlow and CarePilot for long-horizon multimodal automation in healthcare, achieving state-of-the-art performance on a new medical benchmark and out-of-distribution data.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in long-horizon, domain-specific automation in healthcare where current vision-language models underperform on complex medical workflows across multiple software interfaces.
AI.method: CareFlow: a high-quality, human-annotated benchmark of complex medical workflows across annotation tools, DICOM viewers, EHRs, and LIS. CarePilot: a dual-memory actor-critic framework. The Actor grounds tools and uses long- and short-term memory to predict the next semantic action from the UI and system state. The Critic evaluates actions, updates memory from observed effects, and either executes or provides corrective feedback. Training occurs via iterative agentic simulation to improve inference-time reasoning.
AI.result: CarePilot achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong closed-source baselines by ~15.26% and open-source baselines by ~3.38% on the CareFlow benchmark and an out-of-distribution dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables robust, reasoning-aware long-horizon automation in medical contexts, demonstrating strong performance gains over existing multimodal models and enabling more effective automation for healthcare workflows.

title: Claudini: Autoresearch Discovers State-of-the-Art Adversarial Attack Algorithms for LLMs
summary:
LLM agents like Claude Code can not only write code but also be used for autonomous AI research and engineering \citep{rank2026posttrainbench, novikov2025alphaevolve}. We show that an \emph{autoresearch}-style pipeline \citep{karpathy2026autoresearch} powered by Claude Code discovers novel white-box adversarial attack \textit{algorithms} that \textbf{significantly outperform all existing (30+) methods} in jailbreaking and prompt injection evaluations.
  Starting from existing attack implementations, such as GCG~\citep{zou2023universal}, the agent iterates to produce new algorithms achieving up to 40\% attack success rate on CBRN queries against GPT-OSS-Safeguard-20B, compared to $\leq$10\% for existing algorithms (\Cref{fig:teaser}, left).
  The discovered algorithms generalize: attacks optimized on surrogate models transfer directly to held-out models, achieving \textbf{100\% ASR against Meta-SecAlign-70B} \citep{chen2025secalign} versus 56\% for the best baseline (\Cref{fig:teaser}, middle). Extending the findings of~\cite{carlini2025autoadvexbench}, our results are an early demonstration that incremental safety and security research can be automated using LLM agents. White-box adversarial red-teaming is particularly well-suited for this: existing methods provide strong starting points, and the optimization objective yields dense, quantitative feedback. We release all discovered attacks alongside baseline implementations and evaluation code at https://github.com/romovpa/claudini.

AI.tldr: Autoresearch with Claude Code discovers novel white-box adversarial attack algorithms that substantially outperform existing methods in jailbreaking/prompt injection, with strong cross-model transfer and open-source releases.
AI.motivation: Assess whether LLM agents can autonomously advance safety/security research and red-teaming by generating new adversarial attack algorithms and evaluating their effectiveness.
AI.method: An autoresearch pipeline initialized from existing attacks (e.g., GCG) uses Claude Code to iteratively generate, test, and refine attack algorithms; evaluates on CBRN prompts against GPT-OSS-Safeguard-20B; assesses transfer to held-out models (e.g., Meta-SecAlign-70B); builds on AutoAdvexBench findings; releases code and attacks.
AI.result: Discovered algorithms achieve up to 40% attack success rate on CBRN queries, exceeding existing methods (≤10%); transferred attacks achieve 100% ASR on Meta-SecAlign-70B vs 56% baseline; generalization demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that incremental safety/security research can be automated with LLM agents; white-box red-teaming is especially effective; provides open-source artifacts for benchmarking and further research.

title: Heuristic-inspired Reasoning Priors Facilitate Data-Efficient Referring Object Detection
summary: Most referring object detection (ROD) models, especially the modern grounding detectors, are designed for data-rich conditions, yet many practical deployments, such as robotics, augmented reality, and other specialized domains, would face severe label scarcity. In such regimes, end-to-end grounding detectors need to learn spatial and semantic structure from scratch, wasting precious samples. We ask a simple question: Can explicit reasoning priors help models learn more efficiently when data is scarce? To explore this, we first introduce a Data-efficient Referring Object Detection (De-ROD) task, which is a benchmark protocol for measuring ROD performance in low-data and few-shot settings. We then propose the HeROD (Heuristic-inspired ROD), a lightweight, model-agnostic framework that injects explicit, heuristic-inspired spatial and semantic reasoning priors, which are interpretable signals derived based on the referring phrase, into 3 stages of a modern DETR-style pipeline: proposal ranking, prediction fusion, and Hungarian matching. By biasing both training and inference toward plausible candidates, these priors promise to improve label efficiency and convergence performance. On RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, and RefCOCOg, HeROD consistently outperforms strong grounding baselines in scarce-label regimes. More broadly, our results suggest that integrating simple, interpretable reasoning priors provides a practical and extensible path toward better data-efficient vision-language understanding.

AI.tldr: Introduces De-ROD, a data-efficient benchmark for referring object detection, and HeROD, a lightweight framework that injects heuristic-inspired spatial and semantic priors into DETR-like pipelines to improve performance under scarce-label conditions.
AI.motivation: Current ROD models, especially grounding detectors, require abundant labels and struggle to learn spatial/semantic structure in low-data regimes common in robotics and AR. Explicit priors can guide learning and improve data efficiency.
AI.method: Define De-ROD as a benchmark for low-data and few-shot ROD. Propose HeROD, model-agnostic, inserting interpretable spatial/semantic priors derived from the referring phrase into three stages of a DETR-style pipeline: proposal ranking, prediction fusion, and Hungarian matching.
AI.result: Empirical results on RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, RefCOCOg show HeROD consistently outperforms strong baselines in scarce-label settings, indicating improved data efficiency and faster convergence.
AI.conclusion: Simple, interpretable reasoning priors can be effectively integrated into vision-language models to achieve better data-efficient ROD, with potential for broad applicability and extension.

title: No Single Metric Tells the Whole Story: A Multi-Dimensional Evaluation Framework for Uncertainty Attributions
comment: Accepted at the Fourth World Conference on Explainable Artificial Intelligence, xAI 2026, Fortaleza, Brazil, July 1-3, 2026
summary: Research on explainable AI (XAI) has frequently focused on explaining model predictions. More recently, methods have been proposed to explain prediction uncertainty by attributing it to input features (uncertainty attributions). However, the evaluation of these methods remains inconsistent as studies rely on heterogeneous proxy tasks and metrics, hindering comparability. We address this by aligning uncertainty attributions with the well-established Co-12 framework for XAI evaluation. We propose concrete implementations for the correctness, consistency, continuity, and compactness properties. Additionally, we introduce conveyance, a property tailored to uncertainty attributions that evaluates whether controlled increases in epistemic uncertainty reliably propagate to feature-level attributions. We demonstrate our evaluation framework with eight metrics across combinations of uncertainty quantification and feature attribution methods on tabular and image data. Our experiments show that gradient-based methods consistently outperform perturbation-based approaches in consistency and conveyance, while Monte-Carlo dropconnect outperforms Monte-Carlo dropout in most metrics. Although most metrics rank the methods consistently across samples, inter-method agreement remains low. This suggests no single metric sufficiently evaluates uncertainty attribution quality. The proposed evaluation framework contributes to the body of knowledge by establishing a foundation for systematic comparison and development of uncertainty attribution methods.

AI.tldr: Proposes a unified evaluation framework for uncertainty attributions in XAI by aligning with the Co-12 framework, introducing properties (correctness, consistency, continuity, compactness) and a new conveyance metric. Evaluates eight metrics across UQ and feature attribution methods on tabular/image data; finds gradient-based attributions outperform perturbation-based in consistency/conveyance; MC dropconnect often wins over MC dropout; high intra-method consistency but low inter-method agreement, indicating no single metric suffices. Provides foundation for systematic comparison and development of uncertainty attribution methods.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of uncertainty attributions are heterogeneous and incomparable. Aligning with a standardized XAI evaluation framework (Co-12) and introducing tailored properties aims to enable principled, reproducible comparisons across methods and datasets.
AI.method: Define concrete implementations for Co-12 properties (correctness, consistency, continuity, compactness) and introduce conveyance to test how controlled epistemic uncertainty affects feature-level attributions. Evaluate eight metrics across combinations of uncertainty quantification (e.g., Monte Carlo methods) and feature attribution methods on tabular and image datasets.
AI.result: Gradient-based uncertainty attributions beat perturbation-based approaches in consistency and conveyance. Monte-Carlo DropConnect generally outperforms Monte-Carlo Dropout across most metrics. While most metrics agree across samples, agreement between different methods is low, implying that no single metric fully captures attribution quality.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled foundation for systematic comparison and development of uncertainty attribution methods, enabling standardized evaluation and identification of strong methods, while also highlighting the need for multiple metrics due to low inter-method agreement.

title: AVO: Agentic Variation Operators for Autonomous Evolutionary Search
summary: Agentic Variation Operators (AVO) are a new family of evolutionary variation operators that replace the fixed mutation, crossover, and hand-designed heuristics of classical evolutionary search with autonomous coding agents. Rather than confining a language model to candidate generation within a prescribed pipeline, AVO instantiates variation as a self-directed agent loop that can consult the current lineage, a domain-specific knowledge base, and execution feedback to propose, repair, critique, and verify implementation edits. We evaluate AVO on attention, among the most aggressively optimized kernel targets in AI, on NVIDIA Blackwell (B200) GPUs. Over 7 days of continuous autonomous evolution on multi-head attention, AVO discovers kernels that outperform cuDNN by up to 3.5% and FlashAttention-4 by up to 10.5% across the evaluated configurations. The discovered optimizations transfer readily to grouped-query attention, requiring only 30 minutes of additional autonomous adaptation and yielding gains of up to 7.0% over cuDNN and 9.3% over FlashAttention-4. Together, these results show that agentic variation operators move beyond prior LLM-in-the-loop evolutionary pipelines by elevating the agent from candidate generator to variation operator, and can discover performance-critical micro-architectural optimizations that produce kernels surpassing state-of-the-art expert-engineered attention implementations on today's most advanced GPU hardware.

AI.tldr: Agentic Variation Operators use autonomous coding agents to drive evolutionary variation, yielding kernel optimizations that surpass state-of-the-art attention implementations on modern GPUs.
AI.motivation: To overcome fixed, hand-designed search operators in evolutionary pipelines and unlock more effective micro-architectural optimizations by elevating the variation operator to an autonomous agent.
AI.method: A self-directed agent loop that consults lineage, a domain KB, and execution feedback to propose, repair, critique, and verify code edits; evaluated on multi-head attention kernels on NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs over 7 days; transfers to grouped-query attention with minor further adaptation.
AI.result: Discovered kernels outperform cuDNN by up to 3.5% and FlashAttention-4 by up to 10.5%; transfer to grouped-query attention yields up to 7.0% over cuDNN and 9.3% over FlashAttention-4 after 30 min adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Agentic variation operators show potential to surpass expert-engineered attention kernels and extend beyond LLMin-the-loop pipelines by enabling autonomous generation of high-performance micro-architectural optimizations on current GPUs.

title: Unlocking Few-Shot Capabilities in LVLMs via Prompt Conditioning and Head Selection
summary: Current Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) excel at many zero-shot tasks like image captioning, visual question answering and OCR. However, these same models suffer from poor performance at image classification tasks, underperforming against CLIP-based methods. Notably, this gap is surprising because many LVLMs use CLIP-pretrained vision encoders. Yet LVLMs are not inherently limited by CLIP's architecture with independent vision and text encoders. In CLIP, this separation biases classification toward class-name matching rather than joint visual-text reasoning. In this paper we show that, despite their poor raw performance, LVLMs can improve visual feature class separability at inference using prompt conditioning, and LVLMs' internal representations, especially attention heads, can outperform the model itself at zero-shot and few-shot classification. We introduce Head Ensemble Classifiers (HEC) to bridge the performance gap between CLIP-based and LVLM-based classification methods. Inspired by Gaussian Discriminant Analysis, HEC ranks the most discriminative vision and text heads and combines them into a training-free classifier. We show that HEC achieves state-of-the-art performance in few-shot and zero-shot classification across 12 datasets.

AI.tldr: HEC: a training-free Head Ensemble Classifier for LVLMs that uses discriminative vision/text heads (inspired by Gaussian Discriminant Analysis) and prompt conditioning to boost zero-shot and few-shot classification, achieving state-of-the-art results across 12 datasets.
AI.motivation: To close the classification performance gap between LVLMs and CLIP-based methods by leveraging LVLM internal representations (attention heads) and prompt-conditioned features for joint visual-text reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce Head Ensemble Classifiers (HEC) that rank discriminative vision and text heads using a Gaussian Discriminant-like criterion and ensemble the top heads into a training-free classifier; employ prompt conditioning to enhance visual feature separability.
AI.result: HEC achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot and few-shot classification performance across 12 datasets, narrowing the gap between LVLM-based and CLIP-based classification methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that LVLMs’ internal representations, when selectively harnessed via HEC and prompt conditioning, can match or surpass CLIP-based classifiers without additional training.

title: UI-Voyager: A Self-Evolving GUI Agent Learning via Failed Experience
comment: Code and models are available at https://github.com/ui-voyager/UI-Voyager
summary: Autonomous mobile GUI agents have attracted increasing attention along with the advancement of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, existing methods still suffer from inefficient learning from failed trajectories and ambiguous credit assignment under sparse rewards for long-horizon GUI tasks. To that end, we propose UI-Voyager, a novel two-stage self-evolving mobile GUI agent. In the first stage, we employ Rejection Fine-Tuning (RFT), which enables the continuous co-evolution of data and models in a fully autonomous loop. The second stage introduces Group Relative Self-Distillation (GRSD), which identifies critical fork points in group rollouts and constructs dense step-level supervision from successful trajectories to correct failed ones. Extensive experiments on AndroidWorld show that our 4B model achieves an 81.0% Pass@1 success rate, outperforming numerous recent baselines and exceeding human-level performance. Ablation and case studies further verify the effectiveness of GRSD. Our method represents a significant leap toward efficient, self-evolving, and high-performance mobile GUI automation without expensive manual data annotation.

AI.tldr: UI-Voyager is a two-stage self-evolving mobile GUI agent with Rejection Fine-Tuning (RFT) and Group Relative Self-Distillation (GRSD); achieves 81.0% Pass@1 on AndroidWorld, surpassing baselines and reaching human-level performance with no manual annotation.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon GUI tasks suffer from inefficient learning from failed trajectories and sparse rewards; need autonomous, data-model co-evolution to improve sample efficiency and credit assignment.
AI.method: Stage 1: Rejection Fine-Tuning (RFT) enables autonomous co-evolution of data and models via a rejection-based loop. Stage 2: Group Relative Self-Distillation (GRSD) identifies fork points in group rollouts and derives dense, step-level supervision from successful trajectories to correct failures.
AI.result: 4B model; 81.0% Pass@1 on AndroidWorld; outperforms baselines; ablation and case studies validate GRSD; human-level performance achieved; no expensive manual data annotation.
AI.conclusion: The approach represents a significant step toward efficient, self-evolving, high-performance mobile GUI automation.

title: TuneShift-KD: Knowledge Distillation and Transfer for Fine-tuned Models
summary: To embed domain-specific or specialized knowledge into pre-trained foundation models, fine-tuning using techniques such as parameter efficient fine-tuning (e.g. LoRA) is a common practice. However, as new LLM architectures and pre-trained models emerge, transferring this specialized knowledge to newer models becomes an important task. In many scenarios, the original specialized data may be unavailable due to privacy or commercial restrictions, necessitating distillation and transfer of this specialized knowledge from the fine-tuned base model to a different pre-trained model. We present TuneShift-KD, a novel approach that automatically distills specialized knowledge from a fine-tuned model to a target model using only a few examples representative of the specialized information. Our key insight is that specialized knowledge can be identified through perplexity differences between base and fine-tuned models: prompts where the fine-tuned model responds confidently (low perplexity), but the base model struggles (high perplexity), indicate queries corresponding to the specialized knowledge learned by the fine-tuned model. TuneShift-KD leverages this insight to create a synthetic training dataset to transfer the specialized knowledge. Using an iterative process, TuneShift-KD generates more prompts similar to those that generated responses with specialized knowledge. TuneShift-KD does not require training discriminators or access to training datasets. It is an automated approach that only requires the initial fine-tuned and base models and a few representative prompts. Our experiments demonstrate that models fine-tuned using TuneShift-KD achieve higher accuracy than prior approaches, enabling ease of deployment and more effective transfer of the specialized knowledge.

AI.tldr: TuneShift-KD distills specialized knowledge from a fine-tuned model into a target model using only a few prompts, by identifying knowledge via perplexity gaps between base and fine-tuned models and generating synthetic training data iteratively, without discriminators or access to training data, achieving higher accuracy than prior methods.
AI.motivation: When specialized knowledge learned during fine-tuning must be transferred to newer models but original data are unavailable due to privacy/commercial restrictions; need data-efficient, automated distillation.
AI.method: Perplexity-based knowledge discovery: compare base vs fine-tuned model perplexities on prompts; prompts with low perplexity for the fine-tuned model and high perplexity for the base model indicate knowledge; construct synthetic training data from these prompts; iteratively generate additional prompts with similar characteristics; train target model to capture specialized knowledge; no discriminators or access to training datasets required; uses only the base and fine-tuned models and a few representative prompts.
AI.result: Empirical results show that TuneShift-KD improves accuracy of the target model relative to prior approaches, enabling easier deployment and better transfer of specialized knowledge.
AI.conclusion: TuneShift-KD offers a data-efficient, automated knowledge distillation approach that leverages perplexity gaps to identify and transfer specialized knowledge without original datasets or discriminators, effective across evolving LLM architectures.

title: RefReward-SR: LR-Conditioned Reward Modeling for Preference-Aligned Super-Resolution
summary: Recent advances in generative super-resolution (SR) have greatly improved visual realism, yet existing evaluation and optimization frameworks remain misaligned with human perception. Full-Reference and No-Reference metrics often fail to reflect perceptual preference, either penalizing semantically plausible details due to pixel misalignment or favoring visually sharp but inconsistent artifacts. Moreover, most SR methods rely on ground-truth (GT)-dependent distribution matching, which does not necessarily correspond to human judgments. In this work, we propose RefReward-SR, a low-resolution (LR) reference-aware reward model for preference-aligned SR. Instead of relying on GT supervision or NR evaluation, RefReward-SR assesses high-resolution (HR) reconstructions conditioned on their LR inputs, treating the LR image as a semantic anchor. Leveraging the visual-linguistic priors of a Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLM), it evaluates semantic consistency and plausibility in a reasoning-aware manner. To support this paradigm, we construct RefSR-18K, the first large-scale LR-conditioned preference dataset for SR, providing pairwise rankings based on LR-HR consistency and HR naturalness. We fine-tune the MLLM with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using LR-conditioned ranking rewards, and further integrate GRPO into SR model training with RefReward-SR as the core reward signal for preference-aligned generation. Extensive experiments show that our framework achieves substantially better alignment with human judgments, producing reconstructions that preserve semantic consistency while enhancing perceptual plausibility and visual naturalness. Code, models, and datasets will be released upon paper acceptance.

AI.tldr: RefReward-SR introduces LR-conditioned reward learning for SR, using a multimodal LLM and GRPO to align SR outputs with human preferences without GT supervision, backed by the RefSR-18K LR-conditioned preference dataset.
