AI.motivation: Current SR evaluation/optimization relies on GT or NR metrics that misalign with human perception; LR inputs offer semantic anchors; multimodal priors can assess semantic plausibility and naturalness; lack of LR-conditioned preference data hampers perceptual-aligned SR.
AI.method: Fine-tune a Multimodal LLM with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using LR-conditioned ranking rewards; construct RefSR-18K, an LR-conditioned SR preference dataset with pairwise rankings based on LR-HR consistency and HR naturalness; integrate RefReward-SR as the core reward signal to guide SR training toward semantic consistency and perceptual plausibility.
AI.result: Experiments show substantially better alignment with human judgments, producing SR reconstructions that preserve semantic consistency and enhance perceptual plausibility and naturalness; code, models, and datasets to be released.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a paradigm shift in SR evaluation and optimization by leveraging LR-conditioned, perception-aligned rewards learned via MLLMs, reducing GT dependence and improving human-consistency; resources to be released upon acceptance.

title: CliPPER: Contextual Video-Language Pretraining on Long-form Intraoperative Surgical Procedures for Event Recognition
summary: Video-language foundation models have proven to be highly effective in zero-shot applications across a wide range of tasks. A particularly challenging area is the intraoperative surgical procedure domain, where labeled data is scarce, and precise temporal understanding is often required for complex downstream tasks. To address this challenge, we introduce CliPPER (Contextual Video-Language Pretraining on Long-form Intraoperative Surgical Procedures for Event Recognition), a novel video-language pretraining framework trained on surgical lecture videos. Our method is designed for fine-grained temporal video-text recognition and introduces several novel pretraining strategies to improve multimodal alignment in long-form surgical videos. Specifically, we propose Contextual Video-Text Contrastive Learning (VTC_CTX) and Clip Order Prediction (COP) pretraining objectives, both of which leverage temporal and contextual dependencies to enhance local video understanding. In addition, we incorporate a Cycle-Consistency Alignment over video-text matches within the same surgical video to enforce bidirectional consistency and improve overall representation coherence. Moreover, we introduce a more refined alignment loss, Frame-Text Matching (FTM), to improve the alignment between video frames and text. As a result, our model establishes a new state-of-the-art across multiple public surgical benchmarks, including zero-shot recognition of phases, steps, instruments, and triplets. The source code and pretraining captions can be found at https://github.com/CAMMA-public/CliPPER.

AI.tldr: A long-form intraoperative video-language pretraining framework (CliPPER) with novel objectives (VTC_CTX, COP, cycle-consistency, Frame-Text Matching) that achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot temporal recognition (phases, steps, instruments, triplets) on surgical benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Labeled data in the intraoperative domain is scarce, and precise temporal understanding is essential for complex downstream tasks; long-form videos require strong multimodal alignment across time.
AI.method: Pretrain on surgical lecture videos using: (1) Contextual Video-Text Contrastive Learning (VTC_CTX) to leverage temporal and contextual cues; (2) Clip Order Prediction (COP) to model sequence structure; (3) Cycle-Consistency Alignment enforcing bidirectional video-text matches within the same video; (4) Frame-Text Matching (FTM) for refined frame-to-text alignment; overall aim is fine-grained temporal video-text recognition for zero-shot tasks.
AI.result: Achieves new state-of-the-art performance on multiple public surgical benchmarks, including zero-shot recognition of phases, steps, instruments, and triplets; code and captions available at the project repository.
AI.conclusion: The proposed contextual and temporal alignment objectives improve multimodal learning in long-form surgical videos, yielding robust zero-shot temporal recognition and coherent video-text representations.

title: SEGAR: Selective Enhancement for Generative Augmented Reality
summary: Generative world models offer a compelling foundation for augmented-reality (AR) applications: by predicting future image sequences that incorporate deliberate visual edits, they enable temporally coherent, augmented future frames that can be computed ahead of time and cached, avoiding per-frame rendering from scratch in real time. In this work, we present SEGAR, a preliminary framework that combines a diffusion-based world model with a selective correction stage to support this vision. The world model generates augmented future frames with region-specific edits while preserving others, and the correction stage subsequently aligns safety-critical regions with real-world observations while preserving intended augmentations elsewhere. We demonstrate this pipeline in driving scenarios as a representative setting where semantic region structure is well defined and real-world feedback is readily available. We view this as an early step toward generative world models as practical AR infrastructure, where future frames can be generated, cached, and selectively corrected on demand.

AI.tldr: SEGAR integrates a diffusion-based world model with a selective correction stage to generate and cache augmented future frames for AR, particularly in driving scenarios, enabling region-specific edits while aligning critical regions with real observations.
AI.motivation: To enable temporally coherent, pre-rendered augmented reality in real-time applications by reducing on-demand rendering, while preserving safety-critical correctness through a corrective step.
AI.method: A diffusion-based world model generates augmented frames with region-specific edits; a subsequent selective correction stage aligns safety-critical regions with real-world observations without disrupting other augmentations.
AI.result: Demonstrated in driving scenarios where semantic regions are well defined and real-world feedback is available, illustrating feasibility of generative AR pipelines that can generate, cache, and selectively correct future frames.
AI.conclusion: Generative world models can serve as practical AR infrastructure by enabling pre-generated, cached future frames that are selectively corrected on demand, representing an early step toward deployable AR in dynamic environments.

title: HEART-PFL: Stable Personalized Federated Learning under Heterogeneity with Hierarchical Directional Alignment and Adversarial Knowledge Transfer
comment: Accepted at WACV 2026. 8 pages, 7 figures, 3 tables
summary: Personalized Federated Learning (PFL) aims to deliver effective client-specific models under heterogeneous distributions, yet existing methods suffer from shallow prototype alignment and brittle server-side distillation. We propose HEART-PFL, a dual-sided framework that (i) performs depth-aware Hierarchical Directional Alignment (HDA) using cosine similarity in the early stage and MSE matching in the deep stage to preserve client specificity, and (ii) stabilizes global updates through Adversarial Knowledge Transfer (AKT) with symmetric KL distillation on clean and adversarial proxy data. Using lightweight adapters with only 1.46M trainable parameters, HEART-PFL achieves state-of-the-art personalized accuracy on CIFAR-100, Flowers-102, and Caltech-101 (63.42%, 84.23%, and 95.67%, respectively) under Dirichlet non-IID partitions, and remains robust to out-of-domain proxy data. Ablation studies further confirm that HDA and AKT provide complementary gains in alignment, robustness, and optimization stability, offering insights into how the two components mutually reinforce effective personalization. Overall, these results demonstrate that HEART-PFL simultaneously enhances personalization and global stability, highlighting its potential as a strong and scalable solution for PFL(code available at https://github.com/danny0628/HEART-PFL).

AI.tldr: HEART-PFL is a dual-sided personalized federated learning framework that combines depth-aware Hierarchical Directional Alignment (HDA) and Adversarial Knowledge Transfer (AKT) with lightweight adapters to improve client-specific models and global stability under Dirichlet non-IID data, achieving state-of-the-art results and robustness to proxy data.
AI.motivation: To overcome shallow prototype alignment and brittle server-side distillation in existing PFL methods by introducing a dual-sided approach that preserves client specificity while stabilizing global updates.
AI.method: Propose HEART-PFL with two components: (i) depth-aware Hierarchical Directional Alignment (HDA) using cosine similarity in the early (shallow) stage and MSE matching in the deep stage; (ii) Adversarial Knowledge Transfer (AKT) with symmetric KL distillation on clean and adversarial proxy data. Uses lightweight adapters with 1.46M trainable parameters. Code available.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art personalized accuracy on CIFAR-100, Flowers-102, and Caltech-101 under Dirichlet non-IID partitions (63.42%, 84.23%, and 95.67%, respectively) and demonstrates robustness to out-of-domain proxy data. Ablations show HDA and AKT provide complementary gains in alignment, robustness, and optimization stability.
AI.conclusion: HEART-PFL delivers simultaneous personalization and global stability improvements, offering a scalable solution for PFL. Ablation confirms complementary roles of HDA and AKT, and the framework shows strong empirical gains across multiple datasets.

title: LensWalk: Agentic Video Understanding by Planning How You See in Videos
summary: The dense, temporal nature of video presents a profound challenge for automated analysis. Despite the use of powerful Vision-Language Models, prevailing methods for video understanding are limited by the inherent disconnect between reasoning and perception: they rely on static, pre-processed information and cannot actively seek raw evidence from video as their understanding evolves. To address this, we introduce LensWalk, a flexible agentic framework that empowers a Large Language Model reasoner to control its own visual observation actively. LensWalk establishes a tight reason-plan-observe loop where the agent dynamically specifies, at each step, the temporal scope and sampling density of the video it observes. Using a suite of versatile, Vision-Language Model based tools parameterized by these specifications, the agent can perform broad scans for cues, focus on specific segments for fact extraction, and stitch evidence from multiple moments for holistic verification. This design allows for progressive, on-demand evidence gathering that directly serves the agent's evolving chain of thought. Without requiring any model fine-tuning, LensWalk delivers substantial, plug-and-play performance gains on multiple model recipes, boosting their accuracy by over 5\% on challenging long-video benchmarks like LVBench and Video-MME. Our analysis reveals that enabling an agent to control how it sees is key to unlocking more accurate, robust, and interpretable video reasoning.

AI.tldr: LensWalk enables an LLM-based agent to actively control visual observation in video reasoning via a reason-plan-observe loop. It dynamically specifies temporal scope and sampling density, using VLM-based tools to scan, extract, and stitch evidence across moments, achieving plug-and-play gains without fine-tuning (≈+5% on LVBench and Video-MME).
AI.motivation: Video understanding faces a dense temporal structure and a mismatch between reasoning and perception. Current methods rely on static, pre-processed cues and cannot actively seek raw evidence as reasoning unfolds, limiting accuracy and interpretability.
AI.method: Introduce LensWalk: a flexible agentic framework where an LLM reasoner issues plan and observation instructions. The loop consists of reasoning, planning, and observing, with the agent specifying temporal scope and sampling density for video observations. Use VLM-based tools parameterized by these specs to perform broad scans, targeted fact extraction, and multi-moment evidence stitching. No model fine-tuning required; plug-and-play integration with existing models.
AI.result: Significant performance gains on long-video benchmarks LVBench and Video-MME, with accuracy improvements over 5%. Analysis indicates that enabling the agent to control perception improves accuracy, robustness, and interpretability of video reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Allowing an agent to control how it sees is a key driver of improved video reasoning, yielding more accurate, robust, and interpretable results without fine-tuning, via the LensWalk framework.

title: Scaling Recurrence-aware Foundation Models for Clinical Records via Next-Visit Prediction
summary: While large-scale pretraining has revolutionized language modeling, its potential remains underexplored in healthcare with structured electronic health records (EHRs). We present RAVEN, a novel generative pretraining strategy for sequential EHR data based on Recurrence-Aware next-Visit EveNt prediction. Leveraging a dataset of over one million unique individuals, our model learns to autoregressively generate tokenized clinical events for the next visit conditioned on patient history. We introduce regularization on predicting repeated events and highlight a key pitfall in EHR-based foundation model evaluations: repeated event tokens can inflate performance metrics when new onsets are not distinguished from subsequent occurrences. Furthermore, we empirically investigate the scaling behaviors in a data-constrained, compute-saturated regime, showing that simply increasing model size is suboptimal without commensurate increases in data volume. We evaluate our model via zero-shot prediction for forecasting the incidence of a diverse set of diseases, where it rivals fully fine-tuned representation-based Transformer models and outperforms widely used simulation-based next-token approaches. Finally, without additional parameter updates, we show that RAVEN can generalize to an external patient cohort under lossy clinical code mappings and feature coverage gaps.

AI.tldr: RAVEN is a recurrence-aware generative pretraining method for sequential EHRs that predicts next-visit events; it introduces regularization for repeated events, addresses evaluation pitfalls where repeated tokens inflate metrics, analyzes data/data-scale dynamics, achieves strong zero-shot disease forecasting, and generalizes to external cohorts without extra training.
AI.motivation: To explore and harness large-scale pretraining for structured electronic health records (EHRs) to improve next-visit event prediction and clinical forecasting, while identifying eval pitfalls and scaling behavior in data-constrained regimes.
AI.method: Autoregressive generation of tokenized clinical events for the next visit conditioned on patient history using a recurrence-aware architecture (RAVEN); regularization to discourage over-predicting repeated events; evaluation via zero-shot disease incidence forecasting; analysis of scaling in data-constrained, compute-saturated regimes; assessment of cross-cohort/generalization with lossy code mappings.
AI.result: RAVEN achieves competitive zero-shot disease forecasting compared to fine-tuned representation-based Transformer models and outperforms simulation-based next-token approaches; demonstrates robustness to data quality gaps by generalizing to an external patient cohort under lossy mappings without additional parameter updates; highlights that larger models require commensurate data to realize benefits.
AI.conclusion: The study substantiates the value of recurrence-aware pretraining for sequential EHR data, reveals evaluation pitfalls specific to repeated tokens, and underscores data-volume considerations for scaling EHR foundation models while offering practical cross-cohort generalization without extra training.

title: RVLM: Recursive Vision-Language Models with Adaptive Depth
summary: Medical AI systems face two fundamental limitations. First, conventional vision-language models (VLMs) perform single-pass inference, yielding black-box predictions that cannot be audited or explained in clinical terms. Second, iterative reasoning systems that expose intermediate steps rely on fixed iteration budgets wasting compute on simple cases while providing insufficient depth for complex ones. We address both limitations with a unified framework. RVLM replaces single-pass inference with an iterative generate-execute loop: at each step, the model writes Python code, invokes vision sub-agents, manipulates images, and accumulates evidence. Every diagnostic claim is grounded in executable code, satisfying auditability requirements of clinical AI governance frameworks. RRouter makes iteration depth adaptive: a lightweight controller predicts the optimal budget from task-complexity features, then monitors progress and terminates early when reasoning stalls. We evaluate on BraTS 2023 Meningioma (brain MRI) and MIMIC-CXR (chest X-ray) using Gemini 2.5 Flash without fine-tuning. Across repeated runs, RVLM shows high consistency on salient findings (e.g., mass presence and enhancement) and can detect cross-modal discrepancies between Fluid-Attenuated Inversion Recovery (FLAIR) signal characteristics and segmentation boundaries. On MIMIC-CXR, it generates structured reports and correctly recognises view-specific artefacts. Code: https://github.com/nican2018/rvlm.

AI.tldr: Introduces RVLM and RRouter, an auditable, adaptive iterative framework for medical AI that generates executable code and uses vision sub-agents; validated on BraTS 2023 Meningioma and MIMIC-CXR with Gemini 2.5 Flash, showing consistency and artifact reporting without fine-tuning; code available.
AI.motivation: Address the dual limitations of medical VLMs: (1) single-pass, black-box predictions that are hard to audit; (2) fixed-iteration reasoning that wastes compute or lacks depth. The goal is auditable, interpretable, and efficient clinical AI reasoning.
AI.method: RVLM employs an iterative generate-execute loop where at each step the model writes Python code, invokes vision sub-agents, manipulates images, and accumulates evidence with every diagnostic claim grounded in executable code. RRouter provides adaptive iteration depth via a lightweight controller that predicts the optimal budget from task features and terminates when reasoning stalls.
AI.result: Demonstrates high consistency on salient findings (e.g., mass presence and enhancement) and ability to detect cross-modal discrepancies between FLAIR characteristics and segmentation. On MIMIC-CXR, generates structured reports and recognizes view-specific artefacts. Evaluated on BraTS 2023 Meningioma and MIMIC-CXR using Gemini 2.5 Flash without fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The unified RVLM+RRouter framework delivers auditable, adaptive multi-step reasoning for medical imaging, enabling cross-modal checks and structured outputs without fine-tuning; code is publicly available.

title: Anti-I2V: Safeguarding your photos from malicious image-to-video generation
summary: Advances in diffusion-based video generation models, while significantly improving human animation, poses threats of misuse through the creation of fake videos from a specific person's photo and text prompts. Recent efforts have focused on adversarial attacks that introduce crafted perturbations to protect images from diffusion-based models. However, most existing approaches target image generation, while relatively few explicitly address image-to-video diffusion models (VDMs), and most primarily focus on UNet-based architectures. Hence, their effectiveness against Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models remains largely under-explored, as these models demonstrate improved feature retention, and stronger temporal consistency due to larger capacity and advanced attention mechanisms. In this work, we introduce Anti-I2V, a novel defense against malicious human image-to-video generation, applicable across diverse diffusion backbones. Instead of restricting noise updates to the RGB space, Anti-I2V operates in both the $L$*$a$*$b$* and frequency domains, improving robustness and concentrating on salient pixels. We then identify the network layers that capture the most distinct semantic features during the denoising process to design appropriate training objectives that maximize degradation of temporal coherence and generation fidelity. Through extensive validation, Anti-I2V demonstrates state-of-the-art defense performance against diverse video diffusion models, offering an effective solution to the problem.

AI.tldr: A defense (Anti-I2V) against malicious image-to-video diffusion, extending beyond UNet to DiT and other backbones; uses perturbations across L*a*b and frequency domains and targets semantic feature layers to degrade temporal coherence and fidelity, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Mitigate misuse of diffusion-based video generation that animates a person from photos and prompts; existing defenses focus on images or UNet and poorly address Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models.
AI.method: Introduce Anti-I2V; apply perturbations in L*a*b and frequency domains instead of RGB; identify network layers that capture distinct semantic features during denoising and design training objectives to degrade temporal coherence and generation fidelity; provide backbone-agnostic defense across diverse diffusion backbones.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art defense across diverse video diffusion models, including DiT; improves robustness to adversarial attacks and reduces temporal coherence and generation fidelity of attacker outputs.
AI.conclusion: Anti-I2V provides a general, effective defense against image-to-video generation threats across backbones, addressing temporal consistency and fidelity while broadening perturbation domains.

title: InstanceRSR: Real-World Super-Resolution via Instance-Aware Representation Alignment
comment: 4 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. Accepted by ICASSP 2026
summary: Existing real-world super-resolution (RSR) methods based on generative priors have achieved remarkable progress in producing high-quality and globally consistent reconstructions. However, they often struggle to recover fine-grained details of diverse object instances in complex real-world scenes. This limitation primarily arises because commonly adopted denoising losses (e.g., MSE) inherently favor global consistency while neglecting instance-level perception and restoration. To address this issue, we propose InstanceRSR, a novel RSR framework that jointly models semantic information and introduces instance-level feature alignment. Specifically, we employ low-resolution (LR) images as global consistency guidance while jointly modeling image data and semantic segmentation maps to enforce semantic relevance during sampling. Moreover, we design an instance representation learning module to align the diffusion latent space with the instance latent space, enabling instance-aware feature alignment, and further incorporate a scale alignment mechanism to enhance fine-grained perception and detail recovery. Benefiting from these designs, our approach not only generates photorealistic details but also preserves semantic consistency at the instance level. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that InstanceRSR significantly outperforms existing methods in both quantitative metrics and visual quality, achieving new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes InstanceRSR, a diffusion-based real-world super-resolution framework that leverages semantic and instance-level alignment to recover fine-grained details while preserving per-instance semantic consistency, achieving SOTA on real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current RSR methods optimized with standard denoising losses (e.g., MSE) bias toward global consistency and ignore instance-level perception in complex scenes, causing loss of fine-grained details across diverse objects.
AI.method: The framework uses low-resolution (LR) images as global guidance and jointly models image data with semantic segmentation maps to enforce semantic relevance during sampling. It introduces an instance representation learning module to align the diffusion latent space with an instance latent space for instance-aware feature alignment, complemented by a scale alignment mechanism to boost fine-grained perception.
AI.result: Extensive real-world benchmarks show significant improvements over existing methods in both quantitative metrics and visual quality, establishing new state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: InstanceRSR achieves photorealistic detail recovery while preserving semantic consistency at the instance level, demonstrating robust generalization across diverse real-world scenes.

title: VFIG: Vectorizing Complex Figures in SVG with Vision-Language Models
summary: Scalable Vector Graphics (SVG) are an essential format for technical illustration and digital design, offering precise resolution independence and flexible semantic editability. In practice, however, original vector source files are frequently lost or inaccessible, leaving only "flat" rasterized versions (e.g., PNG or JPEG) that are difficult to modify or scale. Manually reconstructing these figures is a prohibitively labor-intensive process, requiring specialized expertise to recover the original geometric intent. To bridge this gap, we propose VFIG, a family of Vision-Language Models trained for complex and high-fidelity figure-to-SVG conversion. While this task is inherently data-driven, existing datasets are typically small-scale and lack the complexity of professional diagrams. We address this by introducing VFIG-DATA, a large-scale dataset of 66K high-quality figure-SVG pairs, curated from a diverse mix of real-world paper figures and procedurally generated diagrams. Recognizing that SVGs are composed of recurring primitives and hierarchical local structures, we introduce a coarse-to-fine training curriculum that begins with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to learn atomic primitives and transitions to reinforcement learning (RL) refinement to optimize global diagram fidelity, layout consistency, and topological edge cases. Finally, we introduce VFIG-BENCH, a comprehensive evaluation suite with novel metrics designed to measure the structural integrity of complex figures. VFIG achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models and performs on par with GPT-5.2, achieving a VLM-Judge score of 0.829 on VFIG-BENCH.

AI.tldr: Proposes VFIG: a scalable Vision-Language Model framework for converting raster figures to vector SVGs, with large-scale dataset VFIG-DATA (66K pairs) and a coarse-to-fine training curriculum (SFT then RL) plus VFIG-BENCH evaluation suite; claims state-of-the-art among open models and parity with GPT-5.2 (VLM-Judge 0.829).
AI.motivation: Recover vector representations from raster diagrams when source files are missing, enabling editability, scalability, and reusability of scientific figures; address data scarcity and diagram complexity.
AI.method: Train VFIG as a family of vision-language models with supervised fine-tuning to learn atomic SVG primitives, followed by reinforcement learning to improve global layout fidelity; create VFIG-DATA by combining real-world paper figures and procedurally generated diagrams; establish VFIG-BENCH with novel metrics for structural integrity.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance among open-source models; on-par with GPT-5.2 in their evaluation; VLM-Judge score 0.829 on VFIG-BENCH.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of coarse-to-fine training for complex figure-to-SVG tasks; provides large-scale dataset and evaluation suite to advance scalable diagram reconstruction; suggests practical applicability and room for further improvements in data diversity and metric design.

title: Polynomial Speedup in Diffusion Models with the Multilevel Euler-Maruyama Method
summary: We introduce the Multilevel Euler-Maruyama (ML-EM) method compute solutions of SDEs and ODEs using a range of approximators $f^1,\dots,f^k$ to the drift $f$ with increasing accuracy and computational cost, only requiring a few evaluations of the most accurate $f^k$ and many evaluations of the less costly $f^1,\dots,f^{k-1}$. If the drift lies in the so-called Harder than Monte Carlo (HTMC) regime, i.e. it requires $ε^{-γ}$ compute to be $ε$-approximated for some $γ>2$, then ML-EM $ε$-approximates the solution of the SDE with $ε^{-γ}$ compute, improving over the traditional EM rate of $ε^{-γ-1}$. In other terms it allows us to solve the SDE at the same cost as a single evaluation of the drift. In the context of diffusion models, the different levels $f^{1},\dots,f^{k}$ are obtained by training UNets of increasing sizes, and ML-EM allows us to perform sampling with the equivalent of a single evaluation of the largest UNet. Our numerical experiments confirm our theory: we obtain up to fourfold speedups for image generation on the CelebA dataset downscaled to 64x64, where we measure a $γ\approx2.5$. Given that this is a polynomial speedup, we expect even stronger speedups in practical applications which involve orders of magnitude larger networks.

AI.tldr: Multilevel Euler-Maruyama (ML-EM) uses a hierarchy of drift approximations to reduce sampling cost in SDEs and diffusion models, achieving ε-approximation with cost ε^{-γ} in HTMC regime (γ>2), improving over EM's ε^{-γ-1}; experiments show up to 4x speedups on CelebA 64x64 with γ≈2.5; potential stronger gains for larger networks.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost of solving SDEs and sampling in diffusion models when the drift is hard to approximate (HTMC). Leverage multi-level idea to reuse cheap approximations and only few evaluations of the expensive model.
AI.method: Construct a hierarchy f^1,...,f^k of drift approximations with increasing accuracy and cost; ML-EM combines evaluations to achieve overall ε-accurate solution with cost ∼ ε^{-γ}; in diffusion models, implement levels by training UNets of increasing size; use MLMC estimator to sample with a single evaluation of the largest UNet.
AI.result: Theoretical improvement: ε cost exponent reduces from γ+1 (standard EM) to γ (ML-EM) for HTMC regime; empirical: up to 4x speedups on CelebA 64x64 with measured γ ≈ 2.5.
AI.conclusion: ML-EM offers substantially cheaper SDE solutions and faster diffusion-model sampling in HTMC settings; gains scale with γ and network size; promising direction for large-scale models and applications.

title: B-MoE: A Body-Part-Aware Mixture-of-Experts "All Parts Matter" Approach to Micro-Action Recognition
summary: Micro-actions, fleeting and low-amplitude motions, such as glances, nods, or minor posture shifts, carry rich social meaning but remain difficult for current action recognition models to recognize due to their subtlety, short duration, and high inter-class ambiguity. In this paper, we introduce B-MoE, a Body-part-aware Mixture-of-Experts framework designed to explicitly model the structured nature of human motion. In B-MoE, each expert specializes in a distinct body region (head, body, upper limbs, lower limbs), and is based on the lightweight Macro-Micro Motion Encoder (M3E) that captures long-range contextual structure and fine-grained local motion. A cross-attention routing mechanism learns inter-region relationships and dynamically selects the most informative regions for each micro-action. B-MoE uses a dual-stream encoder that fuses these region-specific semantic cues with global motion features to jointly capture spatially localized cues and temporally subtle variations that characterize micro-actions. Experiments on three challenging benchmarks (MA-52, SocialGesture, and MPII-GroupInteraction) show consistent state-of-theart gains, with improvements in ambiguous, underrepresented, and low amplitude classes.

AI.tldr: B-MoE is a body-part-aware Mixture-of-Experts model for micro-action recognition. It assigns an expert to each body region, uses a Macro-Micro Motion Encoder (M3E) for long-range and local motion, and a cross-attention routing mechanism to select informative regions, fused with global features; achieves state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Micro-actions are subtle, brief, and exhibit high inter-class ambiguity, making them difficult for conventional models. A structured, region-aware approach with long-range context is needed to capture nuanced motion.
AI.method: B-MoE partitions the model into region-specific experts for head, body, upper limbs, and lower limbs, each built on M3E. A cross-attention routing module learns inter-region relations and selects the most informative regions per micro-action. A dual-stream encoder fuses region-specific cues with global motion features.
AI.result: Experiments on MA-52, SocialGesture, and MPII-GroupInteraction show consistent state-of-the-art gains, particularly improving ambiguous, underrepresented, and low-amplitude micro-actions.
AI.conclusion: A region-aware MoE with cross-region routing and dual-stream fusion effectively captures spatially localized cues and temporally subtle variations, advancing micro-action recognition.

title: Memory-Augmented Vision-Language Agents for Persistent and Semantically Consistent Object Captioning
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often yield inconsistent descriptions of the same object across viewpoints, hindering the ability of embodied agents to construct consistent semantic representations over time. Previous methods resolved inconsistencies using offline multi-view aggregation or multi-stage pipelines that decouple exploration, data association, and caption learning, with limited capacity to reason over previously observed objects. In this paper, we introduce a unified, memory-augmented Vision-Language agent that simultaneously handles data association, object captioning, and exploration policy within a single autoregressive framework. The model processes the current RGB observation, a top-down explored map, and an object-level episodic memory serialized into object-level tokens, ensuring persistent object identity and semantic consistency across extended sequences. To train the model in a self-supervised manner, we collect a dataset in photorealistic 3D environments using a disagreement-based policy and a pseudo-captioning model that enforces consistency across multi-view caption histories. Extensive evaluation on a manually annotated object-level test set, demonstrate improvements of up to +11.86% in standard captioning scores and +7.39% in caption self-similarity over baseline models, while enabling scalable performance through a compact scene representation. Code, model weights, and data are available at https://github.com/hsp-iit/epos-vlm

AI.tldr: A memory-augmented autoregressive VLM that unifies data association, captioning, and exploration for embodied agents, using object-level episodic memory tokens and a discrepancy-based self-supervised training regime; yields stronger captioning and caption consistency.
AI.motivation: Addresses inconsistent object descriptions across viewpoints in embodied vision-language systems due to viewpoint changes and long-term object identity drift. Prior work relies on offline multi-view aggregation or decoupled pipelines with limited cross-time reasoning.
AI.method: A single autoregressive model ingests the current RGB observation, a top-down explored map, and object-level episodic memory serialized into tokens to preserve persistent object identity across long sequences. Trained in self-supervised fashion using a disagreement-based policy and a pseudo-captioning model that enforces consistency across multi-view caption histories in photorealistic 3D environments.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation on a manually annotated object-level test set shows improvements up to +11.86% in standard captioning metrics and +7.39% in caption self-similarity over baseline models. Benefits include scalable performance via a compact scene representation; data and code are released.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates improved caption fidelity and cross-view semantic consistency in embodied settings, enabling scalable, memory-aware VLMs for long-horizon perception and exploration.

title: EndoVGGT: GNN-Enhanced Depth Estimation for Surgical 3D Reconstruction
summary: Accurate 3D reconstruction of deformable soft tissues is essential for surgical robotic perception. However, low-texture surfaces, specular highlights, and instrument occlusions often fragment geometric continuity, posing a challenge for existing fixed-topology approaches. To address this, we propose EndoVGGT, a geometry-centric framework equipped with a Deformation-aware Graph Attention (DeGAT) module. Rather than using static spatial neighborhoods, DeGAT dynamically constructs feature-space semantic graphs to capture long-range correlations among coherent tissue regions. This enables robust propagation of structural cues across occlusions, enforcing global consistency and improving non-rigid deformation recovery. Extensive experiments on SCARED show that our method significantly improves fidelity, increasing PSNR by 24.6% and SSIM by 9.1% over prior state-of-the-art. Crucially, EndoVGGT exhibits strong zero-shot cross-dataset generalization to the unseen SCARED and EndoNeRF domains, confirming that DeGAT learns domain-agnostic geometric priors. These results highlight the efficacy of dynamic feature-space modeling for consistent surgical 3D reconstruction.

AI.tldr: EndoVGGT introduces a deformation-aware graph attention module (DeGAT) that dynamically builds feature-space graphs to capture long-range tissue correlations, enabling robust propagation of structural cues across occlusions for deformable 3D reconstruction; achieves state-of-the-art gains and strong zero-shot generalization on SCARED and EndoNeRF datasets.
AI.motivation: Overcome fragmentation of geometric continuity caused by low-texture surfaces, specular highlights, and instrument occlusions in surgical tissue reconstruction. Fixed-topology methods struggle with non-rigid deformation and occlusions.
AI.method: EndoVGGT with a Deformation-aware Graph Attention (DeGAT) module that constructs dynamic, feature-space semantic graphs, rather than fixed spatial neighborhoods, to model long-range correlations among coherent tissue regions and enforce global consistency during non-rigid deformation recovery.
AI.result: Significant fidelity gains on SCARED (PSNR +24.6%, SSIM +9.1% over prior state-of-the-art). Demonstrates strong zero-shot cross-dataset generalization to unseen SCARED and EndoNeRF domains, indicating that DeGAT learns domain-agnostic geometric priors.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic feature-space modeling via DeGAT provides robust, global-consistency-enforcing reconstruction for deformable tissues and yields strong cross-domain generalization, highlighting the value of semantic-graph approaches in surgical 3D reconstruction.

title: ScrollScape: Unlocking 32K Image Generation With Video Diffusion Priors
summary: While diffusion models excel at generating images with conventional dimensions, pushing them to synthesize ultra-high-resolution imagery at extreme aspect ratios (EAR) often triggers catastrophic structural failures, such as object repetition and spatial fragmentation.This limitation fundamentally stems from a lack of robust spatial priors, as static text-to-image models are primarily trained on image distributions with conventional dimensions.To overcome this bottleneck, we present ScrollScape, a novel framework that reformulates EAR image synthesis into a continuous video generation process through two core innovations.By mapping the spatial expansion of a massive canvas to the temporal evolution of video frames, ScrollScape leverages the inherent temporal consistency of video models as a powerful global constraint to ensure long-range structural integrity.Specifically, Scanning Positional Encoding (ScanPE) distributes global coordinates across frames to act as a flexible moving camera, while Scrolling Super-Resolution (ScrollSR) leverages video super-resolution priors to circumvent memory bottlenecks, efficiently scaling outputs to an unprecedented 32K resolution. Fine-tuned on a curated 3K multi-ratio image dataset, ScrollScape effectively aligns pre-trained video priors with the EAR generation task. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that it significantly outperforms existing image-diffusion baselines by eliminating severe localized artifacts. Consequently, our method overcomes inherent structural bottlenecks to ensure exceptional global coherence and visual fidelity across diverse domains at extreme scales.

AI.tldr: ScrollScape reframes ultra-wide image synthesis as continuous video generation, leveraging video priors (ScanPE and ScrollSR) to achieve up to 32K resolution with improved global coherence over prior image-diffusion baselines.
AI.motivation: Extreme aspect ratio (EAR) image synthesis with diffusion models suffers from structural failures due to weak or absent global spatial priors, as models are trained on conventional image dimensions; this leads to object repetition and fragmentation at EAR.
AI.method: Transform spatial expansion into temporal video progression. Introduce Scanning Positional Encoding (ScanPE) to distribute global coordinates across frames as a moving camera; introduce Scrolling Super-Resolution (ScrollSR) to utilize video SR priors for memory-efficient, scalable upscaling (up to 32K). Fine-tune on a curated 3K multi-ratio image dataset to align video priors with EAR generation.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show ScrollScape significantly outperforms image-diffusion baselines by reducing severe localized artifacts and improving global coherence and visual fidelity across diverse domains at extreme scales.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging video priors and continuous-frame generation, ScrollScape overcomes fundamental long-range structural bottlenecks in EAR synthesis, enabling high-quality, globally coherent outputs up to 32K and broad-domain applicability.

title: TopoMesh: High-Fidelity Mesh Autoencoding via Topological Unification
summary: The dominant paradigm for high-fidelity 3D generation relies on a VAE-Diffusion pipeline, where the VAE's reconstruction capability sets a firm upper bound on generation quality. A fundamental challenge limiting existing VAEs is the representation mismatch between ground-truth meshes and network predictions: GT meshes have arbitrary, variable topology, while VAEs typically predict fixed-structure implicit fields (\eg, SDF on regular grids). This inherent misalignment prevents establishing explicit mesh-level correspondences, forcing prior work to rely on indirect supervision signals such as SDF or rendering losses. Consequently, fine geometric details, particularly sharp features, are poorly preserved during reconstruction. To address this, we introduce TopoMesh, a sparse voxel-based VAE that unifies both GT and predicted meshes under a shared Dual Marching Cubes (DMC) topological framework. Specifically, we convert arbitrary input meshes into DMC-compliant representations via a remeshing algorithm that preserves sharp edges using an L$\infty$ distance metric. Our decoder outputs meshes in the same DMC format, ensuring that both predicted and target meshes share identical topological structures. This establishes explicit correspondences at the vertex and face level, allowing us to derive explicit mesh-level supervision signals for topology, vertex positions, and face orientations with clear gradients. Our sparse VAE architecture employs this unified framework and is trained with Teacher Forcing and progressive resolution training for stable and efficient convergence. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TopoMesh significantly outperforms existing VAEs in reconstruction fidelity, achieving superior preservation of sharp features and geometric details.

AI.tldr: TopoMesh is a sparse voxel-based VAE that unifies ground-truth and predicted meshes under a Dual Marching Cubes topology to enable explicit mesh-level supervision, yielding improved sharp-feature preservation and reconstruction fidelity.
AI.motivation: To solve the representation mismatch between arbitrary-topology ground-truth meshes and VAEs that predict fixed-structure implicit fields, which prevents explicit mesh correspondences and harms fine geometric detail.
AI.method: Remesh arbitrary input meshes into Dual Marching Cubes (DMC) representations with an L∞-distance preserving remeshing; a decoder outputs DMC-compliant meshes; use a sparse VAE with Teacher Forcing and progressive resolution training to stabilize convergence.
AI.result: Significant improvement over existing VAEs in reconstruction fidelity, with enhanced preservation of sharp features and geometric details.
AI.conclusion: Provides explicit vertex/face-level correspondences and topology-aware supervision via a unified DMC framework, enabling higher-quality mesh reconstructions and broader applicability to topology-agnostic meshes.

title: VERIA: Verification-Centric Multimodal Instance Augmentation for Long-Tailed 3D Object Detection
summary: Long-tail distributions in driving datasets pose a fundamental challenge for 3D perception, as rare classes exhibit substantial intra-class diversity yet available samples cover this variation space only sparsely. Existing instance augmentation methods based on copy-paste or asset libraries improve rare-class exposure but are often limited in fine-grained diversity and scene-context placement. We propose VERIA, an image-first multimodal augmentation framework that synthesizes synchronized RGB--LiDAR instances using off-the-shelf foundation models and curates them with sequential semantic and geometric verification. This verification-centric design tends to select instances that better match real LiDAR statistics while spanning a wider range of intra-class variation. Stage-wise yield decomposition provides a log-based diagnostic of pipeline reliability. On nuScenes and Lyft, VERIA improves rare-class 3D object detection in both LiDAR-only and multimodal settings. Our code is available at https://sgvr.kaist.ac.kr/VERIA/.

AI.tldr: VERIA is an image-first multimodal data augmentation framework that synthesizes synchronized RGB-LiDAR instances to address long-tail rare classes in 3D detection, using foundation models and sequential semantic/geometric verification, with stage-wise yield diagnostics; it improves rare-class detection on nuScenes and Lyft in LiDAR-only and multimodal setups.
AI.motivation: Long-tail distributions in driving datasets yield high intra-class diversity with sparse samples for rare classes, hindering robust 3D perception; existing copy-paste/asset-aug methods lack fine-grained diversity and proper scene-context alignment with LiDAR statistics.
AI.method: Generate synchronized RGB-LiDAR synthetic instances via off-the-shelf foundation models; apply sequential semantic and geometric verification to curate instances that match LiDAR statistics and cover intra-class variation; use stage-wise yield decomposition for reliability diagnostics.
AI.result: Empirical improvement in rare-class 3D object detection on nuScenes and Lyft, for both LiDAR-only and multimodal settings; code released.
AI.conclusion: The verification-centric, image-first augmentation broadens intra-class variation while aligning with LiDAR statistics, improving rare-class detection and providing diagnostic insight into pipeline reliability.

title: AMIF: Authorizable Medical Image Fusion Model with Built-in Authentication
summary: Multimodal image fusion enables precise lesion localization and characterization for accurate diagnosis, thereby strengthening clinical decision-making and driving its growing prominence in medical imaging research. A powerful multimodal image fusion model relies on high-quality, clinically representative multimodal training data and a rigorously engineered model architecture. Therefore, the development of such professional radiomics models represents a collaborative achievement grounded in standardized acquisition, clinical-specific expertise, and algorithmic design proficiency, which necessitates protection of associated intellectual property rights. However, current multimodal image fusion models generate fused outputs without built-in mechanisms to safeguard intellectual property rights, inadvertently exposing proprietary model knowledge and sensitive training data through inference leakage. For example, malicious users can exploit fusion outputs and model distillation or other inference-based reverse engineering techniques to approximate the fusion performance of proprietary models. To address this issue, we propose AMIF, the first Authorizable Medical Image Fusion model with built-in authentication, which integrates authorization access control into the image fusion objective. For unauthorized usage, AMIF embeds explicit and visible copyright identifiers into fusion results. In contrast, high-quality fusion results are accessible upon successful key-based authentication.

AI.tldr: Proposes AMIF, an authorized medical image fusion model with built-in authentication to protect IP and data; unauthorized usage is deterred by visible copyright in outputs and key-based access yields high-quality results.
AI.motivation: Mitigate IP leakage and safeguard proprietary multimodal fusion models and training data while enabling legitimate clinical use; address need for standardized acquisition and clinical expertise in radiomics.
AI.method: Incorporates authorization into the fusion objective; embeds copyright identifiers in fusion outputs for unauthorized use; requires key-based authentication for high-quality fusion results.
AI.result: Conceptual framework and architectural proposal; no empirical results reported in abstract; demonstrates mechanism for authorization integration and output watermarking.
AI.conclusion: AMIF offers a path to protect intellectual property in medical image fusion by coupling authorization with fusion objectives and secure access, balancing collaboration with IP protection.

title: Heuristic Self-Paced Learning for Domain Adaptive Semantic Segmentation under Adverse Conditions
summary: The learning order of semantic classes significantly impacts unsupervised domain adaptation for semantic segmentation, especially under adverse weather conditions. Most existing curricula rely on handcrafted heuristics (e.g., fixed uncertainty metrics) and follow a static schedule, which fails to adapt to a model's evolving, high-dimensional training dynamics, leading to category bias. Inspired by Reinforcement Learning, we cast curriculum learning as a sequential decision problem and propose an autonomous class scheduler. This scheduler consists of two components: (i) a high-dimensional state encoder that maps the model's training status into a latent space and distills key features indicative of progress, and (ii) a category-fair policy-gradient objective that ensures balanced improvement across classes. Coupled with mixed source-target supervision, the learned class rankings direct the network's focus to the most informative classes at each stage, enabling more adaptive and dynamic learning. It is worth noting that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on three widely used benchmarks (e.g., ACDC, Dark Zurich, and Nighttime Driving) and shows generalization ability in synthetic-to-real semantic segmentation.

AI.tldr: An RL-based autonomous class scheduler for adaptive curriculum learning in unsupervised domain adaptation for semantic segmentation under adverse weather, using a high-dimensional state encoder and category-fair policy-gradient objective to rank and focus on informative classes; achieves state-of-the-art on ACDC, Dark Zurich, and Nighttime Driving and generalizes to synthetic-to-real scenarios.
AI.motivation: Static, handcrafted curricula in unsupervised domain adaptation cause category bias and fail to adapt to the model’s evolving training dynamics, especially under adverse weather conditions; an adaptive, data-driven approach is needed.
AI.method: Formulate curriculum learning as a sequential decision process. Develop a high-dimensional state encoder that summarizes training status into a latent representation and a category-fair policy-gradient objective that ensures balanced improvement across classes. Use mixed source-target supervision. The learned class rankings guide the network to focus on the most informative classes at each training stage.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on three benchmarks (ACDC, Dark Zurich, Nighttime Driving) and demonstrates generalization to synthetic-to-real semantic segmentation.
AI.conclusion: An RL-driven autonomous class scheduler enables adaptive, balanced curriculum learning for unsupervised domain adaptation in semantic segmentation under adverse conditions, leading to strong empirical performance and cross-domain generalization.

title: Le MuMo JEPA: Multi-Modal Self-Supervised Representation Learning with Learnable Fusion Tokens
summary: Self-supervised learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for learning visual representations without manual annotations, yet most methods still operate on a single modality and therefore miss the complementary structure available from heterogeneous sensors. We present Le MuMo JEPA, a self-supervised framework that learns unified representations from RGB images and aligned companion modalities. In our driving experiments, the second modality is camera-aligned LiDAR depth; we also evaluate RGB-thermal training and transfer on the Teledyne FLIR ADAS benchmark. Our approach extends LeJEPA to the multi-modal setting by learning fusion tokens that act as a latent bottleneck between modality-specific patch stems inside a shared transformer. Our default model employs a pruned fusion strategy: after an initial cross-modal attention layer, modality-specific tokens are dropped, forcing cross-modal information into the shared fusion-token grid as an efficient latent bottleneck before Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg) is applied to the joint multimodal CLS embedding. On Waymo, Le MuMo JEPA gives the strongest performance-efficiency trade-off on downstream patch probes among the from-scratch multimodal baselines, improving CenterNet detection and dense depth while remaining competitive on segmentation. Under from-scratch training on nuScenes, Le MuMo JEPA remains the strongest model, and it also gives the best FLIR results, especially after Waymo-initialized fine-tuning. It also retains the best overall accuracy-efficiency balance in our study at substantially lower compute, memory, and estimated training time.

AI.tldr: Multi-modal self-supervised learning for RGB and aligned companion modalities (depth from LiDAR, RGB-thermal). Introduces Le MuMo JEPA, extending LeJEPA with fusion tokens as a latent bottleneck in a shared transformer and a pruned fusion strategy. Demonstrates strong efficiency-accuracy trade-offs on Waymo, nuScenes, and FLIR benchmarks, with best-from-scratch results and robust cross-modal transfer after initialization.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limitation of single-modality SSL by leveraging heterogeneous sensors to capture complementary structure, enabling unified cross-modal representations that improve downstream perception tasks while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: Extend LeJEPA to multi-modal input by learning fusion tokens that form a latent bottleneck between modality-specific patch stems inside a shared transformer. Use a pruned fusion strategy: after an initial cross-modal attention, drop modality-specific tokens so cross-modal information is funneled into the shared fusion-token grid. Apply Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg) to the joint multimodal CLS embedding.
AI.result: On Waymo, achieves the best performance-efficiency among from-scratch multimodal baselines for patch probes, improves CenterNet detection and dense depth, while remaining competitive on segmentation. On nuScenes, is the strongest model in their study and yields the best FLIR results especially after Waymo-initialized fine-tuning. Maintains the best overall accuracy-efficiency balance with substantially lower compute, memory, and estimated training time.
AI.conclusion: Le MuMo JEPA demonstrates that a latent fusion-token bottleneck with a pruned cross-modal strategy and SIGReg enables effective, efficient, unified multi-modal representations for self-supervised learning, yielding strong cross-modal transfer and competitive downstream performance across diverse datasets.

title: PP-OCRv5: A Specialized 5M-Parameter Model Rivaling Billion-Parameter Vision-Language Models on OCR Tasks
summary: The advent of "OCR 2.0" and large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) has set new benchmarks in text recognition. However, these unified architectures often come with significant computational demands, challenges in precise text localization within complex layouts, and a propensity for textual hallucinations. Revisiting the prevailing notion that model scale is the sole path to high accuracy, this paper introduces PP-OCRv5, a meticulously optimized, lightweight OCR system with merely 5 million parameters. We demonstrate that PP-OCRv5 achieves performance competitive with many billion-parameter VLMs on standard OCR benchmarks, while offering superior localization precision and reduced hallucinations. The cornerstone of our success lies not in architectural expansion but in a data-centric investigation. We systematically dissect the role of training data by quantifying three critical dimensions: data difficulty, data accuracy, and data diversity. Our extensive experiments reveal that with a sufficient volume of high-quality, accurately labeled, and diverse data, the performance ceiling for traditional, efficient two-stage OCR pipelines is far higher than commonly assumed. This work provides compelling evidence for the viability of lightweight, specialized models in the large-model era and offers practical insights into data curation for OCR. The source code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/PaddlePaddle/PaddleOCR.

AI.tldr: A 5M-parameter OCR system (PP-OCRv5) can rival billion-parameter VLMs on OCR benchmarks through a data-centric optimization, achieving strong localization with fewer hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Tackle the computational and localization/hallucination challenges of OCR 2.0 unified architectures and challenge the notion that bigger models are the sole path to accuracy.
AI.method: Systematic data-centric study quantifying data difficulty, data accuracy, and data diversity, followed by extensive experiments showing how high-quality, diverse data boosts performance in traditional two-stage OCR pipelines.
AI.result: PP-OCRv5 achieves competitive performance against large VLMs on standard OCR benchmarks with only 5M parameters, exhibits superior text localization and reduced hallucinations, and demonstrates a high performance ceiling for lightweight OCR when data is well-curated.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight, specialized OCR models can approach or match large-model performance through careful data curation; data quality and diversity are key levers for OCR efficiency and accuracy; code and models are publicly available.

title: GeoRouter: Dynamic Paradigm Routing for Worldwide Image Geolocalization
summary: Worldwide image geolocalization aims to predict precise GPS coordinates for images captured anywhere on Earth, which is challenging due to the large visual and geographic diversity. Recent methods mainly follow two paradigms: retrieval-based approaches that match queries against a reference database, and generation-based approaches that directly predict coordinates using Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). However, we observe distinct error profiles between them: retrieval excels at fine-grained instance matching, while generation offers robust semantic reasoning. This complementary heterogeneity suggests that no single paradigm is universally superior. To harness this potential, we propose GeoRouter, a dynamic routing framework that adaptively assigns each query to the optimal paradigm. GeoRouter leverages an LVLM backbone to analyze visual content and provide routing decisions. To optimize GeoRouter, we introduce a distance-aware preference objective that converts the distance gap between paradigms into a continuous supervision signal, explicitly reflecting relative performance differences. Furthermore, we construct GeoRouting, the first large-scale dataset tailored for training routing policies with independent paradigm predictions. Extensive experiments on IM2GPS3k and YFCC4k demonstrate that GeoRouter significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: A dynamic routing framework (GeoRouter) assigns image geolocalization queries to the most appropriate paradigm—retrieval or generation—using an LVLM-based router and a distance-aware loss, trained on a new GeoRouting dataset, and achieving state-of-the-art results on IM2GPS3k and YFCC4k.
AI.motivation: Retrieval-based methods excel at fine-grained instance matching but struggle with semantic/generalization; generation-based methods offer robust semantic reasoning but can lack precision. Their error profiles are complementary, suggesting a combined approach could outperform either paradigm alone.
AI.method: GeoRouter uses an LVLM backbone to analyze visual content and make routing decisions. It introduces a distance-aware preference objective that converts the performance gap between paradigms into a continuous supervisory signal. A new dataset, GeoRouting, provides independent paradigm predictions to train routing policies.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on IM2GPS3k and YFCC4k show that GeoRouter significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating the benefit of adaptive paradigm routing in worldwide image geolocalization.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive routing between retrieval and generation paradigms leverages their complementary strengths, and the GeoRouting dataset enables effective training of such routing policies. This approach advances the state of the art in global image geolocalization and suggests broader multi-paradigm routing opportunities.

title: ViHOI: Human-Object Interaction Synthesis with Visual Priors
summary: Generating realistic and physically plausible 3D Human-Object Interactions (HOI) remains a key challenge in motion generation. One primary reason is that describing these physical constraints with words alone is difficult. To address this limitation, we propose a new paradigm: extracting rich interaction priors from easily accessible 2D images. Specifically, we introduce ViHOI, a novel framework that enables diffusion-based generative models to leverage rich, task-specific priors from 2D images to enhance generation quality. We utilize a large Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a powerful prior-extraction engine and adopt a layer-decoupled strategy to obtain visual and textual priors. Concurrently, we design a Q-Former-based adapter that compresses the VLM's high-dimensional features into compact prior tokens, which significantly facilitates the conditional training of our diffusion model. Our framework is trained on motion-rendered images from the dataset to ensure strict semantic alignment between visual inputs and motion sequences. During inference, it leverages reference images synthesized by a text-to-image generation model to improve generalization to unseen objects and interaction categories. Experimental results demonstrate that ViHOI achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing methods across multiple benchmarks and demonstrating superior generalization.

AI.tldr: ViHOI leverages Vision-Language Model priors from 2D images to guide diffusion-based 3D HOI generation, using a Q-Former adapter and layer-decoupled priors for improved quality and generalization.
AI.motivation: Capturing physical 3D Human-Object Interactions is difficult from text alone; rich priors from 2D images can enforce realism and semantics in motion generation.
AI.method: Extract sequential visual/textual priors via a large Vision-Language Model with a layer-decoupled strategy; compress high-dimensional features into compact prior tokens using a Q-Former-based adapter; train on motion-rendered images to align visuals with motions; during inference, employ reference images from a text-to-image generator to boost generalization to unseen objects/interactions.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple HOI benchmarks and shows strong generalization to unseen objects and interaction categories.
AI.conclusion: ViHOI demonstrates that diffusion-based HOI generation can be substantially enhanced by incorporating compact, task-specific priors from 2D Vision-Language models, enabling better realism and generalization.

title: Causal Transfer in Medical Image Analysis
summary: Medical imaging models frequently fail when deployed across hospitals, scanners, populations, or imaging protocols due to domain shift, limiting their clinical reliability. While transfer learning and domain adaptation address such shifts statistically, they often rely on spurious correlations that break under changing conditions. On the other hand, causal inference provides a principled way to identify invariant mechanisms that remain stable across environments. This survey introduces and systematises Causal Transfer Learning (CTL) for medical image analysis. This paradigm integrates causal reasoning with cross-domain representation learning to enable robust and generalisable clinical AI. We frame domain shift as a causal problem and analyse how structural causal models, invariant risk minimisation, and counterfactual reasoning can be embedded within transfer learning pipelines. We studied spanning classification, segmentation, reconstruction, anomaly detection, and multimodal imaging, and organised them by task, shift type, and causal assumption. A unified taxonomy is proposed that connects causal frameworks and transfer mechanisms. We further summarise datasets, benchmarks, and empirical gains, highlighting when and why causal transfer outperforms correlation-based domain adaptation. Finally, we discuss how CTL supports fairness, robustness, and trustworthy deployment in multi-institutional and federated settings, and outline open challenges and research directions for clinically reliable medical imaging AI.

AI.tldr: Survey of Causal Transfer Learning (CTL) for medical image analysis, combining causal reasoning with cross-domain representation learning to address domain shift (hospital, scanner, protocol) and improve generalization. Presents a unified taxonomy, task-by-task analysis (classification, segmentation, reconstruction, anomaly detection, multimodal imaging), datasets/benchmarks, empirical gains, and implications for fairness and federated settings; outlines open challenges and directions.
AI.motivation: Medical imaging models degrade across domains due to spurious correlations and distribution shifts; causal methods aim to identify invariant mechanisms for robust, generalizable AI.
AI.method: Frame domain shift as a causal problem; discuss structural causal models, invariant risk minimization, and counterfactual reasoning within transfer learning; organize tasks, shift types, and causal assumptions; propose a unified taxonomy linking causal frameworks with transfer mechanisms.
AI.result: Reviews datasets and benchmarks; discusses empirical gains and scenarios where CTL outperforms correlation-based domain adaptation; synthesizes a practical framework for robust medical imaging AI across institutions and protocols.
AI.conclusion: CTL offers a principled path to clinically reliable medical imaging AI in multi-institution and federated settings; highlights open challenges and future research directions for robust, fair, and trustworthy deployment.

title: Teacher-Student Diffusion Model for Text-Driven 3D Hand Motion Generation
summary: Generating realistic 3D hand motion from natural language is vital for VR, robotics, and human-computer interaction. Existing methods either focus on full-body motion, overlooking detailed hand gestures, or require explicit 3D object meshes, limiting generality. We propose TSHaMo, a model-agnostic teacher-student diffusion framework for text-driven hand motion generation. The student model learns to synthesize motions from text alone, while the teacher leverages auxiliary signals (e.g., MANO parameters) to provide structured guidance during training. A co-training strategy enables the student to benefit from the teacher's intermediate predictions while remaining text-only at inference. Evaluated using two diffusion backbones on GRAB and H2O, TSHaMo consistently improves motion quality and diversity. Ablations confirm its robustness and flexibility in using diverse auxiliary inputs without requiring 3D objects at test time.

AI.tldr: TSHaMo introduces a model-agnostic teacher-student diffusion framework for text-driven hand motion generation that does not require 3D object meshes at test time. The teacher uses auxiliary signals (e.g., MANO parameters) to guide training, while the student learns from text alone via a co-training strategy. It yields improved motion quality and diversity on GRAB and H2O, showing robustness across auxiliary inputs and backbones.
AI.motivation: To enable realistic, text-driven 3D hand motion generation with generality beyond explicit 3D object meshes and independent of full-body motion, addressing limitations of existing methods that either omit hand detail or rely on explicit object geometry.
AI.method: A model-agnostic teacher-student diffusion framework (TSHaMo) where the teacher leverages auxiliary signals (e.g., MANO parameters) to provide structured guidance during training. The student learns to synthesize motions from text-only inputs. A co-training strategy lets the student benefit from the teacher’s intermediate predictions while inference uses only text. Evaluated with two diffusion backbones on GRAB and H2O datasets.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in motion quality and diversity over baselines. Ablations demonstrate robustness and flexibility to diverse auxiliary inputs, and that 3D objects are not required at test time.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables effective, generalizable text-driven hand motion generation without relying on explicit 3D object meshes during inference, achieving superior performance and flexibility across datasets and backbones.

title: The Gait Signature of Frailty: Transfer Learning based Deep Gait Models for Scalable Frailty Assessment
summary: Frailty is a condition in aging medicine characterized by diminished physiological reserve and increased vulnerability to stressors. However, frailty assessment remains subjective, heterogeneous, and difficult to scale in clinical practice. Gait is a sensitive marker of biological aging, capturing multisystem decline before overt disability. Yet the application of modern computer vision to gait-based frailty assessment has been limited by small, imbalanced datasets and a lack of clinically representative benchmarks. In this work, we introduce a publicly available silhouette-based frailty gait dataset collected in a clinically realistic setting, spanning the full frailty spectrum and including older adults who use walking aids. Using this dataset, we evaluate how pretrained gait recognition models can be adapted for frailty classification under limited data conditions. We study both convolutional and hybrid attention-based architectures and show that predictive performance depends primarily on how pretrained representations are transferred rather than architectural complexity alone. Across models, selectively freezing low-level gait representations while allowing higher-level features to adapt yields more stable and generalizable performance than either full fine-tuning or rigid freezing. Conservative handling of class imbalance further improves training stability, and combining complementary learning objectives enhances discrimination between clinically adjacent frailty states. Interpretability analyses reveal consistent model attention to lower-limb and pelvic regions, aligning with established biomechanical correlates of frailty. Together, these findings establish gait-based representation learning as a scalable, non-invasive, and interpretable framework for frailty assessment and support the integration of modern biometric modeling approaches into aging research and clinical practice.

AI.tldr: Public silhouette-based gait dataset for frailty across full spectrum; pretrained gait models adapted with selective freezing outperform full fine-tuning; balance handling and multiple objectives improve discrimination; interpretability aligns with biomechanical frailty markers.
AI.motivation: Frailty assessment is subjective and hard to scale; gait is a sensitive, non-invasive biomarker. A publicly available, clinically realistic gait dataset is needed to benchmark pretrained models under limited data and to motivate scalable representation learning for frailty classification.
AI.method: Evaluate convolutional and hybrid attention gait models pretrained on gait recognition. Transfer representations to frailty classification under limited data. Compare freezing strategies (low-level frozen vs full fine-tuning vs rigid freezing), address class imbalance, and combine complementary learning objectives. Perform interpretability analysis of attention. Dataset includes individuals with walking aids and spans full frailty spectrum.
AI.result: Performance depends on transfer strategy rather than architecture. Selectively freezing low-level gait features while allowing high-level features to adapt yields more stable and generalizable results than full fine-tuning or rigid freezing. Conservative handling of class imbalance improves training stability. Combining complementary learning objectives enhances discrimination between adjacent frailty states. Interpretability shows attention to lower-limb and pelvic regions, aligning with biomechanical frailty correlates.
AI.conclusion: Gait-based representation learning offers a scalable, non-invasive, and interpretable approach to frailty assessment, enabling integration of modern biometric modeling into aging research and clinical practice.

title: Unleashing Vision-Language Semantics for Deepfake Video Detection
summary: Recent Deepfake Video Detection (DFD) studies have demonstrated that pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP exhibit strong generalization capabilities in detecting artifacts across different identities. However, existing approaches focus on leveraging visual features only, overlooking their most distinctive strength -- the rich vision-language semantics embedded in the latent space. We propose VLAForge, a novel DFD framework that unleashes the potential of such cross-modal semantics to enhance model's discriminability in deepfake detection. This work i) enhances the visual perception of VLM through a ForgePerceiver, which acts as an independent learner to capture diverse, subtle forgery cues both granularly and holistically, while preserving the pretrained Vision-Language Alignment (VLA) knowledge, and ii) provides a complementary discriminative cue -- Identity-Aware VLA score, derived by coupling cross-modal semantics with the forgery cues learned by ForgePerceiver. Notably, the VLA score is augmented by an identity prior-informed text prompting to capture authenticity cues tailored to each identity, thereby enabling more discriminative cross-modal semantics. Comprehensive experiments on video DFD benchmarks, including classical face-swapping forgeries and recent full-face generation forgeries, demonstrate that our VLAForge substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods at both frame and video levels. Code is available at https://github.com/mala-lab/VLAForge.

AI.tldr: VLAForge leverages cross-modal semantics from Vision-Language Models to improve deepfake video detection via a ForgePerceiver that learns forgery cues while preserving VLA, plus an Identity-Aware VLA score enhanced by identity-specific prompts, achieving superior frame- and video-level performance.
AI.motivation: To overcome the predominantly visual-feature-based deepfake detection approaches by exploiting rich cross-modal semantics in VLM latent spaces (e.g., CLIP) and maintain pretrained vision-language alignment while learning forgery-specific cues.
AI.method: 1) ForgePerceiver: an independent learner that enhances visual perception to capture granular and holistic forgery cues without eroding pretrained VLA knowledge. 2) Identity-Aware VLA score: couples cross-modal semantics with forgery cues to provide a discriminative cue; augments this score with identity-prior text prompts tailored to each identity to capture authenticity cues.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on video DFD benchmarks including classical face-swapping and recent full-face generation show substantial improvements over SOTA at both frame- and video-level evaluations.
AI.conclusion: VLAForge demonstrates that integrating cross-modal semantics with identity-aware prompting and a purpose-built perceptual module yields superior deepfake detection performance, validating the utility of cross-modal cues in DFD. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

title: OmniWeaving: Towards Unified Video Generation with Free-form Composition and Reasoning
summary: While proprietary systems such as Seedance-2.0 have achieved remarkable success in omni-capable video generation, open-source alternatives significantly lag behind. Most academic models remain heavily fragmented, and the few existing efforts toward unified video generation still struggle to seamlessly integrate diverse tasks within a single framework. To bridge this gap, we propose OmniWeaving, an omni-level video generation model featuring powerful multimodal composition and reasoning-informed capabilities. By leveraging a massive-scale pretraining dataset that encompasses diverse compositional and reasoning-augmented scenarios, OmniWeaving learns to temporally bind interleaved text, multi-image, and video inputs while acting as an intelligent agent to infer complex user intentions for sophisticated video creation. Furthermore, we introduce IntelligentVBench, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to rigorously assess next-level intelligent unified video generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OmniWeaving achieves SoTA performance among open-source unified models. The codes and model will be made publicly available soon. Project Page: https://omniweaving.github.io.

AI.tldr: Open-source unified video generation model OmniWeaving achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source unified models by leveraging multimodal composition and reasoning-based capabilities, supported by the IntelligentVBench benchmark.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where proprietary systems dominate video generation and open-source efforts remain fragmented; a unified, reasoning-enabled framework is needed to handle diverse tasks and infer user intent.
